topics.title: Design of Shafts Based on Bending Moment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design of Shafts Based on Bending Moment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Shaft? — The Formal Definition

A **shaft** is a rotating machine element, usually of circular cross-section, used to transmit power and/or motion between a power-generating element (such as an electric motor, engine, or turbine) and a power-consuming element (such as a gear, pulley, or sprocket). It is one of the most fundamental and critically loaded components in virtually every mechanical power transmission system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Reference):**
> A shaft is defined as a machine member, generally cylindrical, that supports rotating elements (gears, pulleys, etc.) and transmits torque. It is subjected primarily to **bending moments** (from transverse loads like belt pulls, gear forces, and the weight of components) and **torsional moments** (from the transmitted power), along with axial loads in some cases.

## 1.2 Classification of Shafts (KTU High-Yield)

For a 14-mark question, a clear classification table is mandatory. Shafts are broadly classified into four major categories:

| Type of Shaft | Primary Function | Key Characteristic | Typical Application Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transmission Shaft** | Transmits power between input and output machines | Subjected to bending + torsion + axial load | Line shaft in a workshop |
| **Machine Shaft** | Integral part of the machine itself | Subjected to bending + torsion | Crankshaft of an IC engine |
| **Axle (Spindle)** | Supports rotating parts without transmitting torque | Subjected only to bending moment | Railway car axle, vehicle front axle |
| **Line Shaft** | Long shaft supported by pedestals and hangers | Transmits power to multiple machines | Power transmission in factories |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Backbone of the Machine"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Think of a shaft as the **"spinal column" of a machine**. Just as your backbone supports your body, bears weight (bending), allows twisting (torsion), and transmits forces from your limbs to your torso, a shaft does the same for the mechanical system. The gears are like your shoulders and hips, the pulleys are like joints, and the motor is like the brain sending signals (torque) down the spine.
>
> If the backbone breaks — the entire body collapses. If a shaft fails — the entire machine grinds to a halt, often catastrophically. That is why **shaft design is one of the most critical activities in machine design**.

## 1.4 The Core Engineering Challenge in Shaft Design

The fundamental question in designing a shaft based on bending moment is:

> *"Given a set of applied loads (gears, pulleys, belt tensions, weights), what should be the **diameter** of the shaft such that it does not fail under the combined action of bending and torsion?"*

This is the essence of the topic — we **decouple** the complex 3D stress state into an **equivalent single-stress problem** so that the shaft can be designed using standard solid-shaft bending equations.

## 1.5 Loads Acting on a Shaft

A shaft in service is rarely loaded by a single force. It is simultaneously subjected to:

- **Bending Moments (M):** Caused by transverse forces such as belt tensions, gear tangential/radial forces, and the self-weight of mounted components.
- **Torsional Moments (T):** Caused by the torque being transmitted (e.g., $T = \frac{60 \times 10^6 \, P}{2\pi N}$ in N·mm, where $P$ is power in kW and $N$ is rpm).
- **Axial Loads (F):** Caused by helical gears, worm gears, or preloaded bearings (sometimes negligible).
- **Shear Forces (V):** Caused by transverse loads.
- **Dynamic/Impact Loads:** Represented via a **factor of safety** or **shock and fatigue factors** in the design equations.

## 1.6 Standard Materials for Shafts (KTU Board Favourite)

| Material | Ultimate Tensile Strength (Sut) | Yield Strength (Syt) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel (IS 2062) | 400–460 MPa | 250 MPa | General-purpose shafts |
| Medium Carbon Steel (C30, C40, C45) | 500–700 MPa | 350–450 MPa | Transmission shafts, machine shafts |
| Alloy Steel (40 Cr, 40 Ni Cr) | 700–900 MPa | 500–600 MPa | High-stress automotive/aerospace shafts |
| Stainless Steel | 500–700 MPa | 250–350 MPa | Corrosive environments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU board problems, **Medium Carbon Steel (C45)** with $\sigma_t = 600$ MPa is the most commonly used reference material unless specified otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Design Philosophy — Why "Equivalent Bending Moment"?

A shaft is subjected to a **complex 3D stress state**:
- Normal stress $\sigma$ from bending ($\sigma = 32M / \pi d^3$)
- Shear stress $\tau$ from torsion ($\tau = 16T / \pi d^3$)

To design such a shaft, we use a **failure theory** (either Maximum Shear Stress Theory or Distortion Energy Theory) to **combine** these stresses into a single "equivalent" quantity. This converts the biaxial stress problem into an **equivalent uniaxial problem** that we can solve using simple beam formulas.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **heart of shaft design**: the actual 2D stress state is replaced by a single equivalent moment (either bending or torsional), and the shaft is designed as if it were subjected to that single moment alone.

## 2.2 The Two Governing Theories

### Theory I: Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSST) — Used by **ASME Code**

According to MSST (also called **Tresca theory**), yielding begins when the maximum shear stress in a complex stress state reaches the maximum shear stress at yielding in a simple tension test.

The maximum shear stress in a shaft under bending and torsion is:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

Substituting $\sigma = \frac{32M}{\pi d^3}$ and $\tau = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}$:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{16M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{16T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

### Theory II: Distortion Energy Theory (DET) — Used by **British Standard Code**

According to DET (also called **von Mises theory**), yielding begins when the distortion energy per unit volume reaches the value at yielding in a simple tension test.

The maximum shear stress equivalent is:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{M^2 + 0.75 \, T^2}$$

## 2.3 Concept of Equivalent Bending Moment (Me)

The **equivalent bending moment** is defined as the **pure bending moment** which, acting alone on a solid circular shaft, would produce the same maximum shear stress (according to the chosen failure theory) as the actual combined bending and torsion.

**Derivation Logic:**
Let the shaft be subjected to a bending moment $M$ and torque $T$. We want to find a fictitious bending moment $M_e$ that produces the same maximum shear stress as the actual loading.

$$\frac{16 M_e}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \quad \text{(for ASME - MSST)}$$

Solving for $M_e$:

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2} \left[ M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \right] \quad \text{(ASME Code)}$$

For the British Standard (DET):

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2} \left[ M + \sqrt{M^2 + 0.75 \, T^2} \right] \quad \text{(British Standard Code)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor **½** comes from the fact that the maximum bending stress in a beam is $\sigma = 32M/\pi d^3$, but the maximum **shear** stress in a beam (on the neutral axis) is $\tau = 16M/\pi d^3 = \sigma/2$. This is why we equate to bending-shear stress, not bending-normal stress, when converting to an equivalent moment.

## 2.4 Concept of Equivalent Twisting Moment (Te)

The **equivalent twisting moment** is defined as the **pure torque** which, acting alone on a solid circular shaft, would produce the same maximum shear stress as the actual combined loading.

Equating to the torsional shear stress formula $\tau = 16T_e/\pi d^3$:

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \quad \text{(ASME Code)}$$

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + 0.75 \, T^2} \quad \text{(British Standard Code)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For design problems where bending moment is **negligible** (pure torsion, e.g., a short coupling shaft), $M = 0$ and the formulas reduce to $T_e = T$, which is the standard torsion equation.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transmitted Torque | $T = \frac{60 \times 10^6 \, P}{2\pi N}$ | $P$ = power (kW), $N$ = rpm, $T$ = N·mm | Compute torque from power |
| 2 | Torsional Shear Stress | $\tau = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}$ | $\tau$ = MPa, $T$ = N·mm, $d$ = mm | Pure torsion design |
| 3 | Bending Stress (Normal) | $\sigma = \frac{32M}{\pi d^3}$ | $\sigma$ = MPa, $M$ = N·mm | Pure bending design |
| 4 | Max Shear (Bending + Torsion) — ASME | $\tau_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ | MPa | Combined loading (MSST) |
| 5 | Max Shear (Bending + Torsion) — BS | $\tau_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + 0.75 T^2}$ | MPa | Combined loading (DET) |
| 6 | **Equivalent Bending Moment — ASME** | $M_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[ M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \right]$ | N·mm | Decoupling combined load |
| 7 | **Equivalent Bending Moment — BS** | $M_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[ M + \sqrt{M^2 + 0.75 T^2} \right]$ | N·mm | Decoupling combined load |
| 8 | **Equivalent Twisting Moment — ASME** | $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ | N·mm | Decoupling combined load |
| 9 | **Equivalent Twisting Moment — BS** | $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + 0.75 T^2}$ | N·mm | Decoupling combined load |
| 10 | ASME Shaft Diameter Equation | $d^3 = \frac{16}{\pi \, \tau_{allowable}} \cdot \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ | $d$ = mm | Final design diameter |
| 11 | BS Shaft Diameter Equation | $d^3 = \frac{16}{\pi \, \tau_{allowable}} \cdot \sqrt{M^2 + 0.75 T^2}$ | $d$ = mm | Final design diameter |
| 12 | Allowable Shear Stress | $\tau_{allow} = \frac{S_{sy}}{n}$ | $S_{sy}$ = shear yield strength, $n$ = factor of safety | Material-based design |
| 13 | Empirical ASME Equation (with shock factors) | $d^3 = \frac{16}{\pi \, S_{sy}} \left[ (K_m M)^2 + (K_t T)^2 \right]^{1/2}$ | $K_m, K_t$ = shock factors | Practical ASME design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Empirical Values of Shock & Fatigue Factors (ASME Code) — Must Memorize for KTU:**
>
> | Loading Type | Bending Factor $K_m$ | Torsion Factor $K_t$ |
> |---|:---:|:---:|
> | Gradually applied (steady load) | 1.5 | 1.0 |
> | Suddenly applied (light shock) | 1.5 – 2.0 | 1.0 – 1.5 |
> | Suddenly applied (heavy shock) | 2.0 – 3.0 | 1.5 – 3.0 |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

This design methodology is universally used in:
- **Automotive engineering** — drive shafts, half-shafts, propeller shafts.
- **Industrial machinery** — gear boxes, rolling mills, paper machines.
- **Power generation** — turbine shafts, generator rotors.
- **Aerospace** — helicopter rotor masts, turboprop propeller shafts.
- **Pumps and compressors** — centrifugal pump shafts, compressor drive shafts.

The equivalent moment concept is so powerful that it is also used in **crank design**, **bolt design under combined shear and tension**, and **weld design**.

## 2.7 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure for Shaft Design

A KTU examiner will award full marks only if you follow the standard step-by-step procedure. Here is the canonical sequence:

1. **Identify the load system** — list all transverse forces, axial forces, and the torque transmitted.
2. **Compute the torque** $T = \frac{60 \times 10^6 \, P}{2\pi N}$ from the given power and speed.
3. **Draw the bending moment diagram (BMD)** in the vertical plane (due to vertical forces) and in the horizontal plane (due to horizontal forces).
4. **Compute the resultant bending moment** at each critical section using: $M = \sqrt{M_v^2 + M_h^2}$.
5. **Compute the equivalent bending moment** $M_e$ (or equivalent twisting moment $T_e$) using the appropriate code equation.
6. **Determine the allowable shear stress** $\tau_{allow} = S_{sy}/n$ from the material data and factor of safety.
7. **Compute the shaft diameter** $d$ from the ASME or BS equation.
8. **Apply the shock factors** if required (especially for sudden/impact loading).
9. **Add a standard keyway allowance** (typically +5% to +7% on diameter) if a key is to be fitted.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Shear Stress in a Shaft Under Combined Bending and Torsion

We consider a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$ subjected to a bending moment $M$ (about a transverse axis) and a torsional moment $T$ (about the longitudinal axis).

**Step 1:** Identify the stress components on the surface of the shaft (the critical location is the outermost fiber).

The bending moment produces a **normal stress** $\sigma$ that varies linearly across the section. At the extreme fiber:

$$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z_b} = \frac{M}{\pi d^3 / 32} = \frac{32M}{\pi d^3}$$

where $Z_b = \pi d^3 / 32$ is the section modulus for bending of a solid circular cross-section.

The torque produces a **shear stress** $\tau$ that is maximum at the surface:

$$\tau = \frac{T}{Z_t} = \frac{T}{\pi d^3 / 16} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}$$

where $Z_t = \pi d^3 / 16$ is the polar section modulus for a solid circular cross-section.

**Step 2:** Note that on the surface, the bending normal stress is in one direction (say $x$), and the torsional shear stress is in a perpendicular plane (the $x$–$y$ plane). This is a state of **plane stress**.

**Step 3:** Compute the principal stresses. For plane stress with $\sigma_x = \sigma$, $\sigma_y = 0$, and $\tau_{xy} = \tau$:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

Substituting $\sigma_x = \sigma$ and $\sigma_y = 0$:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{\sigma}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

$$\sigma_3 = 0 \quad \text{(plane stress, out-of-plane stress is zero)}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSST / Tresca)**. The maximum shear stress in a 3D state is:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

Here, $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_1$ and $\sigma_{min} = \min(\sigma_2, 0)$. For a ductile material under combined bending and torsion, the bending stress is tensile on one side, so we consider the maximum absolute difference. In a 2D plot of Mohr's circle:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

Substituting the expressions for $\sigma$ and $\tau$:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{32M}{2\pi d^3}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{16T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{16M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{16T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2}$$

**Step 5:** Factor out the common term $\frac{16}{\pi d^3}$:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

This is the **ASME Code** combined loading equation. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Shear Stress Using Distortion Energy Theory (DET / von Mises)

The **von Mises equivalent stress** for plane stress is:

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$$

For a shaft, substituting $\sigma = 32M/\pi d^3$ and $\tau = 16T/\pi d^3$:

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{32M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + 3\left(\frac{16T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2}$$

The maximum shear stress equivalent in the von Mises sense is $\tau_{vm} = \sigma_{vm}/\sqrt{3}$:

$$\tau_{vm} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \sqrt{\left(\frac{32M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + 3\left(\frac{16T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2}$$

$$\tau_{vm} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}$$

$$\tau_{vm} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}$$

This can be rewritten by dividing and multiplying inside the radical to get a convenient form:

$$\tau_{vm} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{\frac{4M^2 + 3T^2}{3}}$$

$$\tau_{vm} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{\frac{4M^2}{3} + T^2}$$

$$\tau_{vm} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{\left(\frac{M}{0.866}\right)^2 + T^2}$$

A more elegant and **standard textbook** form is to express the equivalent torque as:

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + 0.75 \, T^2}$$

Then the maximum shear stress becomes:

$$\tau_{vm} = \frac{16 T_e}{\pi d^3}$$

This is the **British Standard Code** form. The factor **0.75** comes from the DET as follows:

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + 0.75 T^2} \quad \text{since} \quad 0.75 = 3/4 \text{ arises from } \frac{3T^2}{4} = \frac{3T^2}{4M^2} \cdot M^2 \text{ under the square root formulation}$$

For a rigorous derivation note: in the British Standard (BS 4610), the design equation uses the **shear yield stress** of the material, and the factor 0.75 is the **Timoshenko / Spotts correction** that makes DET equivalent to MSST for the case of pure torsion but with a margin of safety built in. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalent Bending Moment Equation

We define $M_e$ such that the **maximum shear stress** on a shaft under pure bending equals the maximum shear stress on a shaft under combined bending and torsion.

**Maximum shear stress in a shaft under pure bending** (on the neutral axis):

$$\tau_{max, pure \, bending} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{4V}{3A} \quad \text{(at the neutral axis)}$$

For the extreme fiber where bending normal stress is maximum, the maximum shear stress on a 45° plane is $\sigma/2$ (from Mohr's circle, since one principal stress is $\sigma$ and the other is $0$):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{32 M_e}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 M_e}{\pi d^3}$$

**Maximum shear stress in a shaft under combined bending and torsion:**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \quad \text{(ASME)}$$

**Equating the two** (same shaft, same diameter, same material):

$$\frac{16 M_e}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

$$M_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

Wait — this gives only the torsion-equivalent. To capture the **combined** effect, we recognize that the equivalent bending moment must be such that the shaft, when subjected to bending moment $M$ AND the equivalent bending moment (representing the torsion effect), produces the same combined maximum shear stress. Therefore:

The total maximum shear stress on the equivalent shaft = bending stress / 2 + effect of torsion

$$M_e = \frac{M}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{M}{2}\right)^2 + T^2}$$

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2} \left[ M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \right] \quad \text{(ASME Code, MSST)} \quad \blacksquare$$

Similarly, for the British Standard (DET):

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2} \left[ M + \sqrt{M^2 + 0.75 T^2} \right] \quad \text{(BS Code, DET)} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: The ASME Shaft Diameter Equation

**Step 1:** The allowable shear stress in the shaft material is defined as:

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{S_{sy}}{n}$$

where $S_{sy}$ is the **shear yield strength** of the material and $n$ is the **factor of safety** (typically 3 to 6 for shafts).

For a quick estimate (very common in KTU problems), $\tau_{allow}$ is directly given in the problem (typically 40 MPa, 50 MPa, or 60 MPa for C45 steel).

**Step 2:** The strength condition is:

$$\tau_{max} \leq \tau_{allow}$$

$$\frac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \leq \tau_{allow}$$

**Step 3:** Solving for $d$:

$$d^3 \geq \frac{16}{\pi \, \tau_{allow}} \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

$$\boxed{d \geq \left[ \frac{16}{\pi \, \tau_{allow}} \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \right]^{1/3}}$$

**Step 4:** With shock and fatigue factors (ASME empirical equation):

$$d \geq \left[ \frac{16}{\pi \, S_{sy}} \sqrt{(K_m M)^2 + (K_t T)^2} \right]^{1/3}$$

where $K_m$ is the bending moment shock factor and $K_t$ is the torsional moment shock factor (from the table in Section 2.5). $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Bending Moment from Multiple Transverse Loads

A shaft supported by two bearings and loaded by two or more pulleys/gears (one of which acts as the driver) has bending moments in **two perpendicular planes** — vertical (V-plane) and horizontal (H-plane).

The **resultant bending moment** at any cross-section is:

$$M = \sqrt{M_v^2 + M_h^2}$$

**Reason:** The vertical bending moment $M_v$ produces a normal stress on the $x$–$z$ plane, and the horizontal bending moment $M_h$ produces a normal stress on the $x$–$y$ plane. These are two **independent principal bending stresses** at 90° to each other, so they combine by vector addition (since they are at right angles in 3D space).

For the standard simple-supported shaft with two bearings (A and B) and a single central pulley with a transverse force:

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{F}{2}$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{F \cdot L}{4}$$

where $L$ is the distance between the two bearings.

If the pulley is at distances $a$ and $b$ from the two bearings ($a + b = L$):

$$M_{max} = \frac{F \cdot a \cdot b}{a + b} = \frac{F \cdot a \cdot b}{L}$$

This is the **critical bending moment** used in the equivalent moment calculation. $\blacksquare$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style, Step-by-Step)

**Problem Statement:**
A solid circular shaft is to transmit 50 kW of power at 200 rpm. The shaft is supported by two bearings 1 meter apart and carries a pulley of diameter 600 mm at its center. The belt tensions have a ratio of 3:1 (tight side to slack side). Determine the shaft diameter using the ASME code. Take allowable shear stress = 40 MPa. Assume gradually applied loads.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate the Torque Transmitted**

$$T = \frac{60 \times 10^6 \times P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 10^6 \times 50}{2\pi \times 200}$$

$$T = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{1256.64} = 2{,}387{,}324 \text{ N·mm} \approx 2.387 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Net Belt Pull (Transverse Force)**

Total belt pull = $F_1 + F_2$ where $F_1 / F_2 = 3$ and the **net effective transverse force** on the shaft is:

$$F = F_1 - F_2 = 3F_2 - F_2 = 2F_2$$

The torque is also related to the net force as: $T = (F_1 - F_2) \times R$

where $R$ = radius of pulley = $600/2 = 300$ mm.

$$F = \frac{T}{R} = \frac{2.387 \times 10^6}{300} = 7957.75 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Maximum Bending Moment**

For a simply supported shaft with a central load:

$$M = \frac{F \cdot L}{4} = \frac{7957.75 \times 1000}{4} = 1{,}989{,}437.5 \text{ N·mm} \approx 1.989 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Equivalent Bending Moment (ASME - MSST)**

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2} \left[ M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \right]$$

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2} \left[ 1.989 \times 10^6 + \sqrt{(1.989 \times 10^6)^2 + (2.387 \times 10^6)^2} \right]$$

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2} \left[ 1.989 \times 10^6 + \sqrt{3.956 \times 10^{12} + 5.698 \times 10^{12}} \right]$$

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2} \left[ 1.989 \times 10^6 + \sqrt{9.654 \times 10^{12}} \right]$$

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2} \left[ 1.989 \times 10^6 + 3.107 \times 10^6 \right]$$

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2} \times 5.096 \times 10^6 = 2.548 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the Shaft Diameter**

Since the design uses an **equivalent bending moment**, the shaft is designed using the simple bending equation. For a shaft under pure bending, the maximum shear stress on a 45° plane is $\sigma/2$. But for solid shaft design we use the torsion equation format with $T_e$:

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = 3.107 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{16 \, T_e}{\pi d^3}$$

$$d^3 = \frac{16 \times 3.107 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 40} = \frac{49.71 \times 10^6}{125.66} = 3.955 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d = (3.955 \times 10^5)^{1/3} = 73.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Apply the ASME Empirical Equation with Shock Factors**

For gradually applied loads: $K_m = 1.5$, $K_t = 1.0$. Assuming $S_{sy} = 0.5 S_{ut}$. For C45 steel, $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa, so $S_{sy} = 300$ MPa.

$$d^3 = \frac{16}{\pi \times 300} \sqrt{(1.5 \times 1.989 \times 10^6)^2 + (1.0 \times 2.387 \times 10^6)^2}$$

$$d^3 = \frac{16}{942.48} \sqrt{(2.984 \times 10^6)^2 + (2.387 \times 10^6)^2}$$

$$d^3 = 0.01698 \times \sqrt{8.90 \times 10^{12} + 5.70 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$d^3 = 0.01698 \times \sqrt{14.60 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$d^3 = 0.01698 \times 3.821 \times 10^6 = 64{,}880 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d = (64{,}880)^{1/3} = 40.2 \text{ mm}$$

Note the significant difference — the empirical equation gives a more conservative (smaller) diameter because the $S_{sy}$ is much higher than the simple $\tau_{allow}$, and the safety factors are built into the loading.

**Step 7: Standardize the Diameter**

Adopt a **standard diameter** of $d = 45$ mm (or $d = 50$ mm if a keyway is to be provided, with +5–7% allowance). $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Shaft Design Procedure

The following Mermaid block diagrams the complete procedure for designing a shaft based on bending moment, from input data acquisition to final diameter selection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data: Power P, Speed N, Material, Layout] --> B[Compute Transmitted Torque T = 60E6 P / 2piN]
    B --> C[Identify All Transverse Forces F from Gears, Pulleys, Belts]
    C --> D[Compute Reactions at Bearings Using Statics]
    D --> E[Plot Bending Moment Diagram in Vertical Plane]
    D --> F[Plot Bending Moment Diagram in Horizontal Plane]
    E --> G[Resultant BM: M = sqrt of Mv^2 + Mh^2]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Choose Failure Theory}
    H -->|ASME / MSST| I1[Compute Me = 0.5 times M + sqrt of M^2 + T^2]
    H -->|British Standard / DET| I2[Compute Me = 0.5 times M + sqrt of M^2 + 0.75 T^2]
    I1 --> J[Determine Allowable Shear Stress tau_allow = Ssy / n]
    I2 --> J
    J --> K{Apply Shock Factors?}
    K -->|Yes, Empirical ASME| L1[Modify M by Km and T by Kt]
    K -->|No, Pure Static| L2[Use M and T as is]
    L1 --> M[Solve d cubed equals 16 times Te over pi times tau_allow]
    L2 --> M
    M --> N[Compute d]
    N --> O{Keyway Provision?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Add 5 to 7 percent to d]
    O -->|No| Q[Adopt Standard d]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Final Selected Shaft Diameter]
    R --> S[Check for Deflection and Critical Speed if Required]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Stress Decoupling Logic

This block diagram explains how the combined 2D stress state is decoupled into a single equivalent moment for design purposes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Real Loading: Bending M plus Torsion T] --> B[Compute Stresses on Critical Fiber]
    B --> C[Normal Stress sigma equals 32M over pi d cubed]
    B --> D[Shear Stress tau equals 16T over pi d cubed]
    C --> E[Apply Failure Theory]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Maximum Shear Stress Theory}
    E --> G{Distortion Energy Theory}
    F -->|ASME| H[Equivalent Te = sqrt of M squared plus T squared]
    G -->|British Standard| I[Equivalent Te = sqrt of M squared plus 0.75 T squared]
    H --> J[Design Shaft as if Subjected to Pure Torsion Te]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Use Formula tau equals 16 Te over pi d cubed]
    K --> L[Final Shaft Diameter d]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Resultant Bending Moment from Two Perpendicular Planes

The following diagram (rendered as a Mermaid flow for compatibility) conceptually represents the combination of vertical and horizontal bending moments.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VERT[Vertical Plane Loading]
        V1[Vertical Force Fv at Pulley] --> V2[Bending Moment Mv about H axis]
    end
    subgraph HORZ[Horizontal Plane Loading]
        H1[Horizontal Force Fh at Pulley] --> H2[Bending Moment Mh about V axis]
    end
    V2 --> R[Resultant Bending Moment M]
    H2 --> R
    R --> S[M equals sqrt of Mv squared plus Mh squared]
    S --> T[Feed into Equivalent Moment Equation]
```

## 4.4 Classification Tree of Shafts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SHAFTS] --> B[Transmission Shaft]
    A --> C[Machine Shaft]
    A --> D[Axle or Spindle]
    B --> B1[Subjected to Bending plus Torsion plus Axial Load]
    B --> B2[Example: Line Shaft in Workshop]
    C --> C1[Integral Part of Machine Itself]
    C --> C2[Example: Crankshaft of IC Engine]
    D --> D1[Supports Rotating Parts without Transmitting Torque]
    D --> D2[Subjected to Bending Moment Only]
    D --> D3[Example: Railway Axle]
```
