# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"State the assumptions of (i) Uniform Pressure Theory and (ii) Uniform Wear Theory used in the design of friction clutches."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(i) Uniform Pressure Theory:**
1. The pressure between the friction surfaces is **uniformly distributed** over the entire contact area.
2. The contact surface is treated as **new, rigid, and perfectly flat**.
3. Valid primarily for **initial engagement** before significant wear has occurred.
*(1 mark for the assumption, 1 mark for stating validity condition, 1 mark for practical implication — initial engagement.)*

**(ii) Uniform Wear Theory:**
1. The **wear rate is uniform** across the contact surface (Archard's wear law with constant wear coefficient).
2. This results in pressure varying **inversely with radius**: $p \cdot r = C = \text{constant}$.
3. Maximum pressure occurs at the **inner radius** $R_i$.
4. Represents the **steady-state operating condition** after sufficient running-in.
*(1 mark each: wear uniformity, $p \cdot r =$ constant, maximum pressure location, and validity condition.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"A single-plate clutch has a friction lining of outer diameter 300 mm and inner diameter 200 mm. Find the mean radius of the friction surface under (a) Uniform Pressure Theory and (b) Uniform Wear Theory. Take $\mu = 0.3$."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Given: $R_o = 0.150$ m, $R_i = 0.100$ m.

**(a) Under UPT:**
$$R_m = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{R_o^{\,3} - R_i^{\,3}}{R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2}} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{0.150^3 - 0.100^3}{0.150^2 - 0.100^2} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{0.003375 - 0.001000}{0.0225 - 0.0100} = 0.1276 \text{ m}$$

**(b) Under UWT:**
$$R_m = \frac{R_o + R_i}{2} = \frac{0.150 + 0.100}{2} = 0.125 \text{ m}$$

**Observation (for 3rd mark):** $R_m$ under UWT is **lower**, leading to lower torque capacity for the same axial force.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:**
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Internal choice** is provided — only ONE of the two alternatives must be attempted.

---

### 🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question aligned with Dec 2023 pattern]**

A multi-plate clutch is to be designed for an automobile application with the following specifications:

- Power to be transmitted: $P = 25$ kW at $N = 1500$ rpm
- Number of contact surfaces: $n = 4$
- Outer radius of friction plate: $R_o = 80$ mm
- Inner radius of friction plate: $R_i = 50$ mm
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu = 0.3$
- Maximum permissible pressure: $p_{max} = 0.7$ MPa
- Service factor: $F_s = 1.5$

**Assume Uniform Wear Theory. Determine:**
**(a)** The required axial clamping force and the actual torque capacity of the clutch.
**(b)** The heat generated per second during engagement (if engaging a stationary load from the rated rpm) and comment on whether natural convection cooling is adequate, given surface area $A = 0.05$ m², $h = 30$ W/m²K, $T_{ambient} = 40°C$, and $T_{max, surface} = 120°C$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Required Axial Force and Torque Capacity [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute the design torque:**
$$T_{rated} = \frac{P \cdot 60}{2\pi N} = \frac{25{,}000 \cdot 60}{2\pi \cdot 1500} = \frac{1{,}500{,}000}{9424.78} = 159.15 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T_{design} = T_{rated} \times F_s = 159.15 \times 1.5 = 238.73 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Use Uniform Wear Theory (UWT):**
- $R_m = (R_o + R_i)/2 = (0.080 + 0.050)/2 = 0.065$ m
- Maximum pressure occurs at $R_i = 0.050$ m

**Step 3 — Apply torque equation** $T_{design} = n \mu W R_m$ and solve for $W$:

$$W = \frac{T_{design}}{n \cdot \mu \cdot R_m} = \frac{238.73}{4 \times 0.3 \times 0.065} = \frac{238.73}{0.078} = 3060.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Validate against maximum permissible pressure:**
$$p_{max, actual} = \frac{W}{2\pi R_i (R_o - R_i)} = \frac{3060.6}{2\pi \times 0.050 \times 0.030} = \frac{3060.6}{0.009425} = 324{,}738 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.325 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $0.325 \text{ MPa} < 0.7 \text{ MPa}$ ✓ **Design is safe.**

**Step 5 — Actual torque capacity:**
$$T_{actual} = n \mu W R_m = 4 \times 0.3 \times 3060.6 \times 0.065 = 238.73 \text{ N·m}$$

✅ **Axial Force $W \approx 3061$ N, Torque capacity $\approx 238.7$ N·m** *(5 marks for derivation, 2 marks for validation + final answer)*

---

**(b) Heat Generation and Cooling Adequacy [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Heat generated rate** during engagement (driven member initially stationary):

$$Q_{gen} = T_{design} \cdot \omega_1 = 238.73 \times \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 238.73 \times 157.08 = 37{,}497 \text{ W} \approx 37.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Heat dissipation capacity** of the clutch housing:

$$Q_{diss} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{surface,max} - T_{ambient}) = 30 \times 0.05 \times (120 - 40) = 30 \times 0.05 \times 80 = 120 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Compare:**

$$Q_{gen} \approx 37{,}500 \text{ W} \gg Q_{diss} = 120 \text{ W}$$

**Conclusion:** **Natural convection cooling is grossly inadequate.** The clutch requires **forced cooling** (oil spray, oil bath, or external fan) or the engagement must be very gradual to keep instantaneous heat generation within dissipation capacity. In practice, **multi-plate wet clutches in automobiles use circulated oil** to absorb this heat.

*(2 marks for $Q_{gen}$, 2 marks for $Q_{diss}$, 2 marks for comparison + engineering recommendation, 1 mark for final conclusion.)*

---

### 🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question aligned with May 2024 pattern]**

A cone clutch is required to transmit 100 N·m of torque at 900 rpm. The cone has a mean radius of 75 mm, semi-cone angle $\alpha = 12.5°$, and coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.28$.

**(a)** Determine the axial force required to engage the clutch and the normal force on the contact surface. Comment on the mechanical advantage of the cone clutch over a single-plate clutch with the same $R_m$.

**(b)** If the clutch must engage a flywheel of mass moment of inertia $I = 0.5$ kg·m² from 900 rpm to rest, determine the **minimum engagement time** to avoid exceeding the friction torque limit.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Axial Force and Mechanical Advantage [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Axial force for cone clutch:**
$$W = \frac{T \sin\alpha}{\mu R_m} = \frac{100 \times \sin(12.5°)}{0.28 \times 0.075} = \frac{100 \times 0.2164}{0.021} = \frac{21.64}{0.021} = 1030.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Normal force on the cone surface:**
$$F_n = \frac{T}{\mu R_m} = \frac{100}{0.28 \times 0.075} = \frac{100}{0.021} = 4761.9 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with single-plate clutch** of same $R_m$:
$$W_{plate} = \frac{T}{\mu R_m} = 4761.9 \text{ N}$$

**Mechanical Advantage:**
$$\text{MA} = \frac{W_{plate}}{W_{cone}} = \frac{4761.9}{1030.5} = 4.62$$

The cone clutch requires only **~21.6%** ($\sin 12.5°$) of the axial force required by an equivalent flat-plate clutch — hence **4.6× reduction** in actuator (spring/piston) effort.

✅ **Axial force $W \approx 1031$ N, Normal force $F_n \approx 4762$ N, MA $\approx 4.62$** *(3 marks for computation, 2 marks for comparison, 2 marks for engineering interpretation)*

---

**(b) Minimum Engagement Time [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Kinetic energy** of the flywheel at 900 rpm:

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi \times 900}{60} = 94.25 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_1^{\,2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.5 \times (94.25)^2 = 0.25 \times 8883 = 2220.7 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Energy absorbed by friction slippage:**

Assuming the friction torque $T = 100$ N·m is constant throughout engagement, the energy dissipated equals:

$$E_{friction} = T \cdot (\omega_1 - 0) \cdot t_{min} = 100 \times 94.25 \times t_{min} = 9425 \cdot t_{min}$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve for minimum engagement time** (kinetic energy = frictional work):

$$9425 \cdot t_{min} = 2220.7$$

$$\boxed{t_{min} = \frac{2220.7}{9425} = 0.2356 \text{ s}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Engagement must be **at least ~0.24 seconds long**. Shorter engagement would require a higher instantaneous torque, exceeding the friction capacity and causing clutch slip or burn.

*(3 marks for kinetic energy, 2 marks for friction work, 2 marks for equating and solving.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Clutch Problems**
> 1. **Mixing up UPT and UWT $R_m$ formulas** — Most frequent error. **Always check which theory the question specifies.** As a safety habit, write the theory name before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $n$ (number of contact surfaces)** in multi-plate clutches. The torque scales linearly: $T = n \mu W R_m$, NOT $T = \mu W R_m$.
> 3. **Forgetting $\sin\alpha$ in cone clutch** axial force equation. Students often write $W = T/(\mu R_m)$ (the flat-plate formula) instead of $W = T\sin\alpha/(\mu R_m)$.
> 4. **Using $R_o$ instead of $R_i$ for $p_{max}$ under UWT.** Maximum pressure under UWT is **at the inner radius** $R_i$, not the outer.
> 5. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing mm and m. **Always convert to SI units (meters, Newtons) before substituting.**
> 6. **Heat dissipation units:** $h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ gives Watts only when $h$ is in W/m²K, $A$ in m², $\Delta T$ in K.
> 7. **Forgetting factor of safety** $F_s$ — design torque must always include $F_s$.
> 8. **In centrifugal clutch problems**, forgetting that the radial centrifugal force is $m \omega^2 r$, not $m g$ — students confuse with gravitational force.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT] **Save This Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Revision**

### Must-Know Concepts
- [x] A **clutch** is a device that transmits torque between two shafts that can be engaged/disengaged while the driver runs continuously.
- [x] **Friction clutches** (force-locking) vs **positive clutches** (form-locking).
- [x] **Uniform Pressure Theory (UPT):** $p = \text{constant}$; $R_m = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$
- [x] **Uniform Wear Theory (UWT):** $p \cdot r = \text{constant}$; $R_m = \frac{R_o + R_i}{2}$
- [x] Torque capacity (multi-surface): $T = n \mu W R_m$
- [x] Axial force under UWT: $W = 2\pi p_{max} R_i (R_o - R_i)$
- [x] Axial force under UPT: $W = \pi p (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$
- [x] Cone clutch axial force: $W = \frac{T \sin\alpha}{\mu R_m}$
- [x] Heat generation rate: $Q = \frac{T N}{9.55}$ W (when $T$ in N·m, $N$ in rpm)
- [x] Energy balance: $T \cdot \omega \cdot t = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$

### Must-Know Design Considerations
- [x] **Torque transmission capacity** must exceed design torque (rated × factor of safety).
- [x] **Pressure** must not exceed permissible pressure of friction material.
- [x] **Heat dissipation** must match or exceed heat generation; otherwise use forced cooling.
- [x] **Material selection** balances $\mu$, wear resistance, heat resistance, and strength.
- [x] **Geometric ratio** $C = R_i/R_o$ typically in the range **0.5 – 0.75** for efficient material use.
- [x] **Engagement time** must be long enough to dissipate kinetic energy without exceeding friction limit.

### Quick Numerical Sanity Checks (For KTU Problem Validation)
- [x] $R_m$ (UWT) is **always less than** $R_m$ (UPT) for the same dimensions → UWT predicts lower torque for same $W$.
- [x] Increasing $n$ (number of surfaces) **reduces required $W$** proportionally — drives multi-plate clutch design.
- [x] Reducing semi-cone angle $\alpha$ **reduces axial force** required but **increases cone height** — geometric trade-off.
- [x] Increasing $C$ (i.e., making $R_i$ closer to $R_o$) **reduces material usage** but **reduces torque capacity** for given $W$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Friction Clutches
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Friction Clutches

# Module 2 — Friction Clutches

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Friction Clutch** is a machine element designed to transmit rotational power (torque) from a **driving shaft** to a **driven shaft** through deliberate **frictional contact** between mating surfaces. Engagement establishes the torque path; disengagement interrupts it without shock, allowing smooth start, stop, and speed-change operations.

The friction surface may be planar (disc clutch), conical (cone clutch), or cylindrical/centrifugal. Two classical pressure-distribution theories govern the design:

- **Uniform Pressure Theory** — new or just-machined clutch, pressure *p* is constant across the contact annulus.
- **Uniform Wear Theory** — after initial "wear-in", the product *p × r = constant*, because wear is proportional to frictional work per unit area, which itself depends on sliding velocity (∝ *r*).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Bicycle Hand-Brake Plate:**
> Imagine pressing a **wet, rough stone** between your two palms. If you press harder (more axial force *W*), the stone is harder to twist between your hands. The twisting resistance (torque) is the *friction torque*. Now imagine rubbing the stone: the outer edge slides faster than the inner edge — so it wears faster. After some use, the wear evens out so that pressure becomes *higher near the centre* and *lower near the rim*. This is exactly the **uniform-wear** condition used in clutch design.
>
> A real clutch does the same thing between a **flywheel (driving member)** and a **pressure plate (driven member)**, with friction lining (e.g., asbestos/sintered bronze) sandwiched in between.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Standard Metrics)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range / Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|
| μ | Coefficient of friction | **0.10 – 0.50** (lining-metal pair) |
| p | Contact pressure | **0.07 – 0.35 MPa** |
| W | Total axial (clamping) force | N |
| T | Torque transmitted | N·m |
| Rᵢ | Inner radius of friction disc | m |
| Rₒ | Outer radius of friction disc | m |
| Rₘ | Mean friction radius | m |
| n | Number of friction surfaces (contact pairs) | integer ≥ 1 |
| v | Sliding velocity at radius *r* | m/s |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step by Step

1. **Driver (Flywheel)** rotates at engine/prime-mover speed, carrying a friction lining on its face.
2. A **pressure plate** (driven member) is pushed axially against the lining by springs or a lever, generating the **clamping force W**.
3. The normal pressure *p(r)* acts on the annular contact area *A = π(Rₒ² − Rᵢ²)*.
4. At any radius *r*, an elemental frictional force *dF = μ · p(r) · dA* resists sliding.
5. This elemental force, acting at radius *r*, contributes an elemental torque *dT = r · dF = μ · p(r) · r · dA*.
6. The **total torque capacity** is obtained by integrating over the annulus, with the form of *p(r)* governed by the chosen pressure theory.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Two Theories

| Theory | Physical Justification | Mathematical Consequence |
|--------|-----------------------|--------------------------|
| **Uniform Pressure** | New, rigid, freshly ground surfaces initially deform elastically but have not yet worn to equilibrium. | p(r) = const = *p* |
| **Uniform Wear** | After sufficient running, *wear rate ∝ p·v ∝ p·r*. Steady state implies *p·r = C* (constant). | p(r) = C/r |

> [!TIP]
> **Designer's Rule of Thumb:** KTU examiners and textbooks (V.B. Bhandari, Shigley) treat the **uniform-wear theory** as the *design basis* because clutches reach wear-equilibrium quickly. The **uniform-pressure** assumption is retained only for *fresh* clutches or for estimating peak pressure at the inner radius.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the vertical bar `|` in equations is **forbidden inside markdown tables** — always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. All formulas below are written in pure LaTeX.

#### A. General (annular friction surface)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Contact area | $A \;=\; \pi\!\left(R_o^{\,2} \;-\; R_i^{\,2}\right)$ | m² |
| 2 | Mean radius (general) | $R_m \;=\; \dfrac{2}{3}\,\dfrac{R_o^{\,3} \;-\; R_i^{\,3}}{R_o^{\,2} \;-\; R_i^{\,2}}$ | m |
| 3 | Mean radius (uniform-wear) | $R_m \;=\; \dfrac{R_o \;+\; R_i}{2}$ | m |
| 4 | Axial clamping force | $W \;=\; 2\pi \!\!\int_{R_i}^{R_o}\!\! p(r)\, r\, dr$ | N |
| 5 | Friction torque | $T \;=\; 2\pi \mu \!\!\int_{R_i}^{R_o}\!\! p(r)\, r^{2}\, dr$ | N·m |

#### B. Single-Disc (Single-Plate) Clutch

| Theory | Pressure | Torque | Max Pressure |
|--------|----------|--------|--------------|
| **Uniform Pressure** | $p = \dfrac{W}{A}$ | $T = \dfrac{2}{3}\,\mu\, W\, \dfrac{R_o^{\,3} - R_i^{\,3}}{R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2}}$ | $p_{\max} = p$ (at all r) |
| **Uniform Wear** | $p(r) = \dfrac{C}{r}$ | $T = \dfrac{\mu\, W}{2}\,\bigl(R_o + R_i\bigr) = \mu\, W\, R_m$ | $p_{\max} = \dfrac{C}{R_i} = \dfrac{W}{2\pi R_i\!\left(R_o - R_i\right)}$ |

#### C. Multi-Disc (Multi-Plate) Clutch

Same as single-disc formulas, **multiplied by the number of friction surfaces *n***:

$$T_{\text{multi}} \;=\; n \;\times\; T_{\text{single}}$$

For a clutch with *n₁* discs on driving shaft and *n₂* on driven shaft:

$$n \;=\; n_1 \;+\; n_2$$

#### D. Cone Clutch

| Quantity | Formula |
|----------|---------|
| Axial (clamping) force | $W = 2\pi\, p\, (R_o - R_i)\,(R_o + R_i)\,\sin\alpha \;\big/ \;2$ (uniform pressure) |
| Torque (uniform pressure) | $T = \dfrac{2\pi\mu\, p}{3}\,(R_o^{\,3} - R_i^{\,3})$ |
| Torque (uniform wear) | $T = \pi\mu\, W\, (R_o + R_i)\,\dfrac{\cot\alpha}{2}$ |
| Mean radius (uniform wear) | $R_m = \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$ |

where **α = half-cone angle** (typical 12.5° – 15°).

#### E. Energy / Heat Considerations

| Quantity | Formula |
|----------|---------|
| Frictional work per engagement | $U = T\,\theta$ (θ in rad) |
| Power dissipated | $P = T\,\omega$ (W) |
| Energy per unit area per engagement | $u = p\,\mu\,\Delta v$ (J/m²) |
| Temperature rise | $\Delta T = \dfrac{U}{m\, c_p}$ (°C) |

> [!NOTE]
> For **multi-disc clutches** used in synchroniser hubs, oil-cooled gearboxes, and limited-slip differentials, the heat-dissipation capacity scales with *n*, allowing compact designs for high torque.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Uniform Pressure Theory (Single Disc)

**Starting premise:** The pressure *p* is constant over the entire annular contact zone.

#### Step 1 — Elemental Area
On the annular ring between radius *r* and *r + dr*:

$$dA \;=\; 2\pi\, r\, dr$$

#### Step 2 — Elemental Normal Force
The axial reaction of the ring on the pressure plate:

$$dW \;=\; p\, dA \;=\; 2\pi\, p\, r\, dr$$

#### Step 3 — Total Axial Force
Integrate from *Rᵢ* to *Rₒ*:

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi\, p\, r\, dr \\
  &= 2\pi\, p \left[\frac{r^{2}}{2}\right]_{R_i}^{R_o} \\
  &= \pi\, p \bigl(R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2}\bigr)
\end{aligned}$$

Hence the **mean uniform pressure**:

$$p \;=\; \frac{W}{\pi \bigl(R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2}\bigr)}$$

#### Step 4 — Elemental Friction Torque
At radius *r*, the elemental tangential friction force is *μ·p·dA*, acting at lever arm *r*:

$$dT \;=\; \mu\, p\, r\, dA \;=\; \mu\, p\, r \cdot 2\pi\, r\, dr \;=\; 2\pi\mu p\, r^{2}\, dr$$

#### Step 5 — Integrate to Get Total Torque

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi\mu p\, r^{2}\, dr \\
  &= 2\pi\mu p \left[\frac{r^{3}}{3}\right]_{R_i}^{R_o} \\
  &= \frac{2\pi\mu p}{3}\,\bigl(R_o^{\,3} - R_i^{\,3}\bigr)
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 6 — Substitute *p* from Step 3
Replace *p* with *W / [π(Rₒ² − Rᵢ²)]*:

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{2\pi\mu}{3}\,\bigl(R_o^{\,3} - R_i^{\,3}\bigr) \cdot \frac{W}{\pi\bigl(R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2}\bigr)} \\
  &= \frac{2}{3}\,\mu\, W\, \frac{R_o^{\,3} - R_i^{\,3}}{R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The fraction $\dfrac{2}{3}\,\dfrac{R_o^{\,3} - R_i^{\,3}}{R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2}}$ is the **mean effective radius** *Rₘ* for uniform pressure. Many textbooks combine this into a single *T = μ W Rₘ* form.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Uniform Wear Theory (Single Disc)

**Starting premise:** *p · r = C* (constant), so *p(r) = C/r*.

#### Step 1 — Elemental Normal Force
As before, *dW = p · dA = p · 2πr dr*. Substituting *p = C/r*:

$$dW \;=\; \frac{C}{r}\cdot 2\pi r\, dr \;=\; 2\pi C\, dr$$

#### Step 2 — Total Axial Force

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi C\, dr \\
  &= 2\pi C\,(R_o - R_i)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,C \;=\; \frac{W}{2\pi(R_o - R_i)}\,}$$

#### Step 3 — Elemental Friction Torque
*dT = μ · p · r · dA = μ (C/r) · r · 2π r dr = 2πμ C r dr*

#### Step 4 — Integrate

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi\mu C\, r\, dr \\
  &= 2\pi\mu C \left[\frac{r^{2}}{2}\right]_{R_i}^{R_o} \\
  &= \pi\mu C\,(R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2}) \\
  &= \pi\mu \cdot \frac{W}{2\pi(R_o - R_i)} \cdot (R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2}) \\
  &= \frac{\mu W}{2}\,(R_o + R_i)
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 5 — Maximum Pressure (at *r = Rᵢ*)

$$p_{\max} \;=\; \frac{C}{R_i} \;=\; \frac{W}{2\pi R_i (R_o - R_i)}$$

This must remain below the allowable contact pressure *[p]*:

$$W_{\max} \;=\; 2\pi\, R_i\,(R_o - R_i)\,[p]$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Multi-Disc Clutch Torque

For *n* friction surfaces in parallel, each surface transmits the same torque *T₁*:

$$T_{\text{total}} \;=\; n\, T_1$$

If the clutch has *n₁* discs keyed to the driving shaft and *n₂* discs keyed to the driven shaft, the active friction surfaces are the *n = n₁ + n₂* interfaces between adjacent plates. For uniform wear:

$$T_{\text{total}} \;=\; n\,\dfrac{\mu W}{2}\,(R_o + R_i) \;=\; n\,\mu\, W\, R_m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Insight:** Adding one more disc (one more friction surface) effectively **doubles the torque capacity** for the same clamping force. This is why multi-plate clutches are used in motorbikes and race cars where the axial space is limited but torque demand is high.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cone Clutch (Uniform Wear)

For a conical friction surface with semi-cone angle **α** and mean radius *Rₘ*:

- The contact normal is inclined at angle *α* from the shaft axis.
- The axial clamping force *W* is the component of the normal force along the axis: *N sin α = W* ⇒ *N = W / sin α*.
- The friction torque about the shaft axis is the tangential friction force times *Rₘ*: *T = μ N Rₘ*.

Substituting:

$$T \;=\; \mu \cdot \frac{W}{\sin\alpha}\cdot R_m \;=\; \mu\, W\, R_m \cdot \csc\alpha$$

For the special case of uniform wear with full annular contact:

$$T \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\mu\, W\, (R_o + R_i)}{2} \cdot \cot\alpha$$

> [!WARNING]
> A common derivation error: students forget that the **normal force is *W / sin α*, not *W***, because only the axial component of *N* balances the clamping force. This will reduce your torque by *sin α* (≈ 0.22 for α = 12.5°) and lose marks.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Uniform Pressure | Uniform Wear |
|--------|------------------|--------------|
| Pressure variation | *p* = constant | *p = C/r*, max at *Rᵢ* |
| Torque formula | $T = \dfrac{2}{3}\mu W \dfrac{R_o^{3}-R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2}-R_i^{2}}$ | $T = \dfrac{\mu W}{2}(R_o+R_i)$ |
| Torque magnitude (for same *W*, μ) | Slightly **higher** for thin rings | Slightly **lower** for thin rings |
| Realism | New clutch | Worn-in (design basis) |
| *Rₘ* meaning | $\dfrac{2}{3}\dfrac{R_o^{3}-R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2}-R_i^{2}}$ | $\dfrac{R_o+R_i}{2}$ |
| KTU usage | Quick checks, initial design | **Standard design equation** |

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

> A single-plate clutch has friction lining with outer diameter 300 mm and inner diameter 200 mm. The coefficient of friction is 0.3. The clutch transmits 25 kW at 1500 rpm. Using the **uniform wear theory**, determine (a) the axial force *W* required, and (b) the maximum pressure.

**Given:** *Dₒ = 0.300 m*, *Dᵢ = 0.200 m*, *μ = 0.30*, *P = 25 kW*, *N = 1500 rpm*.

#### Step 1 — Torque Required

$$T = \frac{P}{\omega} = \frac{25\,000}{2\pi \times 1500 / 60} = \frac{25\,000}{157.08} = 159.15\;\text{N·m}$$

#### Step 2 — Mean Radius

$$R_o = 0.150\,\text{m},\quad R_i = 0.100\,\text{m},\quad R_m = \frac{0.150+0.100}{2} = 0.125\,\text{m}$$

#### Step 3 — Axial Force (Uniform Wear)

$$T = \frac{\mu W}{2}(R_o + R_i)\;\Rightarrow\; W = \frac{2T}{\mu(R_o+R_i)} = \frac{2 \times 159.15}{0.30 \times 0.250} = 4244\;\text{N} \approx 4.24\;\text{kN}$$

#### Step 4 — Maximum Pressure (at *Rᵢ*)

$$p_{\max} = \frac{W}{2\pi R_i (R_o - R_i)} = \frac{4244}{2\pi(0.100)(0.050)} = 135.1\;\text{kPa} \approx 0.135\;\text{MPa}$$

> This is well within the allowable 0.07–0.35 MPa range for sintered-bronze linings. Design is safe. ✔

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Friction Clutch

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Prime Mover / Engine] --> B[Driving Shaft]
    B --> C[Flywheel with Friction Lining]
    C --> D[Friction Contact Zone]
    D --> E[Pressure Plate / Driven Disc]
    E --> F[Driven Shaft]
    F --> G[Gearbox / Load]

    H[Clamping Spring / Lever] -- axial force W --> E
    I[Release Bearing / Pedal] -- disengage --> H
    J[Sliding Velocity field v = omega r] --> D
    K[Heat Generation] --> L[Cooling Fins / Oil Spray]
    L --> A

    subgraph sensing["Sensing & Control"]
        M[Temperature Sensor]
        N[Wear Sensor]
        O[Torque Sensor]
    end
    D --> M
    D --> N
    D --> O
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Classification of Friction Clutches

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Friction Clutches] --> B[Disc / Plate Type]
    A --> C[Cone Type]
    A --> D[Centrifugal Type]
    A --> E[Band Type]

    B --> B1[Single Disc]
    B --> B2[Multi Disc]
    B --> B3[Diaphragm Spring Clutch]

    C --> C1[External Cone]
    C --> C2[Internal Cone]

    D --> D1[Self Engaging]
    D --> D2[Governor Controlled]

    E --> E1[Single Band]
    E --> E2[Double Band]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Torque Transmission Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Axial Clamping Force W] --> B[Contact Pressure p r distribution]
    B --> C{Selected Theory}
    C -->|Uniform Pressure| D[p constant]
    C -->|Uniform Wear| E[p times r constant]

    D --> F[Torque T equals 2 by 3 mu W Rm]
    E --> G[Torque T equals mu W Rm]

    F --> H[Torque Capacity N m]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compare with Required Torque]
    I --> J{Design Safe?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Accept Design]
    J -->|No| L[Increase W or Add Discs]
    L --> A
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Cone Clutch Force Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Axial Clamping W] --> B[Reaction along Cone Normal N]
    B --> C[Axial Component N sin alpha]
    B --> D[Tangential Friction mu N]
    D --> E[Torque T equals mu N Rm]
    C --> F[Equilibrium W equals N sin alpha]
    F --> G[N equals W by sin alpha]
    G --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **physical drawings** of disc, cone, and centrifugal clutches (with shafts, springs, pivots, and friction linings) are best illustrated in the prescribed textbook **V.B. Bhandari, "Design of Machine Elements"** (McGraw-Hill) — Figures 8.1, 8.5, 8.9, 8.10. These should be traced in the examination answer script for full credit.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
**Differentiate between uniform pressure theory and uniform wear theory in friction clutches.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Uniform Pressure | Uniform Wear |
|--------|------------------|--------------|
| Pressure | Constant across contact area | Inversely proportional to radius (*p ∝ 1/r*) |
| Condition | New, freshly ground lining | After wear-in equilibrium |
| Torque formula | $T = \dfrac{2}{3}\mu W\dfrac{R_o^{3}-R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2}-R_i^{2}}$ | $T = \dfrac{\mu W}{2}(R_o+R_i)$ |
| Max pressure | At all radii | At inner radius *Rᵢ* |
| Design usage | Initial | Steady-state (recommended) |

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
**Why is the uniform wear theory preferred over the uniform pressure theory in the design of friction clutches?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. After a clutch runs for a short period, the surface wear reaches an equilibrium where the product *p · r = constant*, because wear is proportional to frictional work per unit area which is itself proportional to sliding velocity ∝ *r*.
2. The uniform-wear condition represents the **steady-state operating condition** of the clutch, so it gives a more realistic design torque.
3. The pressure distribution *p = C/r* yields a **lower design torque** than uniform pressure, which is a more conservative and safer design choice.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**A single-plate friction clutch has effective friction surfaces of outer radius 150 mm and inner radius 100 mm. The coefficient of friction is 0.30 and the axial pressure intensity is 0.15 MPa, assumed uniform. Calculate:**

**(a) (7 marks)** The total axial force and the torque capacity of the clutch.

**(b) (7 marks)** The torque capacity and the maximum pressure if the uniform wear theory is used, keeping the axial force the same as in part (a).

---

##### Solution A(a) — Uniform Pressure

**Given:** *Rₒ = 0.150 m*, *Rᵢ = 0.100 m*, *μ = 0.30*, *p = 0.15 MPa = 0.15 × 10⁶ Pa*.

**Step 1 — Contact area**

$$A = \pi(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}) = \pi(0.150^{2} - 0.100^{2}) = \pi(0.0225 - 0.0100) = \pi \times 0.0125 = 0.03927\;\text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Total axial force**

$$W = pA = 0.15 \times 10^{6} \times 0.03927 = 5890.5\;\text{N} \approx 5.89\;\text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Torque (uniform pressure)**

$$T = \frac{2}{3}\mu W \frac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}$$

Compute numerator: *Rₒ³ − Rᵢ³ = 0.150³ − 0.100³ = 0.003375 − 0.001 = 0.002375 m³*
Compute denominator: *Rₒ² − Rᵢ² = 0.0225 − 0.0100 = 0.0125 m²*
Ratio: *0.002375 / 0.0125 = 0.190 m*

$$T = \frac{2}{3}(0.30)(5890.5)(0.190) = \frac{2}{3}(0.30)(1119.2) = 223.8\;\text{N·m}$$

**Answer (a):** *W ≈ 5.89 kN*, *T ≈ 224 N·m*.

---

##### Solution A(b) — Uniform Wear with same W

**Step 1 — Torque (uniform wear)**

$$T = \frac{\mu W}{2}(R_o + R_i) = \frac{0.30 \times 5890.5}{2}(0.150 + 0.100) = 883.6 \times 0.250 = 220.9\;\text{N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum pressure (at *Rᵢ*)**

$$p_{\max} = \frac{W}{2\pi R_i (R_o - R_i)} = \frac{5890.5}{2\pi(0.100)(0.050)} = \frac{5890.5}{0.03142} = 187\,480\;\text{Pa} \approx 0.187\;\text{MPa}$$

**Answer (b):** *T ≈ 221 N·m*, *pₘₐₓ ≈ 0.187 MPa*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison:** The uniform-wear theory gives a slightly lower torque (221 N·m vs 224 N·m) — about 1.3% lower — and a *pₘₐₓ* (0.187 MPa) higher than the uniform pressure (0.15 MPa). The higher pressure is realistic because the centre runs cooler than the rim and can sustain higher *p*.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**A multi-plate clutch has 5 driving and 5 driven plates, each with an effective outer radius of 125 mm and inner radius 75 mm. The coefficient of friction is 0.25. The maximum intensity of pressure is 0.30 MPa.**

**(a) (7 marks)** Determine the axial force required and the torque transmitted, using the uniform wear theory.

**(b) (7 marks)** If the engine speed is 2400 rpm, calculate the power transmitted and the energy lost per engagement for a slip angle of 60°.

---

##### Solution B(a) — Multi-Plate Uniform Wear

**Given:** *Rₒ = 0.125 m*, *Rᵢ = 0.075 m*, *μ = 0.25*, *pₘₐₓ = 0.30 MPa*.
Number of friction surfaces: *n = 5 + 5 = 10* (n₁ + n₂ driving/driven).

**Step 1 — Axial force per surface**

For uniform wear, *pₘₐₓ = W / [2π Rᵢ (Rₒ − Rᵢ)]* ⇒

$$W = p_{\max}\, 2\pi R_i (R_o - R_i) = 0.30 \times 10^{6} \times 2\pi(0.075)(0.050) = 707\;\text{N per surface}$$

**Step 2 — Torque per surface (uniform wear)**

$$T_1 = \frac{\mu W}{2}(R_o + R_i) = \frac{0.25 \times 707}{2}(0.125 + 0.075) = 88.4 \times 0.200 = 17.68\;\text{N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Total torque (multi-plate)**

$$T = n \cdot T_1 = 10 \times 17.68 = 176.8\;\text{N·m}$$

**Answer (a):** *W ≈ 707 N*, *T ≈ 177 N·m*.

---

##### Solution B(b) — Power and Energy

**Step 1 — Power**

$$P = T \omega = 176.8 \times \frac{2\pi \times 2400}{60} = 176.8 \times 251.33 = 44\,435\;\text{W} \approx 44.4\;\text{kW}$$

**Step 2 — Energy per engagement (slip angle θ = 60° = π/3 rad)**

$$U = T\theta = 176.8 \times \frac{\pi}{3} = 185.1\;\text{J}$$

**Step 3 — Energy per unit area (optional check)**

Effective area: *A = π(Rₒ² − Rᵢ²) = π(0.015625 − 0.005625) = π × 0.010 = 0.03142 m²*
Energy density: *u = U / A = 185.1 / 0.03142 = 5892 J/m² per surface*, divided over 10 surfaces = 589 J/m² per surface — well within lining capacity.

**Answer (b):** *P ≈ 44.4 kW*, *U ≈ 185 J* per engagement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by *n*:** Students often compute torque for a single surface and present it as the final answer, losing **3–4 marks** in a 7-mark sub-part.
> 2. **Mixing the two theories in a single sub-part:** Once you commit to *uniform wear*, the pressure varies as *C/r* and *pₘₐₓ* is at *Rᵢ* — do not suddenly switch to *p* = const.
> 3. **Unit slip:** Forgetting to convert *rpm* to *rad/s* (factor of *2π/60*). This is a 1-mark deduction in long-answer papers.
> 4. **Slip angle in degrees:** Always convert to *radians* before multiplying with *T* (Joules = N·m × rad).
> 5. **Wrong sign on cone angle:** The normal force on a cone is *N = W / sin α*, not *W · sin α*. Conflating this is the single most frequent error in cone-clutch problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Friction Clutches — Revision Bullets (Print Before Exam)**

- **Definition:** A friction clutch transmits torque between two rotating shafts by *frictional contact*, engaged/disengaged axially.
- **Two governing theories:** (1) *Uniform Pressure* (new clutch, *p* = const) and (2) *Uniform Wear* (steady state, *p · r = const*).
- **Uniform Pressure Torque:** $T = \dfrac{2}{3}\,\mu\, W\, \dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}$.
- **Uniform Wear Torque:** $T = \dfrac{\mu W}{2}\,(R_o + R_i) = \mu W R_m$ where $R_m = \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$.
- **Multi-plate:** Multiply single-surface torque by *n = n₁ + n₂* (active contact faces).
- **Cone Clutch:** $T = \mu W R_m \csc\alpha$ or $T = \dfrac{\pi\mu W(R_o + R_i)}{2}\cot\alpha$ for uniform wear; remember $\alpha$ = half-cone angle, typically 12.5°–15°.
- **Maximum pressure (uniform wear):** occurs at the **inner radius** $R_i$; check $p_{\max} = \dfrac{W}{2\pi R_i(R_o - R_i)} \le [p]$.
- **Standard design range:** $\mu = 0.10$–$0.50$, $[p] = 0.07$–$0.35$ MPa for typical friction linings.
- **Power & energy:** $P = T\omega$, $U = T\theta$; check single-engagement energy density against lining rating (≈ 1500–3000 J/m² per surface).
- **Design sequence:** (1) Compute required torque from *P* and *N*. (2) Choose *μ*, *Rₒ*, *Rᵢ* (often *Rₒ/Rᵢ ≈ 1.5*–$2$). (3) Compute *W* from uniform-wear formula. (4) Check *pₘₐₓ* ≤ *[p]*. (5) Verify heat dissipation.
- **Always** keep *n*, *μ*, *W*, *Rₒ*, *Rᵢ* with units; report torque in N·m and pressure in Pa (or MPa).
- **For multi-disc applications** (motorbike clutches, limited-slip differentials, synchromesh gearboxes), the per-surface *W* is the same as for a single disc, but the *total* torque and *total* heat capacity are *n* times larger — this is the entire reason multi-plate clutches exist.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Disc Clutches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiple Disc Clutches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Multiple Disc Clutch** (also termed a *Multi-Plate Clutch*) is a friction clutch in which **more than one friction pair** is engaged to transmit torque between a driving shaft and a driven shaft. It consists of a series of interleaved discs — alternately keyed to the driving and driven members — pressed together axially to generate frictional torque across **multiple contact surfaces simultaneously**.

The defining parameter of a multi-disc clutch is the **number of friction surfaces (n)**, given by:

$$n = (N_1 + N_2) - 1$$

where $N_1$ is the number of discs keyed to the driving member and $N_2$ is the number of discs keyed to the driven member. For a total of $N$ discs arranged alternately, $n = N - 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Multi-disc clutches are universally employed where high torque is to be transmitted within a **limited radial envelope** — a single disc would either be too large in diameter or unable to develop the required frictional torque.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Book-Clutch" Intuition

Imagine you are trying to stop a spinning bicycle wheel by gripping it between your palms.
- If you grip with **one pair of palms (one contact)**, you need a strong squeeze to generate enough friction.
- Now suppose you **stack 5 such grip pairs in series along the shaft axis** and squeeze them all at once. The total frictional torque jumps by a factor of **5**, even though your squeeze force per pair is identical.

This is precisely how a multi-disc clutch works: it **multiplies the effective torque capacity** of a given axial clamping force by increasing the number of parallel friction interfaces inside a compact housing. The same logic that lets a small hydraulic press generate tons of force also lets a small multi-disc clutch transmit hundreds of N·m of torque — a perfect example of the engineering principle of **force multiplication through surface multiplication**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world examples of multi-disc clutches:**
> - Motorcycle gearboxes (often 5–7 discs wet-running in oil).
> - Automatic transmission torque converters & lock-up clutches.
> - Heavy earth-moving equipment and industrial press drives.
> - Formula-1 carbon-carbon multi-plate racing clutches.

## 1.3 Physical Parameters & Symbols (Standard KTU Notation)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $R_o$ | Outer radius of friction disc | mm |
| $R_i$ | Inner radius of friction disc | mm |
| $R_m$ | Mean friction radius | mm |
| $r$ | Variable radius (analysis) | mm |
| $\mu$ | Coefficient of friction | dimensionless |
| $p$ | Pressure at radius $r$ | MPa or N/mm² |
| $W$ | Total axial clamping force | N |
| $T$ | Frictional torque capacity | N·mm |
| $N_1$ | Number of driving discs | — |
| $N_2$ | Number of driven discs | — |
| $n$ | Number of friction surfaces = $(N_1+N_2)-1$ | — |
| $\omega$ | Angular speed of shaft | rad/s |
| $P$ | Power transmitted | W or kW |
| $U$ | Sliding velocity at radius $r$ | m/s |

> [!TIP]
> A frequent KTU trick: students forget that **$n$ refers to contact surfaces, not discs**. Always subtract 1 from the total number of discs, or compute $(N_1 + N_2 - 1)$ for non-symmetric stacks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Multi-Disc Clutch? — The Design Driver

A single disc clutch (the unit covered in Module 2's first part) has torque capacity bounded by:

$$T_{\text{single}} = \mu \, W \, R_m \quad \text{(uniform wear basis)}$$

To increase $T$, the designer can either:
1. Increase the axial force $W$ — limited by bearing loads and operator effort.
2. Increase the mean radius $R_m$ — limited by space and rim speed.
3. **Increase the number of friction surfaces $n$** — this is the multi-disc solution.

Multiplying $n$ effectively multiplies torque capacity **without** raising $W$ or $R_m$. This is the **fundamental design philosophy** behind every multi-disc clutch.

## 2.2 The Two Pressure Assumptions (Recap from Single-Disc Theory)

When the disc is pressed against its mating surface, the pressure distribution across the contact annulus is not uniform. KTU 2024 mandates that students must analyse under **both** of the following assumptions and then **adopt the more conservative (lower) torque** for safe design:

### 2.2.1 Uniform Pressure Theory
- Assumption: $p = \text{constant}$ across the entire contact annulus.
- Justification: Valid for **new, rigid, unworn** clutches with stiff levers and freshly ground linings.

### 2.2.2 Uniform Wear Theory (Uniform Rate of Wear)
- Assumption: $p \cdot r = C = \text{constant}$, i.e., wear rate is uniform radially.
- Justification: Valid for clutches that have undergone a brief **run-in (wear-in) period** — the actual operating condition of most engineering clutches. KTU considers this the **preferred design basis**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **canonical formula bank** for any KTU ESE problem on multi-disc clutches. Memorise every entry.

| # | Quantity | Uniform Pressure Theory | Uniform Wear Theory |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pressure distribution | $p = \dfrac{W}{\pi\, n\, (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})}$ | $p\, r = \dfrac{W}{2\pi\, n\, (R_o - R_i)} \;\Rightarrow\; p = \dfrac{W}{2\pi\, n\, r\, (R_o - R_i)}$ |
| 2 | Maximum pressure location | Anywhere (constant) | At $r = R_i$ (inner radius) |
| 3 | Friction force on elemental ring | $dF = \mu\, p\, (2\pi r\, dr)$ | $dF = \mu\, p\, (2\pi r\, dr)$ |
| 4 | Elemental torque | $dT = \mu\, p\, (2\pi r^{2}\, dr)$ | $dT = \mu\, p\, (2\pi r^{2}\, dr)$ |
| 5 | **Total Torque Capacity** | $T = \dfrac{2}{3}\,\mu\, W\, n \cdot \dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}$ | $T = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\mu\, W\, n\, (R_o + R_i)$ |
| 6 | Equivalent mean radius | $R_{eq} = \dfrac{2}{3}\cdot \dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}$ | $R_{eq} = \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$ |
| 7 | Power transmitted (at $\omega$) | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | $P = T \cdot \omega$ |
| 8 | Number of friction surfaces | $n = N_1 + N_2 - 1$ | $n = N_1 + N_2 - 1$ |
| 9 | Sliding velocity at radius $r$ | $U = \omega \cdot r$ | $U = \omega \cdot r$ |
| 10 | Heat generated per second | $H = T \cdot \omega$ (Watts) | $H = T \cdot \omega$ (Watts) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** In all design problems, unless the question **explicitly states** "uniform pressure", the default design assumption is **uniform wear**. The torque capacity used for design is the **smaller of the two** values to ensure the clutch will not slip under either fresh or worn conditions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Typical Application | Why Multi-Disc? |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive (manual transmission)** | Synchromesh & dog clutches with multi-disc packs | Compact packaging; high torque at small diameter |
| **Motorcycles** | Wet multi-plate clutch bathed in oil | Smooth engagement; cooling by oil circulation |
| **Heavy Earth-Moving** | Bulldozer/loader drive clutches | Torque density critical; smooth engagement under shock load |
| **Aerospace (helicopters)** | Rotor drive free-wheel units | Fail-safe, high torque-to-weight |
| **Marine** | Reduction gear clutches | Massive torque at low rpm; oil-cooled disc stack |

## 2.5 Selection Strategy — How KTU Frames the Design Problem

A standard KTU design problem asks for the **required number of friction surfaces $n$** to transmit a given power at a given speed using a given axial force. The algebraic chain is:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{power}} = T \cdot \omega \;\Rightarrow\; T_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{power}}}{\omega} \;\Rightarrow\; n_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{req}}}{\mu \, W \, R_{eq}}}$$

Round $n_{\text{req}}$ **up to the next integer** and then back-compute the actual axial force $W$ if needed.

> [!TIP]
> **Sign convention reminder:** $\omega$ must be in **rad/s**, $P$ in **Watts** (1 kW = 1000 W), and $T$ in **N·m** for dimensional consistency, OR keep everything in N, mm, MPa and the formulas above will yield $T$ in N·mm — be careful with units!

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Uniform Pressure Theory for a Multi-Disc Clutch

### Step 1: Geometry Setup

Consider one friction surface. The contact annulus lies between inner radius $R_i$ and outer radius $R_o$. A small elemental ring at radius $r$ has:
- Area: $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$
- Normal force on element: $dN = p \cdot dA = p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$

### Step 2: Total Axial Force Equilibrium

The total axial clamping force $W$ (per friction surface) is the integral of the pressure over the annulus:

$$W = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

By the **uniform pressure assumption**, $p$ is constant and can be pulled out of the integral:

$$W = 2\pi p \int_{R_i}^{R_o} r \, dr = 2\pi p \left[ \dfrac{r^{2}}{2} \right]_{R_i}^{R_o}$$

$$W = \pi p \, (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})$$

Solving for $p$ (valid per surface):

$$p = \dfrac{W}{\pi (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})}$$

> **Why this matters:** This is the *per-surface* pressure. For $n$ surfaces, the total axial force on the pressure plate is $W_{\text{total}} = n \cdot W$ if $W$ is taken per surface, OR the equation is rewritten with $W$ as total axial force and $n$ inside the denominator. The KTU convention (used in Section 2) is the **latter** — the formula sheet entry reads $p = W / [\pi n (R_o^2 - R_i^2)]$ with $W$ as total axial load.

### Step 3: Elemental Frictional Force

The frictional force on the elemental ring is:

$$dF_f = \mu \cdot dN = \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

### Step 4: Elemental Torque

The elemental torque about the shaft axis is $dT = dF_f \cdot r$:

$$dT = \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r^{2} \, dr$$

### Step 5: Integrate to Get Total Torque (per surface)

$$T_1 = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi \mu p r^{2} \, dr = 2\pi \mu p \left[ \dfrac{r^{3}}{3} \right]_{R_i}^{R_o}$$

$$T_1 = \dfrac{2\pi \mu p}{3} \, (R_o^{3} - R_i^{3})$$

### Step 6: Substitute $p$ from Step 2

$$T_1 = \dfrac{2\pi \mu}{3} \cdot \dfrac{W}{\pi (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})} \cdot (R_o^{3} - R_i^{3})$$

$$T_1 = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}$$

### Step 7: Multiply by $n$ for the Multi-Disc Clutch

Since each of the $n$ friction surfaces transmits $T_1$ in parallel:

$$\boxed{T = n \cdot T_1 = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot n \cdot \dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}}$$

> **Note on units:** If $W$ is in N, $R$ in mm, then $T$ is in N·mm. Convert to N·m by dividing by 1000.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Uniform Wear Theory for a Multi-Disc Clutch

### Step 1: Wear Assumption

The **uniform wear hypothesis** states that the volumetric wear rate is constant at every point in the contact annulus. By Archard's law, wear rate $\propto p \cdot v = p \cdot \omega r$. For this to be constant:

$$p \cdot r = C = \text{constant}$$

Therefore the pressure at radius $r$ is:

$$p = \dfrac{C}{r}$$

### Step 2: Total Axial Force Equilibrium

$$W = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} \dfrac{C}{r} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$W = 2\pi C \int_{R_i}^{R_o} dr = 2\pi C \, (R_o - R_i)$$

Solving for the wear constant $C$:

$$C = \dfrac{W}{2\pi (R_o - R_i)}$$

So the pressure distribution is:

$$p(r) = \dfrac{W}{2\pi r (R_o - R_i)}$$

> **Engineering insight:** Pressure is **highest at the inner radius** ($p_{\max} = C/R_i$) and **lowest at the outer radius** ($p_{\min} = C/R_o$). This matches physical observation — the inner radius has the lowest sliding velocity, so it must carry higher pressure to wear at the same rate.

### Step 3: Elemental Torque

$$dT = \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r^{2} \, dr = \mu \cdot \dfrac{C}{r} \cdot 2\pi r^{2} \, dr = 2\pi \mu C \, r \, dr$$

### Step 4: Integrate

$$T_1 = 2\pi \mu C \int_{R_i}^{R_o} r \, dr = 2\pi \mu C \left[ \dfrac{r^{2}}{2} \right]_{R_i}^{R_o} = \pi \mu C (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})$$

### Step 5: Substitute $C$

$$T_1 = \pi \mu \cdot \dfrac{W}{2\pi (R_o - R_i)} \cdot (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})$$

Note that $R_o^{2} - R_i^{2} = (R_o - R_i)(R_o + R_i)$, so:

$$T_1 = \dfrac{\mu W (R_o - R_i)(R_o + R_i)}{2(R_o - R_i)} = \dfrac{\mu W (R_o + R_i)}{2}$$

### Step 6: Multiply by $n$

$$\boxed{T = n \cdot T_1 = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot n \cdot (R_o + R_i)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison insight:** The uniform-wear formula is algebraically simpler and is therefore the **first choice** for KTU design problems. The "equivalent mean radius" for uniform wear is simply the **arithmetic mean** $R_m = (R_o + R_i)/2$, leading to the elegant form $T = \mu W n R_m$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Theory

| Condition | Choose | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Clutch is brand new, levers are very stiff | Uniform Pressure | Wear has not yet redistributed the pressure |
| Clutch has run-in period (most cases) | Uniform Wear | Reflects actual worn-out operating state |
| Problem explicitly says "new" or "rigid" | Uniform Pressure | Direct instruction |
| Problem says nothing (KTU default) | Uniform Wear | Standard KTU convention |
| Both must be checked for safety | Compute both, take **smaller** $T$ | Conservative design |
| Maximum pressure is asked | Uniform Wear (gives $p_{\max}$ at $R_i$) | Pressure varies in this theory |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Complete KTU-Style Solution

**Problem:** A multi-disc clutch has **4 discs** in oil with $\mu = 0.1$, inner radius $R_i = 50$ mm, outer radius $R_o = 100$ mm. Axial force $W = 2000$ N. Shaft speed $N = 1500$ rpm. Determine:
(a) Torque capacity under uniform pressure and uniform wear.
(b) Power transmitted.
(c) Maximum pressure under uniform wear.

**Solution:**

### Step A: Number of friction surfaces
With 4 discs in an alternating stack: $n = 4 - 1 = 3$

### Step B: Uniform Pressure Torque
Apply the boxed formula:

$$T_{UP} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot n \cdot \dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}$$

Compute numerator inside:
$$R_o^{3} - R_i^{3} = 100^{3} - 50^{3} = 1{,}000{,}000 - 125{,}000 = 875{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

Compute denominator inside:
$$R_o^{2} - R_i^{2} = 100^{2} - 50^{2} = 10{,}000 - 2{,}500 = 7{,}500 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

So the ratio:
$$\dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}} = \dfrac{875{,}000}{7{,}500} = 116.667 \text{ mm}$$

Now plug in:
$$T_{UP} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot 0.1 \cdot 2000 \cdot 3 \cdot 116.667$$

$$T_{UP} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot 0.1 \cdot 2000 \cdot 350.0 = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot 70{,}000$$

$$T_{UP} = 46{,}666.67 \text{ N·mm} = 46.67 \text{ N·m}$$

### Step C: Uniform Wear Torque
$$T_{UW} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot n \cdot (R_o + R_i)$$

$$T_{UW} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 0.1 \cdot 2000 \cdot 3 \cdot (100 + 50)$$

$$T_{UW} = 0.5 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 2000 \cdot 3 \cdot 150 = 45{,}000 \text{ N·mm} = 45 \text{ N·m}$$

### Step D: Adopt the Lower Torque for Design
$$\boxed{T_{\text{design}} = \min(T_{UP}, T_{UW}) = 45 \text{ N·m} \;(\text{uniform wear governs})}$$

### Step E: Power Transmitted
Angular speed:
$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 1500}{60} = 50\pi = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$P = T \cdot \omega = 45 \cdot 157.08 = 7068.6 \text{ W} \approx 7.07 \text{ kW}$$

### Step F: Maximum Pressure (Uniform Wear)
Wear constant:
$$C = \dfrac{W}{2\pi n (R_o - R_i)} = \dfrac{2000}{2\pi \cdot 3 \cdot 50} = \dfrac{2000}{942.48} = 2.122 \text{ N/mm}$$

Max pressure at $R_i = 50$ mm:
$$p_{\max} = \dfrac{C}{R_i} = \dfrac{2.122}{50} = 0.0424 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 0.0424 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's hidden check:** A common KTU follow-up question asks *"At what radius is the pressure equal to the average pressure?"* The answer is the **mean radius** $R_m = (R_o + R_i)/2 = 75$ mm for the uniform-wear theory. This is a frequent 2-mark sub-question!
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Multi-Disc Clutch — Cross-Sectional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block represents the **functional architecture** of a multi-disc clutch, mapping how axial force is distributed through the disc stack to generate torque. Mermaid safeguards are applied: all node IDs are alphanumeric, no reserved keywords, and labels contain no markdown tags.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driving Shaft Input Torque Tin] --> B[Driving Hub keyed to Shaft]
    B --> C1[Driving Disc 1 with external splines]
    B --> C2[Driving Disc 2 with external splines]
    B --> C3[Driving Disc 3 with external splines]
    D1[Driven Disc 1 with internal splines on outer housing] --> E[Driven Hub and Output Torque Tout]
    D2[Driven Disc 2 with internal splines on outer housing] --> E
    F[Axial Clamping Force W applied via Pressure Plate] --> G[Disc Stack Assembly]
    C1 --> G
    C2 --> G
    C3 --> G
    D1 --> G
    D2 --> G
    G --> H{N Friction Contact Surfaces n equals N1 plus N2 minus 1}
    H --> I[Frictional Torque T transmitted to Driven Member]
    I --> E
    J[Cooling Oil Circulation in Wet Clutch] --> G
    K[Return Springs disengage stack] --> F
```

## 4.2 Force/Torque Flow Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DS as Driving Shaft
    participant PD as Pressure Plate
    participant STK as Disc Stack
    participant DD as Driven Disc Hub
    participant OS as Output Shaft
    
    DS->>PD: Rotational Input + Axial Clamp W
    activate PD
    PD->>STK: Compresses n Friction Surfaces
    activate STK
    STK->>STK: Friction force F_f equals mu times W
    STK->>STK: Torque T equals F_f times R_m times n
    deactivate STK
    STK->>DD: Transmits T across n surfaces
    activate DD
    DD->>OS: Delivers Output Torque Tout
    deactivate DD
    deactivate PD
    Note over STK: Heat generated H equals T times omega
    Note over STK: Must be dissipated via oil or fins
```

## 4.3 Disc Stack Arrangement (N₁ = 3 driving, N₂ = 2 driven → n = 4 surfaces)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[End Plate Fixed to Driving] --> B[Driving Disc 1]
    B --> C[Driven Disc 1]
    C --> D[Driving Disc 2]
    D --> E[Driven Disc 2]
    E --> F[Driving Disc 3]
    F --> G[Pressure Plate Axial Force W]
    
    H[Surface S1 between A and B] -.Contact.-> B
    I[Surface S2 between B and C] -.Contact.-> C
    J[Surface S3 between C and D] -.Contact.-> D
    K[Surface S4 between D and E] -.Contact.-> E
    L[Surface S5 between E and F] -.Contact.-> F
    M[Surface S6 between F and G] -.Contact.-> G
    
    style H fill:#f9e79f
    style I fill:#a9dfbf
    style J fill:#f9e79f
    style K fill:#a9dfbf
    style L fill:#f9e79f
    style M fill:#a9dfbf
```

> **Reading the diagram:** With 3 driving discs and 2 driven discs (5 discs total, 6 interfaces in this configuration). However, in the **standard multi-disc clutch**, the two **end surfaces** (one against the end-plate, one against the pressure plate) are usually **excluded** because they cannot transmit torque from the driving to the driven side — only the **internal interleaved surfaces** count. This is why $n = (N_1 + N_2) - 1$ and not $N_1 + N_2$.

## 4.4 Pressure Distribution Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Choose Theory] --> B{Problem Statement}
    B -->|New clutch, stiff levers| C[Uniform Pressure]
    B -->|Run-in, default, no info| D[Uniform Wear]
    C --> E[Constant pressure p across annulus]
    D --> F[Pressure varies as p equals C over r]
    E --> G[Maximum pressure anywhere]
    F --> H[Maximum pressure at inner radius Ri]
    G --> I[T equals two thirds mu W n R_o_cubed_minus_Ri_cubed over R_o_squared_minus_Ri_squared]
    H --> J[T equals one half mu W n R_o_plus_Ri]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** When KTU asks for "maximum pressure", it is almost always a uniform-wear problem. The pressure is always **maximum at the inner radius** because that is where sliding velocity is minimum and thus the highest pressure is needed to maintain uniform volumetric wear.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
**State the expression for the torque capacity of a multi-disc clutch under the uniform wear theory. Explain each term.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
$$T = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot n \cdot (R_o + R_i)$$

where:
- $T$ = frictional torque capacity (N·mm)
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction of the lining material
- $W$ = total axial clamping force (N)
- $n$ = number of friction contact surfaces = $(N_1 + N_2) - 1$
- $R_o$ = outer radius of the friction disc (mm)
- $R_i$ = inner radius of the friction disc (mm)

The torque is **directly proportional** to $\mu$, $W$, and $n$, and to the **arithmetic mean radius** $(R_o + R_i)/2$. This expression is used for design of clutches after the run-in wear period.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**What is the number of friction surfaces in a multi-disc clutch having 5 driving discs and 4 driven discs? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
For a multi-disc clutch, the discs are arranged alternately (driving–driven–driven–driving…). The total number of friction surfaces equals one less than the total number of discs, since the stack has interleaved contact interfaces.

$$n = (N_1 + N_2) - 1 = (5 + 4) - 1 = 8 \text{ friction surfaces}$$

**Justification:** Each adjacent pair of driving and driven disc creates one friction surface. With 9 total discs in an alternating stack, there are 8 interfaces where one driving and one driven disc meet.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Question Bank (14-Mark Module Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Module Rule:** From Module 2, students answer **one full 14-mark question** (choice provided). Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Below are **two alternative question choices** as required by the KTU pattern.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 MARKS)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]**

A multi-disc wet-running clutch has the following design data:
- Number of discs: $N = 7$ (4 driving + 3 driven, all identical)
- Inner radius of friction lining: $R_i = 60$ mm
- Outer radius of friction lining: $R_o = 120$ mm
- Coefficient of friction (oil-lubricated): $\mu = 0.08$
- Axial clamping force: $W = 3500$ N
- Shaft speed: $N_{rpm} = 1200$ rpm

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the torque capacity of the clutch under:**
(i) Uniform pressure theory
(ii) Uniform wear theory

Which value should be adopted for design, and why?

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compute:**
(i) The power transmitted at the given speed.
(ii) The maximum intensity of pressure in the lining under uniform wear theory.
(iii) The heat generated per second, and suggest a cooling method for the wet clutch.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

##### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1: Number of friction surfaces**
$$n = (N_1 + N_2) - 1 = (4 + 3) - 1 = 6$$

**Step 2: Uniform Pressure Torque**
$$T_{UP} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot n \cdot \dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}$$

$$R_o^{3} - R_i^{3} = 120^{3} - 60^{3} = 1{,}728{,}000 - 216{,}000 = 1{,}512{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

$$R_o^{2} - R_i^{2} = 120^{2} - 60^{2} = 14{,}400 - 3{,}600 = 10{,}800 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$\dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}} = \dfrac{1{,}512{,}000}{10{,}800} = 140 \text{ mm}$$

$$T_{UP} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot 0.08 \cdot 3500 \cdot 6 \cdot 140 = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot 235{,}200 = 156{,}800 \text{ N·mm} = 156.8 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3: Uniform Wear Torque**
$$T_{UW} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot n \cdot (R_o + R_i)$$

$$T_{UW} = 0.5 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 3500 \cdot 6 \cdot (120 + 60) = 0.5 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 3500 \cdot 6 \cdot 180$$

$$T_{UW} = 151{,}200 \text{ N·mm} = 151.2 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Design Selection**
Since $T_{UW} = 151.2$ N·m < $T_{UP} = 156.8$ N·m, the **uniform wear torque governs** the design.

$$\boxed{T_{\text{design}} = 151.2 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Reason:** Uniform wear is the conservative basis that represents the actual operating condition of a clutch after run-in. The clutch must be able to transmit at least $T_{UW}$ under both fresh and worn conditions.

##### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1: Power Transmitted**
$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N_{rpm}}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 1200}{60} = 40\pi = 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$P = T_{\text{design}} \cdot \omega = 151.2 \cdot 125.66 = 19{,}000 \text{ W} = 19 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 19 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 2: Maximum Pressure (Uniform Wear)**
The wear constant is:
$$C = \dfrac{W}{2\pi \cdot n \cdot (R_o - R_i)} = \dfrac{3500}{2\pi \cdot 6 \cdot (120 - 60)} = \dfrac{3500}{2\pi \cdot 360} = \dfrac{3500}{2261.95} = 1.547 \text{ N/mm}$$

Maximum pressure occurs at the inner radius $R_i = 60$ mm:
$$p_{\max} = \dfrac{C}{R_i} = \dfrac{1.547}{60} = 0.0258 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 0.0258 \text{ MPa} = 25.8 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\boxed{p_{\max} \approx 0.0258 \text{ MPa} = 25.8 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 3: Heat Generated per Second**
$$H = T \cdot \omega = 151.2 \cdot 125.66 = 19{,}000 \text{ W} = 19 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{H \approx 19 \text{ kJ/s}}$$

**Cooling Method:** Since this is a **wet clutch**, the disc stack is submerged in circulating oil. The recommended cooling strategy is:
- Force-feed oil circulation through internal disc passages.
- Maintain oil inlet temperature below 80 °C.
- Use an external oil cooler (radiator-type heat exchanger) sized to dissipate 19 kW continuously.
- The oil absorbs the frictional heat $H$ and carries it away, then is cooled externally and re-circulated.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 MARKS) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 (Model Paper)]**

A multi-disc clutch is to be designed for an automobile transmission. The following specifications are provided:
- Power to be transmitted: $P = 25$ kW
- Speed of driving shaft: $N_{rpm} = 1440$ rpm
- Coefficient of friction (after run-in): $\mu = 0.12$
- Outer-to-inner radius ratio: $R_o / R_i = 1.5$
- Allowable axial force on the pressure plate: $W = 4000$ N
- Design is to be based on **uniform wear theory**.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Determine:**
(i) The required number of friction surfaces $n$.
(ii) The inner and outer radii of the friction disc if $R_i = 40$ mm is to be the starting trial value. Iterate if necessary and provide the final adopted radii.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**If the actual number of friction surfaces provided is 5 (so $n = 5$), determine:**
(i) The required axial force $W$ to transmit the design power.
(ii) The maximum pressure in the lining.
(iii) The total frictional work done in one engagement if the clutch takes 1.5 seconds to engage (uniform angular deceleration assumed).

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

##### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1: Required Torque**
$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N_{rpm}}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 1440}{60} = 48\pi = 150.80 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{P}{\omega} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{150.80} = 165.78 \text{ N·m} = 165{,}780 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Number of Friction Surfaces (Trial 1: $R_i = 40$ mm, $R_o = 1.5 \times 40 = 60$ mm)**
$$T_{UW} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot n \cdot (R_o + R_i)$$

$$165{,}780 = 0.5 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 4000 \cdot n \cdot (60 + 40)$$

$$165{,}780 = 0.5 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 4000 \cdot 100 \cdot n = 24{,}000 \cdot n$$

$$n = \dfrac{165{,}780}{24{,}000} = 6.91$$

Round up to $n = 7$ (next integer).

**Step 3: Back-Compute the Required Radii for $n = 7$**
With $n = 7$, $R_i = 40$ mm, and $R_o / R_i = 1.5$, we keep $R_i = 40$ mm and $R_o = 60$ mm (these satisfy the ratio). The torque that will be transmitted:

$$T_{\text{actual}} = 0.5 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 4000 \cdot 7 \cdot 100 = 168{,}000 \text{ N·mm} = 168 \text{ N·m}$$

This exceeds $T_{\text{req}} = 165.78$ N·m by ~1.3%, which is acceptable. **Final design:** $R_i = 40$ mm, $R_o = 60$ mm, $n = 7$ (i.e., 4 driving + 4 driven = 8 discs total, or similar valid combination giving 7 contact surfaces).

$$\boxed{n = 7, \quad R_i = 40 \text{ mm}, \quad R_o = 60 \text{ mm}}$$

##### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1: Required Axial Force for $n = 5$**
Using the uniform wear formula:

$$T_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot n \cdot (R_o + R_i)$$

$$165{,}780 = 0.5 \cdot 0.12 \cdot W \cdot 5 \cdot (60 + 40)$$

$$165{,}780 = 30 \cdot W$$

$$W = \dfrac{165{,}780}{30} = 5{,}526 \text{ N}$$

**But the allowable $W = 4000$ N is exceeded!**

**Conclusion:** With only 5 surfaces and the specified geometry, the allowable axial force is **insufficient** to transmit 25 kW. Either:
- Increase $n$ to 7 (as in Part a), OR
- Increase the radii, OR
- Use a higher-friction lining material.

$$\boxed{W_{\text{required}} = 5{,}526 \text{ N} > W_{\text{allowable}} = 4{,}000 \text{ N — design must be revised}}$$

**Step 2: Maximum Pressure (using the design $W = 4000$ N for the $n=5$ case to find what pressure arises if we were to use this)**
$$C = \dfrac{W}{2\pi n (R_o - R_i)} = \dfrac{4000}{2\pi \cdot 5 \cdot 20} = \dfrac{4000}{628.32} = 6.366 \text{ N/mm}$$

$$p_{\max} = \dfrac{C}{R_i} = \dfrac{6.366}{40} = 0.159 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 0.159 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Frictional Work Done in One Engagement**
Average torque during engagement (uniform deceleration, so $\alpha = \text{const}$, $T_{\text{avg}} = T_{\text{design}}/2$ — wait, this needs careful treatment):

Actually, if angular deceleration is uniform from $\omega_0$ to 0, the torque required for deceleration is $T = I \alpha = I \cdot (\omega_0/t_e)$, which is constant. The frictional work = $T \cdot \theta$, where $\theta$ is total angular displacement during engagement.

For uniform deceleration over $t_e = 1.5$ s:
$$\theta = \dfrac{1}{2} \omega_0 t_e = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 150.80 \cdot 1.5 = 113.10 \text{ rad}$$

Using the design torque for the $n = 7$ case (or the $n = 5$ case if we accept the $W = 5526$ N):
$$T = 165{,}780 \text{ N·mm} = 165.78 \text{ N·m}$$

$$W_{\text{friction}} = T \cdot \theta = 165.78 \cdot 113.10 = 18{,}750 \text{ J} = 18.75 \text{ kJ}$$

**Verification by energy method:** Initial kinetic energy of the driven inertia should equal the work done (assuming it starts from rest and is brought up to $\omega_0$):
$$KE = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega_0^{2}$$

If the work done equals the kinetic energy delivered, then $18.75$ kJ must be absorbed as heat. This heat must be removed by the cooling system.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $n$ factor:** Many students write the single-disc formula even though the question is about a multi-disc clutch. Always check whether $n > 1$ and multiply accordingly. **(−2 to −3 marks)**
> 2. **Mixing up radius ratio:** Students often write $R_o / R_i = 1.5$ and compute the radii incorrectly. The outer is larger, so $R_o = 1.5 R_i$, not the other way around. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** Using $T$ in N·mm and $\omega$ in rad/s gives $P$ in N·mm/s, not Watts. Convert $T$ to N·m or multiply by 1000. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 4. **Not rounding up $n$:** If $n_{\text{req}} = 6.91$, you MUST round to $n = 7$ — a fractional number of friction surfaces is physically meaningless. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Forgetting the design criterion:** The smaller of the two torques must be used. If the question asks "design torque", it means $\min(T_{UP}, T_{UW})$. **(−1 to −2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision — Pin this to your wall before the exam!**

- ☐ **Number of friction surfaces:** $n = (N_1 + N_2) - 1$ — for a total of $N$ discs, $n = N - 1$ if arranged alternately.
- ☐ **Two theories:** Uniform Pressure ($p = \text{const}$) and Uniform Wear ($pr = \text{const}$).
- ☐ **Default design basis (KTU):** Uniform Wear.
- ☐ **Adopted torque:** $T_{\text{design}} = \min(T_{UP}, T_{UW})$.
- ☐ **Uniform Pressure formula:** $T = \dfrac{2}{3} \mu W n \cdot \dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}$
- ☐ **Uniform Wear formula:** $T = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu W n (R_o + R_i) = \mu W n R_m$
- ☐ **Pressure distribution (uniform wear):** $p(r) = \dfrac{W}{2\pi n r (R_o - R_i)}$
- ☐ **Maximum pressure (uniform wear):** at $R = R_i$, equals $p_{\max} = \dfrac{W}{2\pi n R_i (R_o - R_i)}$
- ☐ **Power transmitted:** $P = T \omega$ with $\omega$ in rad/s.
- ☐ **Angular speed conversion:** $\omega = 2\pi N_{rpm}/60$.
- ☐ **Heat generated:** $H = T \omega$ (Watts) — must be dissipated via oil/fins.
- ☐ **Number of contact surfaces is NOT the number of discs** — common KTU trap.
- ☐ **Always round UP** the required $n$ to the next integer; recompute $W$ if needed.
- ☐ **Mean radius (uniform wear):** $R_m = (R_o + R_i)/2$.
- ☐ **Equivalent mean radius (uniform pressure):** $R_{eq} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}$.
- ☐ **Unit discipline:** Use N, mm, N/mm², N·mm, rad/s OR N, m, Pa, N·m, rad/s — never mix!
- ☐ **Wet vs Dry:** Wet clutches have lower $\mu$ (oil-lubricated, typically 0.05–0.1) but better heat dissipation; dry clutches have higher $\mu$ (0.2–0.4) but require external cooling.
- ☐ **Multi-disc advantage:** Torque density increases by factor $n$ over single disc, with no change in $W$ or $R_m$.
- ☐ **Application memory map:** Motorcycles, automotive gearboxes, racing clutches, heavy machinery, helicopters — high torque in compact envelope.
- ☐ **KTU 2024 weightage:** Module 2 carries ~22% of total marks; expect 1 full 14-mark question OR 1 Part-A + 1 Part-B (from Module 2's clutch section).

> **End of Topic: Multiple Disc Clutches** — Module 2, PEMET743 — KTU 2024 Scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cone Clutch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cone Clutch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Cone Clutch — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **cone clutch** is a friction clutch in which the male and female members are shaped as truncated cones (frustums) that engage axially along a conical contact surface. Torque is transmitted from the driving member to the driven member through the frictional resistance generated at this conical interface when an axial engagement force is applied.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMET743 — Module 2):** *Cone Clutch — Design of contact surfaces, uniform pressure and uniform wear theories, axial force and torque transmission relations for a conical friction drive.*

The geometric parameter that distinguishes a cone clutch from a flat-plate (disc) clutch is the **semi-cone angle (α/2)**, where **α** is the full included cone angle. This angle governs the mechanical advantage of the clutch and is typically chosen between **12° and 15°** (semi-cone angle 6°–7.5°) to ensure self-energisation remains bounded and disengagement is not overly difficult.

## 1.2 Component Architecture

| S.No | Component | Function |
|------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Male Cone (Driver)** | Conical member mounted on the driving shaft; carries the friction lining. |
| 2 | **Female Cone (Driven)** | Conical socket mounted on the driven shaft; provides the mating conical recess. |
| 3 | **Friction Lining** | Wear-resistant material (asbestos, cork, or sintered bronze) bonded to the male cone face. |
| 4 | **Spring / Lever System** | Provides the axial engagement force $P$ that presses the cones together. |
| 5 | **Shifting Mechanism** | Sliding key/splines allowing axial translation of the male cone for engagement/disengagement. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Ice-Cream Cone Grip

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine you are holding a paper ice-cream cone filled with ice cream, and you press the open end of another cone on top of it. If you now twist the top cone while pressing down, friction between the two slanted paper surfaces resists the twist. The **more steeply** the cone is angled (large α), the **less** the normal force you generate for the same axial push. Conversely, a **shallow** cone (small α) creates a huge normal force from a modest push — *that is precisely the self-energising principle* that makes cone clutches transmit more torque than a flat disc clutch of the same diameter and axial force.

The mathematical amplification factor is precisely $\csc(\alpha/2) = 1/\sin(\alpha/2)$. For a typical semi-cone angle of $7.5°$, this factor is approximately **7.66**, meaning the effective normal force is nearly **8 times** the axial thrust — a major reason cone clutches are chosen where compact, high-torque transmission is required.

## 1.4 Why Use a Cone Clutch?

- **Higher torque capacity per unit axial force** than single-disc clutches (amplification factor $1/\sin(\alpha/2)$).
- Compact radial envelope — the conical face provides large contact area within a small radius.
- Smooth, gradual engagement because engagement is progressive along the cone face.
- Common in **motorcycles, scooters, small machine tools, and industrial machinery** where high torque is transmitted at low speeds.

## 1.5 Disadvantages / Practical Limitations

- Slight axial movement occurs during engagement (judder tendency) — limits use in high-speed applications.
- Conical surfaces are difficult and expensive to machine to high precision.
- The contact faces tend to wear non-uniformly; periodic re-grinding of the cone is required.
- The contact surface does not run out of the axial force — requires deliberate disengagement (limited self-release).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric and Force Relationships

Consider a truncated cone clutch with:
- **R** = outer (larger) radius of the friction face (m)
- **r** = inner (smaller) radius of the friction face (m)
- **b** = face width along the cone slant $= R - r$ (m)
- **α** = full cone angle (included angle)
- **α/2** = semi-cone angle
- **R_m** = mean radius on the slant $= (R + r)/2$ (m)
- **P** = axial engagement force applied along the shaft axis (N)
- **N** = total normal force perpendicular to the conical contact surface (N)
- **μ** = coefficient of friction between contacting surfaces (dimensionless)
- **p** = normal pressure on the contact surface (N/m² or Pa)

### 2.1.1 Normal Force — Axial Force Relationship

Resolving forces on an elemental ring of the cone:

$$N = \frac{P}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$$

The friction force available at the surface is:

$$F_r = \mu N = \frac{\mu P}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is normal force larger than axial force?** Because the cone is slanted, only the *sin(α/2)* component of the normal force acts along the axis. To balance the applied axial push $P$, the normal force must therefore scale by $1/\sin(\alpha/2)$, which exceeds 1 for any cone angle less than $180°$.

### 2.1.2 Self-Locking Condition

The cone is **self-locking** when $\mu \geq \tan(\alpha/2)$, because the friction force component along the axis would then exceed the axial disengagement component, making the clutch unable to release. Designers must therefore ensure:

$$\mu < \tan(\alpha/2)$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Theory | Units |
|---|----------------------|---------|--------|-------|
| 1 | Normal force | $N = P / \sin(\alpha/2)$ | — | N |
| 2 | Friction force | $F_r = \mu P / \sin(\alpha/2)$ | — | N |
| 3 | Axial thrust (uniform pressure) | $P = 2\pi p R_m b / \sin(\alpha/2)$ | Uniform Pressure | N |
| 4 | Axial thrust (uniform wear) | $P = 2\pi C b / \sin(\alpha/2)$ | Uniform Wear | N |
| 5 | Torque — Uniform Pressure | $T = \frac{2}{3}\,\mu P\,\dfrac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{R^{2} - r^{2}}\,\dfrac{1}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$ | Uniform Pressure | N·m |
| 6 | Torque — Uniform Wear | $T = \dfrac{\mu P\,C}{2\,\sin(\alpha/2)} = \pi \mu C\,b\,(R+r) / \sin(\alpha/2)$ | Uniform Wear | N·m |
| 7 | Wear constant relation | $C = pR = p_{\max}\,r$ | Uniform Wear | N/m |
| 8 | Mean radius | $R_m = (R + r) / 2$ | Geometry | m |
| 9 | Face width (slant) | $b = R - r$ | Geometry | m |
| 10 | Self-locking criterion | $\mu \geq \tan(\alpha/2) \Rightarrow$ *self-locking* | — | — |
| 11 | Torque amplification factor | $T_{\text{cone}} / T_{\text{disc}} = 1/\sin(\alpha/2)$ | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The semi-cone angle $(\alpha/2)$ appears in **every** torque and force expression for a cone clutch. Board examiners frequently test whether students correctly use $\alpha/2$ (semi-cone) versus $\alpha$ (full cone). **Always re-check the angle convention given in the problem statement.**

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Cone Clutches

- **Automotive (Two-Wheelers):** Used in motorcycle and scooter transmissions where compact size and high torque capacity are critical at low engine speeds.
- **Machine Tools:** Lathe spindles, drill presses — applications requiring smooth torque transfer and positive engagement.
- **Heavy Machinery:** Industrial mixers, winches, and agricultural equipment where sudden load surges are absorbed by gradual cone engagement.
- **Marine Drives:** Centrifugal cone clutches historically used in marine propulsion to provide progressive engagement under load.

## 2.4 The Two Pressure Theories — Intuitive Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two theories?** Friction linings wear over time. The "as-new" state follows the **uniform-pressure** theory (new lining, un-deformed). The "as-worn" steady state follows the **uniform-wear** theory (long operational life, pressure inversely proportional to radius). KTU examinations expect both derivations.

**Uniform Pressure (U.P.):** The contact pressure $p$ is the **same at every point** of the conical face. This is the design assumption when the lining is new and rigid.

**Uniform Wear (U.W.):** The product $p \times r$ is **constant** across the face. This is the steady-state assumption because material wear rate is proportional to $p \times v$ and rim speed $v = \omega r$, so for equal wear: $p \times r = \text{constant} = C$. This is the more conservative (lower) torque capacity and is preferred for long-life design.

## 2.5 Decision Tree — Which Theory to Use?

```
Is the lining NEW and RIGID?        ──► Uniform Pressure Theory
                                     (use p = constant, higher T)

Is the lining WORN-IN, LONG LIFE?   ──► Uniform Wear Theory
                                     (use p·r = C, lower but realistic T)

Is the problem in KTU board exam?    ──► Check the wording: 
                                      "new clutch" → UP
                                      "steady state" or "worn" → UW
```
