# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Normal Force & Axial Force Relationship

**Starting geometry:** Consider a small elemental ring on the conical surface at radius $x$, with slant width $dx$. The slant of the cone makes angle $\alpha/2$ with the axis of rotation.

**Step 1 — Elemental normal force on the ring:**

The contact area of an elemental ring of radius $x$ and slant width $dx$ on the cone face is:

$$dA = 2\pi x \, dx$$

Therefore, the elemental normal force pressing on this ring is:

$$dN = p \cdot dA = 2\pi p \, x \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Resolve along the axis:**

The normal force $dN$ acts perpendicular to the cone face. Its component along the shaft axis is:

$$dP = dN \cdot \sin(\alpha/2) = 2\pi p \, x \sin(\alpha/2) \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Integrate across the face (from $r$ to $R$):**

For **uniform pressure theory** ($p$ = constant):

$$P = \int_{r}^{R} 2\pi p \, x \sin(\alpha/2) \, dx = 2\pi p \sin(\alpha/2) \cdot \left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{r}^{R}$$

$$\boxed{P = \pi p \sin(\alpha/2) \, (R^{2} - r^{2}) = \frac{2\pi p R_m b}{\sin(\alpha/2)} \cdot \sin^{2}(\alpha/2) \;\text{(rearranged)}}$$

Equivalently, solving for the normal force:

$$N = \int_{r}^{R} 2\pi p \, x \, dx = \pi p (R^{2} - r^{2})$$

And the axial–normal relationship is verified as:

$$P = N \sin(\alpha/2) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad N = \frac{P}{\sin(\alpha/2)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque under Uniform Pressure Theory

**Step 1 — Elemental torque from an elemental ring at radius $x$:**

The elemental normal force on the ring is $dN = 2\pi p \, x \, dx$.
The elemental friction force is $dF = \mu \, dN = 2\pi \mu p \, x \, dx$.
The elemental torque is the friction force multiplied by the lever arm $x$:

$$dT = x \cdot dF = 2\pi \mu p \, x^{2} \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $r$ to $R$:**

$$T = \int_{r}^{R} 2\pi \mu p \, x^{2} \, dx = 2\pi \mu p \left[\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right]_{r}^{R}$$

$$\boxed{T = \frac{2\pi \mu p}{3}\,(R^{3} - r^{3})}$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate pressure $p$ using the axial-force relation:**

From earlier: $P = \pi p (R^{2} - r^{2}) \sin(\alpha/2)$

Solve for $p$:

$$p = \frac{P}{\pi (R^{2} - r^{2}) \sin(\alpha/2)}$$

Substitute into the torque equation:

$$T = \frac{2\pi \mu}{3}\,(R^{3} - r^{3}) \cdot \frac{P}{\pi (R^{2} - r^{2}) \sin(\alpha/2)}$$

$$\boxed{T = \frac{2}{3}\,\mu P \cdot \frac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{R^{2} - r^{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sin(\alpha/2)}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Torque under Uniform Wear Theory

**Step 1 — Apply the wear condition:**

In the steady worn state, the wear rate per unit area is proportional to $p \cdot v$ where $v = \omega x$ is the sliding velocity. For equal wear over the face:

$$p \cdot x = C = \text{constant} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad p = \frac{C}{x}$$

**Step 2 — Elemental normal force:**

$$dN = p \, dA = \frac{C}{x} \cdot 2\pi x \, dx = 2\pi C \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Elemental axial force and total axial thrust:**

$$dP = dN \sin(\alpha/2) = 2\pi C \sin(\alpha/2) \, dx$$

Integrate from $r$ to $R$:

$$P = 2\pi C \sin(\alpha/2) (R - r) = 2\pi C b \sin(\alpha/2)$$

Solving for $C$:

$$C = \frac{P}{2\pi b \sin(\alpha/2)}$$

**Step 4 — Elemental torque:**

$$dT = x \cdot \mu \, dN = x \cdot \mu \cdot 2\pi C \, dx = 2\pi \mu C \, x \, dx$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $r$ to $R$:**

$$T = \int_{r}^{R} 2\pi \mu C \, x \, dx = 2\pi \mu C \left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{r}^{R} = \pi \mu C (R^{2} - r^{2})$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $C$ and simplify:**

$$T = \pi \mu (R^{2} - r^{2}) \cdot \frac{P}{2\pi b \sin(\alpha/2)} = \frac{\mu P (R^{2} - r^{2})}{2 b \sin(\alpha/2)}$$

Using $R^{2} - r^{2} = (R+r)(R-r) = (R+r)b$:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{\mu P (R + r)}{2 \sin(\alpha/2)}}$$

Or in the equivalent compact form:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{\mu P \, C}{2 \sin(\alpha/2)}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection between the two theories:** The uniform-wear torque is *lower* than the uniform-pressure torque for the same $P$ and dimensions — a result of the wear process redistributing pressure from the high-velocity rim toward the lower-velocity hub. KTU design problems almost always ask for the **uniform-wear** torque when the design is for "long life / worn condition."

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Two Theories

| Quantity | Uniform Pressure (U.P.) | Uniform Wear (U.W.) |
|----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Pressure distribution | $p$ = constant | $p = C / r$ |
| Pressure is maximum at | everywhere equal | inner radius $r$ (hub) |
| Pressure is minimum at | everywhere equal | outer radius $R$ (rim) |
| Axial thrust | $P = \pi p (R^{2} - r^{2}) \sin(\alpha/2)$ | $P = 2\pi C (R - r) \sin(\alpha/2)$ |
| Torque (compact) | $T = \tfrac{2}{3}\mu P \cdot \dfrac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{R^{2} - r^{2}} \cdot \csc(\alpha/2)$ | $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu P (R + r) \csc(\alpha/2)$ |
| Torque ratio $T / (P \csc(\alpha/2))$ | $\tfrac{2}{3}\cdot\dfrac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{R^{2} - r^{2}}$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}(R + r)$ |
| Practical applicability | New, rigid lining | Worn steady-state (preferred) |
| KTU 2024 weightage | High (Part B) | Very High (Part B) |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Cone Clutch vs Single-Plate Disc Clutch — Torque Ratio

Consider both clutches having the same outer radius $R$, inner radius $r$, axial thrust $P$, and coefficient of friction $\mu$.

**Single-disc clutch (uniform pressure) torque:**

$$T_{\text{disc}} = \frac{2}{3}\,\mu P \cdot \frac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{R^{2} - r^{2}}$$

**Cone clutch (uniform pressure) torque:**

$$T_{\text{cone}} = \frac{2}{3}\,\mu P \cdot \frac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{R^{2} - r^{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$$

**Ratio:**

$$\boxed{\frac{T_{\text{cone}}}{T_{\text{disc}}} = \frac{1}{\sin(\alpha/2)} = \csc(\alpha/2)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

This is the *theoretical torque advantage* of a cone clutch over a flat disc clutch, and is the single most important conceptual question in KTU board examinations.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cone Clutch Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driving Shaft] --> B[Male Cone Member]
    B --> C{Conical Contact Face Friction Lining}
    C --> D[Female Cone Member Recess]
    D --> E[Driven Shaft]
    F[Axial Engagement Lever] -->|Force P| B
    G[Return Spring] -->|Releases on Disengagement| B
    H[Shifting Spline Key] -.->|Permits Axial Translation| B

    subgraph FORCE_ANALYSIS
        I[Axial Force P along shaft axis]
        J[Normal Force N perpendicular to cone face]
        K[Friction Force mu times N tangential]
    end

    I --> J
    J --> K
    K --> L[Torque T transmitted to driven shaft]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force Resolution on Cone Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Axial Engagement Force P] -->|Presses cone into socket| B[Contact Interface]
    B -->|Resolved perpendicular| C[Normal Force N equals P over sin alpha over 2]
    B -->|Resolved tangential| D[Friction Force F equals mu times N]
    D -->|At mean radius Rm| E[Transmitted Torque T equals mu P Rm over sin alpha over 2]

    subgraph ENERGY_FLOW
        F[Input mechanical power from driver] --> G[Conversion via friction at cone face] --> H[Output mechanical power to driven shaft]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Design Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Problem Statement] --> B{Operating Condition}
    B -->|New Lining| C[Uniform Pressure Theory p constant]
    B -->|Worn Steady State| D[Uniform Wear Theory p times r constant]
    C --> E[Compute axial thrust P equals pi p R squared minus r squared sin alpha over 2]
    D --> F[Compute axial thrust P equals 2 pi C b sin alpha over 2]
    E --> G[Compute torque T equals two thirds mu P R cubed minus r cubed over R squared minus r squared csc alpha over 2]
    F --> H[Compute torque T equals mu P times R plus r over 2 times csc alpha over 2]
    G --> I[Check self-locking mu less than tan alpha over 2]
    H --> I
    I -->|Satisfied| J[Design Acceptable]
    I -->|Violated| K[Increase alpha or Reduce mu]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description (Text Form)

For a standard cone clutch engaged by axial force $P$:

- The **male cone** has its tip pointing in the direction of engagement; the **female cone** is the matching socket.
- The friction lining is bonded on the slant surface of the male cone.
- When force $P$ is applied along the axis (by a lever or spring), the cone face is pressed into the socket.
- The **normal force** $N = P/\sin(\alpha/2)$ acts perpendicular to the slant.
- The **friction force** $\mu N$ acts tangentially at the contact, opposing the relative slip.
- The **transmitted torque** equals $\mu N$ multiplied by the mean radius $R_m = (R + r)/2$.

## 4.5 Key Geometric Relations Visualised

| Geometry | Value | Notes |
|----------|-------|-------|
| Slant face width | $b = R - r$ | Width along the slant, not the axial direction |
| Mean radius | $R_m = (R + r)/2$ | Used for first-order torque estimates |
| Included cone angle | $\alpha$ | Full angle at the cone apex |
| Semi-cone angle | $\alpha/2$ | Angle between the slant and the axis |
| Axial face projection | $b \sin(\alpha/2)$ | Width of the cone as projected on a plane perpendicular to the axis |
| Radial face projection | $b \cos(\alpha/2)$ | Component along the radius direction |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Part A Format:** 2-mark conceptual / 3-mark short derivation. Answers should be **crisp, formula-driven, and reference the governing equation** for full marks.

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**State and explain the term "self-energising" with reference to a cone clutch. Why is the semi-cone angle restricted to 12°–15° in practice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A cone clutch is said to be **self-energising** when the friction force component acting on the driving member creates an additional normal force at the contact, helping to transmit the torque without requiring an external increase in the axial engagement force $P$.

Mathematically, the friction force $\mu N$ has a component along the axis of magnitude $\mu N \cos(\alpha/2)$. Substituting $N = P/\sin(\alpha/2)$, this axial component becomes $\mu P \cot(\alpha/2)$. When this is *added* to the applied axial force, the effective normal pressure rises automatically.

**Why 12°–15° full cone angle:** Below this range, the clutch becomes *self-locking* because $\mu \geq \tan(\alpha/2)$ — it will not disengage cleanly. Above this range, the mechanical advantage $1/\sin(\alpha/2)$ falls sharply and the torque advantage over a disc clutch is lost. Hence a practical range of $\alpha/2 = 6°\text{ to }7.5°$ is universally adopted.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Derive an expression for the axial thrust required to engage a cone clutch, assuming uniform pressure distribution over the contact surface.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Consider an elemental ring of radius $x$ and slant width $dx$ on the conical face. Its elemental normal force is:

$$dN = p \cdot 2\pi x \, dx$$

The component of this normal force along the shaft axis is:

$$dP = dN \sin(\alpha/2) = 2\pi p \sin(\alpha/2) \, x \, dx$$

Integrating from $r$ to $R$ under uniform pressure $p$ = constant:

$$P = 2\pi p \sin(\alpha/2) \int_{r}^{R} x \, dx = 2\pi p \sin(\alpha/2) \left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{r}^{R}$$

$$\boxed{P = \pi p (R^{2} - r^{2}) \sin(\alpha/2) = 2\pi p R_m b \sin(\alpha/2)}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Part B Format:** 14-mark analytical / design problem, usually with two sub-parts (7 + 7 marks) and **internal choice** (the student attempts either Q-A or Q-B in full). Each sub-part must show all derivations and numerical substitutions for full credit.

---

### Part B — Option A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Model Paper Module 2]**

**Q. A (a)** A cone clutch is required to transmit **15 kW at 1440 rpm**. The cone has a semi-cone angle of **7.5°**, mean radius of **150 mm**, and a face width of **65 mm**. The coefficient of friction is **0.2** and the allowable normal pressure is **0.35 N/mm²**. Determine:
   (i) The axial force required for engagement. **(4 marks)**
   (ii) The torque capacity of the clutch under uniform pressure. **(3 marks)**

**Q. A (b)** For the same clutch as in (a), determine the torque capacity and axial thrust under the **uniform wear theory**, and compare it with the uniform pressure result. State which theory is more appropriate for long-life design. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q. A (a)

**Given:**
- $P_{\text{power}} = 15{,}000$ W
- $N = 1440$ rpm $\Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi N/60 = 2\pi(1440)/60 = 150.80$ rad/s
- $\alpha/2 = 7.5° \Rightarrow \sin(\alpha/2) = 0.1305$
- Mean radius $R_m = 150$ mm $= 0.150$ m
- Face width $b = R - r = 65$ mm
- $\mu = 0.2$
- $p_{\max} = 0.35$ N/mm² $= 0.35 \times 10^{6}$ N/m²

**Step 1 — Required torque from power:**

$$T = \frac{P_{\text{power}}}{\omega} = \frac{15{,}000}{150.80} = 99.47 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for R and r individually:**

We are given the mean radius:

$$R_m = \frac{R + r}{2} = 150 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R + r = 300 \text{ mm}$$

Face width:

$$R - r = 65 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R = 182.5 \text{ mm}, \quad r = 117.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Axial force (uniform pressure):**

$$P = \pi p (R^{2} - r^{2}) \sin(\alpha/2)$$

$$R^{2} - r^{2} = 182.5^{2} - 117.5^{2} = 33{,}306.25 - 13{,}806.25 = 19{,}500 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$P = \pi \times 0.35 \times 19{,}500 \times 0.1305$$

$$P = 3.1416 \times 0.35 \times 19{,}500 \times 0.1305 = 2798.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Torque under uniform pressure:**

$$T = \frac{2}{3}\,\mu P \cdot \frac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{R^{2} - r^{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$$

$$R^{3} - r^{3} = 182.5^{3} - 117.5^{3} = 6{,}078{,}266 - 1{,}622{,}234 = 4{,}456{,}032 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

$$\frac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{R^{2} - r^{2}} = \frac{4{,}456{,}032}{19{,}500} = 228.51 \text{ mm}$$

$$T = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.2 \times 2798.4 \times \frac{228.51}{1} \times \frac{1}{0.1305}$$

$$T = 0.1333 \times 2798.4 \times 228.51 \times 7.66$$

$$T = 0.1333 \times 2798.4 \times 1750.4 = 653{,}200 \text{ N·mm} = 653.2 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{P = 2798.4 \text{ N} \quad ; \quad T_{\text{UP}} = 653.2 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

### Model Solution to Q. A (b)

**Uniform Wear Theory — Step 1 — Axial thrust:**

For U.W., $p \times r = C$ and at the inner radius $r$, the pressure is maximum:

$$C = p_{\max} \cdot r = 0.35 \times 117.5 = 41.125 \text{ N/mm}$$

The axial thrust is:

$$P = 2\pi C b \sin(\alpha/2) = 2\pi \times 41.125 \times 65 \times 0.1305$$

$$P = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 41.125 \times 65 \times 0.1305 = 2191.6 \text{ N}$$

**Uniform Wear — Step 2 — Torque:**

$$T_{\text{UW}} = \frac{\mu P (R + r)}{2 \sin(\alpha/2)} = \frac{0.2 \times 2191.6 \times 300}{2 \times 0.1305}$$

$$T_{\text{UW}} = \frac{0.2 \times 2191.6 \times 300}{0.2610} = \frac{131{,}496}{0.2610} = 503{,}816 \text{ N·mm} = 503.8 \text{ N·m}$$

**Comparison:**

| Parameter | Uniform Pressure | Uniform Wear |
|-----------|------------------|---------------|
| Axial thrust $P$ | 2798.4 N | 2191.6 N |
| Torque capacity $T$ | 653.2 N·m | 503.8 N·m |
| Reduction in T | — | 22.9% lower than U.P. |

**Conclusion:** The uniform wear theory gives a **lower torque capacity** and is more appropriate for long-life design because it accounts for the actual steady-state pressure distribution after the lining has worn in. The uniform pressure result is an *upper bound* valid only for the new, unworn condition. **KTU 2024 preferred answer: Uniform Wear theory.**

---

### Part B — Option B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 2 Alternate]**

**Q. B (a)** Derive the expression for torque transmitted by a multi-plate cone clutch under the **uniform wear theory**, and show that the result reduces to the standard single-cone expression when the axial face projection is taken as $b\sin(\alpha/2)$. **(7 marks)**

**Q. B (b)** A cone clutch has outer radius $R = 200$ mm, inner radius $r = 100$ mm, semi-cone angle $\alpha/2 = 10°$, and coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.25$. The axial engagement force is $P = 4$ kN. Compute:
   (i) Torque transmitted under **uniform wear** assumption. **(4 marks)**
   (ii) Maximum and minimum pressure on the contact face. **(3 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q. B (a)

**Step 1 — Elemental ring analysis (uniform wear):**

The wear condition gives $p \cdot r = C$. On an elemental ring of radius $x$:

$$dN = p \cdot dA = \frac{C}{x} \cdot 2\pi x \, dx = 2\pi C \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Total normal force on the entire face:**

$$N = \int_{r}^{R} 2\pi C \, dx = 2\pi C (R - r) = 2\pi C b$$

**Step 3 — Torque from elemental ring:**

The elemental friction force is $\mu \, dN = 2\pi \mu C \, dx$. The elemental torque arm is $x$:

$$dT = x \cdot 2\pi \mu C \, dx = 2\pi \mu C x \, dx$$

**Step 4 — Integrate:**

$$T = \int_{r}^{R} 2\pi \mu C x \, dx = 2\pi \mu C \left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{r}^{R} = \pi \mu C (R^{2} - r^{2})$$

**Step 5 — Eliminate $C$ using the axial force relation:**

The axial thrust on an elemental ring is:

$$dP = dN \sin(\alpha/2) = 2\pi C \sin(\alpha/2) \, dx$$

Integrating:

$$P = 2\pi C (R - r) \sin(\alpha/2) = 2\pi C b \sin(\alpha/2)$$

Solving for $C$:

$$C = \frac{P}{2\pi b \sin(\alpha/2)}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into torque expression:**

$$T = \pi \mu (R^{2} - r^{2}) \cdot \frac{P}{2\pi b \sin(\alpha/2)} = \frac{\mu P (R^{2} - r^{2})}{2 b \sin(\alpha/2)}$$

Since $R^{2} - r^{2} = (R+r)(R-r) = (R+r)b$:

$$\boxed{T_{\text{UW}} = \frac{\mu P (R + r)}{2 \sin(\alpha/2)} = \frac{\mu P (R + r)}{2}\csc(\alpha/2)}$$

**Multi-plate reduction:** If there are $n$ identical cones operating simultaneously and sharing the total axial force $P$, then each cone carries $P/n$. The total torque scales as $n$:

$$T_{\text{total}} = n \cdot \frac{\mu (P/n)(R + r)}{2 \sin(\alpha/2)} = \frac{\mu P (R + r)}{2 \sin(\alpha/2)} \quad \text{(independent of } n \text{ for shared } P\text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** If axial force is shared across $n$ cones, total torque capacity **does not change** compared to a single cone. To get $n$-fold torque increase, *each* cone must be independently pressed with full force $P$ — i.e., $n$ separate springs or levers. This is a frequently tested KTU concept.

---

### Model Solution to Q. B (b)

**Given:** $R = 200$ mm, $r = 100$ mm, $\alpha/2 = 10°$, $\mu = 0.25$, $P = 4000$ N.

**Step 1 — Compute semi-cone sine:**

$$\sin(10°) = 0.1736$$

**Step 2 — Torque under uniform wear:**

$$T = \frac{\mu P (R + r)}{2 \sin(\alpha/2)} = \frac{0.25 \times 4000 \times (200 + 100)}{2 \times 0.1736}$$

$$T = \frac{0.25 \times 4000 \times 300}{0.3472} = \frac{300{,}000}{0.3472} = 864{,}055 \text{ N·mm} = 864.1 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Find wear constant $C$ from the axial force relation:**

$$P = 2\pi C b \sin(\alpha/2) \Rightarrow C = \frac{P}{2\pi b \sin(\alpha/2)} = \frac{4000}{2\pi \times 100 \times 0.1736}$$

$$C = \frac{4000}{109.07} = 36.68 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum pressure (at inner radius $r$):**

$$p_{\max} = \frac{C}{r} = \frac{36.68}{100} = 0.3668 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Minimum pressure (at outer radius $R$):**

$$p_{\min} = \frac{C}{R} = \frac{36.68}{200} = 0.1834 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Verification:** $p_{\max} \cdot r = 0.3668 \times 100 = 36.68$ and $p_{\min} \cdot R = 0.1834 \times 200 = 36.68$ — confirms the uniform wear condition. ✓

$$\boxed{T_{\text{UW}} = 864.1 \text{ N·m} \quad ; \quad p_{\max} = 0.367 \text{ MPa} \quad ; \quad p_{\min} = 0.183 \text{ MPa}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Cone Clutch Problems (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Angle Convention Error (–2 marks):** Using $\sin(\alpha)$ instead of $\sin(\alpha/2)$ in torque/force equations. The clutch uses the *semi-cone angle*. Always re-read the problem.
> 2. **Mixing Theories (–3 marks):** Using uniform-pressure *axial force equation* with uniform-wear *torque equation*. Both must come from the same theory in a single design.
> 3. **Units Inconsistency (–1 mark):** Expressing pressure in MPa and length in mm without converting to a common base. **Always convert to N and m, or N and mm, consistently.**
> 4. **Ignoring the self-locking criterion (–2 marks):** Failing to verify $\mu < \tan(\alpha/2)$ in design problems where the angle is given.
> 5. **Multi-plate sign error (–2 marks):** Forgetting that torque scales with $n$ only if *each* cone is independently pressed with the same force, not when a single $P$ is shared.
> 6. **Mean-radius short-cut (–1 mark):** Using $R_m$ as the torque arm in the rigorous uniform-pressure formula. The correct mean term is $\frac{2(R^{3} - r^{3})}{3(R^{2} - r^{2})}$ for U.P., and $(R+r)/2$ for U.W. — **these are not the same!**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Cone Clutch (PEMET743 — Module 2)**

- ☐ **Cone Clutch Definition:** Friction clutch with conical contact surface; torque amplified by $1/\sin(\alpha/2)$.
- ☐ **Semi-cone angle range:** 6°–7.5° (full angle 12°–15°); ensures non-self-locking and meaningful torque gain.
- ☐ **Self-locking criterion:** $\mu < \tan(\alpha/2)$ (must hold for disengagement).
- ☐ **Normal force relation:** $N = P/\sin(\alpha/2)$ — axial-to-normal force amplifier.
- ☐ **Friction force relation:** $F_r = \mu N = \mu P/\sin(\alpha/2)$.
- ☐ **Uniform Pressure Theory (U.P.):**
   - $P = \pi p (R^{2} - r^{2}) \sin(\alpha/2)$
   - $T_{\text{UP}} = \tfrac{2}{3} \mu P \cdot \dfrac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{R^{2} - r^{2}} \cdot \csc(\alpha/2)$
- ☐ **Uniform Wear Theory (U.W.):**
   - $p \cdot r = C$ (constant)
   - $P = 2\pi C b \sin(\alpha/2)$
   - $T_{\text{UW}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \mu P (R + r) \csc(\alpha/2)$
- ☐ **Torque amplification vs disc clutch:** $T_{\text{cone}} / T_{\text{disc}} = \csc(\alpha/2)$.
- ☐ **Face width:** $b = R - r$ measured along the **slant**, not the axial direction.
- ☐ **Mean radius:** $R_m = (R + r)/2$.
- ☐ **Pressure gradient (U.W.):** Higher at inner radius $r$, lower at outer radius $R$.
- ☐ **U.W. theory is preferred for long-life / steady-state design**; U.P. is for new, rigid linings.
- ☐ **Multi-plate independence:** Independent engagement forces are required to gain $n$-fold torque increase.
- ☐ **Applications:** Motorcycles, scooters, lathe spindles, small industrial drives.
- ☐ **Always specify the angle convention** ($\alpha$ vs $\alpha/2$) before writing equations.
- ☐ **Always verify self-locking** in design synthesis problems — it is a 2-mark check that examiners love to test.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centrifugal Clutch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Centrifugal Clutch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centrifugal Clutch:** A *friction clutch* whose engagement is achieved automatically by the **centrifugal force** acting on weighted shoes (or blocks) mounted on the driving member, without any external actuation by the operator. Engagement occurs only when the driving shaft rotates above a predetermined **engagement speed** ($N_e$), at which the centrifugal force overcomes the opposing spring force.

It is a *self-engaging*, *automatic*, and *speed-sensitive* power transmission device. It belongs to the family of **friction clutches** (positive contact type), but is unique because the normal force is **generated internally by the rotating mass itself**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swing-Ball on a String" Intuition

Imagine you are swinging a ball tied to a string above your head in a horizontal circle.

- When you **spin slowly**, the string remains slack — the ball barely pulls outward.
- As you **spin faster**, the ball tries to fly outward with increasing force, tightening the string.
- If we replaced the string with a **spring** holding the ball inward, the ball would push against an outer wall (the **drum**) once the spin is fast enough.

A **centrifugal clutch works exactly like this**:
- The **shoes** are the "balls".
- The **spring** is the inward restraining force.
- The **drum** is the outer wall.
- The **spin speed** decides whether the shoes touch the drum and transmit torque.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples:** Scooters (e.g., TVS Scooty), go-karts, chain saws, concrete mixers, mowers, and small pumps all use centrifugal clutches because they protect the engine from stalling when starting under load.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Material Metrics

- **Coefficient of friction ($\mu$)** for typical cast iron on cast iron: **$\mu = 0.20$ to $0.30$**
- **Allowable pressure** for cast iron shoes on cast iron drum: **$p = 0.25$ to $0.40 \text{ MPa}$**
- **Engagement ratio** (ratio of engagement to operating speed): typically **$N_e / N = 0.70$ to $0.85$**

## 1.4 Classification of Centrifugal Clutches

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Expanding Shoe Clutch** | Shoes pivot outward and press against the inner drum surface | Scooters, mopeds |
| **Contracting Drum Clutch** | Drum contracts onto fixed shoes (rare) | Special machinery |
| **Multi-shoe Clutch** | 2 to 6 shoes equally spaced on the spider | Industrial drives |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus primarily focuses on the **expanding type multi-shoe centrifugal clutch**, which is what we will analyse in the rest of these notes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details

A centrifugal clutch consists of the following components:

1. **Driving spider (hub):** Mounted on the driving shaft (typically engine crankshaft).
2. **Shoes (weights/blocks):** Usually 3 or 4 in number, attached to the spider via **pivots** at radius $a$.
3. **Springs:** Tension/compression springs hold shoes inward at rest.
4. **Drum (driven member):** Cylindrical drum with inner radius $r_1$, mounted on the driven shaft via a key.
5. **Friction lining:** Optional, riveted to the shoes to increase $\mu$ and wear life.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Stepwise Logic

1. **At rest ($N = 0$):** Springs hold shoes fully retracted; there is a small air gap $\delta$ between the shoes and the drum.
2. **Below engagement speed ($N < N_e$):** Centrifugal force is insufficient; shoes remain retracted; **no torque transmitted**.
3. **At engagement speed ($N = N_e$):** Centrifugal force just equals the spring force + any residual friction; shoes just touch the drum.
4. **Above engagement speed ($N > N_e$):** Centrifugal force exceeds spring force; shoes press against the drum with a net normal force; **friction transmits torque** from spider to drum.
5. **Above operating speed ($N$ = rated):** Full rated torque is transmitted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use springs?** To prevent engagement at low speeds (e.g., idling), to ensure smooth start-up, and to disengage the clutch when the engine is throttled down.

## 2.3 Free-Body Analysis of a Single Shoe

Consider one shoe pivoted at point $A$ at radius $a$ from the shaft axis. The contact point $B$ is at radius $r_1$ (inner drum radius). The centre of gravity $G$ of the shoe is at distance $c$ from the pivot, measured radially outward.

Forces acting on the shoe:
- **Centrifugal force** $W = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_{cg}$, acting radially outward through $G$, where $r_{cg} = a + c$.
- **Spring force** $S$, acting radially inward at the spring attachment point.
- **Normal reaction** $R$ from the drum, acting radially inward at $B$.
- **Friction force** $\mu R$ at $B$, tangential to the drum.

> [!NOTE]
> For the *simple radial* shoe (where $G$ lies on the line $AB$), taking moments about the pivot $A$ (frictionless pivot):

$$R \cdot (r_1 - a) = W \cdot (a + c - a) - S \cdot (a + c - a)$$

Wait — let me reconsider the geometry. If the spring acts at $G$ as well:

$$R \cdot (r_1 - a) = (W - S) \cdot c$$

Therefore:

$$R = \frac{(W - S) \cdot c}{r_1 - a}$$

## 2.4 Torque Transmitted

The friction force at $B$ acts at radius $r_1$. Torque from one shoe:

$$T_1 = \mu \cdot R \cdot r_1 = \mu \cdot r_1 \cdot \frac{(W - S) \cdot c}{r_1 - a}$$

For $n$ shoes equally spaced:

$$T = \frac{n \cdot \mu \cdot c \cdot r_1}{r_1 - a} \cdot (W - S)$$

Substituting $W = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot (a + c)$:

$$T = \frac{n \cdot \mu \cdot c \cdot r_1}{r_1 - a} \cdot \left[m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot (a + c) - S\right]$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU exam question on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $W = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_{cg}$ | Centrifugal force on one shoe; $r_{cg} = a + c$ | N |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from rpm | rad/s |
| 3 | $T = \dfrac{P \cdot 60}{2\pi N}$ | Torque from power $P$ (W) and $N$ (rpm) | N·m |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{(W - S) \cdot c}{r_1 - a}$ | Normal reaction at drum-shoe contact | N |
| 5 | $T = \dfrac{n \cdot \mu \cdot c \cdot r_1}{r_1 - a} \cdot (W - S)$ | Torque transmitted by multi-shoe clutch | N·m |
| 6 | $T = \dfrac{n \cdot \mu \cdot c \cdot r_1}{r_1 - a} \cdot \left[m \omega^2 (a + c) - S\right]$ | Expanded form with full substitution | N·m |
| 7 | $S = m \cdot \omega_e^2 \cdot (a + c)$ | Spring force from engagement speed $N_e$ | N |
| 8 | $N_e = N \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{S}{W}}$ | Engagement speed in terms of $W$ and $S$ | rpm |
| 9 | $p = \dfrac{W - S}{A_{shoe}}$ | Bearing pressure on the shoe contact area | Pa |
| 10 | $A_{shoe} = l \cdot b$ | Contact area (length $\times$ width of shoe) | m² |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU answer sheets, examiners award full marks only when **all variables are defined** and **units are written explicitly**. Use this convention religiously.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Two-wheeler transmissions:** Replaces manual clutch — ideal for stop-and-go city traffic.
- **Engine protection:** Prevents stalling when starting a heavy load (e.g., a concrete mixer).
- **Soft start:** Enables gradual engagement, reducing shock loading on driveline components.
- **Safety:** Disengages automatically if engine stops — no power transfer in failure conditions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Torque Equation for Multi-Shoe Expanding Centrifugal Clutch

### Assumptions

1. The clutch is operating at **steady-state rated speed** $N$ (rpm).
2. The pivot at $A$ is **frictionless** (negligible pivot friction).
3. The springs and shoes are arranged **symmetrically** — all $n$ shoes share the load equally.
4. The contact pressure is **uniform** over the shoe's contact area.
5. The shoe's CG lies on the **radial line** joining the shaft axis, pivot, and contact point (radial shoe geometry).

### Step 1 — Identify the Geometry

Define the three critical radii measured from the shaft axis:

- $a$ — radius of the **pivot** $A$ from the shaft axis.
- $a + c$ — radius of the **centre of gravity** $G$ of the shoe from the shaft axis (since $c$ is the distance of $G$ from pivot $A$).
- $r_1$ — **inner radius** of the drum (also the radius of contact point $B$).

### Step 2 — Write the Centrifugal Force

When the spider rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s, the shoe experiences an outward inertial (centrifugal) force:

$$W = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_{cg}$$

where $r_{cg} = a + c$ is the distance of the CG from the rotation axis. Substituting:

$$W = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot (a + c)$$

### Step 3 — Resolve Forces and Take Moments About the Pivot $A$

The shoe experiences three radial forces:
1. $W$ acting **outward** at $G$ (lever arm $c$ from $A$).
2. $S$ acting **inward** at the spring attachment (assume same radial line as $G$, lever arm $c$).
3. $R$ acting **inward** at $B$ (lever arm $r_1 - a$ from $A$).

Taking moments about $A$ (so the pivot reaction drops out), and equating CCW to CW for equilibrium:

$$(W - S) \cdot c = R \cdot (r_1 - a)$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Normal Reaction $R$

Rearranging:

$$R = \frac{(W - S) \cdot c}{r_1 - a}$$

Substituting $W = m \omega^2 (a + c)$:

$$R = \frac{\left[m \omega^2 (a + c) - S\right] \cdot c}{r_1 - a}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The factor $\dfrac{c}{r_1 - a}$ is a **mechanical advantage**. If $c$ is large (CG far from pivot) and $r_1 - a$ is small (shoe short), the reaction $R$ is amplified — this is the **self-energizing effect**.

### Step 5 — Compute the Friction Force

The tangential friction force at the contact point $B$:

$$F_f = \mu \cdot R = \frac{\mu \cdot c \cdot \left[m \omega^2 (a + c) - S\right]}{r_1 - a}$$

### Step 6 — Compute Torque from One Shoe

This friction force acts at radius $r_1$ (drum radius):

$$T_1 = F_f \cdot r_1 = \frac{\mu \cdot c \cdot r_1 \cdot \left[m \omega^2 (a + c) - S\right]}{r_1 - a}$$

### Step 7 — Total Torque from $n$ Shoes

Since the shoes are equally spaced and identical:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{n \cdot \mu \cdot c \cdot r_1}{r_1 - a} \cdot \left[m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot (a + c) - S\right]}$$

### Step 8 — Compute the Spring Force from Engagement Speed

At engagement, the centrifugal force exactly balances the spring force (no contact yet, so $R = 0$):

$$S = m \cdot \omega_e^2 \cdot (a + c)$$

where $\omega_e = \dfrac{2\pi N_e}{60}$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Relationship Between Variables

| Parameter | If Increased | Effect on Torque $T$ | Design Trade-off |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|------------------|
| $n$ (shoes) | ↑ | $T$ increases **linearly** | More weight, more imbalance |
| $\mu$ (friction coeff.) | ↑ | $T$ increases linearly | Material selection constraint |
| $c$ (CG offset) | ↑ | $T$ increases linearly | Larger shoe, more inertia |
| $r_1$ (drum radius) | ↑ | $T$ increases linearly | Larger overall envelope |
| $a$ (pivot radius) | ↑ | $T$ increases (since $r_1 - a$ ↓) | Larger spider, more shaft space |
| $m$ (shoe mass) | ↑ | $T$ increases linearly | Higher $N_e$ needed |
| $\omega$ (speed) | ↑ | $T$ increases **quadratically** | Speed is fixed by application |
| $S$ (spring force) | ↑ | $T$ **decreases** linearly | Higher $N_e$ (delayed engagement) |

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Example — Full KTU-Style Working

> **[KTU University Exam - Style Numerical]** Design a multi-shoe centrifugal clutch to transmit **15 kW at 1440 rpm** with engagement at **$\frac{2}{3}$ of operating speed**. Use the following data:
> - Number of shoes: $n = 4$
> - $\mu = 0.25$
> - $a = 50 \text{ mm}$, $c = 30 \text{ mm}$, $r_1 = 100 \text{ mm}$
> - Assume a square shoe of side $40 \text{ mm}$ in contact area; allowable pressure $p = 0.30 \text{ MPa}$

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Operating angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi (1440)}{60} = 150.80 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Required torque:**

$$T = \frac{P \cdot 60}{2 \pi N} = \frac{15000 \times 60}{2 \pi (1440)} = 99.47 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Engagement angular velocity:**

$$N_e = \frac{2}{3} \times 1440 = 960 \text{ rpm} \implies \omega_e = \frac{2\pi (960)}{60} = 100.53 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Assume spring force and solve for shoe mass $m$:**

From the torque equation:

$$T = \frac{n \mu c r_1}{r_1 - a} \cdot \left[m \omega^2 (a + c) - S\right]$$

And $S = m \omega_e^2 (a + c)$, so:

$$T = \frac{n \mu c r_1}{r_1 - a} \cdot m (a + c) (\omega^2 - \omega_e^2)$$

Plugging in numerical values (SI units):
- $n = 4$, $\mu = 0.25$, $c = 0.030$, $r_1 = 0.100$
- $r_1 - a = 0.100 - 0.050 = 0.050$
- $a + c = 0.050 + 0.030 = 0.080$
- $\omega^2 - \omega_e^2 = 150.80^2 - 100.53^2 = 22740.6 - 10106.3 = 12634.3$

$$T = \frac{4 \times 0.25 \times 0.030 \times 0.100}{0.050} \cdot m \times 0.080 \times 12634.3$$

$$T = 0.0600 \cdot m \cdot 1010.74 = 60.645 \cdot m$$

So:

$$m = \frac{99.47}{60.645} = 1.640 \text{ kg per shoe}$$

**Step 5 — Compute centrifugal force at operating speed:**

$$W = m \omega^2 (a + c) = 1.640 \times (150.80)^2 \times 0.080 = 2983.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Compute spring force:**

$$S = m \omega_e^2 (a + c) = 1.640 \times (100.53)^2 \times 0.080 = 1325.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7 — Compute normal reaction $R$:**

$$R = \frac{(W - S) \cdot c}{r_1 - a} = \frac{(2983.5 - 1325.6) \times 0.030}{0.050} = \frac{1657.9 \times 0.030}{0.050} = 994.74 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8 — Verify bearing pressure:**

Shoe contact area: $A = 0.040 \times 0.040 = 0.0016 \text{ m}^2$

$$p_{actual} = \frac{R}{A} = \frac{994.74}{0.0016} = 0.622 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **$p_{actual} = 0.622 \text{ MPa}$ exceeds $p_{allow} = 0.30 \text{ MPa}$.** The design must be revised — either increase shoe area or use multiple pairs of shoes or select a lining material with higher allowable pressure. In a complete design, we would increase shoe contact area (e.g., $60 \text{ mm} \times 40 \text{ mm}$) or reduce $c/r_1$ ratio.

**Step 9 — Recompute with shoe contact $60 \text{ mm} \times 40 \text{ mm}$:**

$$A = 0.060 \times 0.040 = 0.0024 \text{ m}^2$$

$$p_{actual} = \frac{994.74}{0.0024} = 0.414 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(still slightly high)}$$

**Step 10 — Final iteration with $b = 70 \text{ mm}$:**

$$A = 0.070 \times 0.040 = 0.0028 \text{ m}^2$$

$$p_{actual} = \frac{994.74}{0.0028} = 0.355 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(acceptable, close to limit)}$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love it when you **show two iterations** and **state the design decision explicitly**. Always end with a clearly boxed final answer.

**Final Design Summary:**
| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Mass per shoe $m$ | **1.640 kg** |
| Centrifugal force $W$ | **2983.5 N** |
| Spring force $S$ | **1325.6 N** |
| Normal reaction $R$ | **994.74 N** |
| Bearing pressure | **0.355 MPa** (acceptable) |
| Shoe contact area | **70 mm × 40 mm** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Centrifugal Clutch

The Mermaid diagram below shows the **functional flow** of forces, motions, and energy transfer within the clutch — illustrating how the rotating mass converts kinetic energy into frictional engagement.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input Driving Shaft from Engine] --> nodeB[Mounted Spider with Pivots]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Four Shoes Mass m Each]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Spring Restraint Force S]
    nodeD -.Opposes.-> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE[Inner Drum Surface Radius r1]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Friction Contact Mu times R]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Driven Drum Output Shaft]
    nodeH[Angular Velocity Omega] --> nodeC
    nodeI[Centrifugal Force W = m omega squared a+c] --> nodeC
    nodeJ[Engagement Speed Ne] -.Triggers.-> nodeF
    nodeK[Normal Reaction R] --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeK
```

## 4.2 Radial Geometry Layout of a Single Shoe (Block Schematic)

The following block diagram maps the **geometric and force relationships** that drive the torque equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    centerA[Shaft Axis Center O] --> radA[Radius a equals Pivot]
    radA --> pivotA[Pivot Point A on Spider]
    centerA --> radCG[Radius a plus c equals CG Location]
    radCG --> cgA[Centre of Gravity G of Shoe]
    centerA --> radR1[Radius r1 equals Drum Inner]
    radR1 --> drumA[Contact Point B on Drum]
    pivotA --> leverArm1[Perpendicular Arm c]
    cgA --> leverArm1
    pivotA --> leverArm2[Perpendicular Arm r1 minus a]
    drumA --> leverArm2
    cgA --> fcForce[Centrifugal Force W outward]
    drumA --> nForce[Normal Reaction R inward]
    cgA --> sForce[Spring Force S inward]
```

## 4.3 Functional State Diagram — Engagement Phases

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Rest: Engine OFF
    Rest --> Idle: N less than Ne
    Idle --> Engagement: N equals Ne
    Engagement --> FullDrive: N greater than Nrated
    FullDrive --> PartialDrive: N between Ne and Nrated
    PartialDrive --> Disengaged: N drops below Ne
    Disengaged --> Idle
    Idle --> Rest: Engine OFF
```

## 4.4 Force & Moment Interaction Matrix (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[Mass m of Shoe] --> blockB[Centrifugal W = m omega squared a+c]
    blockB --> blockC{Moment about Pivot A}
    blockC --> blockD[Net Outward Moment = W minus S times c]
    blockD --> blockE[Reaction R at Drum]
    blockE --> blockF[Friction Force = mu times R]
    blockF --> blockG[Torque T1 per Shoe]
    blockG --> blockH[Total Torque T = n times T1]
    blockH --> blockI[Power Transmitted P = 2 pi N T / 60]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each box represents a physical component or calculated quantity. Arrows show how force flows from the rotating mass → through the pivot → into the contact → finally emerging as transmitted torque and power. Trace the path from $W$ to $T$ to understand the full design logic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** 
*State the principle of operation of a centrifugal clutch. Why are springs used in its construction?*

**Model Answer:**

> A centrifugal clutch operates on the principle of **centrifugal force** acting on weighted shoes mounted on the driving member. When the driving shaft rotates above a predetermined **engagement speed** $N_e$, the centrifugal force developed ($W = m\omega^2 r_{cg}$) overcomes the opposing spring force, pushing the shoes outward against the inner surface of the driven drum. The resulting **friction force** transmits torque from the driving to the driven member.
>
> **Springs are used** to (i) keep the shoes retracted at rest and at low speeds (idling), (ii) prevent premature engagement and stalling of the engine, and (iii) provide smooth, progressive engagement as speed rises above $N_e$. The springs also ensure **automatic disengagement** when the engine slows below $N_e$.

*(Marks: Definition = 1, Spring function = 1)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
*List any two applications and two advantages of centrifugal clutches.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Applications:**
> 1. Two-wheelers (scooters, mopeds, small motorcycles) for automatic clutch operation.
> 2. Concrete mixers, chain saws, lawn mowers, and go-karts for overload protection.
>
> **Advantages:**
> 1. **Self-engaging and self-disengaging** — no operator action needed; reduces driver fatigue.
> 2. **Smooth starting** — gradual torque transmission prevents shock loading on driveline.
> 3. **Engine protection** — disengages during stalling, preventing damage.
> 4. **Simple construction** with fewer parts than manual clutches; lower maintenance.

*(Marks: 2 applications = 1, 2 advantages = 1)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Below are **TWO full alternative choices** — answer either.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, derive an expression for the **torque transmitted by a multi-shoe expanding centrifugal clutch** in terms of shoe mass, geometry, friction coefficient, and spring force. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal clutch has **4 shoes**, each of mass **1.2 kg**, with pivot at **45 mm** from the shaft axis, CG at **30 mm from the pivot**, and inner drum radius **100 mm**. The coefficient of friction is **0.20**, and the spring force per shoe is **80 N**. Determine:
- (i) The torque transmitted at **1200 rpm**.
- (ii) The **engagement speed** in rpm. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

##### Part (a) — Derivation

**Sketch:**

```
                  Drum (driven)
              ┌─────────────────┐
              │   ┌───────┐     │
              │   │       │     │
        ◄─────│   │  B •  │     │─────►  (r₁)
              │   │   ↑   │     │
              │   │   R   │     │
              │   │   ↓   │     │
              │   │  • G  │     │  ◄── CG of shoe
              │   │   ↑   │     │
              │   │   W   │     │  ◄── Centrifugal force
              │   │   ↓   │     │
              │   │  • A  │     │  ◄── Pivot
              │   │       │     │
              │   └───────┘     │
              └─────────────────┘
                     │
                  Shaft (driving)
```

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state operation; no acceleration of shoes.
2. Frictionless pivot.
3. Uniform pressure distribution on shoe-drum contact.
4. All shoes are identical and equally spaced (radial symmetry).
5. The CG lies on the line $AB$.

**Derivation** (covered in full in SECTION 3.1 above):

- Centrifugal force: $W = m\omega^2 (a + c)$.
- Moment equilibrium about pivot $A$:
$$(W - S) \cdot c = R \cdot (r_1 - a)$$
$$R = \frac{(W - S) \cdot c}{r_1 - a}$$
- Friction force: $\mu R$ acting at radius $r_1$.
- Torque per shoe: $T_1 = \mu R r_1$.
- Total torque:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{n \mu c r_1}{r_1 - a} \cdot \left[m \omega^2 (a + c) - S\right]}$$

##### Part (b) — Numerical

**Given:**
- $n = 4$, $m = 1.2 \text{ kg}$, $a = 0.045 \text{ m}$, $c = 0.030 \text{ m}$
- $r_1 = 0.100 \text{ m}$, $\mu = 0.20$, $S = 80 \text{ N}$
- Operating speed $N = 1200 \text{ rpm}$

**(i) Torque at 1200 rpm:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$W = m \omega^2 (a + c) = 1.2 \times (125.66)^2 \times (0.045 + 0.030)$$

$$W = 1.2 \times 15791.4 \times 0.075 = 1421.2 \text{ N}$$

Now substitute into the torque equation:

$$T = \frac{4 \times 0.20 \times 0.030 \times 0.100}{0.100 - 0.045} \cdot (1421.2 - 80)$$

$$T = \frac{0.00240}{0.055} \cdot 1341.2 = 0.04364 \times 1341.2 = 58.52 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 58.52 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(ii) Engagement speed:**

At engagement, $W = S$, i.e., $m \omega_e^2 (a + c) = S$:

$$\omega_e^2 = \frac{S}{m (a + c)} = \frac{80}{1.2 \times 0.075} = \frac{80}{0.090} = 888.89$$

$$\omega_e = 29.81 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N_e = \frac{\omega_e \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{29.81 \times 60}{2\pi} = \boxed{284.7 \text{ rpm}}$$

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Past Pattern]**

**(a)** Differentiate between a **centrifugal clutch** and a **single-plate friction clutch** based on principle, actuation, applications, and engagement behaviour. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal clutch is required to transmit **22 kW at 1500 rpm**. Design the clutch using the following data:
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.25$
- Number of shoes $n = 4$
- Centrifugal force per shoe at operating speed $W = 2000 \text{ N}$
- Spring force per shoe $S = 600 \text{ N}$
- Pivot radius $a = 50 \text{ mm}$, CG distance from pivot $c = 25 \text{ mm}$, drum radius $r_1 = 120 \text{ mm}$
- Allowable bearing pressure $p = 0.40 \text{ MPa}$

Determine: (i) Torque transmitted, (ii) Engagement speed, (iii) Required shoe contact area and check bearing pressure. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

##### Part (a) — Comparison Table

| Aspect | Centrifugal Clutch | Single-Plate Friction Clutch |
|--------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **Principle** | Centrifugal force on weighted shoes | Friction between driven plate and pressure plate |
| **Actuation** | **Automatic** (speed-dependent) | **Manual** (lever / hydraulic / pneumatic) |
| **Normal force source** | Centrifugal force $m\omega^2 r$ | Spring force / mechanical pressure |
| **Engagement** | Above a set engagement speed $N_e$ | At operator's will, regardless of speed |
| **Applications** | Two-wheelers, mixers, go-karts | Cars, trucks, industrial machinery |
| **Disengagement** | Automatic when $N < N_e$ | Requires operator action |
| **Construction** | Shoes + springs + drum | Friction disc + pressure plate + release levers |

*(Marks: 7 differences / 1 mark each — present at least 7 distinguishing points for full credit.)*

##### Part (b) — Numerical

**Given:** $P = 22 \text{ kW}$, $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$, $W = 2000 \text{ N}$, $S = 600 \text{ N}$, $a = 0.050 \text{ m}$, $c = 0.025 \text{ m}$, $r_1 = 0.120 \text{ m}$, $\mu = 0.25$, $n = 4$, $p_{allow} = 0.40 \text{ MPa}$.

**(i) Torque transmitted:**

$$T = \frac{n \mu c r_1}{r_1 - a} \cdot (W - S)$$

$$T = \frac{4 \times 0.25 \times 0.025 \times 0.120}{0.120 - 0.050} \times (2000 - 600)$$

$$T = \frac{0.00300}{0.070} \times 1400 = 0.04286 \times 1400 = 60.0 \text{ N·m}$$

**Verification against required torque:**

$$T_{required} = \frac{22000 \times 60}{2\pi \times 1500} = 140.1 \text{ N·m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **$T_{calculated} = 60.0 \text{ N·m} < T_{required} = 140.1 \text{ N·m}$** — design is **under-designed**. Either increase shoe mass, increase $\mu$, increase $n$, or adjust geometry. In a real answer, you would **state this clearly** and revise.

**Revised design** — let us **double the number of shoes** to $n = 8$:

$$T = \frac{8 \times 0.25 \times 0.025 \times 0.120}{0.070} \times 1400 = 120.0 \text{ N·m}$$

Still under — let us **double the mass too**, giving $W = 4000 \text{ N}$:

$$T = \frac{8 \times 0.25 \times 0.025 \times 0.120}{0.070} \times (4000 - 600) = 0.08571 \times 3400 = 291.4 \text{ N·m}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam tip:** If a design parameter combination fails, KTU examiners expect you to **identify the issue, propose a fix, and re-evaluate**. This demonstrates design thinking and earns full marks.

**(ii) Engagement speed:**

At engagement, $W_{engagement} = S$, i.e., $m \omega_e^2 (a + c) = S$.

Operating $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$.

$W_{op} = m \omega^2 (a + c) = 2000 \text{ N}$ ⇒ $m(a+c) = \dfrac{2000}{(157.08)^2} = 0.0811$.

Then:

$$\omega_e^2 = \frac{S}{m(a+c)} = \frac{600}{0.0811} = 7399.5$$

$$\omega_e = 86.02 \text{ rad/s} \implies N_e = \frac{86.02 \times 60}{2\pi} = \boxed{821.4 \text{ rpm}}$$

**(iii) Shoe contact area:**

Normal reaction $R$:

$$R = \frac{(W - S) \cdot c}{r_1 - a} = \frac{1400 \times 0.025}{0.070} = 500 \text{ N}$$

Required area: $A = \dfrac{R}{p_{allow}} = \dfrac{500}{0.40 \times 10^6} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 1250 \text{ mm}^2$.

Choose a square shoe: $b \approx \sqrt{1250} \approx 35.4 \text{ mm}$. Round to **$b = 40 \text{ mm}$**.

Adopted contact area: $A = 40 \times 40 = 1600 \text{ mm}^2$.

$$p_{actual} = \frac{500}{1600 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.3125 \text{ MPa} < 0.40 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Design Summary:**
| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Torque transmitted | 60.0 N·m (initial) → revise to 120 N·m with $n=8$ |
| Engagement speed | **821.4 rpm** |
| Shoe contact | **40 mm × 40 mm** |
| Actual pressure | **0.3125 MPa** ✓ |

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in Centrifugal Clutch problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the spring force** in the torque equation — many students write $T = n\mu R r_1$ without subtracting $S$. KTU key answers always include the $(W - S)$ term. *Loss: 2 to 3 marks.*
>
> 2. **Mixing up units** — using $r$ in mm without converting to m in the SI version of the formula. Always convert all lengths to **metres** before plugging into equations.
>
> 3. **Incorrect engagement speed calculation** — confusing "engagement" (where $W = S$) with "operating" (where $W > S$). Engagement is where shoes **just touch** the drum; no torque is yet transmitted.
>
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** in derivation questions — KTU rubrics award **1 mark specifically for listing assumptions**. Skipping this is a guaranteed mark loss.
>
> 5. **Skipping bearing pressure check** — KTU 2024 scheme stresses **design validation**. Always verify $p_{actual} < p_{allowable}$ and explicitly state it.
>
> 6. **Not defining variables** — examiners deduct marks when symbols like $a$, $c$, $r_1$ are used without being defined first.
>
> 7. **Writing the formula in mixed units** — e.g., $r_1$ in mm but $\omega$ in rad/s. **Always pick ONE unit system and stick to it.**

---

## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Centrifugal Clutch**

- ☐ Centrifugal clutch is a **speed-dependent automatic friction clutch**.
- ☐ **Engagement occurs** when $W \geq S$, i.e., when $m\omega^2(a+c) \geq S$.
- ☐ The fundamental torque equation is:

$$T = \frac{n \cdot \mu \cdot c \cdot r_1}{r_1 - a} \cdot \left[m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot (a + c) - S\right]$$

- ☐ The factor $\dfrac{c}{r_1 - a}$ is the **mechanical advantage** — keep it moderate (typically 0.5 to 1.0) for stable designs.
- ☐ **Spring force** = centrifugal force at engagement speed: $S = m \cdot \omega_e^2 \cdot (a+c)$.
- ☐ **Engagement ratio** $N_e/N$ is usually **$\dfrac{2}{3}$ to $\dfrac{4}{5}$**; lower ratio = earlier engagement.
- ☐ **Typical materials:** Cast iron shoes on cast iron drum; $\mu = 0.20$–$0.30$; $p = 0.25$–$0.40 \text{ MPa}$.
- ☐ **Number of shoes:** Usually 3 or 4 (rarely more); must be balanced dynamically.
- ☐ **Advantages:** Automatic, smooth, engine-protecting, simple, low maintenance.
- ☐ **Disadvantages:** Not suitable for very high speeds; fixed engagement ratio; cannot be modulated mid-operation.
- ☐ **Always** state **assumptions**, **define variables**, **check bearing pressure**, and **verify design torque** vs required torque.
- ☐ **Common KTU trap:** Forgetting the spring force $S$ in the final torque equation — double-check!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Brakes: Block Brake
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Brakes: Block Brake

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Block Brake** (also called a **Shoe Brake** or **Friction Brake**) is a mechanical device used to retard or stop the motion of a rotating drum or disc by means of a friction force generated when a block (shoe) lined with friction material is pressed against the rotating surface. The braking action is achieved by converting the kinetic energy of the rotating mass into heat through friction, with the resulting heat being dissipated into the surrounding atmosphere.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *A block brake is a friction brake in which a friction block (shoe) is pressed against a rotating drum by means of a lever mechanism pivoted at one end. The braking torque is produced by the friction force acting at the drum–block interface, given by $T_b = \mu W \cdot R$, where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction, $W$ is the normal load, and $R$ is the drum radius.*

## 1.2 Classification of Block Brakes

1. **External Block Brake** – Block acts on the outer surface of the drum.
   - **Single Block / Single Shoe Brake**
   - **Double Block / Double Shoe Brake (Symmetric or Differential)**
2. **Internal Expanding Brake** – Two blocks press outward against the inner surface of a drum.
3. **Pivoted vs. Non-Pivoted (Floating Lever)** brakes.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are riding a bicycle and you press the brake lever. The brake pad (block) is squeezed against the rim of the wheel (drum). The harder you press, the greater the friction force, and the faster you stop. The lever pivots at one end (the caliper mount), and your hand applies a force at the other end. If the friction between the pad and rim is high, even a small hand force can produce a large stopping force — this is the principle of **self-energization**. If the friction is *very* high, the pad might "grab" the rim without any hand force — this is the **self-locking** condition.

The same logic applies to industrial block brakes used in cranes, hoists, presses, and elevators.

## 1.4 Key Physical Parameters (Bold Constants)

- **Coefficient of friction ($\mu$):** Cast iron on cast iron ≈ 0.15–0.20; Leather on cast iron ≈ 0.20–0.30; Asbestos-based lining on cast iron ≈ 0.30–0.45.
- **Maximum pressure on friction surface ($p_{max}$):** 0.35 to 1.0 MPa (typical design value).
- **Drum diameter ($D$):** 100 mm to 1500 mm (application-dependent).
- **Block width ($c$):** Usually between 30 mm and 100 mm.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Forces Acting on a Pivoted Block Brake

Consider a single block brake with the block pivoted at one end (say, the left). Let the drum rotate in the **anticlockwise** direction. The forces acting on the block are:

- **$N$** → Normal reaction from drum on block (perpendicular to drum surface).
- **$\mu N$** → Frictional force on block from drum (tangential, in the direction of relative motion of drum surface at the contact).
- **$W$** → Actuating force applied at distance $a$ from the pivot.
- **$R_p$** → Reaction at the pivot.

The block has a width $c$, and the line of action of the normal force lies at distance $b$ from the pivot.

## 2.2 Theory of Friction (Uniform Pressure vs. Uniform Wear)

### (a) Uniform Pressure Theory
The pressure is assumed to be **uniformly distributed** over the contact area. This is valid for new, rigid, well-fitted brake linings.

- Line of action of $N$ passes through the **midpoint** of the contact length.
- Therefore, $b = \dfrac{c}{2}$ from one end (the end farther from pivot).

### (b) Uniform Wear Theory (Most Common in KTU Problems)
After some running time, the lining wears so that the **rate of wear is constant** over the contact area. Since wear rate is proportional to $p \cdot v$ and $v$ varies linearly across the contact width, the pressure $p$ varies **hyperbolically** (highest at the entry side, lowest at exit).

- The line of action of $N$ passes through a point located at a distance of $\dfrac{c}{3}$ from the leading edge or equivalently $\dfrac{2c}{3}$ from one end.
- For standard KTU textbook problems (V.B. Bhandari), the **$b = 0.577\,c$** convention is sometimes used for approximate location of the resultant when $c$ is small compared to drum radius.

## 2.3 Moment Equation About the Pivot

Taking moments about the pivot, the actuating moment $M$ required for equilibrium differs based on the rotation direction of the drum.

Let:
- $a$ = distance from pivot to the line of action of $W$ (the actuating force).
- $b$ = distance from pivot to the line of action of $N$.
- $c$ = block width.

**For anticlockwise rotation of the drum (friction $\mu N$ acts to assist $W$):**

$$M_1 = (a - \mu b)\,N$$

**For clockwise rotation of the drum (friction $\mu N$ acts to oppose $W$):**

$$M_2 = (a + \mu b)\,N$$

## 2.4 Self-Energizing, Self-De-Energizing, and Self-Locking

- **Self-Energizing:** When the friction moment **assists** the actuating moment → $M$ is reduced. Condition: $a > \mu b$ (so $M_1 > 0$ and is smaller than $M_2$).
- **Self-De-Energizing:** When the friction moment **opposes** the actuating moment → $M$ is increased.
- **Self-Locking:** When $\mu b \geq a$. The brake grips without any external force — dangerous and avoided in practice.
- **Reversibility:** $M_2 > M_1$. The same brake requires more force to apply during clockwise rotation than anticlockwise — an important design consideration.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state explicitly whether your assumed rotation direction is CW or CCW, and identify whether the brake is self-energizing or self-de-energizing in that configuration.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Braking torque (shoe) | $T_b = \mu N R$ | N·m | $R$ = drum radius |
| 2 | Tangential braking force | $F_t = \mu N$ | N | Acts tangentially on drum |
| 3 | Uniform pressure: location of $N$ | $b = \dfrac{c}{2}$ | m | $c$ = block width |
| 4 | Uniform wear: location of $N$ | $b = \dfrac{c}{3}$ (from leading) or $0.577c$ from one end | m | Standard textbook convention |
| 5 | Moment CCW rotation | $M_1 = (a - \mu b) N$ | N·m | Friction assists |
| 6 | Moment CW rotation | $M_2 = (a + \mu b) N$ | N·m | Friction opposes |
| 7 | Self-locking condition | $\mu b \geq a$ | – | Dangerous; avoid in design |
| 8 | Braking power | $P = T_b \cdot \omega = 2\pi N T_b / 60$ | W | $N$ in rpm |
| 9 | Heat dissipation rate | $H = P$ | W | Energy converted to heat per second |
| 10 | Block contact area | $A = c \cdot w$ | m² | $w$ = block depth (axial) |
| 11 | Max pressure (uniform pressure) | $p = \dfrac{N}{c \cdot w}$ | Pa | $p \leq p_{allowable}$ |
| 12 | Max pressure (uniform wear) | $p_{max} \cdot p_{min} = \left(\dfrac{N}{c \cdot w}\right)^2$ | Pa² | Hyperbolic pressure variation |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hoists and Cranes:** Block brakes hold the load stationary when power is cut.
- **Elevators:** Both motor-side and emergency brakes use block/shoe principles.
- **Hydraulic Presses:** Used to halt the ram at the end of stroke.
- **Locomotive Tread Brakes:** Classical block-on-wheel application.
- **Conveyor Systems:** Emergency stopping during power failure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Braking Torque of a Single Pivoted Block Brake

**Free-Body Diagram of the Block:**

The block is in equilibrium under four forces:
1. $W$ (actuating force) at distance $a$ from pivot.
2. $N$ (normal force from drum) at distance $b$ from pivot.
3. $\mu N$ (frictional force) at the same point as $N$.
4. $R_p$ (reaction at pivot).

**Step 1: Take moments about the pivot to eliminate $R_p$.**

For anticlockwise rotation, $\mu N$ creates a moment in the same sense as $W$, so:

$$\sum M_{pivot} = 0$$

$$W \cdot a - N \cdot b - (\mu N) \cdot 0 = 0 \quad \text{(if } \mu N \text{ passes through pivot)}$$

But in the **general case**, the line of action of $\mu N$ is at the same vertical level as the contact, which is offset from the pivot. Taking $\mu N$ at the contact (height $h$ above or below pivot), and resolving carefully:

$$M_1 = W \cdot a - N \cdot b \cdot \cos\theta - \mu N \cdot r_{drum}$$

For the **simplified standard Bhandari formulation** (where $\mu N$ is considered to act horizontally at the same vertical position as $N$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_1 &= (a - \mu b)\,N \quad \text{(anticlockwise rotation)} \\
M_2 &= (a + \mu b)\,N \quad \text{(clockwise rotation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Braking torque on the drum.**

The drum experiences a tangential force $\mu N$ at radius $R$. The braking torque is:

$$T_b = \mu N R$$

**Step 3: Express $N$ in terms of $W$.**

From the moment equation:

$$N = \frac{M_1}{a - \mu b} \quad \text{(CCW case)}$$

$$N = \frac{M_2}{a + \mu b} \quad \text{(CW case)}$$

**Step 4: Substituting back into $T_b$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_b &= \mu R \cdot \frac{M_1}{a - \mu b} \quad \text{(CCW)} \\
T_b &= \mu R \cdot \frac{M_2}{a + \mu b} \quad \text{(CW)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **governing design equation** for a single block brake.

## 3.2 Derivation: Location of Normal Force Under Uniform Wear

Consider a block of width $c$ pressed against a drum rotating with angular velocity $\omega$. The drum surface velocity at a distance $x$ from the entry side of the contact is:

$$v = \omega (R - x)$$

where $R$ is the drum radius. The **wear rate** is assumed proportional to $p \cdot v$. For uniform wear:

$$p \cdot v = \text{constant} = k$$

$$p(x) = \frac{k}{v} = \frac{k}{\omega (R - x)}$$

The total normal force is the integral of pressure over the area (per unit width $w$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= w \int_0^c p(x)\, dx \\
  &= \frac{kw}{\omega} \int_0^c \frac{dx}{R - x} \\
  &= \frac{kw}{\omega} \left[ -\ln(R - x) \right]_0^c \\
  &= \frac{kw}{\omega} \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{R-c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The moment of pressure about the entry side (pivot) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= w \int_0^c x \cdot p(x)\, dx \\
  &= \frac{kw}{\omega} \int_0^c \frac{x\, dx}{R - x}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the substitution $u = R - x \Rightarrow du = -dx$ and $x = R - u$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \frac{kw}{\omega} \int_R^{R-c} \frac{(R - u)(-du)}{u} \\
  &= \frac{kw}{\omega} \int_{R-c}^{R} \left(\frac{R}{u} - 1\right) du \\
  &= \frac{kw}{\omega} \left[ R \ln u - u \right]_{R-c}^{R} \\
  &= \frac{kw}{\omega} \left[ R \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{R-c}\right) - c \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the location of the resultant (line of action of $N$) is:

$$b = \frac{M}{N} = \frac{R \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{R-c}\right) - c}{\ln\!\left(\frac{R}{R-c}\right)}$$

For typical industrial brakes where $R \gg c$ (block width is small compared to drum radius), this reduces to:

$$b \approx \frac{c}{3} \quad \text{from the leading edge}$$

(approximately one-third of the block width from the entering side).

## 3.3 Numerical Illustration: Complete KTU-Style Solution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Practice-Grade):** A single block brake is used on a drum of diameter 500 mm running at 200 rpm. The block has a width of 60 mm and a depth (axial) of 80 mm. The block is pivoted at one end, and the actuating force of 1.5 kN is applied at a distance of 250 mm from the pivot. The coefficient of friction is 0.28. Determine: (i) the normal force on the block, (ii) the braking torque, (iii) the braking power, and (iv) the maximum pressure. Assume uniform pressure theory.

**Given:** $D = 0.5$ m, $N_{rpm} = 200$, $c = 0.06$ m, $w = 0.08$ m, $a = 0.25$ m, $W = 1500$ N, $\mu = 0.28$.

**Assume rotation:** Anticlockwise (so friction assists).

**(i) Normal force $N$:**

For uniform pressure, $b = c/2 = 0.03$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_1 &= W \cdot a = 1500 \times 0.25 = 375 \text{ N·m} \\
M_1 &= (a - \mu b) N \\
375 &= (0.25 - 0.28 \times 0.03) N \\
375 &= (0.25 - 0.0084) N \\
375 &= 0.2416 \, N \\
N &= \dfrac{375}{0.2416} = 1552.0 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Braking torque $T_b$:**

$$
T_b = \mu N R = 0.28 \times 1552.0 \times 0.25 = 108.6 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(iii) Braking power $P$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \dfrac{2\pi N_{rpm}}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 200}{60} = 20.944 \text{ rad/s} \\
P &= T_b \cdot \omega = 108.6 \times 20.944 = 2274.5 \text{ W} \approx 2.27 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Maximum pressure (uniform pressure):**

$$
p = \dfrac{N}{c \cdot w} = \dfrac{1552.0}{0.06 \times 0.08} = 323{,}333 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.323 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Verification of self-energizing condition:** $a = 0.25$ m, $\mu b = 0.28 \times 0.03 = 0.0084$ m. Since $a > \mu b$, the brake is **self-energizing** in the CCW direction. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of a Pivoted Block Brake

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Actuating Force W] --> B[Pivot Point A]
    B --> C{Lever Arm of length a}
    C --> D[Block of width c]
    D --> E[Drum Surface at radius R]
    E --> F[Normal Force N at distance b]
    E --> G[Friction Force muN]
    F --> H[Sum of Moments about pivot = 0]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Equilibrium Check}
    I -->|CCW Rotation| J[M1 equals a minus mu b times N]
    I -->|CW Rotation| K[M2 equals a plus mu b times N]
    J --> L[Braking Torque Tb equals mu N R]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Braking Power equals Tb times omega]
    M --> N[Heat Dissipation to Atmosphere]
```

## 4.2 Force Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block:Inputs
        W["Actuating Force W"]
        mu["Coefficient of Friction"]
        a["Lever Arm a"]
        b["Distance b to N"]
    end
    Inputs --> Geometry["Block-Drum Geometry"]
    Geometry --> Analysis["Moment Equilibrium"]
    Analysis -->|CCW| M1["M1 equals a minus mu b times N"]
    Analysis -->|CW| M2["M2 equals a plus mu b times N"]
    M1 --> N["Normal Force N"]
    M2 --> N
    N --> Tb["Braking Torque muNR"]
    Tb --> Heat["Heat Dissipation"]
    Tb --> Power["Braking Power 2piNT/60"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix: Self-Energizing Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Drum Rotation Direction?"] --> CW["Clockwise"]
    Start --> CCW["Anticlockwise"]
    CW --> CWCheck["Compare a and mu b"]
    CCW --> CCWCheck["Compare a and mu b"]
    CWCheck -->|a greater than mu b| CWSD["Self-De-Energizing"]
    CWCheck -->|a equals mu b| CWSL["Self-Locking Avoid"]
    CWCheck -->|a less than mu b| CWSE["Self-Energizing"]
    CCWCheck -->|a greater than mu b| CCWSE["Self-Energizing"]
    CCWCheck -->|a equals mu b| CCWSL["Self-Locking Avoid"]
    CCWCheck -->|a less than mu b| CCWSD["Self-De-Energizing"]
```

## 4.4 Uniform Pressure vs. Uniform Wear Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UP["Uniform Pressure Theory"]
        UP1["Pressure p constant"]
        UP2["Resultant N at c slash 2"]
        UP3["Use for new rigid linings"]
    end
    subgraph UW["Uniform Wear Theory"]
        UW1["Pressure varies hyperbolically"]
        UW2["Resultant N at c slash 3 from leading edge"]
        UW3["Use for worn or long-running brakes"]
    end
    UP --> Q["Pick theory based on operating phase"]
    UW --> Q
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define a **self-energizing brake** and a **self-locking brake**. Under what conditions does each occur in a single pivoted block brake?

**Model Answer:**

A **self-energizing brake** is one in which the frictional force at the drum–block interface creates a moment that **assists** the actuating moment, thereby reducing the effort required to apply the brake. For anticlockwise drum rotation, the moment equation is $M_1 = (a - \mu b)N$, and the brake is self-energizing when $a > \mu b$.

A **self-locking brake** is an extreme case in which the friction moment alone is sufficient to press the block against the drum, requiring **no external actuating force**. The condition is $\mu b \geq a$, leading to $M_1 \leq 0$. This condition is **avoided in practical design** because it prevents brake release and can cause catastrophic lockup.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Differentiate between **uniform pressure theory** and **uniform wear theory** in the analysis of a block brake. Where is the resultant normal force located in each case?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Uniform Pressure Theory | Uniform Wear Theory |
|--------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Pressure distribution | Constant over the entire contact area | Varies hyperbolically: $p(x) \cdot v(x) = \text{const}$ |
| Validity | New, rigid, well-fitted brake lining | After running-in, steady-state operating condition |
| Resultant $N$ location | Midpoint of block: $b = c/2$ | At $c/3$ from the leading (entering) edge |
| Max pressure formula | $p = N/(c \cdot w)$ | $p_{max} = N/(c \cdot w) \cdot \dfrac{2R - c}{2R}$ (for finite drum radius) |

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the braking torque of a single pivoted block brake. State clearly the self-energizing and self-de-energizing conditions, and explain why $M_2 > M_1$.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A single block brake has a drum of diameter 500 mm and the block is 75 mm wide. The block is pivoted at one end, and the actuating force of 2 kN is applied at a distance of 200 mm from the pivot. The coefficient of friction is 0.30. Calculate the braking torque and braking power at 250 rpm, assuming (i) uniform pressure theory, and (ii) uniform wear theory.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** See **Section 3.1** above for the complete derivation. The key results are:

- CCW: $M_1 = (a - \mu b)N$ → self-energizing when $a > \mu b$.
- CW: $M_2 = (a + \mu b)N$ → always self-de-energizing.
- $M_2 > M_1$ because the friction moment adds to $W$ in CW case (positive sign), but subtracts in CCW case (negative sign).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 0.5$ m $\Rightarrow R = 0.25$ m; $c = 0.075$ m; $a = 0.2$ m; $W = 2000$ N; $\mu = 0.30$; $N_{rpm} = 250$.

Angular velocity: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 250}{60} = 26.18$ rad/s.

**Case (i) — Uniform pressure:**

$b = c/2 = 0.0375$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_1 &= W \cdot a = 2000 \times 0.2 = 400 \text{ N·m} \\
N &= \frac{M_1}{a - \mu b} = \frac{400}{0.2 - 0.3 \times 0.0375} = \frac{400}{0.2 - 0.01125} \\
N &= \frac{400}{0.18875} = 2119.2 \text{ N} \\
T_b &= \mu N R = 0.30 \times 2119.2 \times 0.25 = 158.94 \text{ N·m} \\
P &= T_b \cdot \omega = 158.94 \times 26.18 = 4160.3 \text{ W} \approx 4.16 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case (ii) — Uniform wear (using $b = c/3$ from leading edge = $0.025$ m from pivot if leading edge is at pivot, or $0.05$ m if leading edge is at far end):**

Assuming the pivot is at the leading edge (standard convention for KTU):

$b = c/3 = 0.025$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{400}{0.2 - 0.3 \times 0.025} = \frac{400}{0.2 - 0.0075} = \frac{400}{0.1925} \\
N &= 2077.9 \text{ N} \\
T_b &= 0.30 \times 2077.9 \times 0.25 = 155.84 \text{ N·m} \\
P &= 155.84 \times 26.18 = 4080.0 \text{ W} \approx 4.08 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the operation of an **internal expanding double block brake**. Derive the expression for the braking torque.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A double block (shoe) brake has a drum diameter of 600 mm. Each block subtends an angle of 60° at the center of the drum. The drum rotates at 300 rpm. Each block is pressed against the drum by a force of 4 kN. The coefficient of friction is 0.30. Find (i) the braking torque, (ii) the braking power, and (iii) the maximum intensity of pressure, assuming uniform pressure over the contact area. Block width = 50 mm.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Internal Expanding Double Block Brake:**

In an internal expanding brake, two friction blocks (shoes) are mounted inside a drum and forced outward against the inner cylindrical surface. The actuating mechanism (a cam, linkage, or hydraulic piston) pushes the two shoes apart.

Each shoe experiences:
- Normal force $N_1$ and $N_2$ (perpendicular to the drum surface at the contact).
- Frictional forces $\mu N_1$ and $\mu N_2$ (tangential).

The braking torque is the sum of contributions from both shoes:

$$T_b = T_{b1} + T_{b2} = \mu R (N_1 + N_2)$$

If both shoes are identical and pressed with equal force, $N_1 = N_2 = N$:

$$T_b = 2 \mu N R$$

**Advantages of internal expanding brake:**
- Completely enclosed (protection from dust, water).
- Compact design.
- Acts as a clutch also (in some designs, e.g., expanding mandrel).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 0.6$ m $\Rightarrow R = 0.3$ m; $\theta = 60° = \pi/3$ rad; $N_{rpm} = 300$; $W = 4000$ N per block; $\mu = 0.30$; $c = 0.05$ m (width along drum axis).

For uniform pressure on each block, normal force per unit area is constant. The contact area per block is:

$$A = R \cdot \theta \cdot w = 0.3 \times \frac{\pi}{3} \times 0.05 = 0.01571 \text{ m}^2$$

Wait — here $W$ is the normal pressing force on each block, so $N_1 = N_2 = 4000$ N (treating $W$ as normal load in this context).

**(i) Braking torque:**

$$
T_b = 2 \mu N R = 2 \times 0.30 \times 4000 \times 0.3 = 720 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(ii) Braking power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416 \text{ rad/s} \\
P &= T_b \cdot \omega = 720 \times 31.416 = 22{,}620 \text{ W} \approx 22.62 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Maximum intensity of pressure (uniform pressure):**

The projected contact area per block (in the radial plane) is $A_p = R \cdot \theta \cdot w$ where $w$ is the block width perpendicular to the contact arc. Here $c$ is the block width along the axis of the drum:

$$
p = \frac{N}{R \cdot \theta \cdot c} = \frac{4000}{0.3 \times \frac{\pi}{3} \times 0.05} = \frac{4000}{0.01571} = 254{,}648 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.255 \text{ MPa}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Block Brake Problems:**
>
> 1. **Wrong direction convention:** Students often write $M = (a + \mu b)N$ for *both* directions. This is incorrect. The sign depends on whether friction assists or opposes the actuating moment. **Always** state the assumed rotation direction.
>
> 2. **Confusing $a$ and $b$:** $a$ is the lever arm to the **actuating force $W$**, while $b$ is the lever arm to the **normal force $N$**. Confusing these leads to a 7-mark disaster.
>
> 3. **Uniform pressure vs. uniform wear location of $N$:** Using $b = c/2$ for a uniform-wear problem (or vice versa) loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Missing units:** KTU strict valuation requires **SI units** (N, m, N·m, Pa, W) and 3-significant-figure final answers. Missing units → 0.5 to 1 mark deduction.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to compute braking power:** A common 14-mark question asks for both torque *and* power; many students stop at $T_b$ and lose 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Self-locking condition $\mu b \geq a$:** When this is satisfied, the brake *cannot* be released. Examiners love to ask "comment on safety" — always mention this.
>
> 7. **Double block vs. double shoe:** Some textbooks use the terms interchangeably. In KTU papers, "block" and "shoe" mean the same component, so do not double-count.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Block brake** = friction shoe pressed against a drum by a pivoted lever.
- **Braking torque** $T_b = \mu N R$ — derived from tangential friction force at the drum radius.
- **Moment equation** about the pivot:
  - CCW drum: $M_1 = (a - \mu b)N$
  - CW drum: $M_2 = (a + \mu b)N$
  - Always $M_2 \geq M_1$.
- **Uniform pressure theory:** $b = c/2$. Use for new brakes.
- **Uniform wear theory:** $b = c/3$ (from leading edge). Use for steady-state / worn brakes.
- **Self-energizing condition:** $a > \mu b$ (friction helps).
- **Self-locking condition:** $\mu b \geq a$ (friction alone grips the drum — avoid!).
- **Self-de-energizing:** friction opposes $W$ — needs higher actuating force.
- **Double block (internal expanding) brake:** Two shoes inside a drum; total $T_b = 2 \mu N R$ for symmetric design.
- **Braking power** $P = T_b \cdot \omega$ in watts, where $\omega$ is in rad/s.
- **Block dimensions** must satisfy $p \leq p_{allowable}$ (typically 0.35–1.0 MPa for cast iron linings).
- **Reversibility** of block brakes is a key disadvantage compared to disc brakes.
- **Heat dissipation:** All braking energy becomes heat — ensure adequate thermal design (fins, cooling).
- **Standard KTU notation (Bhandari):** $a$, $b$, $c$, $W$, $N$, $\mu$, $R$ — memorize these.
- **Always include a free-body diagram (FBD)** in 14-mark answers — KTU evaluators award 2 marks purely for a clear FBD.
- **Always specify the rotation direction** assumed in your problem — examiners mark based on this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Band Brake
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Band Brake

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
