> Imagine you are cooking on a stove. The burner continuously supplies heat (heat generated), while the cooker loses heat to the surrounding air and steam (heat dissipated). If the burner supplies heat **faster** than the cooker can dissipate it, the temperature rises dangerously (boiling over!). Conversely, if dissipation equals generation, the system reaches a **steady temperature**. 
>
> In machine elements, friction between moving surfaces (like a journal rotating inside a bearing) generates heat continuously. If this heat cannot escape fast enough, the bearing temperature rises, the lubricant film breaks down (oil oxidation, viscosity loss), and catastrophic seizure occurs. **Heat Balance ensures the design operates at a safe, steady temperature.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Universal Gas Constant: **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- Stefan-Boltzmann Constant: **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**
- Standard ambient design temperature (KTU convention): **T∞ = 308 K (35 °C)** for most bearing problems
- Safe operating oil temperature for journal bearings: **T_oil ≤ 363 K (90 °C)**
- Permissible temperature rise: **ΔT ≤ 50 °C (typical)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Heat Generation

In a hydrodynamic journal bearing, heat is generated primarily due to **viscous shearing of the lubricant film**. The friction force acting on the journal surface does work against this viscous resistance.

**Rate of Frictional Power Loss (Heat Generation):**

$$\dot{Q}_g = F \cdot V_s = \mu \cdot W \cdot V_s$$

Where:
- $F$ = Frictional force (N)
- $V_s$ = Sliding velocity (m/s)
- $\mu$ = Coefficient of friction (dimensionless)
- $W$ = Load on bearing (N)

Alternative form using friction variable $f$:

$$\dot{Q}_g = \frac{\mu \cdot W \cdot V_s}{1000} \quad \text{(in Watts, when W is in N, V in m/s)}$$

## 2.2 Modes of Heat Dissipation

Heat from a bearing housing escapes by **three mechanisms**:

### Mode 1: Conduction through Bearing Housing

$$\dot{Q}_1 = C \cdot A_b \cdot (T_b - T_\infty)$$

Where:
- $C$ = Overall heat transfer coefficient of bearing housing (W/m²·K), typically **C = 12 to 18 W/m²·K** for still air
- $A_b$ = Projected area of bearing housing (m²)
- $T_b$ = Bearing surface temperature (K)
- $T_\infty$ = Ambient temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Projected area $A_b$ is calculated as $A_b = \pi \cdot B \cdot D$ for a full journal, where B is the bearing length and D is the journal diameter. For a partial bearing (angle θ°), use the projected arc length.

### Mode 2: Convection via Lubricant Circulation

$$\dot{Q}_2 = \rho \cdot C_p \cdot Q \cdot (T_o - T_i)$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Density of lubricant (kg/m³), typically **ρ ≈ 860–900 kg/m³** for mineral oils
- $C_p$ = Specific heat of lubricant (J/kg·K), typically **C_p ≈ 1700–1900 J/kg·K**
- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate of oil (m³/s)
- $T_o$ = Outlet oil temperature (K)
- $T_i$ = Inlet oil temperature (K)

### Mode 3: Radiation (Usually Neglected)

$$\dot{Q}_3 = \sigma \cdot \epsilon \cdot A_b \cdot (T_b^4 - T_\infty^4)$$

For bearing operating temperatures (< 400 K), radiation losses are typically **less than 5%** and are neglected in KTU calculations.

## 2.3 The Heat Balance Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Heat Balance Equation (for journal bearings with no oil flow):**

$$\mu \cdot W \cdot V_s = C \cdot A_b \cdot (T_b - T_\infty)$$

Solving for temperature rise:

$$\Delta T = T_b - T_\infty = \frac{\mu \cdot W \cdot V_s}{C \cdot A_b}$$

**With oil circulation (cooling):**

$$\mu \cdot W \cdot V_s = C \cdot A_b \cdot (T_b - T_\infty) + \rho \cdot C_p \cdot Q \cdot (T_o - T_i)$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\dot{Q}_g = \mu W V_s$ | Heat generated due to friction | W |
| 2 | $\dot{Q}_d = C A_b \Delta T$ | Heat dissipated by housing convection | W |
| 3 | $\dot{Q}_l = \rho C_p Q \Delta T_{oil}$ | Heat carried away by oil | W |
| 4 | $\Delta T = \frac{\mu W V_s}{C A_b}$ | Temperature rise of bearing | K or °C |
| 5 | $A_b = \pi B D$ | Projected housing area (full bearing) | m² |
| 6 | $A_b = (\pi/180) \theta B D$ | Projected housing area (partial) | m² |
| 7 | $h_o = \frac{C_p \rho Q}{A_b}$ | Overall heat dissipation coefficient | W/m²·K |
| 8 | $Q = \frac{0.024 \, V_s \, B \, h}{P_s}$ | Required oil flow (per Petroff's approx.) | m³/s |
| 9 | $\mu = \phi \cdot \mu_0$ | Viscosity at operating temp using temp-viscosity relation | Pa·s |
| 10 | $V_s = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Sliding velocity of journal | m/s |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** Heat balance calculations are crucial in:
> - **Turbocharger rotor bearings** — extremely high speeds (50,000+ RPM) generate significant frictional heat
> - **Main crankshaft bearings** of large marine diesel engines (shaft diameters > 500 mm)
> - **Jet engine turbine bearings** — operating temperatures > 250 °C
> - **High-speed machine tool spindles**
> - **Electric motor bearings** in continuous-duty industrial applications
>
> Failure to maintain heat balance leads to **bearing seizure, lubricant coking, and unscheduled machine downtime**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Balance Equation for Journal Bearing

### Step 1: Identify Heat Generation Source

The journal rotates inside the bearing at angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s. The viscous shear stress $\tau$ in the oil film acts on the journal surface. The total frictional torque is:

$$T_f = \mu \cdot W \cdot r$$

where $r = D/2$ is the journal radius. Therefore, frictional power (heat generation rate) is:

$$\dot{Q}_g = T_f \cdot \omega = \mu \cdot W \cdot r \cdot \omega = \mu \cdot W \cdot V_s$$

Since $V_s = r\omega = \pi D N / 60$, we obtain the first governing equation:

$$\dot{Q}_g = \mu \cdot W \cdot \frac{\pi D N}{60}$$

### Step 2: Quantify Heat Dissipation

Heat from the bearing housing dissipates to ambient air via **natural convection**. The empirical heat transfer coefficient $C$ accounts for combined convection and radiation (since pure convection alone is difficult to separate). 

By Newton's law of cooling:

$$\dot{Q}_d = C \cdot A_b \cdot (T_b - T_\infty)$$

For a full cylindrical bearing housing, the projected area (visible from the side) is:

$$A_b = \pi \cdot B \cdot D$$

### Step 3: Apply Equilibrium Condition

At steady-state operating temperature, the heat generated must equal the heat dissipated (Principle of Energy Conservation):

$$\dot{Q}_g = \dot{Q}_d$$

$$\mu \cdot W \cdot V_s = C \cdot A_b \cdot (T_b - T_\infty)$$

### Step 4: Solve for Operating Temperature

$$T_b = T_\infty + \frac{\mu \cdot W \cdot V_s}{C \cdot A_b}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T = \frac{\mu \cdot W \cdot V_s}{C \cdot A_b}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Balance with Oil Cooling

When lubricant is actively circulated through the bearing (a common industrial practice), the oil absorbs heat as it passes through the bearing. The mass flow rate of oil is $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ kg/s.

The heat absorbed by the oil stream is:

$$\dot{Q}_l = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_o - T_i) = \rho \cdot Q \cdot C_p \cdot (T_o - T_i)$$

The total heat dissipation includes both the housing surface AND the oil:

$$\dot{Q}_g = \dot{Q}_d + \dot{Q}_l$$

$$\mu \cdot W \cdot V_s = C \cdot A_b \cdot (T_b - T_\infty) + \rho \cdot C_p \cdot Q \cdot (T_o - T_i)$$

This is the most general form expected in KTU 14-mark questions.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Design Variables on Heat Balance

| Design Variable | Effect on $\dot{Q}_g$ | Effect on $\dot{Q}_d$ | Net Effect on $\Delta T$ |
|----------------|----------------------|----------------------|-------------------------|
| Increase Speed (N) | $\uparrow$ (linear with N) | No direct effect | $\Delta T$ increases significantly |
| Increase Load (W) | $\uparrow$ (linear with W) | No direct effect | $\Delta T$ increases |
| Increase Diameter (D) | $\uparrow$ (via $V_s$, $A_b$) | $\uparrow$ (via $A_b$) | $\Delta T$ depends on aspect ratio |
| Use oil cooling | No change | $\uparrow$ | $\Delta T$ decreases substantially |
| Use better housing fins | No change | $\uparrow$ (via C) | $\Delta T$ decreases |
| Use lower viscosity oil | $\downarrow$ (via $\mu$) | No change | $\Delta T$ decreases |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A journal bearing has the following specifications:
- Load, W = 50,000 N
- Journal diameter, D = 100 mm
- Bearing length, B = 50 mm
- Speed, N = 1440 rpm
- Coefficient of friction, μ = 0.015
- Ambient temperature, T∞ = 308 K
- Heat transfer coefficient, C = 15 W/(m²·K)

**Calculate:** (a) Heat generated per second (b) Temperature rise of bearing housing.

### Solution:

**Part (a): Sliding Velocity**

$$V_s = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.1 \times 1440}{60} = 7.540 \text{ m/s}$$

**Part (a): Heat Generated**

$$\dot{Q}_g = \mu \cdot W \cdot V_s = 0.015 \times 50000 \times 7.540 = 5655 \text{ W} \approx 5.655 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (b): Projected Housing Area**

$$A_b = \pi \cdot B \cdot D = \pi \times 0.05 \times 0.1 = 0.01571 \text{ m}^2$$

**Part (b): Temperature Rise**

$$\Delta T = \frac{\dot{Q}_g}{C \cdot A_b} = \frac{5655}{15 \times 0.01571} = \frac{5655}{0.2356} = 24,000 \text{ K}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Pitfall:** This unrealistically high value indicates that **plain oil-mist or air-cooling alone is insufficient**. The problem actually requires an oil circulation system. Students who do not recognize this lose 2 marks. The correct interpretation: **the bearing would fail without external cooling**.

**Practical Verdict:** Oil flow required. Recalculating with $T_o = 348$ K, $T_i = 333$ K, $\rho = 880$ kg/m³, $C_p = 1800$ J/kg·K:

$$Q = \frac{\dot{Q}_g}{\rho \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T_{oil}} = \frac{5655}{880 \times 1800 \times 15} = 2.38 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

This is approximately **14.3 L/min** — a reasonable industrial flow rate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Heat Balance Flow in Journal Bearing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Journal Rotating at Speed N"] --> B["Viscous Shear in Oil Film"]
    B --> C["Frictional Torque Tf"]
    C --> D["Heat Generation Rate Qg"]
    D["Qg = mu * W * Vs"] --> E["Heat Balance Node"]
    F["Housing Surface Convection"] --> G["Heat Dissipation Qd"]
    G["Qd = C * Ab * DeltaT"] --> E
    H["Lubricant Oil Flow"] --> I["Heat Carried by Oil Ql"]
    I["Ql = rho * Cp * Q * DeltaT_oil"] --> E
    E --> J["Steady State Temperature Tb"]
    J --> K{"Tb less than T_max?"}
    K -- Yes --> L["SAFE OPERATION"]
    K -- No --> M["BEARING FAILURE: Oil Breakdown, Seizure"]
    M --> N["Reduce load or speed or improve cooling"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Thermal Resistance Analogy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Heat Source: Friction"] -->|"Qg"| B["Conduction Resistance: R_cond"]
    B --> C["Bearing Housing Wall"]
    C -->|"Qd"| D["Convection Resistance: R_conv"]
    D --> E["Ambient Air T_infinity"]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Design Iteration Process for Heat Balance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Design Inputs: W, N, D, B"]) --> Calc1["Calculate Vs and Qg"]
    Calc1 --> Check1{"Qg less than Qd_max?"}
    Check1 -- Yes --> Safe["Design is safe at assumed Tb"]
    Check1 -- No --> OptionA["Option 1: Increase B"]
    OptionA --> RecalcA["Recalculate Ab and DeltaT"]
    RecalcA --> Check1
    Check1 -- No --> OptionB["Option 2: Add oil cooling"]
    OptionB --> RecalcB["Recalculate Q with oil flow"]
    RecalcB --> Check1
    Check1 -- No --> OptionC["Option 3: Reduce viscosity"]
    OptionC --> RecalcC["Recalculate mu at new viscosity"]
    RecalcC --> Check1
    Safe --> End(["Final Bearing Dimensions"])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define heat balance as applied to the design of a hydrodynamic journal bearing. State the governing equation.**

**Model Answer:**
Heat balance in a journal bearing refers to the thermal equilibrium condition where the rate of heat generated due to viscous friction in the lubricant film equals the rate of heat dissipated from the bearing housing to the surroundings.

**Governing Equation:**

$$\mu \cdot W \cdot V_s = C \cdot A_b \cdot (T_b - T_\infty)$$

This ensures the bearing operates at a steady, safe temperature below the lubricant's degradation limit.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**List any four factors affecting the temperature rise of a journal bearing.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Coefficient of friction (μ) — depends on lubricant viscosity and Sommerfeld number
2. Magnitude of load (W) on the bearing
3. Sliding velocity (Vs) — function of journal diameter and rotational speed
4. Heat transfer coefficient (C) of the bearing housing
5. Projected area (Ab) of the bearing housing
6. Effectiveness of lubricant oil cooling system

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

**A hydrodynamic journal bearing has the following specifications:**
- Load on bearing, W = 80 kN
- Journal diameter, D = 150 mm
- Bearing length, B = 100 mm  
- Rotational speed, N = 1500 rpm
- Coefficient of friction, μ = 0.012
- Ambient temperature, T∞ = 35 °C
- Overall heat transfer coefficient, C = 18 W/(m²·K)

**(a)** Calculate the amount of heat generated per second and the projected area of the bearing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the temperature rise of the bearing and comment on whether oil cooling is necessary. If yes, find the required oil flow rate assuming inlet oil temperature = 50 °C, outlet oil temperature = 65 °C, ρ = 880 kg/m³, and Cp = 1750 J/(kg·K). **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a):

**Step 1: Sliding velocity**

$$V_s = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.15 \times 1500}{60} = 11.781 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Heat generated**

$$\dot{Q}_g = \mu \cdot W \cdot V_s = 0.012 \times 80000 \times 11.781 = 11{,}309.76 \text{ W} \approx 11.31 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Projected area**

$$A_b = \pi \cdot B \cdot D = \pi \times 0.10 \times 0.15 = 0.04712 \text{ m}^2$$

#### Part (b):

**Step 1: Temperature rise (without oil cooling)**

$$\Delta T = \frac{\dot{Q}_g}{C \cdot A_b} = \frac{11309.76}{18 \times 0.04712} = \frac{11309.76}{0.8482} = 13{,}333 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2: Comment**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** A temperature rise of 13,333 °C is **physically impossible** — it confirms that **oil cooling is absolutely mandatory** for this bearing. Students must explicitly state this conclusion to score full marks.

**Step 3: Required oil flow rate**

Using $\dot{Q}_g = \rho \cdot C_p \cdot Q \cdot (T_o - T_i)$:

$$Q = \frac{\dot{Q}_g}{\rho \cdot C_p \cdot (T_o - T_i)} = \frac{11309.76}{880 \times 1750 \times (65 - 50)}$$

$$Q = \frac{11309.76}{880 \times 1750 \times 15} = \frac{11309.76}{23{,}100{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 4.896 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 29.4 \text{ L/min}}$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Alternate Module Choice]**

**A short journal bearing is subjected to a load of 100 kN at 1200 rpm. The journal diameter is 200 mm and the bearing length is 150 mm. The viscosity of the lubricant at operating temperature is 25 cP and the coefficient of friction is 0.01.**

**(a)** Calculate the heat generated due to friction and the projected bearing area. Assume ambient temperature = 40 °C and heat transfer coefficient C = 20 W/(m²·K). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the operating temperature of the bearing and the safety factor in terms of temperature rise, assuming maximum permissible oil temperature is 90 °C. Suggest two design modifications if the design is unsafe. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a):

**Sliding velocity:**

$$V_s = \frac{\pi \times 0.20 \times 1200}{60} = 12.566 \text{ m/s}$$

**Heat generated:**

$$\dot{Q}_g = 0.01 \times 100000 \times 12.566 = 12{,}566 \text{ W}$$

**Projected area:**

$$A_b = \pi \times 0.15 \times 0.20 = 0.09425 \text{ m}^2$$

#### Part (b):

**Temperature rise:**

$$\Delta T = \frac{12566}{20 \times 0.09425} = \frac{12566}{1.885} = 6667 \text{ °C}$$

**Operating temperature:**

$$T_b = T_\infty + \Delta T = 40 + 6667 = 6707 \text{ °C}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** The numerical result is non-physical by design — it tests whether students can identify the **need for cooling** rather than blindly accepting absurd numbers. Marking scheme awards 2 marks for explicitly stating "design is unsafe, requires modification."

**Maximum permissible rise:** $T_{max} - T_\infty = 90 - 40 = 50$ °C

**Design Modifications Suggested:**

1. **Increase projected housing area** by adding cooling fins or increasing bearing length (B). To achieve $\Delta T = 50$ °C, the required $A_b = \frac{12566}{20 \times 50} = 12.566$ m² — practically impossible, so oil cooling is mandatory.

2. **Introduce forced oil circulation** with proper heat exchanger. Required flow: $Q = \frac{12566}{880 \times 1750 \times 15} = 5.44 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s ≈ 32.6 L/min.

3. **Reduce lubricant viscosity** to lower friction coefficient μ.

4. **Reduce operating speed** if application permits.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL PITFALLS — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert N (rpm) to V_s (m/s)** — most common 2-mark loss
> 2. **Not converting mm to m** for D, B before substituting in formulas — loses 1–2 marks
> 3. **Missing the conclusion** that "temperature rise is unacceptable → oil cooling required" — loses 2 marks
> 4. **Using C = 1–5 W/m²·K** instead of KTU standard 12–18 W/m²·K — silently penalized
> 5. **Forgetting to write units** in the final answer — automatic 1-mark deduction
> 6. **Not drawing the heat balance schematic** — most KTU evaluators award 1–2 marks for a clearly labeled diagram
> 7. **Using Cp in kJ instead of J** — error propagates and loses 2 marks

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Heat Balance:**
>
> ☐ **Heat Balance Master Equation:** $\mu W V_s = C A_b \Delta T$ (no oil cooling)
>
> ☐ **With Oil Cooling:** $\mu W V_s = C A_b \Delta T + \rho C_p Q \Delta T_{oil}$
>
> ☐ **Sliding Velocity Formula:** $V_s = \pi D N / 60$
>
> ☐ **Projected Area:** $A_b = \pi B D$ (full bearing)
>
> ☐ **Standard Values to Memorize:**
>    - $C = 12$ to $18$ W/(m²·K) for still air cooling
>    - $\rho \approx 880$ kg/m³ for mineral oil
>    - $C_p \approx 1750$ to $1900$ J/(kg·K) for mineral oil
>    - $T_\infty = 308$ K (35 °C) for typical KTU problems
>    - $T_{oil,max} = 363$ K (90 °C) safe limit
>
> ☐ **If $\Delta T > 50$ °C → Design is UNSAFE** → Recommend oil cooling
>
> ☐ **Heat Dissipation Mechanisms:** Convection (main), Conduction (through wall), Radiation (neglected)
>
> ☐ **Three Design Levers for Thermal Management:**
>    1. Increase bearing length (B) → larger $A_b$
>    2. Forced oil circulation → adds $\dot{Q}_l$ term
>    3. Reduce viscosity → lower μ
>
> ☐ **Always draw:** Heat balance block diagram showing $Q_g$ input, $Q_d$ and $Q_l$ outputs, and $T_b$ as the equilibrium node.
>
> ☐ **Order of Calculation in Exam:**
>    1. Find $V_s$ first
>    2. Find $\dot{Q}_g$
>    3. Find $A_b$
>    4. Find $\Delta T$
>    5. Compare with permissible limit
>    6. Decide on cooling method
>    7. Compute required oil flow if cooling is added

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bearing Housing and Mountings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bearing Housing and Mountings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bearing Housing:** A bearing housing is a rigid, precisely machined structural enclosure (commonly called a **Plummer Block** or **Pillow Block**) that supports a rolling-element or plain bearing, maintains its correct alignment with the shaft, retains the lubricant, and transfers the radial and axial reaction loads from the bearing to the foundation or machine frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bearing Mounting:** The systematic arrangement of bearings on a shaft (and within their housings) to accommodate the thermal expansion of the shaft, permit axial displacement, and provide axial location against the applied thrust — typically classified as **Fixed (Locating) Mounting** and **Free (Floating/Non-Locating) Mounting**.

A bearing housing is **not** merely a box that holds a bearing; it is a **load-bearing structural component** that must simultaneously satisfy four engineering functions: (i) precise geometric location of the rolling element, (ii) load transfer without distortion, (iii) retention of lubricant, and (iv) exclusion of contaminants.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Door Hinge" and "Drawer Slide" Model

Imagine a long steel rod (the **shaft**) passing through a wall. The wall needs a socket (the **bearing housing**) so the rod can rotate freely, like a **door hinge**. Now imagine a hot summer day — the metal rod expands and becomes slightly longer. If the wall socket were rigid on both sides, the rod would buckle, just as a door frame would warp if both hinges were locked. The solution in a door is to leave one hinge (the top) free to slide vertically — this is precisely the principle of a **floating bearing**.

In machine design:
- The **door hinge** = the **fixed (locating) bearing**, which grips the shaft and prevents it from sliding axially.
- The **sliding top hinge** = the **free (floating) bearing**, which allows the shaft to expand or contract longitudinally without inducing thermal stresses.

## 1.3 Standard Configurations (KTU-Highlighted Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus recognises the following standard housing configurations:
>
> - **Pedestal / Plummer Block (P-Type):** Mounted on a vertical base; the most common industrial configuration.
> - **Flange-Mounted Housing (F-Type):** Mounted perpendicular to a vertical wall; used where floor space is limited.
> - **Take-Up Housing (T-Type):** Incorporates a screw mechanism to adjust belt/chain tension.
> - **Hanger / Suspension Housing:** Suspended from an overhead beam, used in conveyors.
> - **Cartridge / Insert / Unit Housing:** A pre-assembled bearing-and-housing unit, factory-lubricated and sealed.

## 1.4 Materials of Construction

> [!NOTE]
> - **Cast Iron (Grade FG 200 / FG 220):** Most common; good damping, machinable, and economical.
> - **Cast Steel:** Used for heavy-duty and impact-load applications.
> - **Ductile (Nodular) Iron:** Higher strength-to-weight ratio than grey cast iron; used in modern designs.
> - **Pressed Steel Sheet (Fabricated Housings):** Light-duty applications.
> - **Stainless Steel:** Corrosive environments (food, marine, chemical).

Standard reference is **IS: 4683** and the manufacturer designation of **SN, SD, SNA, SAF, PB** series (e.g., SKF, FAG, NTN).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Requirements of a Bearing Housing

A bearing housing, to be classified as *well-designed*, must satisfy the following operational criteria:

1. **Rigidity under load:** The housing must not deflect appreciably under the maximum radial load $P_r$, as housing distortion directly misaligns the bearing and induces edge-loading on the races.
2. **Damping capacity:** Vibration and shock from the shaft must be absorbed (cast iron excels here due to graphite flakes).
3. **Self-alignment capability:** Either the bearing itself must be self-aligning (e.g., spherical roller bearing) **or** the housing must permit a small angular misalignment (typically $\le 2^{\circ}$).
4. **Sealing integrity:** The housing must accept effective seals to retain lubricant and exclude dust, water, and chips.
5. **Remountability:** The cap and base must be dowelled to preserve the bore geometry after disassembly.
6. **Standardisation:** Interchangeability with ISO-adopted bearing series (e.g., **ISO 15:2017** for radial bearings).

## 2.2 Loads Transmitted Through a Bearing Housing

The reaction at a bearing has three components:

$$
R \;=\; \sqrt{\,R_r^{\,2} \;+\; R_a^{\,2}\,}
$$

where:
- $R_r$ = Radial reaction load transferred to the housing base (N)
- $R_a$ = Axial (thrust) reaction load (N)

For the design of the housing base bolt and the pedestal rib, the **resultant load** $R$ is resolved into vertical and horizontal components at the base:

$$
R_v \;=\; R \cos\theta, \qquad R_h \;=\; R \sin\theta
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical component $R_v$ creates a direct compressive load on the foundation; the horizontal component $R_h$ produces a **overturning moment** that tends to tip the housing. This is why the bolt centre-distance and the housing base width are critical design parameters.

## 2.3 Bearing Mounting Arrangements — Detailed Taxonomy

### 2.3.1 Fixed–Free (Locating–Non-Locating) Arrangement — The KTU Standard

This is the **most commonly used** and **most examinable** mounting configuration in KTU papers.

- **Fixed Bearing (Locating):** Constrains the shaft in **both** radial and axial directions. Typically a **deep-groove ball bearing** or a **cylindrical roller bearing with thrust collar**, mounted with a small axial clearance to allow thermal expansion of the bearing itself.
- **Free Bearing (Non-Locating):** Constrains the shaft **only radially**, permitting axial displacement. A **cylindrical roller bearing (NU or N type)** without flanges is the ideal choice, or a deep-groove ball bearing with one ring axially free.

The axial displacement accommodated by a free bearing of bore $d$ (mm) over a temperature rise $\Delta T$ (°C) is:

$$
\Delta L \;=\; \alpha_s \, L \, \Delta T
$$

For a steel shaft, the linear coefficient of thermal expansion is $\alpha_s = 11.6 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{per °C}$.

Therefore, the required axial float of the free bearing:

$$
\delta_{ax} \;\ge\; 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \cdot L \cdot \Delta T \quad \text{(mm)}
$$

The factor **1.2** is a safety multiplier recommended by **SKF** and adopted in IS 4683 to account for bearing internal clearance and assembly tolerance.

### 2.3.2 Cross-Located Arrangement

Both bearings are **angular-contact** types (e.g., tapered roller or angular-contact ball bearings) mounted face-to-face (DF) or back-to-back (DB). This arrangement:
- Carries axial loads in **both directions**,
- Has a relatively large **effective spread** $a$, increasing the moment-carrying capacity,
- Is used in **gearboxes, pumps, and machine-tool spindles** where shaft deflection must be minimised.

### 2.3.3 Floating-Floating (Adjustable) Arrangement

Rarely used; both bearings are axially free. Used only when **inherent self-alignment** of the bearing is required (e.g., spherical roller bearings on a long, flexible shaft).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table consolidates **every formula a student is expected to reproduce** in a KTU ESE for this topic. Memorise the column-wise relationships.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | Thermal expansion of shaft | $\Delta L = \alpha_s \, L \, \Delta T$ | $\alpha_s = 11.6 \times 10^{-6}$ for steel | mm |
| 2 | Required axial float (free bearing) | $\delta_{ax} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \, L \, \Delta T$ | $L$ = span between bearings; $\Delta T$ = temp rise | mm |
| 3 | Resultant housing reaction | $R = \sqrt{R_r^{2} + R_a^{2}}$ | $R_r, R_a$ = radial, axial reactions | N |
| 4 | Vertical load on base | $R_v = R \cos\theta$ | $\theta$ = load angle from vertical | N |
| 5 | Horizontal load on base | $R_h = R \sin\theta$ | same as above | N |
| 6 | Bearing bore (metric series 02) | $d = \sqrt{\,C / P\,}$ (life factor 1) | $C$ = basic dynamic load, $P$ = equivalent load | mm |
| 7 | Housing bore tolerance | **H7** (ISO 286) | loose fit to permit ring expansion | $\mu$m |
| 8 | Shaft tolerance for inner ring | **k5** or **m5** (load rotates) | interference fit for rotating inner ring | $\mu$m |
| 9 | Effective bearing centre distance | $L_{eff} = L + \tfrac{a_1 + a_2}{2}$ | $a_i$ = distance from bearing centre to load point | mm |
| 10 | Cap bolt tightening torque (M10) | $T \approx 0.2 \, d \, F_{pre}$ | $d$ = bolt dia; $F_{pre}$ = preload (N) | N·m |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

The bearing housing-and-mounting system is a **ubiquitous subsystem** in:
- **Electric motors and gearboxes** (fixed–free with deep-groove ball + cylindrical roller).
- **Paper mill rolls** (cartridge units, water-resistant sealing).
- **Conveyor systems** (take-up housings for belt tensioning).
- **Wind turbine main shafts** (split-plummer blocks on multi-MW units).
- **Automotive wheel hubs** (integrated flanged hub units).

A poorly mounted bearing reduces **L10 fatigue life** by a factor of **2 to 10** — making correct mounting one of the highest-leverage design decisions in machine design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Required Axial Float for a Free Bearing

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3.

**Given:**
- Distance between the two bearing centres: $L$ (mm)
- Operating temperature rise of the shaft: $\Delta T$ (°C)
- Material: Steel ($\alpha_s = 11.6 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C)

**Step 1:** The free linear expansion of the shaft is, by the fundamental law of linear thermal expansion:

$$
\Delta L \;=\; \alpha_s \, L \, \Delta T
$$

**Step 2:** Substituting the steel coefficient:

$$
\Delta L \;=\; \left(11.6 \times 10^{-6}\right) \cdot L \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 3:** Factoring out the small coefficient for engineering convenience:

$$
\Delta L \;\approx\; 1.16 \times 10^{-5} \cdot L \cdot \Delta T \quad \text{(mm)}
$$

**Step 4:** Applying the manufacturer safety factor of **1.2** (to account for bearing internal clearance, assembly tolerance, and any additional clearances in the mounting):

$$
\delta_{ax,\text{required}} \;=\; 1.2 \times 1.16 \times 10^{-5} \cdot L \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\delta_{ax,\text{required}} \;\ge\; 1.4 \times 10^{-5} \cdot L \cdot \Delta T \quad \text{(mm)}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The free bearing's **axial clearance C** (available internal float) must be greater than or equal to $\delta_{ax,\text{required}}$. Otherwise, the shaft will build up compressive thermal stress and may bow, leading to premature bearing failure.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Housing Base Bolt Design

**Given:** Radial reaction at the bearing = $R_r$ (N), Axial reaction = $R_a$ (N), load angle = $\theta$ from vertical.

**Step 1 — Compute the resultant at the bearing centre:**

$$
R \;=\; \sqrt{R_r^{2} + R_a^{2}} \quad \text{(N)}
$$

**Step 2 — Resolve at the housing base into vertical $R_v$ and horizontal $R_h$:**

$$
R_v \;=\; R \cos\theta, \qquad R_h \;=\; R \sin\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the overturning moment about the bolt centre-line (lever arm $h$):**

$$
M_{ot} \;=\; R_h \cdot h
$$

where $h$ is the vertical distance from the base mounting plane to the shaft centre-line.

**Step 4 — Bolt tension due to direct shear and prying:**

For a housing mounted by **two bolts** symmetrically placed at a transverse pitch $b$:

$$
F_{bolt} \;\approx\; \frac{R_h}{2} \;+\; \frac{M_{ot}}{b} \quad \text{(per bolt, N)}
$$

**Step 5 — Check bolt shear and bearing stress:**

$$
\tau_{bolt} \;=\; \frac{4 \, F_{bolt}}{\pi \, d_b^{2}} \;\le\; \tau_{allow} \quad \text{(N/mm}^{2}\text{)}
$$

**Step 6 — Select standard bolt size** (preferred M8, M10, M12, M16, M20) such that the above inequality is satisfied, and apply a safety factor of **1.5 to 2.0** on the proof load.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Mounting Arrangements

> [!TIP]
> This is the **highest-yield comparison table** for the KTU ESE 14-mark question. Memorise the "Best For" column.

| Criterion | Fixed–Free (Locating–Non-locating) | Cross-Located (DB / DF) | Floating–Floating | Adjustable Spherical |
|-----------|-------------------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Axial load capacity** | One direction only (at fixed end) | Both directions | Neither | Both (via self-align) |
| **Shaft expansion accommodated** | Yes — by free bearing float | No — must be designed tight | Yes — by both floats | Yes — by self-alignment |
| **Moment stiffness** | Moderate | High (large effective span) | Low | Low |
| **Assembly tolerance sensitivity** | Low | High (preload critical) | Low | Low |
| **Typical bearings** | Deep-groove ball + cylindrical roller | Angular-contact ball / Tapered roller | Spherical roller | Self-aligning ball / spherical roller |
| **Best For (KTU answer)** | **General-purpose, gearboxes, motors** | Machine-tool spindles, gear pinions | Long flexible shafts | Misaligned shaft conditions |
| **KTU mark weightage** | 8–10 marks | 4–6 marks | 2 marks (definition) | 2 marks (definition) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU 2024 Scheme Style

**Problem:**
A shaft of length $L = 800$ mm between bearing centres is supported by a fixed–free mounting arrangement. The operating temperature rise is $\Delta T = 40$ °C. The shaft material is steel. Determine:
(i) the free thermal expansion of the shaft, and
(ii) the minimum axial float required in the free bearing.

**Solution:**

**Step (i):** Free thermal expansion:

$$
\Delta L \;=\; \alpha_s \, L \, \Delta T
$$

$$
\Delta L \;=\; (11.6 \times 10^{-6}) \times 800 \times 40
$$

$$
\Delta L \;=\; 0.3712 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step (ii):** Minimum axial float with the 1.2 safety factor:

$$
\delta_{ax} \;\ge\; 1.2 \times \Delta L
$$

$$
\delta_{ax} \;\ge\; 1.2 \times 0.3712
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\delta_{ax} \;\ge\; 0.445 \;\text{mm} \;\approx\; 0.45 \;\text{mm}\;}
$$

**Step (iii) — Bearing selection (verification):**

A **cylindrical roller bearing of series NU 310** has internal axial clearance of approximately **0.5 to 0.7 mm** in standard CN (normal) clearance class. Since $0.5 \;\text{mm} \;\ge\; 0.45 \;\text{mm}$, the selected bearing **satisfies** the thermal expansion requirement. ✓

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Standard Fits for Bearing Mounting

| Ring | Rotation relative to load | Recommended Shaft Fit | Recommended Housing Bore |
|------|----------------------------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Inner ring | Rotating | **k5** or **m5** (interference) | — |
| Inner ring | Stationary | **g6** or **h6** (loose) | — |
| Outer ring | Rotating | — | **M7** or **N7** (light interference) |
| Outer ring | Stationary | — | **H7** or **J7** (loose / transition) |
| Outer ring | Direction indeterminate | — | **H7** (most common, KTU default) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **ISO 286** fit system is **globally adopted**. In KTU answers, always quote the tolerance grade as a pair, e.g., "Shaft: k5, Housing: H7".
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Fixed–Free Mounting System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Torque from Coupling] --> B[Shaft Section L1]
    B --> C[Bearing 1: Fixed Deep Groove Ball Bearing]
    C --> D[Pillow Block Housing Cap]
    C --> E[Pillow Block Housing Base]
    E --> F[Foundation Bolts M10]
    B --> G[Shaft Section L2 - Free to Expand]
    G --> H[Bearing 2: Floating Cylindrical Roller NU Type]
    H --> I[Housing Allows Axial Sliding]
    I --> J[Output Pulley or Gear]

    C -.axial location.-> K[Locates Shaft in X and Z]
    H -.radial location only.-> L[Allows Thermal Expansion along X]
    D --- M[Top Cap Secured by 4 Bolts]
    E --- N[Oil Level Sight Glass]
    M --- O[Dowel Pins for Remountability]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Node A → B → C** traces the path of the input torque to the fixed bearing.
- **Nodes C and H** are the two bearing locations; notice the **dotted lines** (`.->`) indicate the kinematic constraint — `C` provides both radial AND axial location, while `H` provides **only radial** location, leaving the shaft free to slide.
- **Node I** is the key enabling element — the floating bearing's housing must permit axial displacement (typically achieved with a **clearance fit H7 on the outer ring**).

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Cross-Located Mounting (DB Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Shaft] --> B1[Angular Contact Ball Bearing 1]
    A1 --> C1[Angular Contact Ball Bearing 2]
    B1 --> D1[Load Line 1 - Diverging]
    C1 --> E1[Load Line 2 - Diverging]
    D1 --> F1[Effective Span = L + a1 + a2]
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[High Moment Stiffness]
    G1 --> H1[No Axial Float - No Thermal Expansion Tolerance]
    B1 -.-> I1[Preload Applied by Shim]
    C1 -.-> I1

    J1[Gearbox / Spindle Application] -.uses.-> B1
    J1 -.uses.-> C1
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Bearing Housing Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Top Cap] --> B1[Cap Bolts M8]
    B1 --> C1[Studs into Base]
    A1 --> D1[Bore for Outer Ring - H7]
    C1 --> E1[Base Pedestal]
    E1 --> F1[Base Bolt Holes M10]
    E1 --> G1[Dowel Pin Holes for Reassembly]
    E1 --> H1[Oil Sump - Lubricant Reservoir]
    H1 --> I1[Drain Plug]
    E1 --> J1[Oil Level Plug]
    E1 --> K1[Oil Sight Glass]
    E1 --> L1[Seal Groove on Both Sides]
    L1 --> M1[Lip Seal or Felt Seal]

    style A1 fill:#ffe6cc
    style E1 fill:#cce5ff
    style H1 fill:#d5e8d4
    style M1 fill:#f8cecc
```

**Colour code (interpretation):**
- **Amber (top cap):** The removable cover that allows bearing installation.
- **Blue (base):** The structural pedestal transferring load to foundation.
- **Green (oil sump):** The lubricant reservoir — critical for splash lubrication.
- **Red (seal):** The contamination barrier — common failure point if neglected.

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Selecting a Mounting Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[New Bearing Mounting Design] --> B1{Does shaft expand thermally?}
    B1 -- Yes --> C1{Is the span L greater than 500 mm?}
    B1 -- No --> D1[Use Cross Located DB Mounting]
    C1 -- Yes --> E1[Use Fixed Free Mounting with Cylindrical Roller as Free]
    C1 -- No --> F1[Either Fixed Free or Cross Located acceptable]
    E1 --> G1{Does the application have axial load in both directions?}
    G1 -- Yes --> H1[Use Paired Angular Contact as Fixed End]
    G1 -- No --> I1[Use Deep Groove Ball as Fixed End]
    F1 --> J1{Is moment stiffness critical?}
    J1 -- Yes --> K1[Choose Cross Located]
    J1 -- No --> L1[Choose Fixed Free - Simpler]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Distinguish between a fixed bearing and a floating bearing with a neat sketch."** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fixed (Locating) Bearing | Floating (Non-Locating) Bearing |
|--------|--------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Direction of constraint** | Constrains shaft in **both** radial and axial directions | Constrains shaft in **radial direction only** |
| **Axial load capacity** | Yes — can carry axial thrust | **No** — purely radial load carrier |
| **Typical type** | Deep-groove ball bearing (paired) or angular-contact bearing | Cylindrical roller bearing (NU or N type) |
| **Position in system** | Placed at one end of the shaft span | Placed at the other end of the shaft span |
| **Function** | Provides axial location of the shaft | Permits **thermal expansion** of the shaft |

**Sketch (textual):** *(A neat diagram with the fixed bearing shown locking the shaft axially with arrows from both sides, and the floating bearing shown with clearance gap `δ` on one axial side.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"State any three standard materials used for bearing housings and the relevant Indian Standard."** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
1. **Grey Cast Iron (Grade FG 200 / FG 220)** — Most common; good damping and machinability.
2. **Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron** — Used in modern heavy-duty housings for higher tensile strength (≥ 400 MPa).
3. **Cast Steel (Grade 230-450)** — Used where impact loads are significant.

**Indian Standard:** **IS 4683: Part 1** governs the design of plummer blocks for rolling bearings.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below; examinees answer **one**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a Plummer Block (Pedestal-type bearing housing)**. List the functions of any **four** components. (7 marks)

**(b)** A line shaft of length $L = 1200$ mm between two bearings is supported by a **fixed–free mounting arrangement**. The shaft is made of steel and the temperature rise during operation is $\Delta T = 50$ °C. Determine:
- (i) The free thermal expansion of the shaft.
- (ii) The minimum axial clearance required in the floating bearing.
- (iii) Justify whether a **NU 312** cylindrical roller bearing (internal axial clearance = 0.6 mm, CN class) is suitable as the floating bearing. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction of a Plummer Block:**

A plummer block consists of two main castings — a **base (pedestal)** and a **cap (top cover)** — joined together by **two through-bolts (or studs + nuts)** at the parting plane. The base is bolted to the foundation, and the cap carries the upper half of the bearing outer ring seat.

**Components and Functions:**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Cap (top cover)** | Encloses the upper half of the bearing; allows access for inspection and assembly. |
| **Base (pedestal)** | Houses the lower half of the bearing; transfers reaction load to the foundation. |
| **Bore (machined seat)** | Locates the bearing outer ring with a clearance fit (typically **H7**). |
| **Dowel pins** | Ensure cap and base are re-assembled in the **exact same position**, preserving bore roundness. |
| **Oil sump** | Reservoir for lubricant; provides splash lubrication at low shaft speeds. |
| **Seals (lip / felt)** | Prevent lubricant leakage and ingress of contaminants. |
| **Filling / drain plugs** | Allow lubricant replenishment and replacement. |
| **Mounting bolts** | Anchor the housing to the foundation; carry the reaction loads. |

**Working:** The bearing's outer ring is located in the housing bore with a small clearance fit, allowing the ring to expand slightly under load without inducing hoop stress. The shaft, mounted in the inner ring with an interference fit (**k5**), transmits the load to the inner ring → rolling elements → outer ring → housing bore → base → foundation. The cap simply retains the upper half of the lubricant and outer ring — it is **not** a primary load path.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $L = 1200$ mm
- $\Delta T = 50$ °C
- Material: Steel, $\alpha_s = 11.6 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C
- Bearing candidate: NU 312, internal axial clearance $C_a = 0.6$ mm (CN class)

**Step (i) — Free thermal expansion:**

$$
\Delta L = \alpha_s \, L \, \Delta T
$$

$$
\Delta L = (11.6 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1200 \times 50
$$

$$
\Delta L = 0.696 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step (ii) — Required minimum axial float (with 1.2 safety factor):**

$$
\delta_{ax,\text{req}} = 1.2 \times \Delta L = 1.2 \times 0.696
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_{ax,\text{req}} = 0.835 \;\text{mm}}
$$

**Step (iii) — Justification:**

Comparing with the bearing's available internal clearance $C_a = 0.6$ mm:

$$
C_a = 0.6 \;\text{mm} \;<\; \delta_{ax,\text{req}} = 0.835 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** The **NU 312 in CN clearance is NOT suitable.** The bearing will be axially overloaded once the shaft expands, generating destructive thermal compressive stress.

**Recommendation:** Use the **C3 (greater than normal) clearance class** of NU 312, which provides $C_a \approx 0.9$ to $1.1$ mm, satisfying the requirement. Alternatively, select the next larger series (NU 313) in CN clearance.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**
**(a)** Compare the **fixed–free** and **cross-located (DB)** bearing mounting arrangements under the following heads: axial load capacity, accommodation of thermal expansion, moment stiffness, typical applications, and standard bearings used. (7 marks)

**(b)** A pillow block supports a deep-groove ball bearing 6310 subjected to a radial load $F_r = 12\,000$ N and an axial load $F_a = 4000$ N. The load makes an angle of 30° with the vertical. Calculate:
- (i) The resultant reaction at the bearing.
- (ii) The vertical and horizontal components of the reaction transferred to the housing base.
- (iii) The overturning moment if the shaft centre-line is 100 mm above the foundation plane. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **comparison matrix** is given in Section 3.3 above. The student must reproduce the 6-row table with precise terminology. Key points to score full marks:

- **Axial load capacity:** Fixed-free → one direction only; Cross-located → both directions.
- **Thermal expansion:** Fixed-free → accommodated by free bearing; Cross-located → no accommodation, shaft must be short or preloaded design must account for it.
- **Moment stiffness:** Cross-located has a **larger effective spread**, giving higher moment stiffness — this is the **key reason** for using DB mounting in machine-tool spindles.
- **Typical applications:** Fixed-free → electric motors, gearboxes; Cross-located → machine-tool spindles, gear pinion shafts.
- **Bearings:** Fixed-free → deep-groove ball + cylindrical roller; Cross-located → paired angular-contact ball or tapered roller.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $F_r = 12\,000$ N (radial), $F_a = 4000$ N (axial)
- Load angle $\theta = 30°$ from vertical
- Shaft height above foundation: $h = 100$ mm

**Step (i) — Resultant reaction:**

$$
R = \sqrt{F_r^{2} + F_a^{2}}
$$

$$
R = \sqrt{(12\,000)^{2} + (4\,000)^{2}}
$$

$$
R = \sqrt{1.44 \times 10^{8} + 0.16 \times 10^{8}}
$$

$$
R = \sqrt{1.6 \times 10^{8}}
$$

$$
\boxed{R = 12\,649 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 12.65 \;\text{kN}}
$$

**Step (ii) — Components at the base:**

$$
R_v = R \cos\theta = 12\,649 \times \cos 30°
$$

$$
R_v = 12\,649 \times 0.866
$$

$$
\boxed{R_v = 10\,954 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 10.95 \;\text{kN}}
$$

$$
R_h = R \sin\theta = 12\,649 \times \sin 30°
$$

$$
R_h = 12\,649 \times 0.5
$$

$$
\boxed{R_h = 6\,325 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 6.32 \;\text{kN}}
$$

**Step (iii) — Overturning moment:**

The horizontal component $R_h$ acts at the shaft centre, 100 mm above the foundation plane, creating an overturning moment about the base bolt line:

$$
M_{ot} = R_h \times h
$$

$$
M_{ot} = 6\,325 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{ot} = 632\,500 \;\text{N·mm} \;=\; 632.5 \;\text{N·m}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design implication:** If the housing uses **two foundation bolts** at a transverse pitch of 180 mm, each bolt must resist:
> - A direct shear = $R_h / 2 = 3.16$ kN, and
> - A prying tension = $M_{ot} / 180 = 3.51$ kN.
> Hence, **M12 grade 8.8 bolts** (proof load ≈ 47 kN) are appropriate with a safety factor > 10.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.2 safety factor** in the thermal expansion calculation. Marks are split: $\Delta L$ (3 marks), $\delta_{ax}$ (4 marks). Skipping the safety factor costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the shaft fit and housing fit.** The inner ring is on the shaft (interference fit **k5/m5** if rotating), the outer ring is in the housing (clearance fit **H7**). Reversing these is a fatal error.
> 3. **Not drawing a neat sketch** in the plummer block question. KTU examiners explicitly award 2 marks for the labelled sketch.
> 4. **Using $\Delta T$ in Kelvin** in thermal expansion. The formula uses the **difference** in temperature, so °C and K are numerically equivalent — but write °C to be safe.
> 5. **Forgetting to check the bearing's actual internal clearance** against the required float in the verification step. KTU loves a "justify your selection" sub-question.
> 6. **Mixing up "DB" and "DF"** in cross-located arrangements. DB (back-to-back) gives a **larger effective spread** (good for moment stiffness); DF (face-to-face) gives a **smaller effective spread** (good for compactness).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before the Exam):**
>
> - [x] **Bearing housing = load-bearing structure** that locates the bearing, retains lubricant, and transfers reaction to foundation.
> - [x] **Plummer Block** = most common pedestal-type housing; **SN/SNA/SAF** are SKF series; **IS 4683** is the Indian Standard.
> - [x] **Fixed bearing** = locates shaft axially; **Free bearing** = allows axial expansion (use **NU/N-type cylindrical roller**).
> - [x] **Thermal expansion formula:** $\Delta L = 1.16 \times 10^{-5} \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$; **Required float** = $1.2 \times \Delta L$.
> - [x] **Resultant load:** $R = \sqrt{R_r^{2} + R_a^{2}}$; **Components** resolved at base using $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$.
> - [x] **Standard fits:** Shaft–inner ring = **k5** (rotating) or **g6** (stationary); Housing–outer ring = **H7** (most common).
> - [x] **Cross-located (DB)** mounting = high moment stiffness, no thermal expansion tolerance; use for spindles.
> - [x] **Fixed–free** mounting = accommodates thermal expansion, simpler, used in **~80%** of industrial applications.
> - [x] **Cap and base** must be dowelled to preserve bore geometry across disassembly cycles.
> - [x] **Seals** (lip / felt / labyrinth) are the most commonly **replaced** components in a housing; design for easy access.
> - [x] **Overturning moment** on housing base = $R_h \times h$; design bolts to resist direct shear + prying tension.
> - [x] **Materials:** Cast iron (most common) → ductile iron → cast steel (heavy duty) → stainless (corrosive).
> - [x] **Indian Standards:** IS 4683 (plummer blocks), IS 6455 (rolling bearing nomenclature), ISO 15 (radial bearing boundary dimensions).
> - [x] **KTU favourite question pattern:** "Calculate thermal expansion + check bearing suitability" (combined 7-mark sub-question).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rolling Contact Bearings: Bearing Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rolling Contact Bearings: Bearing Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Rolling Contact Bearing** (also called an **Anti-friction Bearing**) is a machine element that constrains relative motion between two parts and reduces friction by transmitting loads through the rolling motion of hardened balls or rollers placed between two raceways. Unlike sliding-contact bearings, rolling-contact bearings convert sliding friction into rolling friction, dramatically reducing energy losses and wear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim):**
> *"Rolling contact bearings are machine elements used to support a shaft or other rotating member and to permit free rotation with minimum friction. They transmit loads between rotating and stationary members through rolling elements such as balls or rollers."*

In any bearing system, four fundamental components are always present:
1. **Inner Race** – mounted on the shaft (rotates with it).
2. **Outer Race** – mounted in the housing (stationary).
3. **Rolling Elements** – balls or rollers that transmit load.
4. **Cage (Retainer)** – separates and spaces the rolling elements uniformly.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Barrel Roll" Analogy:**
> Imagine you must move a heavy steel barrel across a warehouse floor.
> - **Without bearings (sliding):** You must overcome the immense kinetic friction of the barrel scraping against the floor. It requires huge force, generates heat, and wears out both the floor and the barrel.
> - **With bearings (rolling):** You place the barrel on a set of steel pipes (rollers). Now the barrel *rolls* on the pipes, which themselves *roll* on the floor. Friction drops by an order of magnitude.
>
> *This is precisely what a rolling bearing does at the microscopic scale — it converts destructive sliding friction into gentle rolling friction.*

Another intuitive analogy is a **shopping cart wheel**: the cart rolls smoothly because tiny ball bearings inside the wheel hub prevent the axle from scraping against the wheel frame.

---

## 1.3 Why Rolling Contact Bearings? — Engineering Justification

| Property | Sliding Bearing | Rolling Bearing |
|---|---|---|
| Coefficient of friction ($\mu$) | 0.05 – 0.20 | 0.001 – 0.005 |
| Starting friction | High (≈ kinetic) | Negligible |
| Heat generation | High | Very Low |
| Lubrication requirement | Continuous | Periodic / Grease-packed |
| Speed capability | Limited | High (up to 1,00,000 rpm) |
| Radial/axial load | Both, but axial limited | Both, depending on type |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Constant to Remember:**
> The coefficient of friction for rolling bearings is approximately **0.0025** (a value KTU examiners expect you to recall for Part A questions).

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold-Highlighted)

- **ISO 15 / IS 6455** – standard dimensional series for rolling bearings.
- **Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C)** – the constant radial (or axial, for thrust) load that a bearing can withstand for **1 million (10⁶) revolutions** without fatigue failure.
- **Basic Static Load Rating (C₀)** – the static load that produces a permanent deformation of **0.0001 times the ball diameter** at the most heavily loaded contact point.
- **Life Exponent (p)** – equals **3 for ball bearings** and **10/3 ≈ 3.33 for roller bearings**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

Rolling contact bearings are classified along two orthogonal axes:

**Axis 1 — By Rolling Element Geometry:**
- **Ball Bearings** → spherical rolling elements (point contact)
- **Roller Bearings** → cylindrical/tapered/needle elements (line contact)

**Axis 2 — By Load Direction Supported:**
- **Radial Bearings** → primarily support radial (perpendicular to shaft) loads.
- **Thrust (Axial) Bearings** → primarily support axial (along shaft) loads.
- **Angular Contact Bearings** → support combined radial + axial loads.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Bearing Type Catalog (KTU-Exam-Relevant)

### 2.2.1 Ball Bearings

**(a) Deep-Groove Ball Bearing (Conrad type)**
- Most common, simplest design.
- Raceway has deep grooves → accommodates both radial and small axial loads **in either direction**.
- Non-separable; suitable for high speeds.
- *Application:* Electric motors, gearboxes, pumps.

**(b) Angular Contact Ball Bearing**
- Raceways offset → contact line forms an angle $\alpha$ (typically **15°, 25°, or 40°**).
- Can support combined radial + axial loads; higher axial capacity than deep-groove.
- Must be mounted in pairs (back-to-back DB, face-to-face DF, tandem DT).
- *Application:* Machine tool spindles, automotive wheel hubs.

**(c) Self-Aligning Ball Bearing**
- Outer race has a spherical (concave) raceway; two rows of balls share a common spherical race.
- Tolerates **shaft misalignment up to 2–3°**.
- *Application:* Long shafts prone to deflection (e.g., transmission shafts).

**(d) Thrust Ball Bearing**
- Designed for **pure axial loads**.
- Two flat washers (shaft washer + housing washer) with balls between them.
- *Limitation:* Cannot support radial loads → fails under combined loads.
- *Application:* Vertical shaft supports (e.g., turntable bearings).

---

### 2.2.2 Roller Bearings

**(a) Cylindrical Roller Bearing**
- Line contact instead of point contact → **higher radial load capacity** than same-size ball bearing.
- Designs: NU, N, NUP (with/without flanges).
- *Application:* Heavy machinery, gearboxes, railway axles.

**(b) Needle Roller Bearing**
- Cylindrical rollers with **length-to-diameter ratio ≥ 4:1** (typically 5:1 to 10:1).
- Compact radial cross-section → used where space is severely restricted.
- *Application:* Connecting rod big-ends in small engines, rocker arms.

**(c) Tapered Roller Bearing**
- Rollers and raceways are conical; rollers converge at a point on the bearing axis.
- Supports **combined radial + axial (thrust) loads simultaneously**.
- Mounted in opposing pairs.
- *Application:* Vehicle differentials, automotive wheels, gear reducers.

**(d) Spherical (Barrel) Roller Bearing**
- Barrel-shaped rollers running on a common spherical raceway.
- **Self-aligning** property; tolerates misalignment.
- Very high radial load capacity.
- *Application:* Heavy industrial gearboxes, paper mills, marine propellers.

---

## 2.3 Comparison Matrix — Selection Guide

| Bearing Type | Load Direction | Load Magnitude | Speed | Misalignment Tolerance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Deep-groove ball | Radial + small axial | Medium | High | Low (~0.07°) |
| Angular contact ball | Radial + axial | Medium-High | High | Low |
| Self-aligning ball | Radial + small axial | Medium | Medium-High | **High (2–3°)** |
| Thrust ball | **Axial only** | Medium | Low | None |
| Cylindrical roller | Radial (mostly) | **High** | High | Low |
| Needle roller | Radial | High (small size) | Medium | Low |
| Tapered roller | Radial + axial | **Very High** | Medium | Low |
| Spherical roller | Radial (mostly) + axial | **Very High** | Medium | **High (1.5°)** |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are core to every Module-3 numerical problem in the KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p \times 10^6$ revolutions | **Fundamental bearing life equation** (ISO/IS 6455). $C$ = dynamic load rating, $P$ = equivalent dynamic load, $p=3$ (ball) or $p=10/3$ (roller) |
| 2 | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ hours | Life in operating hours; $n$ = rpm |
| 3 | $C = P \cdot \left(\dfrac{L_{10h} \cdot 60 \cdot n}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$ | **Required dynamic load rating** (used for bearing selection) |
| 4 | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | Equivalent dynamic load; $X, Y$ from manufacturer tables |
| 5 | $P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a$ | Equivalent static load |
| 6 | $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \le e$ ⇒ pure radial case | If axial-to-radial ratio is small, set $X=1, Y=0$ |
| 7 | $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} > e$ ⇒ combined case | Use full $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ |
| 8 | $S_0 = \dfrac{C_0}{P_0}$ | Static safety factor (typically 1.0 to 2.5) |
| 9 | $C_0 = i \cdot z \cdot D_w^2 \cdot k$ | Theoretical static load (for understanding only; not in exams) |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Rolling bearings are the **unsung heroes of modern industry**:
- **Automotive:** A single car contains 100–150 bearings (wheels, transmission, engine, alternator).
- **Aerospace:** Gas turbine engines use hybrid ceramic ball bearings at >25,000 rpm.
- **Wind Energy:** Main shaft spherical roller bearings must survive 20-year service life under cyclic gust loads.
- **Robotics:** Miniature ball bearings enable harmonic-drive reducers with sub-arcminute backlash.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU exam frequently tests **bearing-type identification from a sketch** and **justification of selection** — not just formula recall. Memorize the "shape" of each bearing family.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Bearing Life Equation

The KTU ESE expects you to be able to **derive the L10 life equation** from the basic fatigue model. Here is the complete, exhaustive derivation.

### Step 1 — Fatigue Failure Hypothesis (Lundberg–Palmgren Model)

Under Hertzian contact stress, subsurface material fails by rolling-contact fatigue. The empirical fatigue strength relation is:

$$\tau_0 = z \cdot \sigma_s$$

Where:
- $\tau_0$ = maximum subsurface shear stress (the driver of fatigue crack initiation).
- $z$ = depth factor (≈ 0.5 for line contact, depends on geometry).
- $\sigma_s$ = fatigue strength of the material at $N$ stress cycles.

The fatigue strength follows the **inverse power law** (Wöhler-type curve):

$$\sigma_s = \sigma_{s0} \cdot N^{-1/e}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_{s0}$ = fatigue strength at $N=1$ cycle.
- $e$ = fatigue exponent (typically **10/3 for Hertzian contact**, derived from elasticity theory).

### Step 2 — Linking Stress to Load

For Hertzian contact, the shear stress $\tau_0$ is proportional to load $P$ raised to a fractional power:

$$\tau_0 \propto P^{k}$$

For ball bearings (point contact): $k = 1/3$
For roller bearings (line contact): $k = 1/2$

### Step 3 — Combining Stress and Fatigue Equations

Set $\tau_0 = \sigma_s$ at the failure point $N = L$:

$$P^{k} \cdot C_1 = \sigma_{s0} \cdot L^{-1/e}$$

Where $C_1$ is a constant. Solving for $L$:

$$L = C_2 \cdot P^{-k \cdot e}$$

For ball bearings: $k \cdot e = (1/3)(10/3) = 10/9 \cdot ... \approx 3$ (after empirical correction).
For roller bearings: $k \cdot e \approx 10/3$.

This yields the **universal Weibull-exponent-corrected form**:

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p \times 10^6 \text{ revolutions}$$

### Step 4 — Final Form in Hours

If the shaft rotates at $n$ rpm, the number of revolutions per hour is $60n$. Therefore:

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10}}{60 \cdot n}$$

Substituting:

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p \text{ hours}$$

This is the **workhorse formula** for every Module-3 numerical.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **This worked example illustrates how the formulas are applied — KTU examiners expect this exact algebraic flow.**

**Problem:**
A deep-groove ball bearing carries a radial load $F_r = 4000$ N and an axial load $F_a = 1200$ N at $n = 1450$ rpm. From the manufacturer's catalogue: $C = 33500$ N, $e = 0.30$, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.40$ (when $F_a/F_r > e$). Determine the bearing life in hours.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Check the loading regime:**

$$\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = \dfrac{1200}{4000} = 0.30$$

Compare to $e = 0.30$. Since $F_a/F_r \ge e$, we are in the **combined load regime**.

**Step 2 — Calculate equivalent dynamic load $P$:**

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

Substituting the values:

$$P = 0.56 \cdot 4000 + 1.40 \cdot 1200$$

$$P = 2240 + 1680$$

$$P = 3920 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the life equation** (ball bearing, so $p = 3$):

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot 1450} \cdot \left(\dfrac{33500}{3920}\right)^3$$

Evaluate the load ratio:

$$\dfrac{33500}{3920} = 8.546$$

Raise to power 3:

$$8.546^3 = 624.3$$

Compute the time constant:

$$\dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot 1450} = \dfrac{10^6}{87000} = 11.49 \text{ hours}$$

Final answer:

$$L_{10h} = 11.49 \times 624.3 = 7175 \text{ hours} \approx 0.82 \text{ years of continuous operation}$$

---

## 3.3 Reverse Problem — Finding Required $C$

Rearranging the life equation to find the required dynamic load rating:

$$C = P \cdot \left(\dfrac{L_{10h} \cdot 60 \cdot n}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$$

**Verification with the example:** If the desired life is **20,000 hours** at the same load:

$$C_{\text{req}} = 3920 \cdot \left(\dfrac{20000 \cdot 60 \cdot 1450}{10^6}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$\dfrac{20000 \cdot 60 \cdot 1450}{10^6} = 1740$$

$$1740^{1/3} = 12.03$$

$$C_{\text{req}} = 3920 \cdot 12.03 = 47158 \text{ N}$$

You would now select a bearing with **$C \ge 47158$ N** from the manufacturer's catalogue (e.g., a 6312-series deep-groove ball bearing with $C = 50000$ N).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rolling Contact Bearings] --> B{By Rolling Element}
    B --> C[Ball Bearings]
    B --> D[Roller Bearings]
    C --> E[Deep Groove]
    C --> F[Angular Contact]
    C --> G[Self Aligning]
    C --> H[Thrust Ball]
    D --> I[Cylindrical]
    D --> J[Needle]
    D --> K[Tapered]
    D --> L[Spherical Barrel]
    E --> M{Radial plus small axial}
    F --> N{Combined radial plus axial}
    G --> O{Shaft misalignment}
    H --> P{Pure axial only}
    I --> Q{High radial no axial}
    J --> R{Compact radial section}
    K --> S{High combined radial axial}
    L --> T{Heavy radial plus misalignment}
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Rolling Bearing

```mermaid
block-beta
    block:OuterRace["Outer Race - Mounted in Housing"]
    block:BallA["Ball A"]
    block:BallB["Ball B"]
    block:BallC["Ball C"]
    block:BallD["Ball D"]
    block:CageID["Cage Retainer"]
    block:InnerRace["Inner Race - Mounted on Shaft"]
    OuterRace --> BallA
    OuterRace --> BallB
    OuterRace --> BallC
    OuterRace --> BallD
    BallA --> CageID
    BallB --> CageID
    BallC --> CageID
    BallD --> CageID
    CageID --> InnerRace
```

## 4.3 Load-Vector Schematic — Direction of Supported Loads

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Applied Load] --> M{Type of Bearing}
    M --> R1[Radial Bearing - Supports Fr perpendicular to shaft]
    M --> R2[Thrust Bearing - Supports Fa along shaft axis]
    M --> R3[Angular Bearing - Supports combined Fr plus Fa at angle alpha]
    R1 --> Out1[Ball or Roller positioned between races]
    R2 --> Out2[Ball between two flat washers]
    R3 --> Out3[Raceway offset produces contact angle]
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Deep-Groove Ball Bearing Cross-Section

```
        ┌───────────────────────────┐  ← Outer Race
        │  ●     ●     ●     ●      │  ← Balls (4 shown of 8–12)
        │   ●     ●     ●     ●     │
        │  ●     ●     ●     ●      │  ← Cage pockets between balls
        └───────────────────────────┘
        ┌───────────────────────────┐
        │  ●     ●     ●     ●      │  ← Inner Race (pressed on shaft)
        │   ●     ●     ●     ●     │
        │  ●     ●     ●     ●      │
        └───────────────────────────┘
              ↑
              │ Shaft axis (rotation)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The deep-groove raceway curvature radius is approximately **52% of the ball diameter** — a KTU-favorite numerical fact.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
Differentiate between **deep-groove ball bearing** and **angular contact ball bearing** in terms of construction and load-carrying capacity.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Deep-Groove Ball Bearing | Angular Contact Ball Bearing |
|---|---|---|
| Raceway geometry | Symmetric grooves, raceway shoulder same on both sides | Raceways offset, one shoulder relieved |
| Contact angle $\alpha$ | ≈ 0° (radial contact) | 15°, 25°, or 40° |
| Load direction | Radial + small axial (both directions) | Radial + axial in one direction only |
| Mounting | Single or paired | Always in pairs (DB / DF / DT) |
| Axial capacity | Limited | High |
| Application | Motors, pumps | Machine-tool spindles |

**Marks Distribution (KTU Key):** Definition 1M, table comparison 1.5M, application 0.5M.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
State the significance of the **life exponent $p$** in the bearing life equation. Why is it different for ball and roller bearings?

**Model Answer:**

The life exponent $p$ appears in $L_{10} = (C/P)^p \times 10^6$. It accounts for the **type of contact** between rolling element and raceway:
- For **ball bearings**: $p = 3$ — because contact is **point contact**, Hertzian stress scales as $P^{1/3}$.
- For **roller bearings**: $p = 10/3 \approx 3.33$ — because contact is **line contact**, Hertzian stress scales as $P^{1/2}$, and combined with the fatigue exponent ($e = 10/3$) it yields $p = 10/3$.

Physically: roller bearings have **higher load capacity** and **longer life** than ball bearings of the same size, all else being equal.

**Marks Distribution:** Equation 1M, $p = 3$ ball 1M, $p = 10/3$ roller with reasoning 1M.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Module 3 — Type-Identification & Selection)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **tapered roller bearing**. Why must it be mounted in pairs? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A deep-groove ball bearing has a dynamic load rating $C = 45{,}000$ N. It is subjected to a radial load $F_r = 3500$ N and axial load $F_a = 700$ N at $n = 960$ rpm. The bearing factors are $e = 0.22$, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.95$ (when $F_a/F_r > e$); otherwise $X = 1, Y = 0$. Calculate the expected bearing life in hours. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction:**
- Each rolling element is a **truncated cone (frustum)** whose axis makes an angle with the shaft axis. 
- The **cone (inner race)** is press-fitted on the shaft.
- The **cup (outer race)** sits in the housing.
- The rollers are separated by a cage.
- The lines of contact of all rollers converge on the bearing axis → force vectors add algebraically.

**Working / Load Path:**
- Radial load $F_r$ → transmitted through rollers → resolved into radial + axial components at the contact angle.
- The axial component must be **counteracted by an external force** (from the mating bearing in the pair), otherwise the rollers will climb out of the raceway.

**Why paired mounting?**
- Tapered bearings can carry axial load **in only one direction**.
- A second bearing (with its cone facing the opposite way) provides the reaction force.
- Three standard arrangements: **Direct Mount (X / back-to-back)**, **Indirect Mount (O / face-to-face)**, **Tandem**.

**Sketch (described):** A trapezoidal cross-section showing inner race with conical rollers converging on the axis, enclosed by an outer race with matching conical raceway.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Check the regime:**

$$\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = \dfrac{700}{3500} = 0.20$$

Since $0.20 < e = 0.22$, we are in the **pure radial regime** (with $X = 1, Y = 0$).

**Step 2 — Compute equivalent load:**

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a = 1 \times 3500 + 0 \times 700 = 3500 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute life in revolutions** (ball bearing, $p = 3$):

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^3 \times 10^6$$

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{45000}{3500}\right)^3 \times 10^6$$

$$\dfrac{45000}{3500} = 12.857$$

$$12.857^3 = 2126.2$$

$$L_{10} = 2126.2 \times 10^6 \text{ revolutions}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours:**

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10}}{60 \cdot n} = \dfrac{2126.2 \times 10^6}{60 \times 960}$$

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{2.126 \times 10^9}{57600} = 36{,}910 \text{ hours}$$

**Answer:** $L_{10h} \approx \mathbf{36{,}910 \text{ hours}}$ (≈ 4.2 years of continuous duty).

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]`**

**(a)** Explain the construction of a **spherical roller bearing** with a neat sketch. State two applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical roller bearing has $C = 80{,}000$ N and operates at $n = 1500$ rpm under a pure radial load of $F_r = 5500$ N. Compute the bearing life in hours. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction:**
- Two rows of **barrel-shaped (convex) rollers** run on a **common spherical raceway** in the outer ring.
- Inner race has two separate raceways (one for each roller row), inclined slightly.
- Because rollers are crowned/barrel-shaped and the outer race is spherical, the rollers can **swivel** to accommodate misalignment.
- A single-piece or two-piece machined cage retains the rollers.

**Sketch description:** Barrel-shaped rollers between two inclined inner-race raceways, all rolling against one concave spherical outer-race raceway.

**Working Principle:**
- Line contact → high radial load capacity.
- Spherical outer race → tolerance to **shaft misalignment up to 1.5°**.
- Some designs (e.g., E-type) also accept moderate axial loads.

**Applications (any two):**
1. **Paper mill rolls** — heavy radial load + shaft deflection.
2. **Marine propeller shaft supports** — shock loads + misalignment.
3. **Crushing equipment** (jaw crushers, vibrating screens).
4. **Wind turbine main shafts.**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Pure radial load, so $P = F_r = 5500$ N.**

**Step 2 — Apply the life equation** (roller bearing, $p = 10/3$):

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3} \times 10^6 \text{ revolutions}$$

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{80000}{5500}\right)^{10/3} \times 10^6$$

$$\dfrac{80000}{5500} = 14.545$$

$$14.545^{10/3} = 14.545^{3.333}$$

Compute: $\ln(14.545) = 2.677$
Multiply: $2.677 \times 3.333 = 8.923$
Exponentiate: $e^{8.923} = 7540$

$$L_{10} = 7540 \times 10^6 \text{ revolutions}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hours:**

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{7540 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1500} = \dfrac{7.54 \times 10^9}{90000}$$

$$L_{10h} = 83{,}777 \text{ hours} \approx \mathbf{83{,}800 \text{ hours}}$$

This is ≈ 9.6 years of continuous operation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the regime check:** Many students blindly apply $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ without first computing $F_a/F_r$ and comparing to $e$. If $F_a/F_r \le e$, you **MUST** use $X=1, Y=0$. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Using $p = 3$ for roller bearings:** This is the most frequent error. Roller bearings use $p = 10/3 \approx 3.33$. Loss: **entire numerical is wrong**.
>
> 3. **Confusing L10 with L50:** $L_{10}$ is the life at which **90% of bearings survive**. $L_{50}$ (median life) is approximately $5 \times L_{10}$. KTU always asks for $L_{10}$. Loss: **2 marks** if definition is wrong.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units:** Mixing rpm with revolutions. Always state your units. Loss: **1 mark** (presentation).
>
> 5. **Not sketching for 7-mark descriptive questions:** KTU mandates a **neat, labelled sketch** for any 7+ mark construction question. A 100-word text description **without a sketch** caps marks at **3/7**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bearing** = rolling-element machine element that converts sliding friction to rolling friction; $\mu \approx 0.0025$.
- **Four components:** inner race, outer race, rolling element, cage.
- **Classification axes:** rolling element type (ball/roller) × load direction (radial/axial).
- **Ball bearing types:** Deep-groove, Angular contact, Self-aligning, Thrust.
- **Roller bearing types:** Cylindrical, Needle, Tapered, Spherical.
- **Key formulas:**
  - $L_{10} = (C/P)^p \times 10^6$ revolutions
  - $L_{10h} = (10^6 / 60n) \times (C/P)^p$ hours
  - $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ (combined), $P = F_r$ (pure radial)
  - $p = 3$ for ball, $p = 10/3$ for roller.
- **Regime rule:** If $F_a/F_r \le e$ → use $X=1, Y=0$.
- **Selection logic:** Radial load + high speed → deep-groove ball or cylindrical roller. Combined load → angular contact or tapered roller. Misalignment → self-aligning ball or spherical roller. Pure axial → thrust ball or thrust roller.
- **Static vs Dynamic:** $C$ governs fatigue life (rotating case); $C_0$ governs permanent deformation (stationary case).
- **ISO/IS standards:** IS 6455, ISO 15 — used for dimensional interchangeability.
- **Typical KTU question pattern:** 3-mark "state-and-explain", 7-mark "construction + sketch", 7-mark numerical life calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ball and Roller Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ball and Roller Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **bearing** is a machine element that constrains relative motion and reduces friction between moving parts, typically allowing a shaft to rotate smoothly about a fixed axis. **Ball and roller bearings** are a class of **rolling-contact bearings** (also called **antifriction bearings**) in which the load is transmitted from one raceway to another through rolling elements (balls or rollers), as opposed to sliding-contact (journal) bearings where the load is carried by a continuous film of lubricant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *rolling contact bearing* is a bearing in which the working surfaces are so formed that during relative motion only rolling contact occurs between the surfaces (balls rolling on raceways), and sliding contact is reduced to a negligible minimum.

### 1.2 Classification of Bearings

The formal hierarchy of bearings as per KTU Machine Design syllabus:

```
BEARINGS
├── 1. Sliding / Plain Contact Bearings
│       └── Journal / Sleeve Bearings
└── 2. Rolling Contact / Antifriction Bearings
        ├── A. Ball Bearings
        │       ├── Deep Groove Ball Bearing
        │       ├── Angular Contact Ball Bearing
        │       └── Self-Aligning Ball Bearing
        └── B. Roller Bearings
                ├── Cylindrical Roller Bearing
                ├── Tapered Roller Bearing
                ├── Needle Roller Bearing
                └── Spherical Roller Bearing
```

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Marble Between Two Plates" Intuition

Imagine you are sliding a heavy steel plate across a wooden floor. The friction is enormous — you must push very hard. Now, place a row of marbles between the plate and the floor. Suddenly, the plate glides almost effortlessly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** The marbles convert *sliding friction* into *rolling friction*. Because the contact between a marble and a flat surface is theoretically a single point (zero area), the resistance is drastically reduced. In an actual bearing, the rolling element (ball or roller) is pressed between two precisely hardened rings called **inner race** and **outer race**. The balls spin without slipping, so energy loss is minimal — typically 10 to 100 times less than a comparable plain bearing.

### 1.4 Standard Terminology Used in Bearing Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Glossary (must memorize):**
>
> - **Inner Race / Inner Ring:** Pressed onto the rotating shaft.
> - **Outer Race / Outer Ring:** Stationary, seated in the housing.
> - **Rolling Element:** The ball or roller that transmits load.
> - **Cage / Retainer:** A non-contacting separator that spaces the rolling elements uniformly around the raceway.
> - **Radial Load (Fr):** Load perpendicular to the shaft axis.
> - **Thrust / Axial Load (Fa):** Load parallel to the shaft axis.
