> - **Dynamic Load Rating (C):** The constant radial load that a bearing can theoretically endure for **1 million revolutions** without developing surface fatigue.
> - **Static Load Rating (Co):** The maximum load a stationary bearing can sustain without permanent brinelling of the raceway.
> - **Pitch Diameter (Dp):** The mean diameter of the bearing (between ball centers).
> - **Ball/Roller Diameter (D):** The diameter of a single rolling element.

### 1.5 Why Antifriction Bearings Dominate Modern Machine Design

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> Rolling-element bearings are the standard choice in **electric motors, gearboxes, pumps, automotive wheels, machine-tool spindles, and aircraft engines**. They provide:
> 1. Low starting torque.
> 2. Negligible wear during service life.
> 3. Predictable life governed by statistical fatigue theory (L10 life).
> 4. Compact envelope dimensions.
> 5. Standardization — every manufacturer follows ISO 15 dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" Behind Each Step

The theoretical foundation rests on **Lundberg–Palmgren fatigue theory** (1947), which assumes that subsurface shear stress at the rolling contact initiates micro-cracks that eventually propagate to the surface as *spalling*.

- **Step 1 — Hertzian Contact:** The ball and raceway deform elastically into a small elliptical contact patch. Contact stress is **Hertzian**.
- **Step 2 — Cyclic Loading:** Each revolution subjects the material at the contact patch to one cycle of compression–tension.
- **Step 3 — Cumulative Damage:** After millions of cycles, the cumulative damage reaches a critical value → surface fatigue failure (spalling).
- **Step 4 — Statistical Life:** Because material strength is statistical, bearing life is defined as the revolutions at which **10%** of an identical population fails (L10).

### 2.2 The Master Load-Life Equation

The cornerstone of every bearing calculation:

$$
L_{10} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{n}
$$

Where:
- $L_{10}$ = Rating life in **millions of revolutions** (10% failure criterion).
- $C$ = Basic dynamic load rating (N or kN).
- $P$ = Equivalent dynamic radial load (N or kN).
- $n$ = exponent: $n = 3$ for **ball bearings**, $n = \dfrac{10}{3}$ for **roller bearings**.

### 2.3 Equivalent Dynamic Load

A bearing simultaneously experiences radial load $F_r$ and thrust load $F_a$. These are combined into a single equivalent radial load:

$$
P \;=\; X \cdot F_r \;+\; Y \cdot F_a
$$

Where:
- $X$ = Radial factor.
- $Y$ = Thrust factor.
- $X$ and $Y$ depend on the **ratio $\frac{F_a}{C_0}$** and bearing type (read from manufacturer's table).

### 2.4 Equivalent Static Load

For stationary or very slowly rotating bearings:

$$
P_0 \;=\; X_0 \cdot F_r \;+\; Y_0 \cdot F_a
$$

Where $X_0$ and $Y_0$ are the static load factors.

### 2.5 Life in Hours

Converting millions of revolutions to hours:

$$
L_{h} \;=\; \dfrac{10^{6}}{60 \cdot N} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{\!n}
$$

Where $N$ = speed in **rpm**.

### 2.6 Required Dynamic Load Rating (Design Equation)

Solving the master equation for the required $C$ (used during bearing selection):

$$
C_{\text{req}} \;=\; P \cdot \left(\dfrac{60 \cdot N \cdot L_{h}}{10^{6}}\right)^{\!1/n}
$$

### 2.7 Reliability and Application Factors

Real life differs from catalog rating life. KTU problems often include:

$$
L_{\text{adjusted}} \;=\; a_1 \cdot a_2 \cdot a_3 \cdot L_{10}
$$

| Factor | Symbol | Typical Range | Physical Meaning |
|:------:|:------:|:-------------:|:-----------------|
| Reliability factor | $a_1$ | 1.00 (90%) to 0.21 (99.9%) | Adjusts for required reliability |
| Material factor | $a_2$ | ≈ 1.0 | Adjustment for non-standard steel |
| Operating condition factor | $a_3$ | 0.7 – 1.0 | Lubrication, temperature, contamination |

> [!NOTE]
> For most KTU problems, $a_1 = 1$ (90% reliability) and we work directly with $L_{10}$.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Single Master Table

| # | Equation | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:--:|:---------|:--------|:--------------|
| 1 | Rating Life | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{n}$ | $L_{10}$ in **millions of revolutions** |
| 2 | Exponent | $n = 3$ (ball), $n = \dfrac{10}{3}$ (roller) | — |
| 3 | Equivalent Dynamic Load | $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ | N or kN |
| 4 | Equivalent Static Load | $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$ | N or kN |
| 5 | Life in Hours | $L_h = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60 N}\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{n}$ | $N$ in rpm |
| 6 | Required Dynamic Capacity | $C_{\text{req}} = P\left(\dfrac{60 N L_h}{10^{6}}\right)^{1/n}$ | Design output |
| 7 | Speed–Life Coupling | $N \cdot L_{h} \propto \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{n}$ | Constant for a chosen bearing |
| 8 | Specific Load | $\dfrac{C}{P} = \left(\dfrac{60 N L_h}{10^{6}}\right)^{1/n}$ | Bearing capacity utilization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** Never confuse $C$ with $C_0$. The dynamic rating $C$ is for **rotating bearings under fluctuating load**; the static rating $C_0$ is for **non-rotating or impact loading**.

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above are used to:

1. **Select a bearing from a manufacturer's catalog** (SKF, NTN, FAG, Timken).
2. **Estimate field life** of an existing bearing.
3. **Verify thermal equilibrium** — higher $P/C$ ratio means higher contact stress and friction heat.
4. **Compare alternatives** — a bearing with $C = 50$ kN lasts 8 times longer than one with $C = 25$ kN under the same load (for $n = 3$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Life Equation from Hertzian Stress Theory

The Lundberg–Palmgren fatigue equation is built on three physical postulates:

**Postulate 1 — Hertzian contact pressure.**
The maximum contact pressure between a ball and raceway is:

$$
\sigma_{\max} \;\propto\; \left(\dfrac{P}{D^{2}}\right)^{1/3}
$$

This is the classical Hertz result for sphere-on-sphere contact, where $D$ is the ball diameter.

**Postulate 2 — Maximum orthogonal shear stress at depth $z_0$.**
The shear stress responsible for crack initiation lies below the surface:

$$
\tau_0 \;\propto\; \sigma_{\max}
$$

The depth $z_0$ is itself proportional to the contact ellipse half-width, hence to the ball diameter.

**Postulate 3 — Stress cycles = number of revolutions.**
Every revolution subjects the same material element to one complete stress cycle.

**Combining the postulates** using the Weibull weakest-link statistics gives the cumulative damage function:

$$
\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{S}\right) \;\propto\; N \cdot \tau_0^{c} \cdot V
$$

Where:
- $S$ = survival probability.
- $N$ = number of stress cycles (revolutions).
- $V$ = stressed volume.
- $c$ = Weibull exponent (typically 10/3 for ball bearings).

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the math:** After algebraic rearrangement (see KTU reference textbook "Design of Machine Elements" by V.B. Bhandari, Chapter on Rolling Contact Bearings), and noting that for the rated condition $L_{10} = 1$ when $P = C$, the relation collapses to:

$$
L_{10} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{n}
$$

with $n = 3$ for ball bearings and $n = \dfrac{10}{3}$ for roller bearings. **This is the master KTU formula.**

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical — KTU Standard Bearing Selection Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Full 14-Mark Pattern):**
> A radial deep-groove ball bearing is to be selected for the following operating conditions:
>
> - Radial load $F_r = 3000$ N
> - Axial (thrust) load $F_a = 1500$ N
> - Shaft speed $N = 1440$ rpm
> - Expected life $L_h = 10000$ hours at 90% reliability
> - Inner race rotating, light shock loads
>
> **Tasks:**
> (a) Calculate the equivalent dynamic load $P$.
> (b) Calculate the required dynamic load rating $C$ and select a suitable bearing.

---

#### Step 1 — Determine the Ratio $F_a / C_0$

Since $C_0$ is unknown, we make the standard initial **assumption**:

$$
\dfrac{F_a}{C_0} \;\approx\; 0.25
$$

This assumption is the typical first iteration recommended by SKF and used in KTU textbook problems. (In a real catalog selection, we would iterate: guess → find $C_0$ → recompute ratio → re-check.)

**Textual Logic:** For deep-groove ball bearings, the thrust factor $Y$ becomes significant when the axial-to-radial ratio is moderate. The ratio $F_a / C_0$ tells us which row of the SKF factor table to use. A value around 0.25 places us in the standard operating zone.

---

#### Step 2 — Read Factors $X$ and $Y$ from the Standard Table

For a deep-groove ball bearing with $\dfrac{F_a}{C_0} \approx 0.25$ and $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \le e$:

$$
e \;\approx\; 0.22 \quad \text{(typical for } \tfrac{F_a}{C_0} = 0.25\text{)}
$$

Check the axial-to-radial ratio:

$$
\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \;=\; \dfrac{1500}{3000} \;=\; 0.50
$$

Since $0.50 > e = 0.22$, we use $X = 0.56$ and $Y = 1.46$ (typical values for $\tfrac{F_a}{C_0} = 0.25$).

---

#### Step 3 — Calculate Equivalent Dynamic Load

$$
P \;=\; X \cdot F_r \;+\; Y \cdot F_a
$$

$$
P \;=\; (0.56)(3000) \;+\; (1.46)(1500)
$$

$$
P \;=\; 1680 \;+\; 2190
$$

$$
P \;=\; 3870 \;\text{N}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The radial portion contributes 1680 N and the axial portion contributes 2190 N. The thrust load, although smaller in magnitude, carries a larger coefficient $Y$ — a clear illustration of why axial loads are penalized in ball-bearing design.

---

#### Step 4 — Calculate Life in Millions of Revolutions

$$
L_{\text{millions}} \;=\; \dfrac{60 \cdot N \cdot L_h}{10^{6}}
$$

$$
L_{\text{millions}} \;=\; \dfrac{60 \times 1440 \times 10000}{10^{6}}
$$

$$
L_{\text{millions}} \;=\; \dfrac{864{,}000{,}000}{10^{6}}
$$

$$
L_{\text{millions}} \;=\; 864
$$

**Textual Logic:** 1440 revolutions per minute over 10 000 hours gives 864 million revolutions. Bearings are rated at 1 million revolutions as the baseline — so this bearing must survive 864 times its baseline life.

---

#### Step 5 — Calculate Required Dynamic Load Rating

For a ball bearing, $n = 3$:

$$
C_{\text{req}} \;=\; P \cdot \left(L_{\text{millions}}\right)^{1/n}
$$

$$
C_{\text{req}} \;=\; 3870 \cdot \left(864\right)^{1/3}
$$

Compute $\left(864\right)^{1/3}$:

$$
\left(864\right)^{1/3} \;\approx\; 9.526
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_{\text{req}} \;=\; 3870 \times 9.526
$$

$$
C_{\text{req}} \;\approx\; 36{,}866 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 36.9 \;\text{kN}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The bearing must have a dynamic load rating of at least **36.9 kN** to survive the specified operating life at the given load and speed.

---

#### Step 6 — Select Bearing from Catalog

Consulting an SKF deep-groove ball bearing table for a 30 mm bore (typical for shaft diameter):

- Bearing **6206**: $C = 29.6$ kN, $C_0 = 16.3$ kN → **INSUFFICIENT**
- Bearing **6207**: $C = 35.1$ kN, $C_0 = 19.2$ kN → **STILL INSUFFICIENT**
- Bearing **6208**: $C = 41.0$ kN, $C_0 = 22.8$ kN → **ACCEPTABLE** ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Decision:** Bearing **6208** is selected because its dynamic load rating (41.0 kN) exceeds the required 36.9 kN. This provides a safety margin of about **11%**.

---

#### Step 7 — Verification by Iterating the $F_a / C_0$ Ratio

For the selected bearing 6208, $C_0 = 22.8$ kN:

$$
\dfrac{F_a}{C_0} \;=\; \dfrac{1500}{22800} \;\approx\; 0.066
$$

This gives a new $e \approx 0.20$, and a new set of factors $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.55$. Re-iterating:

$$
P_{\text{new}} \;=\; 0.56 \times 3000 + 1.55 \times 1500 \;=\; 1680 + 2325 \;=\; 4005 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
C_{\text{req, new}} \;=\; 4005 \times 9.526 \;\approx\; 38{,}160 \;\text{N}
$$

Still less than 41 kN → **6208 is confirmed suitable.**

**Textual Logic:** The two-iteration method is the KTU gold standard for bearing selection. Always verify by computing the actual $F_a/C_0$ ratio of the chosen bearing and re-evaluating $P$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Ball vs Roller vs Special Bearings

| Selection Criterion | Deep-Groove Ball | Cylindrical Roller | Tapered Roller | Needle Roller | Spherical Roller |
|:--------------------|:----------------:|:-----------------:|:--------------:|:-------------:|:----------------:|
| **Radial Load Capacity** | Medium | **Very High** | High | Medium | **Very High** |
| **Thrust Load Capacity** | Low (both dirs) | None | **High (one dir)** | None | Low–Medium |
| **Speed Capability** | **Very High** | High | Medium | Low–Medium | Medium |
| **Misalignment Tolerance** | ±2–10 min | ±2–4 min | ±2–4 min | ±2–4 min | **±0.5–2°** |
| **Friction Torque** | **Lowest** | Low | Medium | Low | Medium |
| **Cost (relative)** | 1× | 1.5× | 2× | 1.2× | 3× |
| **Typical Applications** | Motors, pumps | Gearboxes, railway axle | Automotive hubs, gearboxes | Connecting rods, rocker arms | Heavy machinery, vibrating screens |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Rolling Contact Bearing Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shaft with Applied Loads] --> B[Load Analysis Module]
    B --> C{Radial Only?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Deep Groove Ball Bearing]
    C -- No --> E[Combined Radial + Thrust?]
    E -- Yes, Moderate --> F[Angular Contact Ball Bearing]
    E -- Yes, Heavy --> G[Tapered Roller Bearing]
    E -- High Speed --> H[Cylindrical Roller Bearing]
    D --> I[Compute Equivalent Dynamic Load P]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Determine Required Life L10 in million revs]
    J --> K[Compute Required Dynamic Load Capacity Creq]
    K --> L[Consult Manufacturer Catalog]
    L --> M{Catalog C greater than Creq?}
    M -- Yes --> N[SELECT BEARING]
    M -- No --> O[Try Larger Bore Series]
    O --> L
    N --> P[Verify Static Load Co less than Specified Limit]
    P --> Q[Final Design Confirmed]
```

### 4.2 Bearing Selection Decision Tree (Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Start: Operating Conditions Given] --> A1[Compute Required Life in hrs]
    A1 --> A2[Convert to Million Revs]
    A2 --> A3[Assume Fa over Co equals 0.25]
    A3 --> A4[Read X and Y from Table]
    A4 --> A5[Compute P equals X Fr plus Y Fa]
    A5 --> A6[Compute Creq equals P times L to power 1 over n]
    A6 --> A7[Choose Bore Diameter from Shaft]
    A7 --> A8[Select Bearing with C greater than Creq]
    A8 --> A9[Iterate Fa over Co with Actual C0]
    A9 --> A10[Recompute P and Creq]
    A10 --> A11{Converged?}
    A11 -- Yes --> A12[Final Selection]
    A11 -- No --> A8
```

### 4.3 Comparative Bearing Architecture Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BallBearingGroup
    B1[Deep Groove Ball] --> B2[Angular Contact Ball]
    B2 --> B3[Self Aligning Ball]
    end
    subgraph RollerBearingGroup
    R1[Cylindrical Roller] --> R2[Tapered Roller]
    R2 --> R3[Needle Roller]
    R3 --> R4[Spherical Roller]
    end
    subgraph DesignInputs
    DI1[Speed N in rpm] --> EngineDecision
    DI2[Radial Load Fr] --> EngineDecision
    DI3[Axial Load Fa] --> EngineDecision
    DI4[Required Life Lh] --> EngineDecision
    end
    EngineDecision{Selection Engine}
    EngineDecision --> BallBearingGroup
    EngineDecision --> RollerBearingGroup
    BallBearingGroup --> Output[Selected Bearing Part Number]
    RollerBearingGroup --> Output
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagrams:** Each block represents a logical stage of the bearing design process. Inputs flow in at the top, the selection engine (the central decision diamond) routes the engineer to either ball or roller families, and iterative loops ensure convergence on a valid catalog part number.

### 4.4 Conceptual Anatomy of a Ball Bearing (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OuterRing[Outer Race stationary side] --> ContactZone1[Hertzian Contact Patch]
    InnerRing[Inner Race rotating side] --> ContactZone1
    BallGroup[Rolling Element Ball] --> ContactZone1
    ContactZone1 --> CageFunc[Cage Spaces Balls uniformly]
    LubricationChannel[Grease or Oil Film] --> ContactZone1
    SealElement[Seal or Shield] --> OuterRing
    SealElement --> InnerRing
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Define the terms:**
> (i) Basic dynamic load rating ($C$).
> (ii) Basic static load rating ($C_0$).
> (iii) Equivalent dynamic load ($P$).

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Basic dynamic load rating ($C$):**
It is the constant radial load (purely radial for radial bearings, or constant axial + radial combination giving constant equivalent radial load for thrust bearings) which a bearing can theoretically endure for a rating life of **one million revolutions** with **90% reliability**.

**(ii) Basic static load rating ($C_0$):**
It is the load that corresponds to a calculated contact stress of **4000 MPa** at the center of the most heavily loaded ball–raceway contact. It represents the maximum load a stationary or slowly rotating bearing can sustain without permanent brinelling (indentation) exceeding **0.0001 of the ball diameter**.

**(iii) Equivalent dynamic load ($P$):**
It is that constant stationary radial load which, if applied to the bearing, would give the same rating life as the actual combined radial and thrust loads. Mathematically:

$$
P \;=\; X \cdot F_r \;+\; Y \cdot F_a
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** All three definitions must be stated verbatim — examiners reward precise wording.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Compare ball bearings and roller bearings on the basis of:**
> (a) Load-carrying capacity.
> (b) Speed capability.
> (c) Friction and lubrication.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Ball Bearing | Roller Bearing |
|:----------|:-------------|:---------------|
| **(a) Load Capacity** | Limited by Hertzian point contact (smaller contact area); suitable for **light-to-medium** loads. | Line contact gives larger contact area; carries **much higher** radial loads (typically 1.5–3× higher). |
| **(b) Speed Capability** | Lower contact friction allows **higher speeds** (typical DN values up to 1.5 million). | Higher friction limits speed; typically **70–80%** of equivalent ball bearing. |
| **(c) Friction and Lubrication** | Lower sliding component → less heat → **less lubricant** needed. | Higher friction → more heat → requires **more frequent lubrication** or oil bath. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Derive the relationship between rating life, dynamic load capacity, and equivalent dynamic load for ball bearings. Clearly state all assumptions and the value of the exponent. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A deep-groove ball bearing operates under the following conditions:
> - Radial load $F_r = 4500$ N
> - Axial load $F_a = 2000$ N
> - Speed $N = 1500$ rpm
> - Required life $L_h = 8000$ hours
> - 90% reliability, light shock
>
> Calculate the required dynamic load capacity $C$ and select a suitable bearing from the following data:
> | Bearing | Bore (mm) | $C$ (kN) | $C_0$ (kN) |
> |:-------:|:---------:|:--------:|:----------:|
> | 6305    | 25        | 22.5     | 11.6       |
> | 6306    | 30        | 28.1     | 16.0       |
> | 6307    | 35        | 33.2     | 19.0       |
> | 6308    | 40        | 41.0     | 24.0       |
>
> **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

The Lundberg–Palmgren theory combines Hertzian contact stress, Weibull fatigue statistics, and the assumption that one revolution = one stress cycle. For ball bearings:

**Step 1:** Maximum Hertzian contact pressure at ball-raceway interface:

$$
\sigma_{\max} \;\propto\; \left(\dfrac{Q}{D^{2}}\right)^{1/3}
$$

where $Q$ = load per ball.

**Step 2:** Volume of stressed material per cycle:

$$
V \;\propto\; D^{3}
$$

**Step 3:** Combining with Weibull survival probability and integrating over the stressed volume yields:

$$
\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{S}\right) \;\propto\; N \cdot \tau_0^{c} \cdot V
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting and using the rated condition $L_{10} = 1$ when $P = C$, the equation simplifies to:

$$
L_{10} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{n}
$$

with $n = 3$ for ball bearings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key assumption:** 90% reliability (i.e., $S = 0.90$ when $L = L_{10}$). For higher reliability, the exponent of reliability enters as an adjustment factor $a_1$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Step 1:** Assume initial ratio $\dfrac{F_a}{C_0} \approx 0.25$.

$$
e \;\approx\; 0.22, \quad X = 0.56, \quad Y = 1.46
$$

Check:

$$
\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \;=\; \dfrac{2000}{4500} \;=\; 0.444 \;\;>\;\; e \;=\; 0.22 \quad \text{(so use } X, Y \text{ from combined-load row)}
$$

**Step 2:** Equivalent dynamic load:

$$
P \;=\; X F_r + Y F_a \;=\; (0.56)(4500) + (1.46)(2000) \;=\; 2520 + 2920 \;=\; 5440 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 3:** Life in millions of revolutions:

$$
L_{\text{millions}} \;=\; \dfrac{60 \cdot N \cdot L_h}{10^{6}} \;=\; \dfrac{60 \times 1500 \times 8000}{10^{6}} \;=\; 720
$$

**Step 4:** Required dynamic load rating:

$$
C_{\text{req}} \;=\; P \cdot \left(L_{\text{millions}}\right)^{1/3} \;=\; 5440 \times \left(720\right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
\left(720\right)^{1/3} \;\approx\; 8.963
$$

$$
C_{\text{req}} \;\approx\; 5440 \times 8.963 \;\approx\; 48{,}758 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 48.8 \;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 5:** Catalog selection:

| Bearing | $C$ (kN) | Verdict |
|:-------:|:--------:|:-------:|
| 6305 | 22.5 | ✗ Insufficient |
| 6306 | 28.1 | ✗ Insufficient |
| 6307 | 33.2 | ✗ Insufficient |
| **6308** | **41.0** | **✗ Marginal — needs recheck** |

**Step 6:** Iterate with $C_0 = 24.0$ kN for bearing 6308:

$$
\dfrac{F_a}{C_0} \;=\; \dfrac{2000}{24000} \;\approx\; 0.083
$$

New factors: $e \approx 0.20$, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.55$.

$$
P_{\text{new}} \;=\; 0.56 \times 4500 + 1.55 \times 2000 \;=\; 2520 + 3100 \;=\; 5620 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
C_{\text{req, new}} \;=\; 5620 \times 8.963 \;\approx\; 50{,}372 \;\text{N}
$$

Still **exceeds 41.0 kN**. Therefore, even 6308 is unsuitable.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conclusion:** None of the listed bearings meet the required $C_{\text{req}} = 50.4$ kN. The student should propose either:
> 1. A **larger bore series** (e.g., 6310 with $C \approx 62$ kN).
> 2. Use a **tapered roller bearing** of equivalent bore to achieve higher load capacity.
> 3. **Reduce life requirement** or **redesign for lower load**.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the construction and working of:
> (i) Deep-groove ball bearing.
> (ii) Tapered roller bearing.
> Compare their suitability for combined radial and thrust loads. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cylindrical roller bearing carries a radial load of $F_r = 8000$ N at $N = 1200$ rpm. The required life is $L_h = 5000$ hours. Calculate the required basic dynamic load rating and check if the following bearing is suitable:
> - NU 308: $C = 53$ kN, $C_0 = 53$ kN.
> **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working:**

**(i) Deep-groove ball bearing (single-row):**
The outer race has a continuous groove of radius slightly larger than the ball radius. The inner race has a matching groove. Balls are inserted between the races and separated by a pressed-steel or brass cage. The races are typically made of through-hardened chromium steel (AISI 52100). The bearing can carry radial load and a small amount of thrust in either direction.

**(ii) Tapered roller bearing:**
Both the inner race (cone) and outer race (cup) have tapered raceways. The rollers are conical and guided by a flange on the inner ring. The apexes of the rollers, inner race, and outer race converge to a common point on the bearing axis, ensuring true rolling without skidding. These bearings can carry heavy radial loads and substantial one-directional thrust loads.

**Comparison for combined loads:**

| Parameter | Deep-Groove Ball | Tapered Roller |
|:----------|:-----------------|:---------------|
| Radial load capacity | Moderate | **High** |
| Thrust load capacity | Low (≤ 0.5 × radial) | **High** (one direction) |
| Combined load handling | Suitable for moderate | **Superior** |
| Limiting speed | **Higher** | Lower due to sliding at flange |
| Mounting complexity | Simple | Requires precise adjustment |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always include a labelled sketch (at least a textual description) showing the taper angle and common apex — this alone fetches 2 marks.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (Roller Bearing):**

**Step 1:** Pure radial load on cylindrical roller bearing → $P = F_r = 8000$ N.

**Step 2:** Life in millions of revolutions:

$$
L_{\text{millions}} \;=\; \dfrac{60 \times 1200 \times 5000}{10^{6}} \;=\; 360
$$

**Step 3:** Required dynamic load rating (roller, $n = \dfrac{10}{3}$):

$$
C_{\text{req}} \;=\; P \cdot \left(L_{\text{millions}}\right)^{1/n}
$$

$$
C_{\text{req}} \;=\; 8000 \times \left(360\right)^{3/10}
$$

Compute $\left(360\right)^{3/10}$:

$$
\ln(360) \;=\; 5.886
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{3}{10}\right) \times 5.886 \;=\; 1.766
$$

$$
e^{1.766} \;\approx\; 5.85
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_{\text{req}} \;\approx\; 8000 \times 5.85 \;=\; 46{,}800 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 46.8 \;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 4:** Compare with NU 308 ($C = 53$ kN):

$$
53 \;\text{kN} \;>\; 46.8 \;\text{kN} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \textbf{SUITABLE}
$$

**Step 5:** Margin of safety:

$$
\text{Excess capacity} \;=\; \dfrac{53 - 46.8}{46.8} \times 100\% \;\approx\; 13.2\%
$$

**Textual Logic:** A 13% margin is acceptable for lightly shocked industrial applications. If shock were heavy, the next larger series (NU 309 with $C \approx 75$ kN) would be preferred.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to iterate** the $F_a / C_0$ ratio after selecting the bearing. The catalog $C_0$ will almost always be different from the assumed value, and the factors $X, Y$ change accordingly. Failure to iterate loses **2 full marks**.
> 2. **Using wrong exponent:** $n = 3$ for ball, $n = 10/3$ for roller. Mixing them gives a wildly wrong $C_{\text{req}}$ — examiners deduct **3 marks** for this.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Convert $N$ to rpm consistently; convert $L$ to millions of revolutions before applying the formula. Forgetting $10^{6}$ is a classic 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Misreading $C$ vs $C_0$:** Many students confuse the dynamic and static ratings. Always check whether the bearing is rotating or stationary.
> 5. **Forgetting the application factor** for heavy shock (1.2–1.8 multiplier on $P$). When specified, the problem expects $P_{\text{effective}} = a_f \cdot P$.
> 6. **Neglecting to convert hours to millions of revolutions:** The life equation $L_{10} = (C/P)^n$ works in millions of revolutions; using hours directly gives an answer that is off by a factor of $10^6$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> ✅ **Bearing types:** Ball (deep-groove, angular contact, self-aligning); Roller (cylindrical, tapered, needle, spherical).
>
> ✅ **Load-Life Equation:** $L_{10} = (C/P)^{n}$ — memorize first.
>
> ✅ **Exponent:** $n = 3$ (ball), $n = 10/3$ (roller) — memorize second.
>
> ✅ **Equivalent load:** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ — coefficients depend on $F_a/C_0$.
>
> ✅ **Life in hours:** $L_h = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60 N} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{n}$ — memorize third.
>
> ✅ **Required capacity:** $C_{\text{req}} = P \left(\dfrac{60 N L_h}{10^{6}}\right)^{1/n}$ — the design equation.
>
> ✅ **Static load equation:** $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$ — for non-rotating bearings.
>
> ✅ **Selection procedure (KTU standard):**
> 1. Assume $F_a/C_0 \approx 0.25$.
> 2. Read $X, Y$.
> 3. Compute $P$.
> 4. Compute $C_{\text{req}}$.
> 5. Pick bearing with $C > C_{\text{req}}$.
> 6. Iterate using actual $C_0$ of chosen bearing.
>
> ✅ **Reliability:** $L_{10} = 1 \times 10^{6}$ rev corresponds to 90% reliability.
>
> ✅ **Selection rule of thumb:** When in doubt, choose the next larger bore — never smaller.
>
> ✅ **Static safety check:** $C_0 \geq 2 \times P$ (for normal operation), $C_0 \geq 1.5 \times P$ (for shock-free).
>
> ✅ **Bearing limit speeds:** Ball bearings generally allow DN values up to 1.5 × 10⁶; roller bearings about 70% of this.
>
> ✅ **Lubrication:** Grease for low/moderate speeds; oil bath or oil-mist for high-speed applications.
>
> ✅ **Misalignment limits:** Deep-groove ball = ±2–10 min; Spherical roller = ±0.5–2°.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static and Dynamic Load Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic Load Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Load Capacity ($C_0$)** is the maximum static radial load (for radial bearings) or axial load (for thrust bearings) that a rolling contact bearing can sustain without causing a permanent deformation of the rolling element greater than **0.0001 times the diameter of the rolling element** at the most heavily loaded contact point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Load Capacity ($C$)** is the constant, stationary radial (or axial, for thrust bearings) load that a population of apparently identical bearings can endure for a basic rating life of **one million revolutions** of the inner ring with a **90% reliability**, where the rings and rolling elements are made of high-quality, hardened bearing steel and operating under adequate lubrication.

Both these parameters are **catalog values** provided by the bearing manufacturer (e.g., SKF, NTN, FAG) and form the basis of all bearing life calculations.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Water-Bottle Carrier Analogy**
> Imagine you are a daily-wage laborer carrying sealed water bottles up a staircase:
> - **Static Load Capacity** = The absolute heaviest single bottle you can hold in your hand *just once* before your fingers bruise permanently (i.e., one-time peak load without damage). You will never take that single step again.
> - **Dynamic Load Capacity** = The lighter weight of a bottle that you can carry, trip after trip, for a *full 8-hour shift* (1 million trips = 1 million revolutions) before your fingers tire and fail. It is always much **smaller** than the static capacity, because fatigue builds up with every cycle.

> **Key Realisation:** A bearing can take a *huge* load once (static), but a *much smaller* load if it has to survive billions of cycles (dynamic). This is why high-cycle applications like gearboxes and electric motors require the dynamic capacity equation, not the static one.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Basic Rating Life ($L_{10}$):** 1,000,000 revolutions = **$10^6$ revolutions** (Industry Standard, ISO 281).
- **Permanent Deformation Limit (Static):** **$0.0001 \cdot D$**, where $D$ is the diameter of the rolling element (ball or roller).
- **Reliability at $C$:** **90%** (i.e., 10% of the population may fail by 1 million revolutions).
- **Exponent $p$ (Load-Life Exponent):**
  - **$p = 3$** for **Ball Bearings** (point contact).
  - **$p = 10/3$** for **Roller Bearings** (line contact).
- **Rotation Reference:** Dynamic load $C$ is rated for the **inner ring rotating**. If the outer ring rotates, the capacity is derated by roughly a factor of **0.6 to 0.8** (consult catalog footnote).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic: How the Concept Works

- **Step 1 — Identify the Loading Regime:**
  Ask: *Is the bearing carrying a one-time heavy impact (static) or repeated millions of small loads (dynamic)?* Most machine elements (shafts, gears, pulleys) fall into the **dynamic** category.

- **Step 2 — Read the Manufacturer's Capacity ($C$ and $C_0$):**
  For any catalog part number, two numbers are always printed: dynamic capacity $C$ (in kN or N) and static capacity $C_0$.

- **Step 3 — Calculate the Equivalent Load ($P$):**
  Real applications have combined radial ($F_r$) and axial ($F_a$) loads. These are combined using empirical factors $X$ and $Y$ (supplied in the catalog) into a single **equivalent dynamic load $P$**.

- **Step 4 — Apply the Load-Life Power Law:**
  Because every stress cycle causes a tiny amount of sub-surface fatigue damage (spalling, pitting), the life decreases as a **cube (or 10/3 power)** of the load. A *doubled* load gives **8 times shorter life** for ball bearings.

- **Step 5 — Compare Required vs. Available Capacity:**
  Calculate the *required* dynamic capacity $C_{req} = P \cdot (L_{10})^{1/p}$. Select a catalog bearing with $C \geq C_{req}$.

- **Step 6 — Static Safety Check:**
  Verify $C_0 \geq S_0 \cdot P_0$, where $S_0$ is a static safety factor (typically 1.0 to 2.0, depending on shock severity). This check governs **only** for very slow speeds, heavy impacts, or oscillating motion.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
|---|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | **Basic Rating Life** in millions of revolutions |
| 2 | $p = 3$ (ball), $p = 10/3$ (roller) | Load-life exponent |
| 3 | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | Life in **hours** ($n$ = rpm) |
| 4 | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | **Equivalent Dynamic Load** for radial/deep-groove bearings |
| 5 | $P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a$ | **Equivalent Static Load** |
| 6 | $C_{req} = P \cdot (L_{10})^{1/p}$ | **Required Dynamic Capacity** for a desired life |
| 7 | $C_0 \geq S_0 \cdot P_0$ | **Static Safety Check** |
| 8 | $\dfrac{L_{na}}{L_{10}} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{\epsilon \cdot p}$ | **Modified Life** with reliability factor $\epsilon$ (e.g., $\epsilon = 1$ for 90%, $\epsilon \approx 1.5$ for 99%) |
| 9 | $\sum \dfrac{n_i \cdot N_i}{N_i^*} = 1$ | **Miner's Rule** for variable/spectrum loading |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This system is the universal language of bearing selection used in **automotive gearboxes, electric motors, wind-turbine main shafts, aerospace actuators, and machine-tool spindles**. KTU examiners test this because it is the only ISO-standard method for translating a service life in hours into a bearing size.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

In **wind-turbine design**, a main-shaft bearing must last **175,000 hours (≈ 20 years)** under highly variable wind loads. Designers use Miner's cumulative damage rule together with the dynamic capacity equation to size the bearing. In **automotive wheel hubs**, the static capacity governs during parking impacts (curb strikes), while dynamic capacity governs the rolling fatigue life.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Load–Life Power Law ($L_{10} = (C/P)^p$)

This equation is **empirically** derived by Lundberg and Palmgren (1947) and is universally accepted. We present a clean, mathematically equivalent textbook derivation based on Weibull weakest-link theory.

> **Step 1 — Define the basic rating life reference point.**
> When the applied equivalent load $P$ is exactly equal to the dynamic capacity $C$, by definition the bearing achieves $L_{10} = 1$ million revolutions:
> $$L_{10} = 1 \text{ million revs} \quad \text{when} \quad P = C$$

> **Step 2 — Recognise the load-life proportionality.**
> From experimental Hertzian contact fatigue data, the life is inversely proportional to a *power* of the load. For ball bearings, the exponent is 3:
> $$L_{10} \propto \dfrac{1}{P^3} \quad \text{(ball bearings)}$$

> **Step 3 — Form the proportional constant.**
> Let $K$ be the proportionality constant. Combining Step 1 and Step 2:
> $$L_{10} = \dfrac{K}{P^3}$$

> **Step 4 — Apply the boundary condition.**
> Substitute $L_{10} = 1$ million revs and $P = C$:
> $$1 = \dfrac{K}{C^3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = C^3$$

> **Step 5 — Generalise for any load $P$.**
> Substituting $K = C^3$ back into the life equation:
> $$\boxed{\,L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^3 \text{ million revs} \quad \text{(ball bearing)}\,}$$
> For roller bearings, replace the exponent 3 with $10/3$:
> $$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3} \text{ million revs} \quad \text{(roller bearing)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Dynamic Capacity $C_{req}$

Starting from the load-life equation and rearranging algebraically for $C$:

> **Step 1 — Cube both sides (ball bearing case).**
> $$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^3$$

> **Step 2 — Take the cube root.**
> $$(L_{10})^{1/3} = \dfrac{C}{P}$$

> **Step 3 — Solve for $C$.**
> $$\boxed{\,C_{req} = P \cdot (L_{10})^{1/p}\,}$$
> where $p = 3$ (ball) or $p = 10/3$ (roller), and $L_{10}$ is in **millions of revolutions**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Conversion from Revolutions to Hours

> **Step 1 — Revolutions per hour.**
> At a rotational speed of $n$ revolutions per minute:
> $$N_{rev/hr} = 60 \cdot n$$

> **Step 2 — Revolutions in $L_{10h}$ hours.**
> $$N_{total} = L_{10h} \cdot 60 \cdot n$$

> **Step 3 — But the life in the standard equation is in *millions* of revolutions.**
> Therefore, $L_{10}$ (in millions) = $N_{total} / 10^6$:
> $$\dfrac{L_{10h} \cdot 60 \cdot n}{10^6} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

> **Step 4 — Solve for life in hours.**
> $$\boxed{\,L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p \text{ hours}\,}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Miner's Cumulative Damage Rule (Variable Loads)

> **Step 1 — Define a duty cycle.**
> Suppose a bearing operates at $k$ different load levels. At level $i$, it runs for $n_i$ revolutions under equivalent load $P_i$.

> **Step 2 — Calculate the cycles to failure at each level.**
> $$N_i^* = 10^6 \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P_i}\right)^p \text{ revolutions}$$

> **Step 3 — Sum the damage fractions.**
> Define the damage fraction at level $i$ as $D_i = n_i / N_i^*$. The bearing fails when total damage = 1:
> $$\sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{n_i}{N_i^*} = 1$$
> Substituting the life expression:
> $$\boxed{\,\sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{n_i}{10^6} \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_i}{C}\right)^p = 1\,}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bearing Selection Logic Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Application Requirements] --> B{Static or Dynamic Loading?}
    B -- Static / Very Slow Speed --> C[Use Static Load Capacity C0]
    C --> D[Calculate Equivalent Static Load P0]
    D --> E["C0 >= S0 x P0 ?"]
    E -- No --> F[Select Larger Bearing]
    E -- Yes --> G[Static Design OK]
    B -- Dynamic / Continuous Rotation --> H[Use Dynamic Load Capacity C]
    H --> I[Calculate Equivalent Dynamic Load P]
    I --> J[Apply Load-Life Equation L10]
    J --> K["C_req = P x (L10)^(1/p)"]
    K --> L[Select Catalog Bearing with C >= C_req]
    L --> M{L10h >= Required Service Life?}
    M -- No --> F
    M -- Yes --> N[Final Design: Verify C0 Check]
    N --> O[End: Bearing Selected]
    F --> O
    G --> O
```

## 4.2 Static vs Dynamic Load Capacity – Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SC["STATIC LOAD CAPACITY (C0)"]
        SC1[One-time Peak Load]
        SC2["Limit: 0.0001 x D deformation"]
        SC3[Used for: Slow / Oscillating / Impact]
    end
    subgraph DC["DYNAMIC LOAD CAPACITY (C)"]
        DC1[Repeated Cyclic Load]
        DC2["Limit: 10^6 revolutions at 90% reliability"]
        DC3[Used for: Rotating Shafts / Gears]
    end
    SC2 --> J[Compare on Common Scale of kN]
    DC2 --> J
    J --> K["Usually C0 > C (Typical Ratio 1.5 to 2.5)"]
    DC1 --> E["L10 = (C/P)^p"]
    SC3 --> A[Bridge Bearings, Crane Hooks]
    DC3 --> B[Motors, Gearboxes, Turbines]
```

## 4.3 Load–Life Power-Law Relationship

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Applied Load P] --> PL[Load-Life Power Law]
    C[Dynamic Capacity C] --> PL
    PL --> EQ["L10 = (C/P)^p  where p = 3 or 10/3"]
    EQ --> OUT[Life in Million Revolutions]
    P2[Doubling the Load] --> EFF[Life reduces by factor of 8 balls / 9.2 rollers]
    EFF --> OUT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define static load capacity of a rolling contact bearing. State the limiting permanent deformation criterion.**

> **Model Answer:**
> Static load capacity $C_0$ is the maximum static radial (or axial, for thrust bearings) load that a bearing can support without a permanent indentation at the most heavily loaded rolling-element contact exceeding **0.0001 times the diameter of the rolling element**. It is used for sizing bearings subjected to very slow speeds, oscillatory motion, or static holding. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. What is the significance of the exponent $p$ in the load–life equation?**

> **Model Answer:**
> The exponent $p$ accounts for the type of contact geometry: **$p = 3$ for ball bearings** (Hertzian point contact) and **$p = 10/3$ for roller bearings** (Hertzian line contact). It dictates how rapidly life drops as load rises: a doubled load cuts ball-bearing life by $2^3 = 8\times$ and roller-bearing life by $2^{10/3} \approx 10.08\times$. (3 marks)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, Model Paper Style]**

**(a) Derive the relationship between the basic dynamic load capacity $C$, the equivalent dynamic load $P$, and the basic rating life $L_{10}$ for a ball bearing. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
> - The dynamic capacity $C$ is defined as the load at which a bearing achieves a life of 1 million revolutions at 90% reliability.
> - Let the proportionality be $L_{10} = K / P^3$ where $K$ is a constant.
> - Applying the boundary condition: at $P = C$, $L_{10} = 1 \times 10^6$ revs → $K = C^3$.
> - Substituting: $L_{10} = (C/P)^3$ million revs.
> - For roller bearings: $L_{10} = (C/P)^{10/3}$ million revs.
> - **Final boxed result:** $\boxed{L_{10} = (C/P)^p}$ with $p = 3$ (ball) or $10/3$ (roller). (7 marks)

**(b) A 6308 deep-groove ball bearing carries a radial load of $F_r = 3.2$ kN and an axial load of $F_a = 1.0$ kN at 1440 rpm. The manufacturer’s catalog gives $C = 41$ kN, $C_0 = 22.5$ kN, and the load factors: $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.55$, $X_0 = 0.6$, $Y_0 = 0.5$. Calculate the life in hours. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
> **Step 1 — Calculate the equivalent dynamic load.**
> $$P = X F_r + Y F_a = (0.56)(3.2) + (1.55)(1.0) = 1.792 + 1.55 = 3.342 \text{ kN}$$

> **Step 2 — Apply the load-life equation (millions of revs).**
> $$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^3 = \left(\dfrac{41}{3.342}\right)^3 = (12.268)^3 \approx 1846.5 \text{ million revs}$$

> **Step 3 — Convert to hours at $n = 1440$ rpm.**
> $$L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10} \times 10^6}{60 \cdot n} = \dfrac{1846.5 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1440} = \dfrac{1.8465 \times 10^9}{86400}$$
> $$\boxed{L_{10h} \approx 21{,}375 \text{ hours}}$$

> **Step 4 — Quick static sanity check.**
> $$P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a = (0.6)(3.2) + (0.5)(1.0) = 2.42 \text{ kN} \ll C_0 = 22.5 \text{ kN} \;\; \checkmark$$
> (7 marks)

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Alternative Pattern, Model Paper Style]**

**(a) Explain the concept of equivalent dynamic load and equivalent static load in a rolling contact bearing. State the formulas used. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
> - Real bearing applications rarely carry purely radial or purely axial loads. Manufacturers provide empirical **load factors $X$, $Y$, $X_0$, $Y_0$** which depend on the ratio $F_a/F_r$ and the bearing geometry.
> - **Equivalent Dynamic Load ($P$):** A hypothetical constant radial load (for radial bearings) that, if applied, would give the same bearing life as the actual combined load.
> - Formula: $\boxed{P = X F_r + Y F_a}$
> - **Equivalent Static Load ($P_0$):** A hypothetical static load that produces the same permanent deformation as the actual combined load.
> - Formula: $\boxed{P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a}$
> - For **pure radial load** ($F_a = 0$): $P = F_r$ and $P_0 = F_r$.
> - The values of $X$ and $Y$ are catalog-dependent and must be looked up for the chosen bearing series. (7 marks)

**(b) A 30205 tapered roller bearing is required to carry a 4 kN radial load and 1.5 kN axial thrust at 1000 rpm. The catalog values are $C = 29.2$ kN, $C_0 = 32$ kN, $e = 0.37$, $Y = 1.7$, and $X = 0.4$. Estimate the bearing life in hours. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
> **Step 1 — Compute the thrust ratio.**
> $$\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = \dfrac{1.5}{4} = 0.375$$

> **Step 2 — Compare with $e$.**
> Since $F_a/F_r = 0.375 > e = 0.37$, the second-row equation for $X$ and $Y$ applies → $X = 0.4$, $Y = 1.7$.

> **Step 3 — Equivalent dynamic load.**
> $$P = X F_r + Y F_a = (0.4)(4) + (1.7)(1.5) = 1.6 + 2.55 = 4.15 \text{ kN}$$

> **Step 4 — Life in millions of revolutions (roller bearing, $p = 10/3$).**
> $$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3} = \left(\dfrac{29.2}{4.15}\right)^{10/3} = (7.036)^{3.333} \approx 718.4 \text{ million revs}$$

> **Step 5 — Convert to hours.**
> $$L_{10h} = \dfrac{718.4 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1000} = \dfrac{7.184 \times 10^8}{60{,}000} \approx \boxed{11{,}973 \text{ hours}}$$

> Static check: $P_0 = 0.5 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 1.5 = 3.5$ kN $< C_0 = 32$ kN. ✓ (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Top Reasons Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the exponent $p$:** Many students write $(C/P)^3$ even for roller bearings, costing the entire 7-mark derivation. Always check: *ball* → 3, *roller* → 10/3.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in life conversion:** $L_{10}$ from the basic equation is in **millions** of revolutions, not raw revolutions. Failing to multiply by $10^6$ (or divide by it) causes a life error of one million.
> 3. **Wrong $X$ and $Y$ row:** In tapered and deep-groove bearings, the choice of $X$ and $Y$ depends on whether $F_a/F_r$ is *less than* or *greater than* the catalog parameter $e$. Students pick the wrong row and lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the static check:** KTU expects a one-line static safety check ($C_0 \geq P_0$) at the end of a bearing design problem. Skipping it costs 1 mark even if the dynamic life is correct.
> 5. **Forgetting that $C$ and $C_0$ are catalog values in kN, not N:** Mixing SI units (e.g., $F_r$ in N and $C$ in kN) introduces a 1000× error.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Static load $C_0$** is for one-time / slow loads; deformation limit is **$0.0001 \cdot D$**.
- **Dynamic load $C$** is for rotating shafts; reference life is **$10^6$ revs at 90% reliability**.
- **Load-life equation:** $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$; **$p=3$ ball, $p=10/3$ roller**.
- **Life in hours:** $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60\,n}\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$.
- **Required capacity:** $C_{req} = P \cdot (L_{10})^{1/p}$.
- **Equivalent dynamic load:** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$.
- **Equivalent static load:** $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$.
- **Static safety check:** $C_0 \geq S_0 P_0$ ($S_0$ between 1.0 and 2.0).
- **Variable loads:** use Miner's rule $\sum n_i/N_i^* = 1$.
- **Always perform a static check** even in dynamic problems — KTU awards marks for it.
- **Units:** keep $C, P, F_r, F_a$ in the **same units (kN or N)** throughout.
- **Reliability upgrade:** for $L_{na}$ at reliability $a \neq 90\%$, multiply exponent by $\epsilon$ where $\epsilon \approx 1.5$ for 99%, etc.
- **Practical tip:** if $L_{10h}$ is far below the required service life, do **not** just choose a larger bearing — also verify lubrication, alignment, sealing, and balance grade.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalent Dynamic Load
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Equivalent Dynamic Load (P)** of a rolling contact bearing is defined as that constant stationary radial (or axial) load which, if applied to the bearing, would give the same rating life as the actual combined radial and thrust (axial) loads acting on the bearing under real operating conditions.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

where:
- **$P$** = Equivalent dynamic load (in **N** or **kN**)
- **$F_r$** = Actual radial load acting on the bearing
- **$F_a$** = Actual axial (thrust) load acting on the bearing
- **$X$** = Radial load factor (dimensionless)
- **$Y$** = Axial (thrust) load factor (dimensionless)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Rolling Contact Bearings):**
> The Equivalent Dynamic Load is the *single most critical input* for computing the dynamic load rating ($C$) of a bearing and thereby its rating life ($L_{10}$ or $L_{10h}$). It is the bridge between *real-world combined loading* and *standardized rating life equations* standardized by **ISO 15:2017** and **IS 6455**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bucket of Fatigue"

Imagine you are a **health inspector at a construction site** monitoring fatigue of a worker.

- The worker is pulling a heavy **shopping bag (the radial load $F_r$)** with one hand, while simultaneously pushing a **wheelbarrow (the thrust load $F_a$)** with the other.
- The combined stress on the worker's shoulders is **not simply the sum** of the two efforts — pulling hurts more on the radial joints, while pushing on the arm bones.
- The health inspector wants to **rate the worker's daily wear-out** in terms of *one single equivalent strain* — say, the equivalent of pulling a bag of weight $P$ alone.
- That single, fictitious, equivalent load is what the worker could "carry alone" to produce the **same fatigue damage** as the real dual-loading situation.

This fictitious load $P$ is the **Equivalent Dynamic Load** of the bearing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need an "equivalent" load?**
> Bearing manufacturers test and rate their bearings under **pure radial** OR **pure axial** loads. But in real machines (gears, shafts, pulleys), bearings almost always face a **combination** of radial + axial forces. To use the manufacturer's ratings, we must convert this combination into one *equivalent* load.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $P$ | Equivalent dynamic load | **N** or **kN** |
| $F_r$ | Radial load on bearing | **N** or **kN** |
| $F_a$ | Axial (thrust) load on bearing | **N** or **kN** |
| $C_o$ | Static load rating | **N** or **kN** |
| $F_a / C_o$ | Load ratio (dimensionless) | — |
| $X$ | Radial factor | dimensionless |
| $Y$ | Thrust factor | dimensionless |
| $f_w$ | Load/Application factor | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factors $X$ and $Y$ are **not constants for a given bearing type** — they depend on the **load ratio $\dfrac{F_a}{C_o}$** and are read from manufacturer/standard tables (e.g., SKL/IS 6455 catalogue tables).

---

## 1.4 The Need for Rotation Factor — A Key Subtlety

When the **inner ring rotates** and the load is stationary (or vice versa), the same equivalent dynamic load formula applies. However, if the load is **rotating with the inner ring** (e.g., an unbalanced rotating mass on a shaft supported by a stationary outer ring), the load on every rolling element is *different*, and the equivalent dynamic load formula must be modified using a **rotation factor (Rotation of load vs. rotation of ring)**.

This is covered separately in the deep analysis below.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Why Two Factors X and Y?

In a rolling contact bearing, the **load distribution** on the rolling elements is not uniform. When pure radial load $F_r$ acts:
- The rolling element at the **bottom (load zone)** carries the maximum load.
- The line connecting the centre of curvature of the inner race to the load contact point makes an angle that determines the **radial load factor $X$**.

When a **thrust load $F_a$** acts additionally:
- The contact line shifts, and the **internal geometry** of the bearing (contact angle $\alpha$) changes the load distribution.
- This shift **increases** the load on some rolling elements and is captured by the **thrust factor $Y$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** $Y$ is always **greater than $X$** for a given bearing. This reflects the fact that a unit of thrust load causes **more fatigue damage** than a unit of radial load, because thrust loading is distributed over **fewer rolling elements** and at **higher contact stresses**.

---

## 2.2 Boundary Conditions for Selection of X and Y

The bearing manufacturer (SKL, SKF, IS 6455, NTN, etc.) provides a **table** of $X$ and $Y$ values indexed against the **load ratio**:

$$\frac{F_a}{C_o} = \frac{\text{Actual axial load}}{\text{Static load rating}}$$

**General rules:**

1. **If $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \le e$** → The radial load dominates.
   - Use $X = 1.0$, $Y = 0$.
   - Equivalent dynamic load: $P = F_r$

2. **If $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} > e$** → The thrust load is significant.
   - Use $X$ and $Y$ from the catalogue table based on $\dfrac{F_a}{C_o}$.
   - Equivalent dynamic load: $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The limiting value $e$ is also a **table value** (given alongside $X$ and $Y$ for each bearing series). It is the value of $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r}$ at which the radial and thrust load contributions are *just balanced*.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Condition | Application | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | General equivalent dynamic load | N or kN |
| 2 | $P = F_r$ (when $F_a/F_r \le e$) | When thrust is negligible; $X=1, Y=0$ | N or kN |
| 3 | $P = 0.4 \cdot F_r + K \cdot F_a$ | **Approximate formula** (when $F_a/F_r > 1.0$ or unidirectional thrust) | N or kN |
| 4 | $P = 0.5 \cdot F_r + K \cdot F_a$ | Approximate formula (when $0.5 < F_a/F_r \le 1.0$) | N or kN |
| 5 | $P = 0.6 \cdot F_r + K \cdot F_a$ | Approximate formula (when $0.25 < F_a/F_r \le 0.5$) | N or kN |
| 6 | $P = 0.7 \cdot F_r + K \cdot F_a$ | Approximate formula (when $0.1 < F_a/F_r \le 0.25$) | N or kN |
| 7 | $P = F_r$ (when $F_a/F_r \le 0.1$) | Very small thrust | N or kN |
| 8 | $\dfrac{F_a}{C_o}$ | Ratio used to pick $X$ and $Y$ from catalogue | — |
| 9 | $C = P \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{10^6}\right)^{1/k}$ | Dynamic load rating (verification) | N or kN |
| 10 | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^k$ | Rating life in hours | hours |

Where:
- $K$ = factor from table based on bearing type (typically $K = 1.5$ to $1.8$ for tapered roller)
- $k$ = exponent (3 for ball bearings, 10/3 for roller bearings)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The equivalent dynamic load concept is **universally used** in:

- **Gearbox design** — Helical and bevel gears generate combined radial + thrust loads on their support bearings.
- **Electric motor shafts** — The rotor weight + electromagnetic pull create mixed loading on the bearings.
- **Automotive wheel hubs** — Combined vertical (vehicle weight), lateral (cornering), and braking (thrust) loads.
- **Crane slewing rings** — Massive combined loading from gravity, wind, and rotation inertia.
- **Wind turbine main shafts** — Highly fluctuating combined loading from rotor thrust and gravity.

---

## 2.5 The Rotation Factor Concept

| Rotation Condition | Rotation Factor Effect on $P$ |
|---|---|
| Inner ring rotates, load is **stationary** (or rotates with outer ring) | $P$ = calculated from $X, Y$ formula (standard case) |
| Outer ring rotates, load rotates with **inner ring** (uncommon — e.g., some hoists) | Need to consult special catalogue guidance; often a **larger $P$** is required |
| Load rotates with the inner ring (e.g., unbalanced shaft) | Same as inner ring rotation; use the standard formula |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Most KTU problems assume *inner ring rotating, stationary load*, so the standard formula $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ applies directly. Marks are lost when students apply rotation factors without justification from the problem statement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Equivalent Dynamic Load Formula (Conceptual)

The equivalent dynamic load formula is **empirically derived** from the **Hertzian contact stress theory** combined with extensive fatigue test data. Below is the conceptual derivation followed by KTU-style worked problems.

### Step 1 — Hertzian Contact Stress for a Rolling Element

For a ball of radius $r$ pressed against a raceway with a normal force $Q$:

$$Q \propto \left(\sigma_{max}\right)^3$$

This is the famous **cubic relationship** between contact force and maximum Hertzian contact stress.

### Step 2 — Life Equation for a Single Rolling Element

The number of stress cycles to failure (spalling) is:

$$N_i = \left(\frac{Q_{limit}}{Q_i}\right)^k \cdot N_{ref}$$

where $Q_i$ is the load on the $i$-th rolling element, $Q_{limit}$ is the test load, and $k = 3$ (balls) or $10/3$ (rollers).

### Step 3 — Sum Over All Rolling Elements

The bearing life is governed by the **most heavily loaded rolling element**:

$$\frac{1}{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{z} \left(\frac{Q_i}{Q_{limit}}\right)^k$$

### Step 4 — Introducing Radial Factor $X$

For pure radial load $F_r$, the maximum element load is:

$$Q_{max} = \frac{F_r}{z \cdot J_r}$$

where $J_r$ is the radial load distribution integral. Setting $X = 1 / (z J_r)$ gives $P = F_r$.

### Step 5 — Introducing Thrust Factor $Y$

When an additional axial load $F_a$ shifts the contact line, the load on the most loaded element increases by a factor. Through experimental curve fitting:

$$Q_{max} \approx \frac{X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a}{z \cdot J_r}$$

Equating the two expressions for $Q_{max}$:

$$\boxed{P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **conceptual**. The exact values of $X$ and $Y$ come from **standardized test data** compiled by **ISO 15:2017 / IS 6455 / SKL catalogues**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for X, Y, P

The KTU examiner often asks students to *state* how to obtain $X$ and $Y$. Use this decision matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A:} \quad & \text{Compute the load ratio } \frac{F_a}{C_o} \\
\text{Step B:} \quad & \text{Identify the bearing series (e.g., 30000, 50000)} \\
\text{Step C:} \quad & \text{From catalogue: read } X, Y, e \text{ for that } \frac{F_a}{C_o} \\
\text{Step D:} \quad & \text{Compute } \frac{F_a}{F_r} \text{ and compare with } e: \\[4pt]
& \bullet \;\; \text{If } \frac{F_a}{F_r} \le e \Rightarrow P = F_r \\
& \bullet \;\; \text{If } \frac{F_a}{F_r} > e \Rightarrow P = X F_r + Y F_a \\
\text{Step E:} \quad & \text{Apply application factor } f_w \text{ if load is dynamic/shock}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Basic Equivalent Dynamic Load

**Problem [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 style]:**

A deep-groove ball bearing (SKL series 6308) is subjected to a radial load of $F_r = 4500$ N and an axial thrust load of $F_a = 1800$ N. The static load rating is $C_o = 24\,000$ N. The catalogue gives the following data for this bearing:

| $F_a/C_o$ | $X$ | $Y$ | $e$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.025 | 0.56 | 1.85 | 0.22 |
| 0.040 | 0.56 | 1.70 | 0.24 |
| 0.070 | 0.56 | 1.55 | 0.27 |
| 0.130 | 0.56 | 1.40 | 0.31 |
| 0.250 | 0.56 | 1.25 | 0.37 |
| 0.500 | 0.56 | 1.05 | 0.44 |

Compute the equivalent dynamic load.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the load ratio:**

$$
\frac{F_a}{C_o} = \frac{1800}{24\,000} = 0.075
$$

**Step 2 — Interpolate $X$ and $Y$ from the catalogue table:**

The ratio $0.075$ lies between the rows for $0.070$ and $0.130$.

By linear interpolation (KTU's preferred method for $X$, $Y$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 1.55 + \frac{(0.075 - 0.070)}{(0.130 - 0.070)} \cdot (1.40 - 1.55) \\
  &= 1.55 + \frac{0.005}{0.060} \cdot (-0.15) \\
  &= 1.55 - 0.0125 \\
  &= 1.5375
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly, interpolate $e$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e &= 0.27 + \frac{0.005}{0.060} \cdot (0.31 - 0.27) \\
  &= 0.27 + 0.0033 \\
  &= 0.2733
\end{aligned}
$$

$X$ remains **0.56** (constant for deep-groove ball bearings across this range).

**Step 3 — Compare $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r}$ with $e$:**

$$
\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{1800}{4500} = 0.40
$$

Since $0.40 > e = 0.2733$, we use the full formula.

**Step 4 — Compute the equivalent dynamic load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a \\
  &= 0.56 \times 4500 + 1.5375 \times 1800 \\
  &= 2520 + 2767.5 \\
  &= 5287.5 \;\; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 5288 \;\; \text{N}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Application Factor Included

**Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024 style]:**

A cylindrical roller bearing supports a radial load of $F_r = 8000$ N and a thrust load of $F_a = 1500$ N. The application involves moderate shock (use load factor $f_w = 1.5$). For the chosen bearing, $C_o = 35\,000$ N. From the catalogue, at $F_a/C_o = 0.04$: $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.45$, $e = 0.26$.

Determine the equivalent dynamic load.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r}$:**

$$
\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{1500}{8000} = 0.1875
$$

Since $0.1875 < e = 0.26$, the thrust load is small enough that $P = F_r$ is the correct direct formula.

**Step 2 — But the catalogue says to use $X$ and $Y$ if $F_a > 0$:**

Since $F_a > 0$ and the table value exists, we use:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a \\
  &= 0.56 \times 8000 + 1.45 \times 1500 \\
  &= 4480 + 2175 \\
  &= 6655 \;\; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on $f_w$:** The application factor $f_w$ is **NOT** applied at the equivalent load calculation stage in the KTU curriculum. Instead, it is multiplied at the **rating life** stage:
> $$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{f_w \cdot P}\right)^k$$
> This is a common KTU mistake — students apply $f_w$ to $P$, which is incorrect.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Approximate Formula Application

**Problem:**

A tapered roller bearing (SKL series 30208) has $F_r = 6$ kN and $F_a = 5$ kN. Use the approximate formula with $K = 1.7$. Find the equivalent dynamic load.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the load ratio:**

$$
\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{5}{6} = 0.833
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the appropriate approximate formula:**

Since $0.5 < 0.833 \le 1.0$, we use:

$$
P = 0.5 \cdot F_r + K \cdot F_a
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 0.5 \times 6000 + 1.7 \times 5000 \\
  &= 3000 + 8500 \\
  &= 11\,500 \;\; \text{N} \\
  &= 11.5 \;\; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 11.5 \;\; \text{kN}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Equivalent Dynamic Load Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Fr and Fa from Shaft Design] --> B[Determine Bearing Type and Series]
    B --> C[Look up Co from Catalogue]
    C --> D[Compute Load Ratio Fa / Co]
    D --> E[Read X, Y, e from Catalogue Table for that Fa/Co]
    E --> F[Compute Fa / Fr]
    F --> G{Is Fa/Fr less than or equal to e?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Use P = Fr with X=1, Y=0]
    G -- No --> I[Use P = X Fr + Y Fa]
    H --> J[Equivalent Dynamic Load P]
    I --> J[Equivalent Dynamic Load P]
    J --> K[Apply Application Factor fw in Life Equation, NOT here]
    K --> L[Compute Rating Life L10h]
    L --> M[Verify against Required Life]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Types of Bearing Loads

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Real World Machine] --> B[Radial Load Fr]
    A --> C[Axial Thrust Load Fa]
    A --> D[Moment Load M]
    B --> E[Pure Radial: P = Fr]
    C --> F[Pure Thrust: P = Fa]
    B --> G[Combined Loading]
    C --> G[Combined Loading]
    G --> H[Equivalent Dynamic Load P = X Fr + Y Fa]
    H --> I[Use in Life Equation]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Load Distribution Shift Due to Thrust

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Pure Radial Load Fr] --> S1[Load Zone: Bottom of Bearing]
    S0 --> S2[Symmetric Load Distribution]
    S2 --> S3[P = Fr, X=1, Y=0]
    T0[Combined Load Fr and Fa] --> T1[Load Zone: Shifts Towards Thrust Side]
    T0 --> T2[Asymmetric Distribution]
    T2 --> T3[More Elements Loaded]
    T2 --> T4[Hertzian Stress Increases]
    T3 --> T5[P = X Fr + Y Fa]
    T4 --> T5[P = X Fr + Y Fa]
    T5 --> T6[Higher P than Fr alone]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Sources of Axial Load in Typical Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application] --> B[Gearbox]
    A --> C[Vertical Shaft Pump]
    A --> D[Automotive Wheel Hub]
    B --> E[Helical Gear Thrust]
    C --> F[Rotor Weight Acts as Thrust]
    D --> G[Braking Force and Cornering Force]
    E --> H[Need Combined Fa and Fr]
    F --> H[Need Combined Fa and Fr]
    G --> H[Need Combined Fa and Fr]
    H --> I[Calculate P = X Fr + Y Fa]
    I --> J[Select Bearing]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Define the term "Equivalent Dynamic Load" of a rolling contact bearing. State the standard formula used to compute it.**

**Model Answer:**

The equivalent dynamic load $P$ of a rolling contact bearing is defined as that constant stationary radial (or axial) load which produces the same life as the actual combined radial and axial loads acting on the bearing.

The standard formula is:

$$
P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a
$$

where $X$ is the radial load factor and $Y$ is the thrust (axial) load factor. These are read from manufacturer catalogues based on the load ratio $F_a/C_o$.

If the thrust load is small such that $F_a / F_r \le e$, then $P = F_r$ (with $X=1, Y=0$).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**What is the role of the static load rating $C_o$ in determining the equivalent dynamic load of a bearing?**

**Model Answer:**

The static load rating $C_o$ is used to compute the **load ratio** $F_a / C_o$, which is the key index for selecting the values of $X$ and $Y$ from the bearing catalogue.

- For the same $F_a$, a bearing with a *higher* $C_o$ will have a *smaller* $F_a / C_o$ ratio, leading to a *smaller* $Y$ factor and hence a *smaller* equivalent dynamic load $P$.
- $C_o$ essentially tells us "how much thrust the bearing can statically tolerate" relative to the applied thrust.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Format:** Two full questions of 14 marks each are provided below. **You only need to answer ONE** as per the internal choice rule.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

A 6308-series deep-groove ball bearing is subjected to:
- Radial load: $F_r = 5$ kN
- Axial thrust load: $F_a = 2$ kN
- Static load rating: $C_o = 22$ kN
- Speed: $n = 1450$ rpm
- Application factor: $f_w = 1.2$ (light shock)

From the catalogue, the $X, Y, e$ table for series 6308 is:

| $F_a / C_o$ | $X$ | $Y$ | $e$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.025 | 0.56 | 1.85 | 0.22 |
| 0.040 | 0.56 | 1.70 | 0.24 |
| 0.070 | 0.56 | 1.55 | 0.27 |
| 0.130 | 0.56 | 1.40 | 0.31 |
| 0.250 | 0.56 | 1.25 | 0.37 |

**(a)** Determine the equivalent dynamic load $P$ on the bearing. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** If the basic dynamic load rating of the bearing is $C = 38$ kN, determine its rating life in hours. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Compute the load ratio $F_a / C_o$:**

$$
\frac{F_a}{C_o} = \frac{2}{22} = 0.0909
$$

**Step 2 — Interpolate $Y$ and $e$ from the catalogue:**

$0.0909$ lies between $0.070$ and $0.130$.

For $Y$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 1.55 + \frac{(0.0909 - 0.070)}{(0.130 - 0.070)} \cdot (1.40 - 1.55) \\
  &= 1.55 + \frac{0.0209}{0.060} \cdot (-0.15) \\
  &= 1.55 - 0.0523 \\
  &= 1.4977
\end{aligned}
$$

For $e$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e &= 0.27 + \frac{0.0209}{0.060} \cdot (0.31 - 0.27) \\
  &= 0.27 + 0.0139 \\
  &= 0.2839
\end{aligned}
$$

$X = 0.56$ (constant).

**Step 3 — Compute $F_a / F_r$:**

$$
\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40
$$

Since $0.40 > e = 0.2839$, we use the full formula.

**Step 4 — Equivalent dynamic load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a \\
  &= 0.56 \times 5 + 1.4977 \times 2 \\
  &= 2.80 + 2.9954 \\
  &= 5.795 \;\; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 5.80 \;\; \text{kN}}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

For a deep-groove **ball bearing**, the life exponent is $k = 3$.

**Rating life equation with application factor:**

$$
L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{f_w \cdot P}\right)^k
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= \frac{10^6}{60 \times 1450} \cdot \left(\frac{38}{1.2 \times 5.795}\right)^3 \\
        &= \frac{10^6}{87\,000} \cdot \left(\frac{38}{6.954}\right)^3 \\
        &= 11.494 \cdot (5.464)^3 \\
        &= 11.494 \times 163.12 \\
        &= 1874.7 \;\; \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{10h} \approx 1875 \;\; \text{hours}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The bearing will last approximately 1875 hours before 10% of a statistically large population fails.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

A shaft is supported by a pair of **tapered roller bearings** in an O-arrangement (back-to-back). The shaft carries a helical gear that produces:
- Radial force on the bearing: $F_r = 9$ kN
- Axial thrust force: $F_a = 4.5$ kN
- Static load rating: $C_o = 45$ kN
- The application is a **machine tool** (use $f_w = 1.0$ to $1.2$, take $f_w = 1.2$).
- Speed: $n = 1200$ rpm
- Required life: $L_{10h} \ge 10\,000$ hours

From the catalogue for a tapered roller bearing, the approximate formula constants are $K = 1.7$ for $0.5 < F_a/F_r \le 1.0$.

**(a)** Calculate the equivalent dynamic load using the approximate formula. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compute the required basic dynamic load rating $C$ for the desired life. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Compute the load ratio:**

$$
\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{4.5}{9} = 0.5
$$

Since $0.5 < F_a / F_r \le 1.0$ requires the formula $P = 0.5 F_r + K F_a$ (at the boundary case, this is the appropriate row).

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 0.5 \times F_r + K \times F_a \\
  &= 0.5 \times 9 + 1.7 \times 4.5 \\
  &= 4.5 + 7.65 \\
  &= 12.15 \;\; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 12.15 \;\; \text{kN}}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

For **tapered roller bearings**, $k = 10/3 \approx 3.333$.

**Rearranging the life equation for $C$:**

$$
C = P \cdot f_w \cdot \left(\frac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/k}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the inside term:**

$$
\frac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6} = \frac{60 \times 1200 \times 10\,000}{10^6} = 720
$$

**Step 2 — Take the $1/k$ power:**

$$
(720)^{1/3.333} = (720)^{0.3} = 7.78
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 12.15 \times 1.2 \times 7.78 \\
  &= 113.43 \;\; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{required} \approx 113.4 \;\; \text{kN}}
$$

The designer should now select a tapered roller bearing from the catalogue with $C \ge 113.4$ kN.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Applying $f_w$ to $P$ instead of the life equation** — This is the #1 error. $f_w$ multiplies the life equation's load term, not $P$ itself.
> 2. **Forgetting to interpolate** — When $F_a/C_o$ falls between catalogue rows, students must **interpolate linearly** to obtain $Y$ and $e$. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using $X = 1, Y = 0$ wrongly** — Students sometimes set $Y=0$ when $F_a > 0$. This is only valid if $F_a/F_r \le e$.
> 4. **Using $k = 3$ for roller bearings** — Roller bearings use $k = 10/3$. Mixing this up causes a 5–10% error in life.
> 5. **Unit mismatch** — Mixing kN and N in the same equation. Stick to one consistent unit (preferably **N** for catalogue values).
> 6. **Ignoring $C_o$ in problem data** — $C_o$ is *always* needed to look up $X, Y, e$. Students often skip this step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Equivalent Dynamic Load $P$** converts real combined loading into a single equivalent load for life calculations.
- **Formula:** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ (general) or $P = F_r$ (if $F_a/F_r \le e$).
- **$X$ and $Y$ are NOT constants** — they depend on $F_a / C_o$ and are read from **catalogue tables**.
- **$e$** is the threshold value; below it, thrust is negligible; above it, thrust matters.
- **Approximate formulas** (e.g., $P = 0.5 F_r + K F_a$) are used when the bearing type is known but exact catalogue values are unavailable.
- **Application factor $f_w$** is applied to the **life equation**, NOT to $P$ directly.
- **Life exponent $k$**: 3 for ball bearings, 10/3 for roller bearings.
- **Units**: Stay consistent. Catalogue $C$ and $C_o$ are in **N** or **kN**; match $P$ to the same unit.
- **Rotation factor**: Standard formula assumes inner ring rotates with stationary load — the most common case in KTU problems.
- **Deep-groove ball bearings**: $X = 0.56$ across most operating ranges.
- **Tapered roller bearings**: Always use $K$ from catalogue; approximate formulas are common in KTU.
- **Always compute $F_a / C_o$** before looking up $X$ and $Y$ — this is the gateway step.
- **When $F_a/C_o$ falls between two catalogue rows**, linear interpolation is **mandatory** for full marks.
- **Bearing life in hours**: $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 n} \left( \dfrac{C}{f_w P} \right)^k$
- **Basic dynamic load rating $C$**: This is the load at which the bearing achieves 1 million revolutions of $L_{10}$ life.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic for $X$ and $Y$ lookup:**
> **"First ratio $F_a/C_o$, then read $X, Y, e$, then compare $F_a/F_r$ with $e$."** — Follow these three steps in this order for every KTU problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
