topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bearing Life

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Bearing Life – Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Bearing Life** is defined as the number of revolutions (or hours of operation at a constant speed) that a bearing can endure before the first signs of material fatigue (spalling) develop on the raceways or rolling elements. Because of statistical variability in material homogeneity and surface finish, bearing life is always expressed as a *probabilistic* quantity rather than a deterministic one.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Basic Rating Life (L₁₀):** The number of revolutions (or hours) at constant speed that **90 % of a sufficiently large group of identical bearings** will complete or exceed before the first evidence of fatigue develops. This is the standard, universally accepted life metric used by ISO 281, IS 6455, and all bearing manufacturers (SKF, FAG, NBC, Tata).

The remaining 10 % of bearings will fail *before* L₁₀ due to statistical outliers — this is analogous to mortality curves in actuarial science.

### 1.2 Related Life Notions

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Basic Rating Life (90 % reliability)** | L₁₀ | 90 % of bearings survive |
| **Median Life (50 % reliability)** | L₅₀ | 50 % of bearings survive |
| **Adjusted Rating Life** | Lₙₐ | Life modified for lubrication, contamination, and reliability |
| **Basic Dynamic Load Rating** | **C** | Constant stationary radial (or axial) load that a bearing can theoretically endure for **1 million revolutions** |
| **Equivalent Dynamic Load** | **P** | Hypothetical constant load giving same life as actual combined loading |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 1 million revolutions?** Historically chosen by ISO/AFBMA because a typical bearing at 33.33 r/min completes exactly 1 million revolutions in **500 hours**, an easily remembered benchmark for test rigs.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – The "Bulb Failure Curve"

Imagine you buy **1000 identical 5-watt LED bulbs** rated at "10 000 hours". If you switch them all on simultaneously, they will **NOT** all fail exactly at 10 000 hours. Some will burn out at 2 000 h, some at 25 000 h. Statistical analysis shows:

- **50 %** fail by ~10 000 h ⇒ this is the *median life* (L₅₀).
- **10 %** fail before a much shorter time ⇒ this is the *rating life* (L₁₀).
- The shape of the failure distribution is **Weibull**, with the slope (Weibull slope *e*) telling us how "predictable" the failure pattern is.

Bearings behave **identically**: rated life L₁₀ is conservative because it represents the *early-failure* end of the distribution — exactly what an engineer designing a gearbox or motor **must** use to guarantee reliability.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick recall rule:** L₅₀ ≈ **5 × L₁₀** for ball bearings and L₅₀ ≈ **4.4 × L₁₀** for roller bearings, because of the Weibull slope differences (1.5 vs 1.18).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Life Equation (ISO 281 / IS 6455)

The fundamental relationship between dynamic load and fatigue life is the **Palmgren–Lundgren–Miner equation**, simplified for constant load and speed:

$$
L_{10} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}
$$

where the exponent **p** depends on the bearing kinematics:

$$
p \;=\;
\begin{cases}
3 & \text{for ball bearings (point contact)} \\
\dfrac{10}{3} \;=\; 3.333 & \text{for roller bearings (line contact)}
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.2 Why p = 3 for Balls and 10/3 for Rollers? (Engineering Rationale)

- In **ball bearings**, contact is theoretically a *point* (Hertzian contact). Hertz contact theory shows that maximum subsurface shear stress scales with the cube root of load, giving the **inverse-cubic** law.
- In **roller bearings**, contact is theoretically a *line*. Stress analysis (Lundberg–Palmgren) yields the **10/3 power** law because the contact ellipse is longer in one direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rolling elements have NO mechanical wear in normal operation.** Failure is purely **rolling-contact fatigue (RCF)** — subsurface-initiated cracks that eventually spall the surface.

## 2.2.1 Practical Units of Life

Life in the raw equation is in **millions of revolutions**. To convert to engineering units:

$$
L_{10h} \;=\; \dfrac{10^{6} \cdot L_{10}}{60 \cdot n} \quad \text{[hours]}
$$

where *n* = rotational speed in **r/min**.

For variable-speed duty cycles, ISO 281 uses the **equivalent average speed** concept (also called the *mean effective speed*):

$$
n_{eq} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum \, q_i \, n_i \, t_i}{\sum \, q_i \, t_i}
$$

where $q_i = P_i / P_{max}$ and $t_i$ = duration of each operating phase.

## 2.3 Equivalent Dynamic Load – Radial and Axial Combined Cases

A real shaft transmits **both radial and axial loads**. We replace them with a single hypothetical load:

$$
P \;=\; X \, F_r \;+\; Y \, F_a
$$

where:
- **F_r** = radial load on bearing
- **F_a** = axial (thrust) load on bearing
- **X** = radial load factor
- **Y** = thrust load factor

These factors depend on the **ratio F_a / F_r** and on the bearing geometry, and are tabulated in the manufacturer's catalogue. A commonly used "rules-of-thumb" appearance:

| Bearing type | Condition | X | Y |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deep-groove ball (single row) | F_a / F_r ≤ e | 1.0 | 0 |
| Deep-groove ball (single row) | F_a / F_r > e | 0.56 | tabulated |
| Cylindrical roller (no flange) | — | 1.0 | 0 |
| Tapered roller / Angular contact | — | 0.4 | 0.4 cot α |

The **limiting value e** is also catalogue-supplied (typically 0.2 – 0.4).

> [!WARNING]
> The *most common KTU mistake*: students compute X·F_r + Y·F_a using **default** X = 0.56 and Y = 1.4 without first checking the **F_a/F_r ratio against the catalogue value e**. Always verify whether axial or radial load is dominant.

## 2.4 Adjusted Rating Life (a₁ · a₂₃ Factors)

ISO 281:2007 introduced a modified life equation:

$$
L_{na} \;=\; a_1 \, a_{23} \, L_{10}
$$

where:
- **a₁** = reliability factor (1.00 for 90 %, 0.62 for 95 %, 0.21 for 99 %, etc.)
- **a₂₃** = combined factor for lubrication, contamination, and operating temperature. Calculated as:

$$
a_{23} \;=\; a_2 \, \cdot \, a_3
$$

Typical a₂₃ values:

| Operating Condition | a₂₃ |
|---|---|
| Perfect cleanliness, adequate lubrication | 1.0 – 1.5 |
| Normal industrial, filtered oil | 0.5 – 0.8 |
| Heavy contamination, marginal lube | 0.1 – 0.3 |

## 2.5 Application Factors (Service Factors)

For shock, vibration, and misalignment not captured by P, designers often use an **application factor K**:

$$
P_{design} \;=\; K \cdot P_{equivalent}
$$

| Operating Condition | K |
|---|---|
| Smooth, no shock (electric motor, fan) | 1.0 – 1.2 |
| Light shock (gearbox, pump) | 1.2 – 1.5 |
| Heavy shock (crusher, rolling mill) | 1.5 – 3.0 |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Board Examination Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise these 7 equations — they cover ~95 % of bearing-life problems asked in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$ | Basic rating life, millions of revolutions |
| 2 | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}\,L_{10}}{60\,n}$ | Life in hours |
| 3 | $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ | Equivalent dynamic load |
| 4 | $C = P \, L_{10}^{1/p}$ | Required dynamic load rating |
| 5 | $L_{na} = a_1 \, a_{23} \, L_{10}$ | Adjusted life |
| 6 | $P_{design} = K \cdot P$ | With application factor |
| 7 | $n_{eq} = \dfrac{\sum P_i^{p}\,n_i}{\sum P_i^{p}}$ | Variable-speed equivalent |

**Recollection aid:** *Life loves cube, hours love 60n, equivalent load loves X and Y, reliability loves a₁, lubrication loves a₂₃.*

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bearing-life equations are the *backbone* of:

- **Electric-motor design** (sleeve vs ball — 20 000 h vs 40 000 h design lives)
- **Wind-turbine gearbox certification** (L₁₀ ≥ 130 000 h under IEC 61400-1)
- **Automotive wheel bearings** (L₁₀ ≥ 300 000 km ≈ 7 500 h @ 1000 r/min)
- **Aerospace bearing selection** (a₁ = 0.05 for 99.9 % reliability)
- **Railway axle-box design** (UIC 510-2 mandates minimum 1.5 million km)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cubic Life Law (Hertz-Lundberg Model)

We derive the *p = 3* ball-bearing case from Hertz contact theory. A sphere of radius *r* pressed against a flat raceway with normal load *Q* produces a **contact circle of radius *a***:

$$
a \;=\; \left(\dfrac{3\,Q\,r}{4\,E^{*}}\right)^{1/3}
$$

where $E^{*}$ is the effective elastic modulus (combining Young's moduli and Poisson's ratios of ball and race).

The **maximum subsurface shear stress** (which initiates fatigue cracks) lies at depth ≈ 0.78 *a* below the surface and equals:

$$
\tau_0 \;=\; k_1 \, \dfrac{Q^{1/3}}{r^{2/3}}
$$

where $k_1$ collects material constants.

The shear stress *cycles* every time the ball rotates through the loaded zone. If the bearing runs at speed *n*, the **number of stress cycles** per revolution = number of balls × loaded-zone fraction. The critical alternating stress amplitude that causes fatigue after *N* cycles (S-N curve, Basquin's law) is:

$$
\tau_0 \,=\; k_2 \, N^{1/e}
$$

where *e* is the Weibull slope (≈ 10/9 for ball bearings ⇒ *e* ≈ 1.11, commonly rounded to *e* = 10/9).

**Setting the two shear-stress expressions equal** to find *N* as a function of *Q*:

$$
k_1 \, \dfrac{Q^{1/3}}{r^{2/3}} \;=\; k_2 \, N^{1/e}
$$

$$
N^{1/e} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{k_1}{k_2}\right) \dfrac{Q^{1/3}}{r^{2/3}}
$$

$$
N \;=\; \left(\dfrac{k_1}{k_2}\right)^{e} \dfrac{Q^{e/3}}{r^{2e/3}}
$$

Since load is distributed over *z* balls and each carries load $Q = P/z$:

$$
N \;=\; \left(\dfrac{k_1}{k_2}\right)^{e} \dfrac{(P/z)^{e/3}}{r^{2e/3}} \;\propto\; P^{e/3}
$$

Now substitute $e = 10/9$ (empirical value for ball bearings):

$$
\dfrac{e}{3} \;=\; \dfrac{10/9}{3} \;=\; \dfrac{10}{27} \;\approx\; \dfrac{1}{3}
$$

Hence:

$$
N \;\propto\; P^{1/3} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; L \;\propto\; P^{-1/3} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; P \;\propto\; L^{-1/3} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; L \;=\; \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3}
$$

**This is the cubic life law.** For roller bearings, line contact gives $e = 9/8$ (line-contact Weibull slope) and the same derivation yields $L = (C/P)^{10/3}$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU board proof question:** Examiners frequently ask "prove L₁₀ = (C/P)³ for ball bearings." Reproduce the derivation above in 5-6 neat steps — full marks guaranteed.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example – Bearing Selection

**Problem (KTU-type 14-mark):**

A deep-groove ball bearing 6308 operates at 1500 r/min under a radial load of 4 kN and an axial load of 1.5 kN. Required life = 10 000 hours with 95 % reliability. The catalogue data for this bearing are:

- C (basic dynamic load rating) = 41.5 kN
- e = 0.27, X = 0.56, Y = 1.65
- Application factor K = 1.3

**Step 1 – Compute the load ratio F_a/F_r:**

$$
\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \;=\; \dfrac{1.5}{4.0} \;=\; 0.375
$$

Since $0.375 > e = 0.27$, we use the *high axial-load* line of the table → **X = 0.56, Y = 1.65**.

**Step 2 – Equivalent dynamic load:**

$$
P \;=\; X\,F_r \;+\; Y\,F_a \;=\; (0.56)(4.0) \;+\; (1.65)(1.5)
$$

$$
P \;=\; 2.24 \;+\; 2.475 \;=\; 4.715 \;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 3 – Apply application factor:**

$$
P_{design} \;=\; K \cdot P \;=\; 1.3 \times 4.715 \;=\; 6.130 \;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 4 – Compute basic rating life in revolutions:**

$$
L_{10} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{C}{P_{design}}\right)^{3} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{41.5}{6.130}\right)^{3}
$$

$$
\dfrac{41.5}{6.130} \;=\; 6.770
$$

$$
L_{10} \;=\; 6.770^{3} \;=\; 310.4 \;\text{million revolutions}
$$

**Step 5 – Convert to hours:**

$$
L_{10h} \;=\; \dfrac{10^{6} \times 310.4}{60 \times 1500} \;=\; \dfrac{3.104 \times 10^{8}}{9 \times 10^{4}}
$$

$$
L_{10h} \;=\; 3\,449 \;\text{hours}
$$

**Step 6 – Apply reliability factor for 95 %:**

From ISO 281 table, $a_1 = 0.62$ for 95 % reliability:

$$
L_{na} \;=\; a_1 \cdot L_{10} \;=\; 0.62 \times 3\,449 \;=\; 2\,138 \;\text{hours}
$$

**Decision:** $L_{na} = 2138 \;h < 10\,000 \;h$. The bearing is **undersized**. Either select a larger 6310 (C ≈ 62 kN) or use a lubrication/contamination factor.

**Step 7 – Required dynamic load rating for 10 000 h:**

Re-arranging equation:

$$
L_{10h} \;=\; \dfrac{10^{6}}{60\,n} \left(\dfrac{C}{P_{design}}\right)^{3} \;\Longrightarrow\; C_{required} \;=\; P_{design}\cdot\left(\dfrac{60\,n\,L_{10h}}{10^{6}}\right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
C_{req} \;=\; 6.13 \cdot \left(\dfrac{60 \times 1500 \times 10\,000}{10^{6}}\right)^{1/3} \;=\; 6.13 \times (900)^{1/3}
$$

$$
900^{1/3} \;=\; 9.654
$$

$$
C_{req} \;=\; 6.13 \times 9.654 \;=\; 59.2 \;\text{kN}
$$

Therefore, select **6310 bearing** (C = 62 kN), giving a design margin of 4.7 %.

## 3.3 Variable-Speed Life Derivation

When load varies *during* operation, ISO 281 replaces variable load with the *equivalent constant load* $P_m$ such that fatigue damage is identical:

$$
P_m^{p} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum \, P_i^{p}\, t_i \, n_i}{\sum \, t_i \, n_i}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Why $P_i^p$? Because Palmgren-Miner's linear damage rule states that fractional damage per phase = (cycles at $P_i$)/(life at $P_i$) = $(P_i/C)^p$. Summing damages over all phases = 1.

For a *two-speed* cycle (typical in machine tools, presses, conveyors):

$$
P_m \;=\; \left[\dfrac{P_1^{p}\,t_1\,n_1 \;+\; P_2^{p}\,t_2\,n_2}{t_1\,n_1 \;+\; t_2\,n_2}\right]^{1/p}
$$

and life:

$$
L_{10h} \;=\; \dfrac{10^{6}}{60\,n_{eq}} \left(\dfrac{C}{P_m}\right)^{p}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bearing Life Calculation – Master Flowchart

The following Mermaid block depicts the **end-to-end logic** an engineer follows while selecting a rolling-element bearing. Study it before attempting any 14-mark KTU problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Shaft, Speed n, Loads Fr and Fa given] --> B[Compute Load Ratio Fa over Fr]
    B --> C{Fa over Fr greater than or equal to e}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use X and Y from upper line]
    C -- No --> E[Use X = 1.0 and Y = 0]
    D --> F[Compute Equivalent Dynamic Load P = X*Fr + Y*Fa]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Apply Application Factor: Pdes = K * P]
    G --> H{Variable speed?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Compute Equivalent Average Load Pm using Miner Rule]
    H -- No --> J[Use Pdes as Pm]
    I --> K[Compute Basic Rating Life L10 = C over Pm raised to p]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Convert L10 to hours: L10h = 1e6 * L10 over 60 * n]
    L --> M[Apply Reliability Factor a1 for required reliability]
    M --> N[Apply Lubrication Factor a23]
    N --> O{Adjusted Life Lna greater than or equal to Required Life?}
    O -- No --> P[Select Next Higher Bearing Series]
    P --> K
    O -- Yes --> Q[Bearing Selection Complete]
```

## 4.2 Bearing Failure Modes – Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rolling Bearing Failure Modes] --> B[Fatigue Spalling]
    A --> C[Wear]
    A --> D[Plastic Brinelling]
    A --> E[False Brinelling]
    A --> F[Corrosion]
    A --> G[Cage Failure]
    A --> H[Electrical Erosion]
    B --> B1[Subsurface Initiated Cracks]
    B --> B2[Surface Initiated Cracks]
    C --> C1[Abrasive Particles]
    C --> C2[Adhesive Transfer]
    D --> D1[Static Overload]
    D --> D2[Vibration at Rest]
    E --> E1[Micro-slip Oscillation]
```

## 4.3 Force Vector on a Deep-Groove Ball Bearing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Radial Load Fr acting vertically downward on shaft] --> R2[Resolves into contact angle of bearing]
    A1[Axial Load Fa acting along shaft axis] --> R2
    R2 --> R3[Each ball carries Normal Load Qi]
    R3 --> R4[Qi = Load Share Function of Fr Fa and angle]
    R4 --> R5[Equivalent Dynamic Load P = X*Fr + Y*Fa]
```

## 4.4 Relationship Between ISO Life, Reliability, and Weibull Slope

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Weibull Distribution with Slope e] --> L[L10 Life corresponds to 90 percent survival]
    W --> M[L50 Life corresponds to 50 percent survival]
    W --> H[L5 Life corresponds to 5 percent survival]
    L --> L1[L5 over L10 = 0.62 for balls]
    L --> L2[L50 over L10 = 5 for balls]
    L --> L3[L50 over L10 = 4.4 for rollers]
    M --> L1
    M --> L2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Block-diagram revision aid:** for any KTU question, *always start* by writing these four mini-flows on the answer booklet's margin: (i) ratio → X, Y, (ii) P → K·P, (iii) L₁₀ → L₁₀h, (iv) L₁₀ → Lₙₐ via a₁·a₂₃. Examiners reward logical flow, even if the final number has a minor arithmetic slip.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Define the term 'Basic Rating Life' (L₁₀) of a rolling-contact bearing as per ISO 281. State the relationship between basic rating life and basic dynamic load rating C for (i) ball bearings and (ii) roller bearings.**

**Model Answer:**
The basic rating life of a rolling-contact bearing is the number of revolutions (or hours at a constant speed) that **90 %** of a sufficiently large group of identical bearings will complete or exceed before the first evidence of fatigue develops.

(i) **Ball bearings:** $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3}$ – millions of revolutions.

(ii) **Roller bearings:** $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3}$ – millions of revolutions.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**What is the equivalent dynamic load on a rolling-contact bearing? Under what condition does the axial load term vanish in the equivalent dynamic load expression?**

**Model Answer:**
The equivalent dynamic load **P** is a hypothetical constant load (purely radial for radial bearings, or combined radial + axial for angular-contact types) that, when applied, would give the same bearing life as the actual combined radial and axial loads acting on the bearing:

$$
P \;=\; X F_r \;+\; Y F_a
$$

The axial term ($Y F_a$) **vanishes** when $F_a / F_r \le e$, i.e., the axial load is small relative to the radial load. In that case, $X = 1.0$ and $Y = 0$, so $P = F_r$. This is the typical condition for cylindrical roller bearings (without flanges) and lightly-loaded deep-groove ball bearings.

---

## 5.2 PART B – Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE guidelines, *every Module-3 question offers an internal choice*. Question A and Question B below are two fully-solved alternatives covering the same cognitive level and outcome (CO3 – *design rolling-contact bearings for given load and life*).

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

A 6312 deep-groove ball bearing supports a radial load of 6 kN and an axial load of 2 kN. The shaft speed is 1200 r/min. Catalogue data: C = 81.6 kN, e = 0.24, X = 0.56, Y = 2.1 (when Fa/Fr > e), and X = 1.0, Y = 0 (when Fa/Fr ≤ e). The desired life is 12 000 hours at 90 % reliability with light shock application (K = 1.2).

**(a) Compute the expected basic rating life (L₁₀h) of the bearing under the given operating condition.** [7 Marks]

**(b) If a reliability of 99 % is required instead, what is the adjusted rating life? Comment on whether the bearing is suitable.** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution – Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Load ratio check.

$$
\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \;=\; \dfrac{2}{6} \;=\; 0.333
$$

Since $0.333 > e = 0.24$, use X = 0.56, Y = 2.1.

**Step 2:** Equivalent dynamic load.

$$
P \;=\; X F_r + Y F_a \;=\; (0.56)(6) + (2.1)(2) \;=\; 3.36 + 4.20 \;=\; 7.56 \;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply application factor.

$$
P_{des} \;=\; K P \;=\; 1.2 \times 7.56 \;=\; 9.072 \;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 4:** Basic rating life in revolutions.

$$
L_{10} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{C}{P_{des}}\right)^{3} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{81.6}{9.072}\right)^{3} \;=\; (8.995)^{3} \;=\; 727.9 \;\text{million rev}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to hours.

$$
L_{10h} \;=\; \dfrac{10^{6} \times 727.9}{60 \times 1200} \;=\; \dfrac{7.279 \times 10^{8}}{7.2 \times 10^{4}} \;=\; 10\,110 \;\text{hours}
$$

**Conclusion:** $L_{10h} = 10\,110 \;\text{h} < 12\,000 \;\text{h}$. Bearing is **slightly under-designed** at 90 % reliability.

**Model Solution – Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Identify reliability factor for 99 %.

From ISO 281 table: $a_1 = 0.21$.

**Step 2:** Adjusted rating life.

$$
L_{na} \;=\; a_1 \cdot L_{10h} \;=\; 0.21 \times 10\,110 \;=\; 2\,123 \;\text{hours}
$$

**Step 3:** Comment.

The adjusted life (2 123 h) is far below 12 000 h. The bearing is **NOT suitable** at 99 % reliability. A larger bearing (e.g., 6314 with C ≈ 110 kN) must be chosen. Alternatively, the operating conditions must be relaxed (lower load or contamination-free lubrication giving $a_{23} > 1$).

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

A cylindrical roller bearing NU 2308 operates with the following duty cycle:

| Phase | Duration | Speed | Radial Load |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | 6 hours/day | 800 r/min | 12 kN |
| II | 2 hours/day | 1500 r/min | 7 kN |

The catalogue basic dynamic load rating is C = 78 kN. The application factor K = 1.4. Required life = 8 000 hours at 90 % reliability.

**(a) Determine the equivalent constant load $P_m$ using Palmgren-Miner damage rule, assuming roller bearing kinematics.** [7 Marks]

**(b) Compute the basic rating life (L₁₀h) and state whether the bearing is acceptable.** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution – Part (a):**

For a cylindrical roller bearing, the exponent $p = 10/3$.

**Step 1:** Compute weighted $P_i^p \cdot n_i \cdot t_i$ terms.

*Phase I:*

$$
P_1^{10/3} = 12^{10/3}
$$

Compute $12^{1/3} = 2.289$, then $12^{10/3} = (12^{1/3})^{10} = (2.289)^{10}$.

$(2.289)^{2} = 5.240$; $(2.289)^{4} = 27.45$; $(2.289)^{8} = 753.5$; $(2.289)^{10} = 753.5 \times 5.240 = 3\,948$.

$$
P_1^{10/3}\,n_1\,t_1 \;=\; 3\,948 \times 800 \times 6 \;=\; 18.95 \times 10^{6}
$$

*Phase II:*

$7^{1/3} = 1.913$; $(1.913)^{10}$: $(1.913)^{2} = 3.659$; $(1.913)^{4} = 13.39$; $(1.913)^{8} = 179.3$; $(1.913)^{10} = 179.3 \times 3.659 = 656.1$.

$$
P_2^{10/3}\,n_2\,t_2 \;=\; 656.1 \times 1500 \times 2 \;=\; 1.968 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute denominator (weighted speed-duration).

$$
n_1 t_1 + n_2 t_2 = (800)(6) + (1500)(2) = 4800 + 3000 = 7800 \;\text{rev/min}\cdot\text{h}
$$

**Step 3:** Equivalent load.

$$
P_m^{10/3} \;=\; \dfrac{18.95 \times 10^{6} + 1.968 \times 10^{6}}{7800} \;=\; \dfrac{20.92 \times 10^{6}}{7800} \;=\; 2\,682
$$

$$
P_m \;=\; (2\,682)^{3/10} \;=\; (2\,682)^{0.3}
$$

Take logarithms:

$$
\log_{10} P_m \;=\; 0.3 \times \log_{10}(2\,682) \;=\; 0.3 \times 3.4283 \;=\; 1.0285
$$

$$
P_m \;=\; 10^{1.0285} \;=\; 10.68 \;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply application factor.

$$
P_{m,des} \;=\; K P_m \;=\; 1.4 \times 10.68 \;=\; 14.95 \;\text{kN}
$$

**Model Solution – Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Basic rating life.

$$
L_{10} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{C}{P_{m,des}}\right)^{10/3} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{78}{14.95}\right)^{10/3} \;=\; (5.218)^{10/3}
$$

Compute: $\log_{10}(5.218) = 0.7175$. Multiply by 10/3:

$$
\log_{10} L_{10} \;=\; \dfrac{10}{3} \times 0.7175 \;=\; 2.392
$$

$$
L_{10} \;=\; 10^{2.392} \;=\; 246.5 \;\text{million rev}
$$

**Step 2:** Equivalent average speed (one full duty cycle = 8 hours per day).

Per-day revolutions = $(800)(6) + (1500)(2) = 7800$ rev per minute-hour.

Average per-minute speed during operation = $7800 / 8 = 975$ r/min.

**Step 3:** Life in hours.

$$
L_{10h} \;=\; \dfrac{10^{6} \times 246.5}{60 \times 975} \;=\; \dfrac{2.465 \times 10^{8}}{5.85 \times 10^{4}} \;=\; 4\,214 \;\text{hours}
$$

**Conclusion:** $L_{10h} = 4\,214\;\text{h} < 8\,000\;\text{h}$. Bearing is **not acceptable**. The designer must either (i) select the next larger NU 2310 (C ≈ 110 kN), or (ii) reduce the application factor K through better mounting accuracy, or (iii) re-design the load path to reduce $F_r$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mixing units of life:** a very common error is forgetting to multiply by $10^6$ when converting $L_{10}$ from "millions of revolutions" to "revolutions". Always write the conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Exponent confusion:** students frequently write $L = (C/P)^3$ for roller bearings. This gives ~5× over-estimate of life. Memorise: *ball = 3, roller = 10/3*.
> 3. **Skipping the load-ratio check:** using X = 0.56 by default without verifying $F_a / F_r > e$ leads to wrong P. Always state the check explicitly.
> 4. **Ignoring reliability factor a₁:** when the problem states 99 % or 95 %, the examiner expects $L_{na}$ computed, not just $L_{10}$. Missing this loses 3 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting application factor K:** K = 1 means no shock. If the problem says "light shock", K = 1.2 minimum.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap – Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Definition of L₁₀:** 90 % survival, probabilistic metric, Weibull-distributed.
- **Exponent p:** p = 3 (ball), p = 10/3 (roller). *No exceptions in KTU syllabus.*
- **Basic dynamic load rating C:** hypothetical load for 1 million revolutions life.
- **Equivalent dynamic load P:** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$; always verify $F_a/F_r$ vs *e* first.
- **Life in hours:** $L_{10h} = 10^{6} L_{10} / (60 n)$.
- **Reliability factor a₁:** 1.00 (90 %), 0.62 (95 %), 0.21 (99 %), 0.046 (99.9 %).
- **Application factor K:** 1.0 – 1.2 (smooth), 1.2 – 1.5 (light shock), 1.5 – 3.0 (heavy shock).
- **Variable-speed cycle:** $P_m^{p} = \sum P_i^{p} t_i n_i / \sum t_i n_i$.
- **Adjusted life:** $L_{na} = a_1 \cdot a_{23} \cdot L_{10}$.
- **Required C for given life:** $C = P \cdot \left[\dfrac{60 n L_{h}}{10^{6}}\right]^{1/p}$.
- **Median life ≈ 5 × L₁₀** for balls and **≈ 4.4 × L₁₀** for rollers.
- **Selection step-up:** if undersized, increase *C* by the cube (ball) or 10/3-power (roller) of the life-ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line examiner memory hook:** *"C over P cubed for balls, raised to ten-thirds for rollers; multiply by a₁ a₂₃ and convert to hours — that's all KTU wants."* Write this on the first page of your answer booklet to anchor your solution flow.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stribeck's Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stribeck's Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stribeck's Equation** is a foundational design relation in the field of **sliding contact (journal/sleeve) bearings**. It expresses the **bearing pressure (P)** generated on the projected area of a journal bearing, and its associated **PV product (pressure–velocity product)**, which is used as a wear/heat-dissipation criterion for selecting bearing materials and proportions.

Mathematically, the primary Stribeck relation is expressed as:

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot d}$$

Where:
- **P** = Nominal bearing pressure (N/mm² or MPa)
- **W** = Radial load on the bearing (N)
- **L** = Axial length of the bearing (mm)
- **d** = Diameter of the journal (mm)

> [!NOTE]
> The projected area of the bearing is defined as $A_p = L \cdot d$, which represents the rectangular shadow cast by the half-bearing on a plane perpendicular to the load direction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine placing a heavy book flat on a soft foam pad. The pressure the book exerts on the foam equals the **weight** of the book divided by the **area of contact** (its footprint). If you tilt the book on its edge, the same weight now presses onto a much smaller area, and the foam compresses dramatically more.

A journal bearing works on the same idea. The **load (W)** is the weight of the rotor, while the **projected area (L × d)** is the "footprint" the bearing surface presents to the journal. Stribeck's equation simply quantifies how concentrated that load becomes. If the projected area is too small, the pressure skyrockets, leading to wear, seizure, or fatigue.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Limiting PV value (PV limit):** Material-dependent; typically between **0.5 to 4.0 N·m/(mm²·s)** for common babbitt and bronze linings.
> - **Limiting bearing pressure (P):** Ranges from **5 to 30 MPa** depending on the bearing material, load type, and operating speed.
> - **L/d ratio (length-to-diameter):** Standard range is **0.5 to 2.0**, with **1.0** being the most common starting design value.

### 1.4 KTU Syllabus Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme – PEMET743 (Module 3)**, students are expected to:
> - Derive and apply **Stribeck's equation for bearing pressure**.
> - Compute the **PV product** and check it against material limits.
> - Use the **ZN/p parameter** (Hersey number) to verify hydrodynamic film formation.
> - Relate bearing dimensions (L, d) to load, speed, and viscosity for design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic – Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Stribeck framework for a journal bearing design follows this logical chain:

- **Step 1 — Load Identification:** Determine the radial load **W** acting on the journal bearing from the shaft, gear forces, or pulleys.
- **Step 2 — Geometry Selection:** Choose a tentative **L/d ratio** (typically 0.5 to 2.0). The value **L/d = 1** is the standard starting point recommended in KTU textbooks.
- **Step 3 — Projected Area Calculation:** Compute the projected bearing area as $A_p = L \cdot d$. This is the rectangular footprint on which the load is distributed.
- **Step 4 — Bearing Pressure Computation:** Apply the Stribeck relation $P = W / (L \cdot d)$ and verify that $P \leq P_{allowable}$ for the chosen material.
- **Step 5 — Surface Velocity Calculation:** Find the rubbing velocity at the bearing surface using $V = \pi d N / 60$ (when N is in rpm, V in m/s).
- **Step 6 — PV Product Check:** Compute $PV = P \cdot V$ and verify it lies below the material's allowable **PV limit** to prevent overheating and wear.
- **Step 7 — Lubrication Verification (Hersey Number):** Compute the dimensionless parameter $\frac{Z \cdot N}{P}$ (where Z is absolute viscosity in centipoise) and confirm it falls within the hydrodynamic regime, ensuring full fluid-film separation.
- **Step 8 — Iterative Refinement:** If any of the above limits are violated, adjust **L**, **d**, or the bearing material and repeat.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why projected area and not curved area?** Because the pressure distribution over the bearing's curved surface is not uniform; using a flat projected area gives a conservative, easy-to-evaluate average pressure.
- **Why PV product?** Heat generated per unit time is proportional to **friction force × velocity = (P × A) × V**, and the temperature rise (which drives failure) is proportional to **PV** for a given bearing geometry.
- **Why L/d = 1?** Empirical studies (Stribeck, 1902) showed that bearings much shorter than their diameter fail by edge loading, while very long bearings trap heat and misalign easily.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Description / Limiting Value |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Projected Area | $A_p = L \cdot d$ | mm² | Rectangular footprint of half-bearing |
| 2 | Bearing Pressure | $P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot d}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | $P_{allowable} = 5$–$30$ MPa |
| 3 | Surface Velocity | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60}$ | m/s | N in rpm |
| 4 | PV Product | $PV = \dfrac{W \cdot \pi \cdot N}{60 \cdot L}$ | N·m/(mm²·s) or MPa·m/s | $PV_{allowable} = 0.5$–$4.0$ N·m/(mm²·s) |
| 5 | Hersey Number | $H = \dfrac{Z \cdot N}{P}$ | — (dimensionless) | $H \geq \pi/4 \approx 0.785$ for hydrodynamic regime |
| 6 | L/d Ratio | $L/d = 0.5$ to $2.0$ | — | Optimal near **1.0** |
| 7 | Heat Generated | $H_g = \mu \cdot W \cdot V$ | W (J/s) | $\mu$ = coefficient of friction |
| 8 | Heat Dissipated | $H_d = h \cdot A_s \cdot (T_b - T_a)$ | W | $h$ = heat transfer coefficient, $A_s$ = surface area |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** In the Stribeck PV product equation, the **journal diameter d cancels out**, meaning the PV limit governs the **length** selection directly for a given load and speed.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Engineering:** Stribeck's equation guides the design of crankshaft main bearings and connecting-rod big-end bearings, where high PV values are routine.
- **Turbomachinery:** Large steam turbine and centrifugal compressor journal bearings are sized using the projected-area pressure check.
- **Industrial Machinery:** Electric motor rotor bearings, gear reducers, and rolling-mill bearings all rely on the **PV limit** as a first-pass wear check.
- **Tribology Research:** The Stribeck curve (friction coefficient vs. Hersey number ZN/P) defines the boundary, mixed, and hydrodynamic lubrication regimes in modern machine design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stribeck's Bearing Pressure Equation

**Starting premise:** A cylindrical journal of diameter **d** rotates inside a stationary sleeve bearing of axial length **L**, carrying a radial load **W** perpendicular to the journal axis.

**Step 1 — Define the projected area.**
The load is assumed to be supported on the projected (rectangular) area of the bearing, which is the product of length and diameter. This is a design convention because actual contact along the curved surface is non-uniform.

$$A_p = L \cdot d$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of pressure.**
Pressure is force per unit area. Substituting the load and projected area:

$$P = \frac{\text{Load}}{\text{Projected Area}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expressions.**

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot d}$$

This is **Stribeck's first equation** (bearing pressure equation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The pressure $P$ is inversely proportional to the length $L$ and the diameter $d$. Doubling the diameter (at constant load and length) halves the bearing pressure.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Surface Velocity at the Journal-Bearing Interface

**Starting premise:** The journal rotates at **N revolutions per minute (rpm)**. A point on the journal surface traces a circle of circumference $\pi d$ per revolution.

**Step 1 — Distance travelled per revolution.**

$$s_{rev} = \pi \cdot d$$

**Step 2 — Distance travelled per minute.**

$$s_{min} = \pi \cdot d \cdot N$$

**Step 3 — Convert to distance per second (since SI velocity uses seconds).**

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60}$$

This is the rubbing (sliding) velocity at the bearing surface in **m/s** when d is in **meters** and N is in **rpm**.

**Alternative form (d in mm):**

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60\,000} \quad \text{(m/s, with d in mm)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The PV Product (Stribeck Wear Criterion)

**Starting premise:** Multiply the bearing pressure (N/mm²) by the surface velocity (m/s) to obtain the **PV product**, a measure of frictional power per unit bearing area.

**Step 1 — Start with the definitions.**

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot d}, \qquad V = \frac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply P and V.**

$$PV = \frac{W}{L \cdot d} \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the journal diameter d.**

$$\boxed{\,PV = \frac{W \cdot \pi \cdot N}{60 \cdot L}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Verify dimensional consistency.**

- W in Newtons, N in rev/min, L in meters.
- Numerator: $N \cdot \frac{rev}{min}$ = N·rev/min
- Denominator: $m$
- Result: $\frac{N \cdot rev}{m \cdot min}$ = $\frac{N}{m \cdot s}$ (since 1 rev/min = 1/60 rev/s, the $\pi$ and 60 combine correctly)
- Units: **Pa·m/s** or **N·m/(mm²·s)** when P is in MPa.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The diameter $d$ has vanished from the PV product!** This means, for a given load and speed, the bearing **length L alone** controls the PV value. Doubling the length halves the PV product and thus the heat load per unit area.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hersey Number (Stribeck's Lubrication Parameter)

**Starting premise:** The Hersey number (also called the Stribeck parameter or Sommerfeld-variable precursor) groups viscosity, speed, and pressure into a single dimensionless quantity to identify the lubrication regime.

**Step 1 — Definitions.**
- **Z** = absolute (dynamic) viscosity of the lubricant, in centipoise (cP) or Pa·s
- **N** = journal speed, in rpm
- **P** = bearing pressure, in N/mm²

**Step 2 — Form the ratio.**

$$H = \frac{Z \cdot N}{P}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation thresholds.**

- **H < 0.785 ($\pi/4$):** Boundary lubrication — direct metal-to-metal contact, high wear risk.
- **H ≈ 0.785 to ~1.0:** Mixed lubrication — partial film, transitional region.
- **H > 1.0:** Hydrodynamic regime — full fluid film separates surfaces.

**Step 4 — Note on units.**
Strictly speaking, for dimensional consistency, Z must be in reyns (lbf·s/in²) when P is in psi, or in Pa·s when P is in Pa. KTU textbook formulations often retain cP·rpm/MPa as the working unit, treating it as an empirical index rather than a strict dimensionless number.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Length of Bearing from PV Limit (Inverse Design)

**Starting premise:** Given a load W, a speed N, a known journal diameter d, and a material with allowable PV = (PV)ₐ, find the required length L.

**Step 1 — Start from the PV product equation.**

$$PV = \frac{W \cdot \pi \cdot N}{60 \cdot L}$$

**Step 2 — Equate to the allowable value and solve for L.**

$$L = \frac{W \cdot \pi \cdot N}{60 \cdot (PV)_a}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-check against the pressure limit.**

$$L = \frac{W}{d \cdot P_a}$$

The longer of the two L values is selected to satisfy **both** the pressure and the PV constraint.

> [!NOTE]
> This dual check (P-limit and PV-limit) is a **mandatory KTU exam requirement** — the longer length is the design choice.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Design Scenarios

| Scenario | Given | Find | Governing Equation | Typical KTU Sub-Question |
|----------|-------|------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | W, L, d | P | $P = W/(Ld)$ | Compute bearing pressure |
| 2 | d, N | V | $V = \pi d N / 60$ | Compute rubbing velocity |
| 3 | W, N, L | PV | $PV = W \pi N / (60L)$ | Check against material limit |
| 4 | W, N, (PV)ₐ | L | $L = W \pi N / [60 (PV)_a]$ | Find required length |
| 5 | W, d, Pₐ | L | $L = W / (d P_a)$ | Find length from pressure |
| 6 | Z, N, P | Hersey H | $H = ZN / P$ | Identify lubrication regime |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Journal Bearing & Stribeck Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data: Load W, Speed N, Diameter d] --> B[Choose Initial L/d Ratio, typically 1.0]
    B --> C[Compute Projected Area Ap = L × d]
    C --> D[Compute Bearing Pressure P = W / Ap]
    D --> E{Check P ≤ P_allowable}
    E -- No --> F[Increase L or d, return to Step B]
    E -- Yes --> G[Compute Surface Velocity V = π d N / 60]
    G --> H[Compute PV Product = P × V]
    H --> I{Check PV ≤ PV_allowable}
    I -- No --> J[Increase L to reduce PV, return to Step G]
    I -- Yes --> K[Compute Hersey Number H = ZN / P]
    K --> L{Check H ≥ π/4, Hydrodynamic Regime}
    L -- No --> M[Change Lubricant or Adjust Viscosity Z]
    L -- Yes --> N[Final Design Validated: L, d, Material Selected]
    F --> B
    J --> G
    M --> K

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style N fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style E fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style I fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style L fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — PV Product Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Radial Load W] --> C1[Multiplication: P × V]
    B1[Bearing Pressure P] --> C1
    D1[Surface Velocity V] --> C1
    C1 --> E1[PV Product Value]
    E1 --> F1[Compare with Material PV Limit Table]
    F1 --> G1{Acceptable?}
    G1 -- Yes --> H1[Design OK]
    G1 -- No --> I1[Iterate: Increase L, Recheck]

    style A1 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style B1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style D1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style C1 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style H1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style I1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
```

### 4.3 Lubrication Regime Map (Hersey Number Boundaries)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[H less than 0.785] --> R1[Boundary Lubrication - High Wear Risk]
    S2[H between 0.785 and 1.0] --> R2[Mixed Lubrication - Partial Film]
    S3[H greater than 1.0] --> R3[Full Hydrodynamic - Safe Operation]

    style S1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style R1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style S2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style R2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825

    style R3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**State and explain Stribeck's equation for a journal bearing. How is the projected area defined?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1 mark):** Stribeck's equation for bearing pressure in a journal bearing is given by $P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot d}$, where W is the radial load (N), L is the axial length of the bearing (mm), and d is the journal diameter (mm).
- **Projected area definition (1 mark):** The projected area is the rectangular footprint $A_p = L \cdot d$ obtained by projecting the half-bearing surface onto a plane perpendicular to the load direction. It is used (instead of the curved surface area) because pressure distribution along the curved surface is non-uniform.
- **Use (1 mark):** It gives the **nominal bearing pressure** P, which must be compared with the allowable pressure of the bearing material to ensure the design is safe against crushing or fatigue.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define the PV product. Why is it considered a critical design parameter for sleeve bearings?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The PV product is the product of the nominal bearing pressure (P, in N/mm²) and the rubbing velocity at the journal surface (V, in m/s). It has the units **N·m/(mm²·s)** or **MPa·m/s**. Mathematically, $PV = \dfrac{W \pi N}{60 L}$.
- **Physical meaning (1 mark):** PV represents the **frictional heat generated per unit bearing area per unit time**. A high PV value indicates intense localized heating, which can lead to loss of lubricant viscosity, scoring, and seizure.
- **Design use (1 mark):** Bearing materials (babbitt, bronze, PTFE, etc.) are rated with a **limiting PV value**. The calculated PV must be less than this limit, making it a fundamental wear and thermal criterion in journal bearing design.

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

A journal bearing supports a radial load of **W = 50 kN** at a shaft speed of **N = 1500 rpm**. The journal diameter is **d = 100 mm**. The bearing material is **babbitt metal** with the following limits:
- Allowable bearing pressure: $P_a = 10$ MPa
- Allowable PV value: $(PV)_a = 2.0$ N·m/(mm²·s)

**(a)** Determine the **required length L** of the bearing using both the pressure and the PV criteria. State which value governs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the selected length, compute the **rubbing velocity V**, the **Hersey number H**, and identify the **lubrication regime**. Take the lubricant viscosity as **Z = 30 cP**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $W = 50\,000$ N
- $N = 1500$ rpm
- $d = 100$ mm
- $P_a = 10$ N/mm²
- $(PV)_a = 2.0$ N·m/(mm²·s)

**Step 1 — Length from pressure criterion (3 marks).**
From Stribeck's pressure equation:

$$L_1 = \frac{W}{d \cdot P_a} = \frac{50\,000}{100 \times 10} = \frac{50\,000}{1000} = 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Length from PV criterion (3 marks).**
From the PV product equation:

$$L_2 = \frac{W \cdot \pi \cdot N}{60 \cdot (PV)_a} = \frac{50\,000 \times \pi \times 1500}{60 \times 2.0}$$

Numerator: $50\,000 \times \pi \times 1500 = 235\,619\,449$ (approx.)

$$L_2 = \frac{235\,619\,449}{120} \approx 1\,963\,495 \text{ (units: N·m/min / N·m/(mm²·s))}$$

Unit conversion check: $(PV)_a$ is in N·m/(mm²·s), so we need V in m/s and L in meters.

$$V = \frac{\pi \times 0.1 \times 1500}{60} = \frac{471.24}{60} = 7.854 \text{ m/s}$$

$$L_2 = \frac{W \cdot \pi \cdot N}{60\,000 \cdot (PV)_a} \quad \text{(L in m, W in N, N in rpm)}$$

$$L_2 = \frac{50\,000 \times \pi \times 1500}{60\,000 \times 2.0} = \frac{235\,619\,449}{120\,000} \approx 1.963 \text{ m} = 1963 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Select the governing length (1 mark).**
The required length is the **larger** of the two values:

$$L = \max(L_1, L_2) = \max(50, 1963) = 1963 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is to take the **smaller** length, thinking "the smallest safe length" suffices. **The correct interpretation is: the length must satisfy BOTH criteria simultaneously, so the larger value governs.** This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction trap.

However, $L = 1963$ mm with $d = 100$ mm gives $L/d \approx 19.6$, which is **far outside** the standard 0.5 to 2.0 range. This indicates the chosen PV limit (2.0) is too restrictive for the load-speed combination. In practice, the designer would:
- Increase the journal diameter d, **or**
- Select a higher-grade bearing material with a larger $(PV)_a$, **or**
- Use multiple bearings to share the load.

For this exam problem, we report the **calculated required length** as $L = 1963$ mm, and conclude that **the PV criterion governs**.

**Final answer for (a):** $L_1 = 50$ mm (from pressure), $L_2 = 1963$ mm (from PV). **The PV criterion governs; required L ≈ 1963 mm.**

---

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Rubbing velocity V (2 marks).**

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.1 \times 1500}{60} = 7.854 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Bearing pressure P with the selected length (2 marks).**
Using $L = 1.963$ m = 1963 mm:

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot d} = \frac{50\,000}{1963 \times 100} = \frac{50\,000}{196\,300} \approx 0.255 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 0.255 \text{ MPa}$$

(Note: This is well below the allowable 10 MPa, confirming PV was the binding constraint.)

**Step 3 — Hersey number H (2 marks).**

$$H = \frac{Z \cdot N}{P} = \frac{30 \times 1500}{0.255} = \frac{45\,000}{0.255} \approx 176\,471$$

**Step 4 — Lubrication regime identification (1 mark).**
Since $H = 176\,471 \gg 1.0 \gg \pi/4 \approx 0.785$, the bearing operates in the **full hydrodynamic regime** with a thick, stable fluid film.

**Final answer for (b):** $V = 7.854$ m/s, $P = 0.255$ MPa, $H \approx 1.76 \times 10^5$. **Regime: Full hydrodynamic lubrication.**

---

### Question B. [Internal Choice — Alternative 14-Mark Question]

A sleeve bearing is to be designed for a **W = 30 kN** radial load at **N = 1200 rpm**. The journal diameter is **d = 80 mm**. The bearing material is **phosphor bronze** with:
- $P_a = 15$ MPa
- $(PV)_a = 1.5$ N·m/(mm²·s)
- Lubricant viscosity **Z = 25 cP**

**(a)** Calculate the **minimum bearing length L** satisfying both the pressure and the PV constraints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the selected length, determine (i) the actual bearing pressure, (ii) the rubbing velocity, and (iii) the Hersey number. Comment on whether the design is safe from a lubrication standpoint. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step (7 marks)**

**Given:** $W = 30\,000$ N, $N = 1200$ rpm, $d = 80$ mm = 0.08 m, $P_a = 15$ MPa, $(PV)_a = 1.5$ N·m/(mm²·s).

**Step 1 — Length from pressure (2.5 marks).**

$$L_1 = \frac{W}{d \cdot P_a} = \frac{30\,000}{80 \times 15} = \frac{30\,000}{1200} = 25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Length from PV criterion (3 marks).**

$$L_2 = \frac{W \cdot \pi \cdot N}{60\,000 \cdot (PV)_a} = \frac{30\,000 \times \pi \times 1200}{60\,000 \times 1.5}$$

Numerator: $30\,000 \times \pi \times 1200 = 113\,097\,336$

Denominator: $60\,000 \times 1.5 = 90\,000$

$$L_2 = \frac{113\,097\,336}{90\,000} \approx 1256.6 \text{ mm} \approx 1.257 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Governing length and L/d check (1.5 marks).**
$L = \max(25, 1256.6) = 1256.6$ mm. $L/d = 1256.6 / 80 = 15.7$, again outside the 0.5–2.0 range. **PV criterion governs.** In practice, a different material (e.g., babbitt with higher PV limit) or a larger d is required.

**Final (a):** $L_1 = 25$ mm, $L_2 = 1256.6$ mm; **PV governs, required L ≈ 1257 mm.**

---

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Actual pressure with L = 1256.6 mm (2 marks).**

$$P = \frac{30\,000}{1256.6 \times 80} = \frac{30\,000}{100\,528} \approx 0.298 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Rubbing velocity (2 marks).**

$$V = \frac{\pi \times 0.08 \times 1200}{60} = \frac{301.59}{60} = 5.026 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Hersey number (2 marks).**

$$H = \frac{Z \cdot N}{P} = \frac{25 \times 1200}{0.298} = \frac{30\,000}{0.298} \approx 100\,672$$

**Step 4 — Lubrication safety comment (1 mark).**
$H \approx 1.01 \times 10^5 \gg 0.785$, so the bearing is in the **full hydrodynamic regime** and is **lubrication-safe**. However, the very high L/d ratio indicates the **PV limit is too restrictive** for this load-speed-material combination, and a more robust bearing material should be considered.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Traps on Stribeck's Equation Questions:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency in PV:** Forgetting to convert d from mm to m (or vice versa) when computing V. A factor-of-1000 error is the most common 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Smaller vs. larger L:** Selecting the **smaller** of $L_1$ and $L_2$ instead of the **larger** one. The bearing must satisfy **both** constraints — this is a 1–2 mark trap.
> 3. **Forgetting to report both calculations:** Even if PV clearly governs, examiners expect **both** $L_1$ and $L_2$ to be shown explicitly. Omitting one leads to a 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Misidentifying the lubrication regime:** Using the Hersey number **without** the $\pi/4$ threshold comparison. The criterion is $H \geq \pi/4 \approx 0.785$, not just "H is large."
> 5. **Ignoring L/d range:** Reporting a length that gives $L/d > 2$ without commenting on it shows incomplete design understanding — at least **1 mark** is reserved for the L/d reasonableness check in KTU marking schemes.
> 6. **Confusing Stribeck's pressure equation with the rolling-contact basic rating equation** $C = P (L_{10h}/L_{10})^{1/a}$. These are **different** Stribeck relations for **different** bearing types.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Stribeck's Equations (Module 3 – DME)**

- **Stribeck's bearing pressure equation:** $P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot d}$. Always use **projected area** $A_p = L \cdot d$, never the curved surface.
- **Rubbing velocity:** $V = \dfrac{\pi \, d \, N}{60}$ (m/s) when N is in rpm and d in meters.
- **PV product (Stribeck wear criterion):** $PV = \dfrac{W \, \pi \, N}{60 \, L}$ — note that $d$ **cancels out**.
- **Two design limits must be checked simultaneously:** $P \leq P_a$ **and** $PV \leq (PV)_a$. The **larger** of the two computed L values is the design length.
- **Hersey number:** $H = \dfrac{Z \, N}{P}$. Hydrodynamic regime requires $H \geq \pi/4 \approx 0.785$.
- **Standard L/d range:** 0.5 to 2.0; **L/d = 1** is the conventional starting point.
- **Material-dependent limits:** Babbitt ≈ low P but moderate PV; Bronze ≈ higher P; PTFE ≈ very low P but self-lubricating.
- **PV unit:** N·m/(mm²·s) or MPa·m/s. Watch unit conversions in numericals.
- **Two Stribeck equations are different:** (1) Journal-bearing pressure/PV (this module), (2) Rolling-contact basic dynamic load rating (next module). Do not mix them.
- **Examiner focus areas:** Unit conversion, choosing the larger L, reporting L/d, identifying the lubrication regime, and justifying material selection based on PV limit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selection of Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bearing Selection** is the systematic engineering process of choosing an appropriate rolling-element or plain bearing (from a manufacturer's standard catalogue) that can withstand the applied radial and/or axial loads, operate at the required rotational speed, deliver the desired service life (typically expressed in millions of revolutions or operating hours), and function reliably under given lubrication, temperature, contamination, and misalignment conditions.

A **bearing** is a machine element that constrains relative motion to only the desired motion and reduces friction between moving parts. In design, the **selection procedure** is the *reverse engineering* of bearing sizing: given the operational requirements (load, speed, life, environment), we choose the smallest, most economical bearing from a standard series that satisfies the load-life-speed relationship.

### KTU 2024 Syllabus Scope (Module 3)
- Selection of **Rolling Contact Bearings** (Ball & Roller)
- Static and Dynamic Load Carrying Capacity
- Equivalent Load Calculation (Radial + Axial combined)
- Bearing Life under Variable Loads
- Reliability and Application Factors
- Catalogue-based Design as per **ISO 15** / **SKF** / **NTN** standards

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chair Wheel" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a rotating bicycle wheel hub.** The shaft is the "axle" and the housing is the "frame". Without a bearing, the metal-on-metal contact would weld, seize, and destroy the wheel in minutes. The bearing is the **"carefully arranged set of steel balls or rollers"** that lets the axle spin *without sliding friction*—only *rolling friction* (which is 50–100× lower).

**Selection analogy:** Suppose you must carry a 200 kg heavy suitcase across an airport.
- A small **ball-bearing roller** (like on a cheap suitcase) handles light loads for a short trip.
- A heavy-duty **needle roller bearing** (like on industrial machinery) handles massive loads but at moderate speed.
- A **ceramic hybrid bearing** (like in a jet engine) handles high speed + heat but costs a fortune.

**Bearing selection = matching the "suitcase capacity" to the "trip distance and weight".**

In engineering terms: matching **Load Capacity (C)** to **Equivalent Load (P)** over a required **Life (L₁₀)** at a given **Speed (n)**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Bearings (Foundation for Selection)

| Type | Sub-Type | Key Feature | Typical Application |
|------|----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Rolling Contact** | Ball Bearing | Point contact → lower load, higher speed | Electric motors, gearboxes |
| **Rolling Contact** | Roller Bearing (Cylindrical, Tapered, Spherical, Needle) | Line contact → higher load, moderate speed | Railway axles, heavy gearboxes |
| **Sliding Contact** | Journal / Thrust | Hydrodynamic film, no rolling elements | Turbo-machinery, large turbines |

**KTU focus is on Rolling Contact Bearings** since the design uses **ISO 15 dimensional standards** and catalogue-based selection.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C):** the constant radial (or axial for thrust) load that a bearing can theoretically endure for **1 million revolutions** with a **90% reliability** (i.e., L₁₀ life).
> - **Basic Static Load Rating (C₀):** the load that produces a permanent deformation of **0.0001 × D** (ball diameter) at the most heavily loaded contact point.
> - **Reference Speed:** the speed at which thermal equilibrium is reached under defined load/lubrication (no symbol, catalogue value).
> - **Limiting Speed:** the maximum mechanically and thermally permissible speed (mechanical limit).
> - **Viscosity Ratio (κ or k):** ratio of actual operating viscosity to rated viscosity; critical for lubrication verification.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Bearing Equation (ISO 281 / L₁₀ Life)

The most critical equation in bearing selection links **life**, **load**, and **rating**:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

Where:
- **L₁₀** = Rating life in **millions of revolutions** (90% reliability)
- **C** = Basic dynamic load rating (**N** or **kN**)
- **P** = Equivalent dynamic bearing load (**N** or **kN**)
- **p** = Exponent of the life equation
  - **p = 3** for **ball bearings**
  - **p = 10/3** for **roller bearings**

### Conversion to Operating Hours

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p = \frac{16666}{n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \text{ hours}$$

Where **n** = rotational speed in **rpm**.

---

## 2.2 Modified Rating Life (ISO 281:2007)

For modern high-precision applications where reliability > 90% or contamination/viscosity deviates from ideal:

$$L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

Where:
- **a₁** = **Life adjustment factor for reliability** (≠ 90%)
- **a_ISO** = **Life modification factor** (accounts for lubrication, contamination, fatigue limit)

| Reliability % | a₁ |
|---------------|------|
| 90 | 1.00 |
| 95 | 0.85 |
| 96 | 0.77 |
| 97 | 0.68 |
| 98 | 0.57 |
| 99 | 0.44 |
| 99.2 | 0.40 |
| 99.5 | 0.31 |
| 99.9 | 0.21 |

> [!NOTE]
> At KTU level, the **a₁ factor** is usually retained for higher-reliability questions, but the **a_ISO** factor is generally assumed **= 1** unless explicitly mentioned in the question.

---

## 2.3 Equivalent Dynamic Load (P) — The Selection Cornerstone

Bearings are rarely loaded by *pure radial* or *pure axial* forces only. The **Equivalent Dynamic Load (P)** is the *single hypothetical load* (constant in magnitude and direction) that would give the **same life** as the actual combined loading.

### For Radial Ball Bearings (Single Row Deep Groove)

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

### For Radial Roller Bearings (Cylindrical, Needle)

$$P = F_r \quad \text{(axial load is generally negligible, } X = 1, Y = 0\text{)}$$

### For Tapered Roller & Angular Contact Bearings

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

| Bearing Type | Load Ratios | X | Y |
|--------------|------------|------|------|
| **Deep Groove Ball** | Fa/Fr ≤ e | 1 | 0 |
| | Fa/Fr > e | 0.56 | Catalogue Y |
| **Angular Contact Ball (α = 40°)** | Fa/Fr ≤ 1.14 | 1 | 0 |
| | Fa/Fr > 1.14 | 0.35 | 0.57 |
| **Tapered Roller (single row)** | Fa/Fr ≤ e | 1 | 0 |
| | Fa/Fr > e | 0.4 | 0.4·cot α |

> **Catalogue values of e, X, Y are a function of Fa/C₀ (axial to static capacity ratio)** — student must look up the table from the manufacturer (SKF) or PSG data book.

---

## 2.4 Equivalent Static Load (P₀)

Used to check that the bearing is not overloaded at **standstill or very low speeds** (n < 10 rpm):

$$P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a$$

For most catalogue checks, the safety condition is:

$$P_0 \leq C_0$$

---

## 2.5 Application, Reliability, and Temperature Factors

The actual **required dynamic load rating** is increased by factors representing real-world deviations:

$$C_{req} = P \cdot \left(\frac{L_{10h} \cdot n \cdot 60}{10^6}\right)^{1/p} \cdot \frac{1}{a_1 \cdot a_2}$$

Or equivalently, using the **modified life equation**:

$$C_{req} = P \cdot \left(\frac{L_{10h} \cdot n \cdot 60}{10^6 \cdot a_1}\right)^{1/p}$$

Where:
- **a₁** = Reliability factor (from §2.2 table)
- **a₂ / a₃** = Application / operating condition factors (shock, vibration)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **KTU 2024 standard problems**, a **single combined "service factor" (K_s or K_a)** is typically applied to the load:
> $$P_{actual} = K_s \cdot (X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a)$$
> Typical values: **K_s = 1.0 to 1.5** (smooth), **1.5 to 2.0** (light shock), **2.0 to 3.0** (heavy shock).

---

## 2.6 Variable Load Bearing Life (Palmgren-Miner Linear Damage Rule)

When the load varies with time (typical for gearboxes, vehicles):

$$L = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{q_i}{L_i}}$$

Where:
- **L** = total life under variable loading
- **q_i** = fraction of time operating at load P_i
- **L_i** = life that would be obtained if P_i acted constantly

Equivalently, the **equivalent constant load** is:

$$P_{eq} = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{k} q_i \cdot P_i^p\right)^{1/p}$$

---

## 2.7 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Rating life (rev) | L₁₀ = (C/P)^p | millions | p=3 (ball), p=10/3 (roller) |
| 2 | Rating life (hours) | L₁₀ₕ = 16666·(C/P)^p / n | hours | n in rpm |
| 3 | Modified life | L_nm = a₁·a_ISO·(C/P)^p | millions | a_ISO≈1 unless stated |
| 4 | Eq. dynamic load (ball/angular) | P = X·F_r + Y·F_a | N | Catalogue lookup |
| 5 | Eq. dynamic load (cylindrical roller) | P = F_r | N | Axial not supported |
| 6 | Eq. static load | P₀ = X₀·F_r + Y₀·F_a | N | Check P₀ ≤ C₀ |
| 7 | Required C (life-based) | C_req = P·(L·n·60/10⁶)^(1/p) | N | Catalogue selection |
| 8 | Variable load eq. | P_eq = (Σ q_i·P_i^p)^(1/p) | N | Palmgren-Miner |
| 9 | Reliability factor | a₁ (table) | – | Use a₁ for 95%, 99% etc. |
| 10 | Safety against static | P₀ ≤ C₀ | N | Always check |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Gearbox design:** The output shaft bearing sees combined radial (gear tooth force) + axial (helical gear thrust) load — equivalent load calculation is *mandatory*.
- **Automotive wheel hub:** Uses **tapered roller bearings** because of combined radial + thrust loads and the need for rigidity.
- **Electric motor (small):** Uses **deep groove ball bearings** — pure radial load, high speed, light axial from rotor weight.
- **Wind turbine main shaft:** Uses **spherical roller bearings** — accommodate misalignment and massive radial loads.
- **Machine tool spindle:** Uses **angular contact ball bearings** in back-to-back arrangement for combined load + rigidity.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Lundberg-Palmgren Bearing Life Equation (Conceptual)

The L₁₀ life equation $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ is empirical but is derived from the **fatigue theory of stressed volume** under Hertzian contact. We present the *engineering derivation path* relevant to KTU:

### Step 1: Hertzian Contact Stress

For a ball on a raceway (point contact), the maximum contact pressure is:

$$p_0 = \frac{1}{\pi} \cdot \left(\frac{6 \cdot F \cdot E^{*2}}{R^2}\right)^{1/3}$$

Where:
- **F** = contact load on a single ball
- **E*** = effective elastic modulus
- **R** = effective radius of curvature

**Explanation:** As load on the bearing **increases**, the contact stress at the ball-raceway junction **rises proportionally** to the power 1/3. This is the root of fatigue failure in bearings.

### Step 2: Subsurface Shear Stress (Initiator of Fatigue Cracks)

The maximum orthogonal shear stress occurs **below the surface** at depth $\approx 0.3a$ (contact radius $a$):

$$\tau_0 = 0.30 \cdot p_0$$

**Explanation:** Fatigue cracks initiate below the surface where shear is maximum. Repeated rolling over this zone causes **sub-surface fatigue spalling** — the dominant failure mode for properly lubricated bearings.

### Step 3: Cycles to Failure (Basquin-type fatigue)

For a stressed volume V, the number of stress cycles N to initiate a fatigue crack is:

$$N \propto \frac{1}{\tau_0^c \cdot V}$$

Where **c ≈ 9–10** (for ball bearings) and **c ≈ 8** (for roller bearings), determined experimentally by Lundberg & Palmgren (1947, 1952).

### Step 4: Aggregate over all loaded contacts

Summing the inverse lives over all rolling elements and the stressed volume swept by the inner race gives:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

With $p = 3$ for ball bearings and $p = 10/3$ for roller bearings (the higher exponent for rollers reflects **line contact** vs **point contact**).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU does NOT require reproducing this derivation. However, knowing **why p = 3 for balls and 10/3 for rollers** is a frequently asked **Part A (3-mark) question**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Conversion of Life Equation to Operating Hours

Starting from:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \text{ [in millions of revolutions]}$$

**Step 1:** Convert revolutions to hours.

$$N_{total} = n \cdot t \cdot 60$$

Where:
- **N_total** = total revolutions
- **n** = speed in rpm
- **t** = time in hours

**Step 2:** Number of revolutions per million-rev unit:

$$1 \text{ million rev} = 10^6 \text{ rev}$$

**Step 3:** Life in hours = (total rev) / (rev per million):

$$L_{10h} = \frac{N_{total}}{10^6} = \frac{n \cdot t \cdot 60}{10^6}$$

Wait — we need to express L₁₀ₕ in terms of C/P. So set **t = L₁₀ₕ** and the *required* total revolutions equal to what the bearing can survive:

$$n \cdot L_{10h} \cdot 60 = 10^6 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

**Step 4:** Solve for L₁₀ₕ:

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p = \frac{16666.67}{n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

This is the **canonical formula** used in **every KTU Part B problem**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Part B Style)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Style):** A 6308 deep groove ball bearing operates at **n = 1450 rpm** under a **radial load F_r = 4500 N** and an **axial load F_a = 1500 N**. The required L₁₀ life is **8000 hours**. Determine the **required basic dynamic load rating C**. Assume a service factor of 1.2.

#### Step 1: Find Equivalent Dynamic Load

The bearing is a deep groove ball bearing. We need **Fa/C₀** to determine X and Y. But we don't know C₀ yet — so we use the *iterative* method or assume a preliminary catalogue row. 

For a **6308 deep groove ball bearing** (typical catalogue):
- C ≈ 41.2 kN
- C₀ ≈ 22.0 kN
- e ≈ 0.22 (approximate for SKF 6308)

**Step 2: Compute Fa/Fr and compare to e**

$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{1500}{4500} = 0.333$$

**Step 3: Compare Fa/Fr with e**

Since **0.333 > 0.222 (e)**, we use:
- **X = 0.56**
- **Y ≈ 1.46** (from catalogue, when Fa/C₀ ≈ 1500/22000 ≈ 0.068 → Y ≈ 1.46)

> **Why this value?** From the SKF/PSG data book: for 6308 series, when Fa/C₀ is between 0.056 and 0.084, Y is approximately 1.46 and e = 0.22. KTU students are *expected* to look this up from a table.

**Step 4: Calculate P (without service factor)**

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a = 0.56 \cdot 4500 + 1.46 \cdot 1500$$
$$P = 2520 + 2190 = 4710 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Apply Service Factor K_s = 1.2**

$$P_{actual} = K_s \cdot P = 1.2 \cdot 4710 = 5652 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6: Calculate Required C (Ball Bearing → p = 3)**

$$C_{req} = P_{actual} \cdot \left(\frac{L_{10h} \cdot n \cdot 60}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$$

Plug in: L₁₀ₕ = 8000, n = 1450, p = 3.

$$C_{req} = 5652 \cdot \left(\frac{8000 \cdot 1450 \cdot 60}{10^6}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 7: Evaluate the bracketed term**

$$\frac{8000 \cdot 1450 \cdot 60}{10^6} = \frac{8000 \cdot 87000}{10^6} = \frac{696 \times 10^6}{10^6} = 696$$

**Step 8: Take the cube root**

$$696^{1/3} \approx 8.867$$

**Step 9: Compute C_req**

$$C_{req} = 5652 \cdot 8.867 \approx 50,120 \text{ N} = 50.12 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 10: Catalogue Selection**

Catalogue row of 6308: **C = 41.2 kN** — *insufficient*.

Try **6309** (next size up):
- C ≈ 52.7 kN
- C₀ ≈ 28.5 kN

Since **52.7 > 50.12**, **6309 is selected**.

**Step 11: Static Load Check**

$$P_0 = F_r = 4500 \text{ N (assuming pure radial at standstill)}$$

Check: **P₀ = 4500 N ≤ C₀ = 28,500 N** ✓ (Safe)

> **Verdict:** Select **6309 deep groove ball bearing**.

---

## 3.4 Summary of Calculation Steps (Analytical Matrix)

| Step | Action | Formula / Source |
|------|--------|------------------|
| 1 | Identify bearing type & series | From problem statement |
| 2 | Estimate C₀ (iterative, from catalogue guess) | PSG/SKF data book |
| 3 | Compute Fa/Fr ratio | $F_a/F_r$ |
| 4 | Compute Fa/C₀ ratio (table lookup) | Catalogue |
| 5 | Read X, Y, e from catalogue | Catalogue |
| 6 | Compare Fa/Fr to e, pick X, Y | If Fa/Fr > e → use X, Y; else X=1, Y=0 |
| 7 | Compute P = X·Fr + Y·Fa | Eq. dynamic load |
| 8 | Apply service factor | $P_{act} = K_s \cdot P$ |
| 9 | Compute C_req | $C = P \cdot (L \cdot n \cdot 60/10^6)^{1/p}$ |
| 10 | Pick catalogue bearing with C ≥ C_req | Selection |
| 11 | Static check: P₀ ≤ C₀ | Safety verification |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** *Differentiate between Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C) and Basic Static Load Rating (C₀) of a rolling contact bearing. State the ISO standard reference for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C) | Basic Static Load Rating (C₀) |
|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Definition** | The constant radial load that a bearing can theoretically endure for **1 million revolutions** without fatigue failure | The static load that causes a **permanent deformation of 0.0001 × D** (ball diameter) at the most stressed contact point |
| **ISO Standard** | **ISO 281:2007** | **ISO 76:2006** |
| **Reliability** | 90% (L₁₀) | – |
| **Application** | Used for life calculation at operating speed | Used to check overload at standstill (n < 10 rpm) |

> **Key takeaway (1 mark):** C is for *dynamic* (rotating) life; C₀ is for *static* (standstill) deformation check.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** *Why is the exponent p = 3 used for ball bearings and p = 10/3 for roller bearings in the bearing life equation?*

**Model Answer:**

The exponent p in $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ comes from the **Lundberg-Palmgren fatigue theory** (1947). The exponent reflects the **stress-volume relationship** under the rolling contact:

- **Ball bearings** have **point (Hertzian point) contact** at the ball-raceway interface. The exponent is empirically **p = 3** (from $c \approx 9$ in the inverse stress power).
- **Roller bearings** have **line (Hertzian line) contact**. The line contact produces a **larger stressed volume** and a slightly different sub-surface shear stress distribution, giving **p = 10/3 ≈ 3.33**.
- The higher exponent for rollers means: for the *same load increase*, a roller bearing's life **decreases faster** than a ball bearing's, but its absolute load capacity is also **higher**.

> **Why this matters (1 mark):** Cylinder-on-flat contact (roller) sustains ~1.5× the load of sphere-on-flat (ball) of the same dimensions.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]** A 6309 series deep groove ball bearing carries a **radial load of 5000 N** and an **axial load of 2000 N** at a speed of **1500 rpm**. The desired L₁₀ life is **10000 hours**. The operating temperature is **100 °C**. Determine:
> **(a)** The required basic dynamic load rating C, and select a suitable bearing from the catalogue. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Verify the static load safety and check if the lubrication is adequate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given:**
- F_r = 5000 N, F_a = 2000 N, n = 1500 rpm, L₁₀ₕ = 10000 h
- Bearing series: 6309 deep groove ball
- Operating temp: 100 °C

**Part (a): Required C and Catalogue Selection**

**Step 1: Preliminary Catalogue Reference (iteration assumption)**

From PSG/SKF data book, for **6309 deep groove ball bearing**:
- C₀ ≈ 28.5 kN, C ≈ 52.7 kN (approximate)

**Step 2: Compute Fa/C₀ ratio**

$$\frac{F_a}{C_0} = \frac{2000}{28500} = 0.0702$$

**Step 3: Catalogue lookup for 6309 series**

From PSG data book (Table 11.x), for 6309:
- When Fa/C₀ ≈ 0.070: **e ≈ 0.24, Y ≈ 1.38, X = 0.56**

**Step 4: Compute Fa/Fr ratio**

$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{2000}{5000} = 0.40$$

**Step 5: Compare Fa/Fr to e**

$$0.40 > 0.24 = e \quad \Rightarrow \text{Use } X = 0.56, \ Y = 1.38$$

**Step 6: Compute Equivalent Dynamic Load P**

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a = 0.56 \cdot 5000 + 1.38 \cdot 2000$$
$$P = 2800 + 2760 = 5560 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7: Apply Service Factor K_s = 1.0 (assume smooth operation)**

$$P_{act} = 1.0 \cdot 5560 = 5560 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8: Compute Required C (p = 3 for ball bearing)**

$$C_{req} = P_{act} \cdot \left(\frac{L_{10h} \cdot n \cdot 60}{10^6}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$C_{req} = 5560 \cdot \left(\frac{10000 \cdot 1500 \cdot 60}{10^6}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$C_{req} = 5560 \cdot (900)^{1/3} = 5560 \cdot 9.6549 = 53,681 \text{ N} = 53.68 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 9: Catalogue Verification**

Available 6309: C = 52.7 kN. **52.7 < 53.68** → **6309 is INSUFFICIENT**.

Try **6310**:
- C ≈ 61.8 kN, C₀ ≈ 38.0 kN

Since **61.8 > 53.68**, **select 6310** ✓

**Part (b): Static Load Check and Lubrication Verification**

**Step 1: Static Load P₀**

For deep groove ball bearing, **X₀ = 0.6, Y₀ = 0.5** (standard):

$$P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a = 0.6 \cdot 5000 + 0.5 \cdot 2000$$
$$P_0 = 3000 + 1000 = 4000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Safety Check**

$$P_0 = 4000 \text{ N} \leq C_0 = 38000 \text{ N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{STATICALLY SAFE} \ \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Lubrication Adequacy (Viscosity Ratio κ)**

For 6310 at 1500 rpm and 100 °C:
- Required kinematic viscosity ν₁ ≈ 13 mm²/s (from catalogue diagram)
- Assume ISO VG 68 oil is used → ν at 100 °C ≈ 9.5 mm²/s

$$\kappa = \frac{\nu}{\nu_1} = \frac{9.5}{13} \approx 0.73$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

Since **κ < 1**, lubrication is *marginal*. Recommendation: Use **ISO VG 100 oil** (ν at 100 °C ≈ 13 mm²/s) → κ ≈ 1.0, lubrication adequate.

**Final Selection:** **6310 Deep Groove Ball Bearing** with **ISO VG 100 oil** at 100 °C.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** A radial cylindrical roller bearing operates under the following load spectrum at constant speed of **1200 rpm**:
> - 25% of time: F₁ = 6000 N
> - 45% of time: F₂ = 9000 N
> - 30% of time: F₃ = 4000 N
>
> The required life is **5000 hours** with **95% reliability**.
> **(a)** Compute the **equivalent constant load** P_eq using the Palmgren-Miner hypothesis. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Determine the **required basic dynamic load rating C** and select a catalogue bearing. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a): Equivalent Constant Load (Palmgren-Miner)**

**Step 1: Bearing type — Radial Cylindrical Roller (p = 10/3)**

For pure radial loading on a cylindrical roller bearing:
$$P = F_r \quad (\text{no axial component})$$

**Step 2: Apply the equivalent load formula**

$$P_{eq} = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{3} q_i \cdot P_i^p\right)^{1/p} = \left(q_1 \cdot F_1^p + q_2 \cdot F_2^p + q_3 \cdot F_3^p\right)^{1/p}$$

With **p = 10/3 ≈ 3.3333** and q₁=0.25, q₂=0.45, q₃=0.30.

**Step 3: Compute each term**

For F₁ = 6000 N:
$$F_1^{10/3} = 6000^{3.3333}$$
$$\log_{10}(F_1^{10/3}) = 3.3333 \cdot \log_{10}(6000) = 3.3333 \cdot 3.7782 = 12.594$$
$$F_1^{10/3} = 10^{12.594} = 3.929 \times 10^{12}$$

For F₂ = 9000 N:
$$\log_{10}(F_2^{10/3}) = 3.3333 \cdot \log_{10}(9000) = 3.3333 \cdot 3.9542 = 13.181$$
$$F_2^{10/3} = 10^{13.181} = 1.517 \times 10^{13}$$

For F₃ = 4000 N:
$$\log_{10}(F_3^{10/3}) = 3.3333 \cdot \log_{10}(4000) = 3.3333 \cdot 3.6021 = 12.007$$
$$F_3^{10/3} = 10^{12.007} = 1.016 \times 10^{12}$$

**Step 4: Weighted sum**

$$\sum q_i \cdot F_i^{10/3} = 0.25 \cdot (3.929 \times 10^{12}) + 0.45 \cdot (1.517 \times 10^{13}) + 0.30 \cdot (1.016 \times 10^{12})$$

$$= 0.982 \times 10^{12} + 6.826 \times 10^{12} + 0.305 \times 10^{12} = 8.113 \times 10^{12}$$

**Step 5: Take the 1/p power**

$$P_{eq} = (8.113 \times 10^{12})^{3/10} = (8.113 \times 10^{12})^{0.3}$$

$$\log_{10}(P_{eq}) = 0.3 \cdot \log_{10}(8.113 \times 10^{12}) = 0.3 \cdot 12.909 = 3.873$$

$$P_{eq} = 10^{3.873} \approx 7463 \text{ N} \approx 7.46 \text{ kN}$$

**Part (b): Required C with 95% Reliability**

**Step 1: Reliability factor for 95%**

$$a_1 = 0.85$$

**Step 2: Modified Life Equation**

$$L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

Solving for C:
$$C_{req} = P_{eq} \cdot \left(\frac{L_{10h} \cdot n \cdot 60}{10^6 \cdot a_1}\right)^{1/p}$$

**Step 3: Plug values**

- L₁₀ₕ = 5000 h, n = 1200 rpm, a₁ = 0.85, p = 10/3

$$\frac{L_{10h} \cdot n \cdot 60}{10^6 \cdot a_1} = \frac{5000 \cdot 1200 \cdot 60}{10^6 \cdot 0.85} = \frac{360 \times 10^6}{0.85 \times 10^6} = 423.53$$

**Step 4: Take the 1/p = 3/10 power**

$$423.53^{0.3}$$
$$\log_{10}(423.53^{0.3}) = 0.3 \cdot \log_{10}(423.53) = 0.3 \cdot 2.6268 = 0.7880$$
$$423.53^{0.3} = 10^{0.7880} = 6.137$$

**Step 5: Compute C_req**

$$C_{req} = 7463 \cdot 6.137 = 45,807 \text{ N} \approx 45.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Catalogue Selection**

From PSG data book, for radial cylindrical roller bearings with C ≥ 45.8 kN:
- **NU 308 EC** : C ≈ 55 kN, C₀ ≈ 53 kN ✓
- **NU 309 EC** : C ≈ 71 kN (oversized, but acceptable)

**Select NU 308 EC cylindrical roller bearing.**

**Step 7: Static Check**

$$P_0 = F_{r,max} = 9000 \text{ N (worst case)}$$
$$C_0 = 53,000 \text{ N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 9000 \leq 53000 \ \checkmark$$

**Final Selection:** **NU 308 EC Cylindrical Roller Bearing** with L₁₀ = 5000 h at 95% reliability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting p value (3 vs 10/3):** A ball bearing uses **p = 3**, a roller bearing uses **p = 10/3**. Mixing these up changes C_req by ~20–30%. (Lose 2–3 marks.)
> 2. **Not converting to millions of revolutions:** The factor $L \cdot n \cdot 60 / 10^6$ MUST be dimensionless and represent the number of million revolutions. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 3. **Using X = 1, Y = 0 universally:** For combined loading where Fa/Fr > e, you MUST look up X and Y from the catalogue. (Lose 3 marks.)
> 4. **Forgetting the service factor K_s:** Even on "smooth" machines, examiners expect K_s = 1.0–1.2. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 5. **Skipping the static check:** Always verify P₀ ≤ C₀ at the end. Many students omit it. (Lose 2 marks.)
> 6. **Using p = 3 for roller bearings:** This is the **most common error** and costs the most marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ The **fundamental bearing life equation** is $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ where **p = 3 (ball)** and **p = 10/3 (roller)**.
- ✅ **C** = Basic Dynamic Load Rating (constant load for 1 million rev, 90% reliability, ISO 281).
- ✅ **C₀** = Basic Static Load Rating (load causing 0.0001×D deformation, ISO 76).
- ✅ The **equivalent dynamic load** P combines F_r and F_a using **X and Y** from the catalogue.
- ✅ For **deep groove ball bearings**: if F_a/F_r ≤ e → X=1, Y=0; if F_a/F_r > e → X=0.56, Y= catalogue.
- ✅ For **cylindrical roller bearings**: P = F_r (no axial capacity).
- ✅ For **tapered roller & angular contact**: P = X·F_r + Y·F_a (mandatory catalogue lookup).
- ✅ **Modified rating life** with reliability: $L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot (C/P)^p$ (a₁ = 0.85 for 95%, 0.44 for 99%, etc.)
- ✅ **Service factor K_s** is applied to load: P_actual = K_s · P.
- ✅ **Required C** for life-based design: $C = P \cdot (L \cdot n \cdot 60 / 10^6)^{1/p}$.
- ✅ **Static safety check** (always!): P₀ ≤ C₀.
- ✅ **Variable load life** uses **Palmgren-Miner** rule: $P_{eq} = (\Sigma q_i \cdot P_i^p)^{1/p}$.
- ✅ **Lubrication adequacy** is checked via **viscosity ratio κ** (κ ≥ 1 is desired).
