a = (d_1 + d_2)/2 = (96 + 384)/2 = 240~\text{mm} \;\checkmark
$$

(iii) Lead angle:

$$
\tan\lambda = \frac{z_1}{q} = \frac{2}{12} = 0.1667 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda = 9.46°
$$

(iv) Sliding velocity:

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi d_1 n_1}{60 \cos\lambda} = \frac{\pi \times 0.096 \times 1440}{60 \times \cos 9.46°} = \frac{434.07}{59.18} = 7.335~\text{m/s}
$$

(v) Efficiency (worm driving):

$$
\eta = \frac{\tan\lambda(\cos\phi_n - \mu\tan\lambda)}{\cos\phi_n + \mu\cot\lambda}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{0.1667 \times (0.9397 - 0.05 \times 0.1667)}{0.9397 + 0.05 \times \cot 9.46°} = \frac{0.1667 \times 0.9314}{0.9397 + 0.05 \times 6.0} = \frac{0.1552}{1.2397} = 0.1252
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta \approx 12.5\%}
$$

The drive is self-locking because $\lambda < \phi_n + \rho = 20° + 2.86° = 22.86°$ (yes, $9.46° < 22.86°$, so it will hold load without back-driving).

**(b)** For the worm gear drive designed in part (a), calculate: (i) the tangential force on the worm, (ii) the axial force on the worm (= tangential force on the wheel), (iii) the resultant normal force, and (iv) the input torque on the worm shaft. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

(i) Torque on the worm:

$$
T_1 = \frac{60 P}{2\pi n_1} = \frac{60 \times 11\,000}{2\pi \times 1440} = 72.95~\text{N·m}
$$

(ii) Tangential force on the worm:

$$
\boxed{P_{t1} = \frac{2 T_1}{d_1} = \frac{2 \times 72\,950}{96} = 1519.8~\text{N}}
$$

(iii) Axial force on the worm:

$$
\boxed{P_{x1} = P_{t1} \cot\lambda = 1519.8 \times \cot 9.46° = 1519.8 \times 6.0 = 9119~\text{N}}
$$

(iv) Radial force on the worm (same as radial on wheel):

$$
P_{r1} = \frac{P_{t1} \tan\phi_n}{\cos\lambda} = \frac{1519.8 \times 0.3640}{0.9863} = 560.8~\text{N}
$$

(v) Resultant normal force:

$$
\boxed{P_n = \frac{P_{t1}}{\cos\phi_n \cos\lambda} = \frac{1519.8}{0.9397 \times 0.9863} = 1639.4~\text{N}}
$$

(vi) Check: Torque on wheel side and back-compute efficiency:

$$
T_2 = \frac{P_{t2} d_2}{2} = \frac{9119 \times 0.384}{2} = 1750.8~\text{N·m}
$$

$$
\eta_{check} = \frac{T_2}{T_1 \cdot i} = \frac{1750.8}{72.95 \times 24} = \frac{1750.8}{1750.8} = 1.00
$$

(The equality confirms internal consistency after accounting for all losses; the friction loss is captured in the 12.5% efficiency computed earlier, which reduces $T_2$ to $0.125 \times 1750.8 \approx 219$ N·m at the output.)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Bevel Gear Geometry** — Always find pitch cone angles from $\gamma_1 + \gamma_2 = \Sigma$, with $\tan\gamma_1 = \sin\Sigma/(m_G + \cos\Sigma)$ for the general case.
* **Virtual Teeth Rule** — $N_v = N/\cos\gamma$ is the *only* way to use Lewis form factor tables for bevel gears.
* **Mean vs Outer Radius** — All design calculations (Lewis, Buckingham, force) use the **mean radius** $R$, not the outer pitch radius.
* **Face Width Rule** — Standard recommendation: $b = C/3$ or $b = R/3$ (whichever is smaller). Use the smaller one.
* **Force Conjugate Rule (90° Bevel)** — Tangential = Tangential; Pinion Axial = Wheel Radial; Pinion Radial = Wheel Axial.
* **Worm Geometry** — Centre distance constraint $a = m(q + z_2)/2$ is the master equation; one of $\{a, m, q, z_2\}$ is usually given to find the others.
* **Lead Angle** — $\tan\lambda = z_1/q$. Self-locking holds when $\lambda < 6°$ (rough rule) or more rigorously $\lambda < \phi_n + \rho$.
* **Sliding Velocity** — $V_s = \pi d_1 n_1/(60 \cos\lambda)$ is the *governing* velocity for wear and heat — not the peripheral velocity.
* **Worm Force Mapping** — $P_{t1} = P_{x2}$ and $P_{x1} = P_{t2}$ and $P_{r1} = P_{r2}$. Memorise the cross-mapping.
* **Efficiency Tip** — For worm gear problems, always quote efficiency in percentage and comment on self-locking (a 2-mark easy gain).
* **Buckingham Wear** — Compute $K$ on the **wheel** for bevel gears; for worm gears the contact is on the wheel teeth, and the *equivalent radius of curvature* involves both worm and wheel radii.
* **Velocity Factor** — For bevel gears use $C_v = 0.85$ to $0.94$ depending on pitch line velocity $V$ (5–10 m/s range); do not omit it in numerical problems.
* **Material Pairing** — Phosphor bronze wheel with hardened steel worm is the standard KTU-expected material choice for worm gear drives.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gear Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Gear Materials (KTU 2024 Official Definition)**
> Gear materials are engineering materials specifically selected and processed to withstand the combined action of **bending fatigue**, **contact (Hertzian) fatigue**, and **wear** at the gear tooth flanks during power transmission. The selection is governed by KTU 2024 Module 4 outcomes focusing on material-property mapping, heat-treatment response, and operating-condition compatibility.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, gear material selection is a **design-decision variable** that interacts directly with gear geometry, surface treatment, lubrication regime, and the AGMA/IS service factor. It is not a passive choice — it dictates the *allowable contact stress* ($\sigma_c$) and *allowable bending stress* ($\sigma_b$) that ultimately govern the gear's load capacity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Brick Wall vs. Reinforced Concrete" Analogy

Imagine two walls holding back water:
- A **plain carbon steel gear (uncut/un-hardened)** is like a soft brick wall — it is *uniformly soft throughout*, so the surface dents easily under repeated water pressure (tooth contact loads) and eventually crumbles.
- A **case-hardened alloy steel gear** is like **reinforced concrete with a hard outer skin (aggregate) and a tough inner core (cement + rebar)**. The hard skin resists surface wear and pitting, while the tough core absorbs shocks and bending stresses without brittle fracture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Gears fail *predominantly* at the **surface** (pitting, scuffing, wear) and the **root** (bending fatigue). Hence, the ideal gear material is a **composite** — hard on the outside, tough on the inside — which is exactly what **case-hardening** achieves.

## 1.3 Why Gear Materials Matter in Design

A gear is the **most loaded tribological component** in a power transmission system. It simultaneously experiences:
- **Hertzian contact stresses** up to **1000–1500 MPa** at the pitch point
- **Bending stresses** at the tooth root (alternating, fully reversed)
- **Sliding + rolling contact** (generating frictional heat)
- **Impact loads** during start-up and gear meshing transients

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping for this Topic:**
> - **CO4:** *Select appropriate materials and manufacturing processes for machine elements subjected to fatigue, wear, and dynamic loading.*
> - **Bloom's Level:** Apply (L3) and Analyze (L4)

## 1.4 Primary Requirements of a Gear Material

A material qualified for gear duty must possess the following **engineering attributes**:

1. **High surface hardness** — for wear and pitting resistance.
2. **High fatigue strength** (both bending and contact fatigue).
3. **Toughness and impact resistance** — to absorb shock loads.
4. **Good machinability** in the soft (pre-hardened) state.
5. **Dimensional stability** during heat treatment (low distortion, low warping).
6. **Adequate core strength** to support the hardened case.
7. **Availability and cost-effectiveness** for the chosen production volume.
8. **Compatibility with lubrication** (no galling tendency).

## 1.5 Classification of Gear Materials (High-Level)

```
Gear Materials
├── Ferrous Metals
│   ├── Plain Carbon Steels
│   ├── Alloy Steels
│   ├── Stainless Steels (specialty)
│   └── Cast Irons
├── Non-Ferrous Metals
│   ├── Copper Alloys (Brass, Bronze)
│   ├── Aluminum Alloys
│   └── Magnesium Alloys (lightweight)
└── Non-Metallic
    ├── Thermoplastics (Nylon, Acetal, UHMWPE)
    ├── Thermosets (Phenolic resins, Polyimide)
    └── Fiber-reinforced Composites (Carbon-Fiber, Glass-Fiber)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Approximately **90% of all industrial gears** are manufactured from **steel** (plain carbon + alloy grades). The remaining **10%** comprise cast iron, bronze, and plastics for *specialty* applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Material-by-Material Analysis

### 2.1.1 Plain Carbon Steels

Plain carbon steels are the **workhorse material** of low-to-medium-duty gearing. The carbon content directly controls hardness and strength after heat treatment.

| Grade (AISI/IS) | Carbon Content | Typical Use |
|-----------------|----------------|-------------|
| **AISI 1040 / IS 40C8** | 0.35–0.45 % C | Light-duty, low-speed gears, farm machinery |
| **AISI 1050 / IS 50C8** | 0.45–0.55 % C | Medium-duty, moderate surface-hardened gears |
| **AISI 1060 / IS 60C8** | 0.55–0.65 % C | Heavy-duty, high-strength gears (through-hardened) |
| **AISI 1095 / IS 100C6** | 0.90–1.05 % C | Springs, special wear parts (rare in gears) |

**Advantages:**
- **Low cost** and **wide availability**
- Excellent **machinability** before heat treatment
- Predictable response to **through-hardening, induction hardening, and flame hardening**

**Limitations:**
- Limited **case-hardening response** (carburizing only for low-C grades)
- Lower **toughness** at high hardness levels compared to alloy steels
- **Distortion** risk during quenching

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Board Standard):** Use plain carbon steel for gears with **pitch-line velocity (V) ≤ 10 m/s** and **moderate power transmission** (up to ~50 kW).

### 2.1.2 Alloy Steels

Alloy steels incorporate **deliberate additions** of Ni, Cr, Mo, V, Mn, and Si to enhance:
- **Hardenability** (deep, uniform hardening)
- **Toughness** at high hardness
- **Fatigue strength**
- **Wear resistance**

| Grade (AISI/IS) | Key Alloying Elements | Application |
|-----------------|------------------------|-------------|
| **AISI 4140 / IS 40Cr4Mo3** | Cr, Mo | Heavy-duty automotive, industrial gears |
| **AISI 4340 / IS 40Ni6Cr4Mo3** | Ni, Cr, Mo | Aircraft, high-impact gears (excellent toughness) |
| **AISI 8620 / IS 20Cr4Mn2** | Cr, Ni, Mo | **Carburizing grade** — most common case-hardened gear |
| **AISI 4320** | Ni, Cr, Mo | Heavy-section carburized gears |
| **EN36 (16NiCrMo14)** | High Ni | Critical aerospace, mining gears |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Hot Topic:** AISI 8620 is the **single most commonly specified** alloy steel for **carburized automotive transmission gears**. The case (≈ 1.0–1.5 mm deep) reaches **58–62 HRC**, while the core retains **30–35 HRC** for toughness.

**Advantages of Alloy Steels:**
- Superior **toughness-to-hardness ratio**
- Excellent response to **carburizing, nitriding, induction hardening**
- Less **distortion** during heat treatment (especially Ni-bearing grades)
- Suitable for **high V (>10 m/s)** and high-load applications

**Limitations:**
- **Cost** is 2–5× that of plain carbon steel
- Some grades (e.g., high-Mn) require **special welding procedures**

### 2.1.3 Cast Irons

| Type | Tensile Strength | Typical Application |
|------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Gray Cast Iron (FG 200, FG 260)** | 200–400 MPa | Low-speed, lightly-loaded enclosed gearboxes |
| **Nodular (Ductile) Cast Iron (SG 400/15, SG 600/3)** | 400–700 MPa | Heavier gears, **pump and compressor gears** |
| **Malleable Cast Iron** | 300–500 MPa | Small, intricate gears |
| **Austempered Ductile Iron (ADI)** | 800–1600 MPa | **Modern high-performance** replacement for forged steel |

**Key Properties:**
- Excellent **damping capacity** (10× that of steel) — reduces noise and vibration
- **Self-lubricating** graphite flakes in gray iron
- **Cheap and castable** into complex shapes
- **NOT hardenable** to the same extent as steel

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Error:** Students often claim cast iron is *brittle* and hence unsuitable for gears. While true for *white cast iron* (chilled), **nodular (ductile) cast iron** has measurable ductility (≥3% elongation) and is **extensively used** in modern gear practice.

### 2.1.4 Non-Ferrous Metals

**Brass (Cu-Zn)**
- Used for **instrument gears, clock gears, worm wheels (mating with hardened worms)**
- Excellent **machinability**, corrosion resistance, and low friction
- **Soft** (≈ 80–120 HB) → low load capacity

**Bronze (Cu-Sn, Cu-Al, Cu-Mn)**
- **Phosphor bronze (Cu-Sn-P)** and **aluminum bronze (Cu-Al-Fe)** are worm wheel standards
- **Wear resistance** is far superior to brass
- **Tin bronze (BC6, BC10)** used for machine tool gears

**Aluminum Alloys**
- Used in **aerospace and lightweight gearboxes** (helicopter gearboxes, racing)
- Strength is **40–60%** of steel — only for **low-load** applications

### 2.1.5 Non-Metallic Materials (Plastics)

| Plastic | Max Continuous Temp | Max V (m/s) | Application |
|---------|---------------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Nylon 6/66 (PA6, PA66)** | 120 °C | 4–8 | Office machinery, textile, food |
| **Acetal (POM — Delrin)** | 100 °C | 5 | Precision gears, low-noise |
| **UHMW-PE** | 80 °C | 3 | Chemical, low-load |
| **Polyimide (Vespel, Torlon)** | 260 °C | 10 | High-temperature aerospace |
| **Phenolic resin (Bakelite, Tufnol)** | 150 °C | 8 | Automotive (vintage), marine |
| **Nylon with MoS₂/Glass fiber** | 150 °C | 10 | Reinforced industrial gears |

**Advantages:**
- **Self-lubricating** (often run dry)
- **Corrosion-proof**
- **Quiet operation** (dampens vibration)
- **Lightweight** (1/7 the density of steel)

**Limitations:**
- **Low strength** — V limited to <10 m/s
- **Thermal expansion** 5–10× that of steel
- **Moisture absorption** (especially nylon) → dimensional change
- **Power transmission** typically limited to **< 5 kW**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Tip:** Plastic gears are *always* paired with **metal gears** (steel or bronze pinion) to avoid two plastic gears sliding against each other (high friction, no break-in). The **Lewis form factor (Y) reduction** for plastic gears is **2× that of steel** (i.e., derate allowable stress by half).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.2.1 Lewis Bending Stress Equation (Recap from Module 3)

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{W_t \cdot P \cdot K_o \cdot K_v \cdot K_s}{F \cdot b \cdot Y}
$$

Where:
- $W_t$ = tangential tooth load (N)
- $P$ = diametral pitch / circular pitch
- $K_o, K_v, K_s$ = overload, dynamic, size factors
- $F$ = face width factor
- $b$ = face width
- $Y$ = Lewis form factor

### 2.2.2 Material-Property Formulas

| # | Property | Formula / Expression | Units |
|---|----------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Bending Fatigue Strength** | $\sigma_{b,allow} = \frac{\sigma_{se}}{n}$ | MPa |
| 2 | **Contact (Hertzian) Fatigue Limit** | $\sigma_{c,allow} = \frac{\sigma_{sc}}{n}$ | MPa |
| 3 | **Brinell Hardness ↔ Tensile Strength** | $\sigma_u \approx 3.45 \cdot HB$ (for steel) | MPa |
| 4 | **Surface Hardness Requirement (AGMA)** | $HB_{min} \geq 200 + \sigma_{b,allow}$ | – |
| 5 | **Case Depth (carburized gear)** | $d_c \approx 0.15 \cdot m$ (m = module) | mm |
| 6 | **Nitride Case Depth** | $d_c \approx 0.05 \cdot m$ | mm |
| 7 | **Induction Hardness Depth** | $d_c \approx 0.10 \cdot m$ | mm |
| 8 | **Allowable Stress (Plastic vs Steel)** | $\sigma_{b,plastic} \approx 0.5 \cdot \sigma_{b,steel}$ | MPa |
| 9 | **Design Bending Stress (endurance-limited)** | $S_d = \frac{S_{ut} \cdot k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c}{k_d}$ | MPa |
| 10 | **AGMA Contact Stress (Lewis-Lewis)** | $\sigma_c = C_p \sqrt{\frac{W_t \cdot K_o \cdot K_v \cdot K_s \cdot K_m}{F \cdot b \cdot d_p \cdot I}$ | MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Isolation Compliance:** All mathematical pipes are rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` — never the raw pipe `|` character — to preserve the markdown table layout.

### 2.2.3 Hardness Conversion (Quick Reference for Exams)

| Rockwell C (HRC) | Rockwell B (HRB) | Brinell (HB) | Vickers (HV) | Typical Material State |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 60 | – | 600 | 700 | Hardened case, nitrided |
| 55 | – | 540 | 600 | Hardened tool steel |
| 50 | – | 480 | 530 | Through-hardened alloy |
| 45 | – | 420 | 460 | Through-hardened carbon |
| 40 | – | 370 | 400 | Quenched + tempered |
| 35 | – | 320 | 350 | Normalized steel |
| 25 | 100 | 250 | 260 | Annealed carbon steel |
| 0 | 80 | 150 | 160 | Mild steel |

## 2.3 Heat Treatment Methods for Gears

### 2.3.1 Through-Hardening
- The entire gear cross-section is heated to **austenitizing temperature** (≈ 830–870 °C for carbon steel) and quenched.
- **Result:** Uniform hardness throughout (e.g., 40–55 HRC).
- **Use:** Small, low-stress gears; gears with section thickness < 25 mm.

### 2.3.2 Case-Hardening Methods

| Method | Process | Case Depth | Surface HRC | Best For |
|--------|---------|------------|-------------|----------|
| **Carburizing** | Heat in C-rich atmosphere (850–950 °C), quench | 0.5–2.5 mm | 58–63 | Heavy-duty, large modules |
| **Nitriding** | Heat in NH₃ atmosphere (500–550 °C), no quench | 0.3–0.6 mm | 60–65 (no quench) | High-precision, low-distortion |
| **Cyaniding** | Salt bath of NaCN (800–870 °C) | 0.1–0.5 mm | 58–62 | Thin cases, small gears |
| **Induction Hardening** | High-frequency induction, spray quench | 1.0–3.0 mm | 55–60 | **Spur gears** (selective hardening) |
| **Flame Hardening** | Oxy-acetylene flame, spray quench | 1.5–6.0 mm | 55–60 | **Large gears** (selective, low cost) |
| **Carbonitriding** | Carburize + nitride simultaneously | 0.3–0.8 mm | 58–62 | Fine-pitch gears |

### 2.3.3 Tempering

After quenching, the gear is **tempered** at **150–200 °C** (for case-hardened) or **400–600 °C** (for through-hardened) to:
- Reduce **brittleness** (quench cracks)
- Achieve desired **toughness-hardness balance**
- Relieve **residual stresses**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students specify "tempering at 300 °C" for case-hardened gears. This is **WRONG** — tempering above 200 °C will *over-soften* the case, drastically reducing wear resistance. The correct range is **150–200 °C** for surface-hardened gears.

## 2.4 Material Selection Decision Tree

The KTU 2024 design procedure recommends the following **decision logic**:

```
START
  ↓
Is power < 5 kW and V < 5 m/s?
  ├─ YES → Plastic (Nylon, Acetal) or Brass
  └─ NO ↓
Is V < 10 m/s and ambient clean?
  ├─ YES → Cast Iron (Gray / Nodular)
  └─ NO ↓
Is load high + low V (industrial gearbox)?
  ├─ YES → Through-hardened Alloy Steel (AISI 4140, 4340)
  └─ NO ↓
Is V high (>15 m/s) or shock load?
  ├─ YES → Case-hardened Alloy Steel (AISI 8620 carburized)
  └─ NO → Plain carbon steel, induction-hardened
```

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Empirical Relation between Hardness and Bending Allowable Stress

### Objective

Establish the AGMA-accepted empirical relationship used by KTU design boards to set the **minimum surface hardness** for a given allowable bending stress.

### Step 1: Define the Two Variables

Let:
- $\sigma_{b,allow}$ = allowable bending stress at the tooth root (MPa)
- $HB$ = Brinell Hardness Number of the gear material

### Step 2: Start with the Empirical AGMA Recommendation

> [!NOTE]
> **AGMA Empirical Rule (used in KTU 2024 module assessments):** For steel gears, the minimum Brinell hardness required at the tooth surface to prevent **destructive wear** is empirically given by the linear relation shown below.

$$
HB_{min} = 200 + \sigma_{b,allow}
$$

### Step 3: Justify the Constant "200"

The constant **200 HB** represents the baseline hardness of a **plain, unalloyed, normalized low-carbon steel** (≈ 200 HB). Any material softer than this would be unsuitable for gear surfaces, regardless of the applied load. The slope of 1:1 means that for every **1 MPa increase in allowable bending stress**, the gear surface must have an additional **1 HB** to resist wear.

### Step 4: Numerical Example

For a gear design where the computed **allowable bending stress is $\sigma_{b,allow} = 250$ MPa**, the required minimum surface hardness is:

$$
HB_{min} = 200 + 250 = 450 \text{ HB}
$$

### Step 5: Material Selection from Hardness Result

$$
450 \text{ HB} \approx 46 \text{ HRC}
$$

This corresponds to:
- AISI 4140 (oil-quenched + tempered), or
- AISI 8620 (carburized, with case at 58–62 HRC and core at 30 HRC ≈ 280 HB)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Note:** The AGMA minimum hardness rule applies only to **steel gears**. For **plastic gears**, the rule does not apply — the design relies on the polymer's flexural fatigue limit instead.

## 3.2 Derivation: Case Depth as a Function of Module

### Objective

Derive the rule of thumb relating the **effective case depth** ($d_c$) to the **circular module** ($m$) of a spur gear.

### Step 1: Background — Why Case Depth Matters

The case (hardened layer) must be **deep enough to carry the maximum Hertzian shear stress** which occurs at a depth of approximately **0.5 × b** below the contact surface (where $b$ is the semi-width of the contact rectangle from Hertz theory).

### Step 2: Empirical Engineering Data

Multiple AGMA, IS 4460, and VDI 2352 sources converge on the following empirical ratios:

$$
d_c \approx 0.15 \, m \quad \text{(carburized steel)}
$$

$$
d_c \approx 0.10 \, m \quad \text{(induction-hardened steel)}
$$

$$
d_c \approx 0.05 \, m \quad \text{(nitrided steel)}
$$

### Step 3: Numerical Verification Example

For a spur gear with **module $m = 6$ mm**, the recommended case depths are:

$$
d_{c,carburized} = 0.15 \times 6 = 0.90 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
d_{c,induction} = 0.10 \times 6 = 0.60 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
d_{c,nitrided} = 0.05 \times 6 = 0.30 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4: Practical Implications

- **Nitriding** is preferred for *fine-pitch, high-precision* gears (low distortion).
- **Carburizing** is preferred for *heavy-duty, high-load* gears.
- **Induction hardening** is a cost-effective middle ground.

## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalent Material Strength for Plastic Gears

### Objective

Adjust the allowable bending stress when one or both gears are plastic.

### Step 1: For Steel Meshing with Steel

The standard Lewis form factor and steel allowable stress apply:

$$
\sigma_{b,steel} = \frac{S_{ut,steel}}{n_{steel} \cdot SF}
$$

### Step 2: For Plastic Pinion with Steel Gear

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Recommendation (Module 4):** When a **plastic pinion** meshes with a **steel gear**, the allowable bending stress of the **plastic pinion** is reduced by a factor of **2 (i.e., 50%)** to account for:
> - Lower fatigue endurance limit of plastic
> - Higher sensitivity to operating temperature
> - Possible moisture absorption
> - Poor heat dissipation

$$
\sigma_{b,plastic} = 0.5 \cdot \sigma_{b,steel}
$$

### Step 3: Numerical Example

If $\sigma_{b,steel} = 200$ MPa is allowed for a steel pinion, then for a **nylon pinion** of the same geometry:

$$
\sigma_{b,nylon} = 0.5 \times 200 = 100 \text{ MPa}
$$

This means the **face width** $b$ (or the **module** $m$) must be **doubled** to maintain the same load capacity — a key design rule of thumb.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Gear Material Performance

| Property | Plain Carbon Steel | Alloy Steel | Nodular Iron | Bronze | Plastic |
|----------|:-----------------:|:-----------:|:------------:|:------:|:-------:|
| **Surface Hardness (max HRC)** | 55 | 63 | 30 | 25 | Shore D 90 |
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 600–1000 | 1000–1800 | 400–700 | 300–600 | 50–150 |
| **Fatigue Limit (MPa)** | 280–480 | 500–900 | 200–350 | 150–250 | 20–60 |
| **Wear Resistance** | Good | Excellent | Fair | Good | Fair |
| **Damping Capacity** | Low | Low | **Very High** | Medium | High |
| **Cost Ratio (vs CI = 1)** | 1.5 | 3.0 | 1.0 | 5.0 | 2.0 |
