# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"List any three essential properties required for a gear material and justify each in one line."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **High surface hardness** — Resists pitting, wear, and scoring of the tooth flanks.
2. **High bending fatigue strength** — Withstands the fully-reversed cyclic stresses at the tooth root over millions of load cycles.
3. **Adequate core toughness** — Absorbs shock loads and prevents brittle fracture at the root, especially for variable-speed drives.

> [!NOTE]
> Any three from the list of eight in §1.4 earn full 3 marks. The justification line is *compulsory* to fetch the third mark.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between case hardening and through hardening of gears. Give one example material for each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Case Hardening | Through Hardening |
|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Hardness profile** | Hard surface (58–62 HRC) + tough core (28–35 HRC) | Uniform hardness throughout (40–55 HRC) |
| **Process** | Carburize/nitride + quench | Heat entire gear to austenitizing temp + quench |
| **Distortion** | Lower (carburized) / Negligible (nitrided) | Higher (uniform quench) |
| **Example material** | AISI 8620, EN36 | AISI 4140, AISI 4340 |
| **Best for** | High-load, high-V gears | Low-V, small-section gears |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**A.** (a) **Discuss in detail the different heat treatment methods used for gear materials. Compare carburizing and nitriding with respect to case depth, distortion, surface hardness, and process temperature.** **[7 Marks]**

**A.** (b) **A spur gear pair transmitting 15 kW at 1440 rpm with a velocity ratio of 3:1 is to be designed. Recommend a suitable material and heat treatment. Justify your selection. (Pitch-line velocity = 7.5 m/s assumed for the pinion.)** **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — A(a)

**Heat Treatment Methods for Gears:**

1. **Through Hardening:** The gear is heated uniformly to **830–870 °C** (for carbon steel) and quenched in oil/water. The result is uniform hardness (40–55 HRC). Used for small, low-stress gears.

2. **Carburizing:** Low-carbon steel (AISI 8620, EN36) is heated to **850–950 °C** in a carbon-rich atmosphere (CO, CH₄), held for several hours (carbon diffuses into the surface), then quenched. **Case depth: 0.5–2.5 mm**, surface hardness **58–63 HRC**, core remains tough.

3. **Nitriding:** Steel is heated to **500–550 °C** in an ammonia (NH₃) atmosphere. Nitrogen diffuses inward, forming hard nitrides. **No quenching required** → minimal distortion. Case depth **0.3–0.6 mm**, hardness **60–65 HRC** (≈ 1000 HV).

4. **Induction Hardening:** High-frequency induction coil heats the tooth surface rapidly, followed by a water spray quench. Selective, fast, and energy-efficient. Case depth **0.5–1.5 mm**.

5. **Flame Hardening:** Oxy-acetylene flame heats the surface, then quenched. Suitable for **very large gears** (e.g., mill girth gears).

6. **Cyaniding:** Salt bath of NaCN at 800–870 °C, then quench. Produces a thin, hard case (0.1–0.5 mm). Limited by cyanide toxicity.

**Comparison Table — Carburizing vs Nitriding:**

| Property | Carburizing | Nitriding |
|----------|-------------|-----------|
| Temperature | 850–950 °C | 500–550 °C |
| Case Depth | 0.5–2.5 mm | 0.3–0.6 mm |
| Surface Hardness | 58–63 HRC | 60–65 HRC (≈ 1000 HV) |
| Core Hardness | 30–35 HRC | 28–32 HRC |
| Distortion | Moderate (requires grinding) | **Negligible** (no quench) |
| Quench Required? | Yes | No |
| Cycle Time | 4–8 hours | 20–40 hours |
| Cost | Moderate | Higher |
| Best For | Heavy-duty, large modules | Precision, thin sections |

#### Model Solution — A(b)

**Given:** $P = 15$ kW, $N_1 = 1440$ rpm, $V = 7.5$ m/s, gear ratio = 3:1.

**Step 1 — Identify Service Class:**
- Power = 15 kW → Medium-duty
- V = 7.5 m/s → Moderate pitch-line velocity
- Application: Industrial (assumed) → Continuous duty

**Step 2 — Recommended Material:**

> **Material: AISI 4140 (or AISI 8620) Alloy Steel**

**Step 3 — Recommended Heat Treatment:**

> **Through-hardening (for AISI 4140) or Carburizing (for AISI 8620) + Tempering**

**Step 4 — Justification (Marks Distribution):**

1. **Power level** is moderate-to-high → plain carbon steel is *marginal*; alloy steel is preferred.
2. **Pitch-line velocity (7.5 m/s)** is below the 10 m/s threshold for plastics and above the practical range for cast iron.
3. **Continuous-duty industrial application** demands a **hard surface for wear resistance** combined with a **tough core for fatigue** → case-hardened alloy steel is ideal.
4. **Tempering at 150–200 °C** after carburizing yields the optimal 58–62 HRC case + 30 HRC core balance.
5. **Case depth** $= 0.15 \times m$. For an assumed module $m \approx 4$ mm, $d_c \approx 0.6$ mm — achievable by either induction hardening or nitriding.

**Final Recommendation:**
> **Use AISI 4140 through-hardened to 45–50 HRC, OR AISI 8620 case-carburized (0.8 mm case) to 60 HRC, tempered at 180 °C.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**B.** (a) **Classify gear materials in detail. Discuss the application of non-metallic gears with their limitations.** **[7 Marks]**

**B.** (b) **A helical gear pair made of through-hardened AISI 4140 alloy steel transmits 25 kW at 2000 rpm (pinion). The pitch-line velocity is 12 m/s. The required allowable bending stress is 220 MPa. Verify whether the surface hardness meets the AGMA minimum hardness criterion.** **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — B(a)

**Classification of Gear Materials:**

```
I. Metallic
   A. Ferrous
      1. Plain Carbon Steel (AISI 1040, 1050, 1060)
      2. Alloy Steel (AISI 4140, 4340, 8620, EN36)
      3. Cast Iron (Gray, Nodular/Ductile, Malleable, ADI)
   B. Non-Ferrous
      1. Brass (Cu-Zn) — instrument gears
      2. Bronze (Cu-Sn, Cu-Al) — worm wheels
      3. Aluminum Alloys — aerospace

II. Non-Metallic
   A. Thermoplastics
      1. Nylon (PA6, PA66)
      2. Acetal (POM — Delrin)
      3. UHMW-PE
      4. Polyimide (Vespel, Torlon)
   B. Thermosets
      1. Phenolic resin (Bakelite, Tufnol)
      2. Epoxy composites
   C. Fiber-Reinforced
      1. Glass-fiber nylon
      2. Carbon-fiber reinforced polymers
```

**Application of Non-Metallic Gears (with limitations):**

| Application | Material | Why |
|-------------|----------|-----|
| Office machinery (printers, copiers) | Acetal, Nylon | Self-lubricating, quiet |
| Textile machinery | Nylon (moisture-resistant) | No oil contamination |
| Food processing | UHMW-PE, Nylon | FDA-approved, washable |
| Marine / chemical pumps | Phenolic, PTFE-impregnated | Corrosion-proof |
| Aerospace actuators | Polyimide (Vespel) | High temp (260 °C) |
| Clock and instrument gears | Brass, Acetal | Precision, low friction |

**Limitations of Non-Metallic Gears:**

1. **Low power capacity** (< 5 kW typical; < 20 kW exceptional).
2. **Limited velocity** (V < 10 m/s).
3. **High thermal expansion** → backlash changes with temperature.
4. **Moisture absorption** (nylon absorbs 1–3% by weight) → dimensional change.
5. **Low Young's modulus** → high deflection under load; may cause tooth deflection and uneven loading.
6. **Cannot be case-hardened** — full-section strength only.
7. **Sensitive to UV, ozone, and certain chemicals.**
8. **Operating temperature limited** (max 120–150 °C for nylons).
9. **Wear debris contamination** in food-grade or medical applications.
10. **Must be paired with metal gear** to avoid plastic-on-plastic sliding contact failure.

#### Model Solution — B(b)

**Given:**
- Power $P = 25$ kW
- $N_1 = 2000$ rpm (pinion)
- $V = 12$ m/s
- Allowable bending stress $\sigma_{b,allow} = 220$ MPa
- Material: AISI 4140 (through-hardened alloy steel)

**Step 1: Apply the AGMA Minimum Surface Hardness Rule**

$$
HB_{min} = 200 + \sigma_{b,allow}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Given Allowable Stress**

$$
HB_{min} = 200 + 220 = 420 \text{ HB}
$$

**Step 3: Convert 420 HB to HRC for Standard Reference**

From standard conversion tables:
$$
420 \text{ HB} \approx 44 \text{ HRC}
$$

**Step 4: Verify Against AISI 4140's Achievable Hardness**

AISI 4140, oil-quenched and tempered, can readily achieve:
- **42–48 HRC** at the surface and core.
- **Brinell hardness range: 380–460 HB**.

**Step 5: Conclude**

> **YES — The AGMA minimum hardness criterion is satisfied.**
> The required 420 HB (≈ 44 HRC) is well within the achievable range of through-hardened AISI 4140. A typical heat-treatment specification of *oil-quench from 845 °C + temper at 425 °C* yields approximately **44–46 HRC**, comfortably exceeding the 420 HB threshold.

**Step 6: Additional Design Note**

> The power level (25 kW) and V (12 m/s) are *just at the limit* of what through-hardening can sustain. For a more conservative design with a **safety factor of 1.5–2.0**, consider upgrading to **AISI 8620 carburized** (case 60 HRC, core 32 HRC) to gain a **40% margin** in surface hardness and a corresponding improvement in pitting resistance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.5 derating for plastic gears.** Always halve $\sigma_{b,steel}$ when designing a plastic pinion. **Loss: up to 2 marks per part.**
> 2. **Specifying tempering temperature > 200 °C for case-hardened gears.** This drops surface hardness below the AGMA requirement. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Confusing cyaniding and carburizing.** Cyaniding uses a *liquid salt bath*, not a gas atmosphere. Case depths are different (0.1–0.5 mm vs 0.5–2.5 mm). **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Stating "cast iron cannot be used for gears."** It is true for *gray cast iron* in high-load, high-V applications, but *nodular (ductile) cast iron* and *ADI* are extensively used. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Not justifying material choice with operating conditions.** A bare "AISI 4140" answer is incomplete — always tie the choice to **power, V, duty cycle, and heat treatment**. **Loss: 2–3 marks per question.**
> 6. **Mixing up the AGMA HB rule.** It is $HB_{min} = 200 + \sigma_{b,allow}$ — *not* $HB_{min} = 200 \cdot \sigma_{b,allow}$ (multiplicative form is wrong). **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 7. **Forgetting that nitriding needs no quench.** Writing "nitride and quench" is a factual error. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Gear Materials (KTU 2024 Module 4)**

### ✔ Core Knowledge
- [ ] Steel = **90%** of all industrial gears. Always the default unless explicitly otherwise.
- [ ] Carbon content drives hardenability; alloying (Ni, Cr, Mo) *boosts* hardenability and toughness.
- [ ] Gears are **surface-critical** → case-hardening is preferred over through-hardening for high loads.
- [ ] **AISI 8620** is the *most commonly specified* carburizing grade for transmission gears.

### ✔ Empirical Rules of Thumb
- [ ] Carburized case depth: $d_c \approx 0.15 \, m$
- [ ] Nitrided case depth: $d_c \approx 0.05 \, m$
- [ ] AGMA minimum hardness: $HB_{min} = 200 + \sigma_{b,allow}$
- [ ] Plastic gear derating: $\sigma_{b,plastic} = 0.5 \cdot \sigma_{b,steel}$
- [ ] Max V for plastic: 5–10 m/s; for cast iron: 8 m/s; for alloy steel: 30 m/s

### ✔ Heat Treatment Quick-Facts
- [ ] Carburizing → 850–950 °C → quench → 58–62 HRC → temper at **150–200 °C**.
- [ ] Nitriding → 500–550 °C → **no quench** → 60–65 HRC → very low distortion.
- [ ] Induction hardening → selective, fast, ideal for spur gears.
- [ ] Tempering above 200 °C on case-hardened gears → **over-softens case** (mark killer!).

### ✔ Material Selection Mnemonic — **"SICC"**
- [ ] **S**teel (90% default)
- [ ] **I**ron (cast — low V, low load)
- [ ] **C**opper (brass/bronze — instruments, worm wheels)
- [ ] **C**omposite (plastic — low power, dry, quiet)

### ✔ Limitations to Remember
- [ ] Plastics: V < 10 m/s, P < 5 kW, must mesh with *metal* gear.
- [ ] Cast iron: not for high-V, not for high-impact gears.
- [ ] Brass: too soft for power transmission — instruments only.
- [ ] Aluminum alloys: aerospace only — not for industrial gearboxes.

### ✔ Numerical Conversion Reminders
- [ ] $1 \text{ HRC} \approx 10 \text{ HB}$ (approximate, for steel).
- [ ] $\sigma_u \approx 3.45 \cdot HB$ (for steel, in MPa).
- [ ] $60 \text{ HRC} \approx 600 \text{ HB} \approx 700 \text{ HV}$.

### ✔ KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Concepts
- [ ] Compare carburizing vs nitriding (table format).
- [ ] Justify material selection for a given power and V.
- [ ] Apply the AGMA minimum hardness rule.
- [ ] Explain the "hard case + tough core" philosophy.
- [ ] State the 0.5 derating rule for plastic gears.

> **End of Module 4 — Gear Materials Notes**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | PEMET743 | Outcome-Based Education Framework*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tooth Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tooth Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Tooth Loads"?

In **Gear Design**, "Tooth Loads" refers to the **complete set of forces, stresses, and dynamic effects acting on a single meshing pair of gear teeth** during power transmission. When torque is transmitted from a driving pinion to a driven gear, the contact between meshing teeth creates a force system that must be quantified accurately to prevent tooth failure by bending, wear, pitting, or scuffing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Tooth load is the resultant force transmitted across the line of action between two meshing gear teeth. It is resolved into components — tangential, radial, and axial (for helical/bevel) — and then modified by a series of correction factors (dynamic, service, load distribution, temperature) to obtain the **Effective Tooth Load** (Wₒ), which forms the design input for both beam strength (Lewis) and wear strength (Buckingham) equations.*

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — Plain English

Imagine two interlocking combs being pushed against each other. When the handle of the driving comb is turned, every tooth pushes its neighbor on the other comb. That push is **not a single, clean force** — it is:

- A **sideways push** (the actual turning effort → **tangential force, Wₜ**)
- A **push apart from each other** (the gears try to fly apart → **radial force, Wᵣ**)
- A **push along the gear axis** (only for helical gears, like a corkscrew → **axial force, Wₐ**)

Now add real-life nuisances:
- The motor causes **vibrations** (dynamic effects).
- The **load itself is not perfectly smooth** (shocks from milling cutters, pistons).
- The shaft might **deflect slightly**, putting load on one tooth edge (edge contact).
- Oil gets **warm at high speeds**, losing strength (temperature).

So, the **static force (Wₜ)** is multiplied by several correction factors to give the **effective force (Wₒ)** the tooth must survive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Single Golden Rule of Module 4:**
> *Design is always done using the **Effective Tooth Load (Wₒ)**, never the simple tangential transmitted load (Wₜ).*
> $$W_o = C_v \cdot C_s \cdot C_m \cdot C_t \cdot W_t$$
> This is the most tested formula in Module 4.

---

## 1.3 Why is This Topic a KTU High-Weightage Area?

Gear failures are catastrophic (entire transmission destroyed). Tooth load analysis is the **first step** in the design-by-rational approach taught in PEMET743. The examiner almost always asks for:
- Force decomposition (especially for helical gears with the axial component).
- Calculation of effective load with all factors.
- Use of Wₒ as input to Lewis beam strength or Buckingham wear strength equations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Taxonomy of Tooth Loads

Tooth loads are classified in a structured hierarchy:

### A. **Tangential Tooth Load (Wₜ)** — The Prime Mover Force
The tangential force is the **useful force** doing actual work. It acts at the **pitch circle**, tangent to it.

$$W_t = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{2 \cdot T_1}{d_1} = \frac{2 \cdot T_2}{d_2} \quad \text{(in Newtons)}$$

Where:
- **P** = Power transmitted (Watts, **W**)
- **v** = Pitch line velocity (m/s)
- **T₁, T₂** = Torque on pinion and gear (N·m)
- **d₁, d₂** = Pitch circle diameters of pinion and gear (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Same Wₜ acts on both pinion AND gear** (Newton's 3rd Law). Pinion has higher Wₜ per unit area because it is smaller.

### B. **Radial Tooth Load (Wᵣ)** — The Separating Force
Acts along the radius, pushing the gears apart. Causes bearing reactions and housing loads.

**For Spur Gears (20° full-depth):**
$$W_r = W_t \cdot \tan(\alpha) = W_t \cdot \tan(20°) \approx 0.364 \cdot W_t$$

**For Helical Gears:**
$$W_r = \frac{W_t \cdot \tan(\alpha_n)}{\cos(\beta)}$$

### C. **Axial Tooth Load (Wₐ)** — The Thrust Force (Helical Only)
A unique property of helical gears arising from the helix angle β.

$$W_a = W_t \cdot \tan(\beta)$$

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU error: Students use Wₐ = Wₜ · sin(β). **Correct formula is Wₐ = Wₜ · tan(β)**. Memorize correctly.

### D. **Total (Normal) Tooth Load (Wₙ)** — The True Contact Force
The resultant of Wₜ and Wᵣ, acting along the line of action (pressure line).

**Spur Gears:**
$$W_n = \frac{W_t}{\cos(\alpha)} = \frac{W_t}{\cos(20°)} \approx 1.064 \cdot W_t$$

**Helical Gears:**
$$W_n = \frac{W_t}{\cos(\alpha_n) \cdot \cos(\beta)}$$

---

## 2.2 The Four Correction Factors

The simple Wₜ is **never** used for design. The **Effective Load (Wₒ)** accounts for real-world imperfections:

### 1. **Dynamic Factor (Cᵥ or Kᵥ)** — Velocity Factor
Accounts for vibration, inertia of reciprocating parts, and impact at high speeds.

| Velocity (v) | Velocity Factor Cᵥ |
|---|---|
| $v \le 10$ m/s (or 2000 fpm) | $C_v = \dfrac{3}{3 + v}$ |
| $v > 10$ m/s (or 2000 fpm) | $C_v = \dfrac{3.05}{3.05 + v}$ (or Buckingham's interpolation) |

> [!NOTE]
> Different textbooks use slightly different forms. For KTU, the most accepted is the AGMA-type empirical curve, but the **simple rational form** above is preferred for exam problems.

### 2. **Service Factor (Cₛ or Kₛ)** — Application Factor
Accounts for shock loads inherent in the driven machine + driver.

| Power Source | Uniform Load | Light Shock | Medium Shock | Heavy Shock |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform (motor, turbine) | 1.00 | 1.25 | 1.50 | 1.75 |
| Light Shock (multicyl. engine) | 1.25 | 1.50 | 1.75 | 2.00 |
| Medium Shock (single cyl. engine) | 1.50 | 1.75 | 2.00 | 2.25 |

### 3. **Load Distribution Factor (Cₘ or Kₘ)**
Accounts for non-uniform load distribution across the face width due to:
- Shaft deflection
- Bearing deflection
- Misalignment
- Deflection under load

$$C_m = 1 + C_{mc} \left(C_{pf} \cdot C_{pm} + C_{ma} \cdot C_e\right)$$

For preliminary design, **Cₘ ≈ 1.1 to 1.3** is typically used.

### 4. **Temperature Factor (Cₜ or Kₜ)**
Oil loses viscosity and load capacity at high temperatures.

| Temperature (°C) | Cₜ |
|---|---|
| Up to 100 °C | 1.00 |
| 100–150 °C | 1.10 – 1.25 |
| Above 150 °C | Must use heat-resistant alloys |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula (Spur)** | **Formula (Helical)** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tangential Load | $W_t = \dfrac{P}{v} = \dfrac{2T}{d}$ | Same as spur | **N** |
| 2 | Radial Load | $W_r = W_t \tan \alpha$ | $W_r = \dfrac{W_t \tan \alpha_n}{\cos \beta}$ | **N** |
| 3 | Axial Load | **Not applicable** | $W_a = W_t \tan \beta$ | **N** |
| 4 | Normal Load | $W_n = \dfrac{W_t}{\cos \alpha}$ | $W_n = \dfrac{W_t}{\cos \alpha_n \cos \beta}$ | **N** |
| 5 | Pitch Line Velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi d N}{60{,}000}$ | Same | **m/s** |
| 6 | Dynamic Factor (v < 10) | $C_v = \dfrac{3}{3+v}$ | Same | — |
| 7 | Dynamic Factor (v > 10) | $C_v = \dfrac{3.05}{3.05+v}$ | Same | — |
| 8 | Effective Load | $W_o = C_v \cdot C_s \cdot C_m \cdot C_t \cdot W_t$ | Same | **N** |
| 9 | Lewis Form Factor (y) | $y = 0.154 - \dfrac{0.912}{Z}$ (spur) | $y_N = 0.485 \cos^2 \beta / \cos \alpha_n \cdot \text{...}$ | — |
| 10 | Beam Strength (Lewis) | $F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi m \cdot y$ | $F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi m_n \cdot y$ | **N** |
| 11 | Wear Strength (Buckingham) | $S_w = b \cdot Q \cdot K \cdot d_p$ | $S_w = b \cdot Q \cdot K \cdot d_p$ | **N** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** Whenever you see "design load" in Module 4 problems, **immediately substitute Wₒ for Wₜ** in the Lewis and Buckingham equations. A common KTU trap is students using Wₜ in the strength equations instead of Wₒ.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Gearboxes:** Tooth load analysis determines bearing selection, housing wall thickness, and shaft diameter.
- **Wind Turbines:** Helical gear axial thrust calculations dictate thrust bearing sizing (megawatt-class).
- **Aerospace:** Spur gear Wᵣ + Wₐ reactions are used for finite element analysis of the gearbox casing.
- **Industrial Robotics:** Exact Wₒ values predict fatigue life cycles for high-cycle applications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Wₜ = 2T/d

The power transmitted by a rotating shaft is:

$$P = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{(watts, with T in N·m, N in rpm)}$$

But the power at the gear contact is also the product of tangential force and velocity:

$$P = W_t \cdot v$$

At the pitch circle, the linear velocity is:

$$v = \frac{\pi d N}{60{,}000} \quad \text{(m/s, with d in mm)}$$

Setting both expressions of P equal:

$$W_t \cdot v = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60}$$

Substituting v:

$$W_t \cdot \frac{\pi d N}{60{,}000} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60}$$

Cancelling $\pi N / 60$ from both sides:

$$W_t \cdot \frac{d}{1000} = 2T \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_t = \frac{2000 T}{d}$$

> **Boxed Final Form (when d in mm, T in N·m):**
> $$\boxed{W_t = \frac{2000 \, T}{d} \quad \text{N}}$$

This is the **most-rewritten equation in Module 4**. Memorize its three equivalent forms:
1. $W_t = P/v$
2. $W_t = 2T/d$ (with consistent units)
3. $W_t = 2000T/d$ (d in mm, T in N·m) — most common in KTU problems

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Load Equation

The **effective load (Wₒ)** is the actual force a gear tooth experiences in service. It is obtained by sequentially applying correction factors to the basic tangential load:

**Step 1:** Start with the **nominal transmitted tangential load** $W_t$.

**Step 2:** Multiply by the **dynamic factor** $C_v$ to account for impact and vibration:
$$W_d = C_v \cdot W_t \quad \text{(Dynamic Load)}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by the **service factor** $C_s$ to account for external shock:
$$W_{ds} = C_s \cdot W_d = C_s \cdot C_v \cdot W_t$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by the **load distribution factor** $C_m$ to account for misalignment and edge contact:
$$W_{dsm} = C_m \cdot C_{ds} = C_m \cdot C_s \cdot C_v \cdot W_t$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by the **temperature factor** $C_t$ to account for hot oil losing film strength:
$$W_o = C_t \cdot W_{dsm} = C_t \cdot C_m \cdot C_s \cdot C_v \cdot W_t$$

**Combined Formula (consolidated):**
$$\boxed{W_o = C_v \cdot C_s \cdot C_m \cdot C_t \cdot W_t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The product $C_v \cdot C_s \cdot C_m \cdot C_t$ is sometimes written as a single **overload factor K** in older textbooks, but KTU 2024 expects the four-factor form.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Problem:** A helical gear pair transmits **12 kW** at **1450 rpm** of the pinion. Pinion has **20 teeth**, module = **5 mm**, helix angle = **15°**, normal pressure angle = **20°**. Take $C_v = 1.5$, $C_s = 1.5$, $C_m = 1.2$, $C_t = 1.0$. Find Wₜ, Wᵣ, Wₐ, Wₙ, and Wₒ.

### Step 1: Compute Pitch Circle Diameter
$$d_1 = m \cdot Z_1 = 5 \times 20 = 100 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2: Compute Pitch Line Velocity
$$v = \frac{\pi d_1 N}{60{,}000} = \frac{\pi \times 100 \times 1450}{60{,}000} = 7.59 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 3: Compute Tangential Load
$$W_t = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{12{,}000}{7.59} = 1581 \text{ N}$$

### Step 4: Compute Radial Load (Helical Form)
$$W_r = \frac{W_t \cdot \tan \alpha_n}{\cos \beta} = \frac{1581 \times \tan 20°}{\cos 15°} = \frac{1581 \times 0.3640}{0.9659} = 595.8 \text{ N}$$

### Step 5: Compute Axial Load
$$W_a = W_t \cdot \tan \beta = 1581 \times \tan 15° = 1581 \times 0.2679 = 423.5 \text{ N}$$

### Step 6: Compute Normal Load
$$W_n = \frac{W_t}{\cos \alpha_n \cdot \cos \beta} = \frac{1581}{\cos 20° \cdot \cos 15°} = \frac{1581}{0.9397 \times 0.9659} = 1741.7 \text{ N}$$

### Step 7: Compute Effective Load
$$W_o = C_v \cdot C_s \cdot C_m \cdot C_t \cdot W_t = 1.5 \times 1.5 \times 1.2 \times 1.0 \times 1581 = 4268.7 \text{ N}$$

### Verification
Note that $W_o \approx 2.7 \times W_t$, which is a reasonable multiplier for medium-shock industrial applications.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Formula to Use When?"

| **Scenario** | **Use This** | **Common Mistake** |
|---|---|---|
| Force in a spur gear, asked to find Wᵣ | $W_r = W_t \tan(20°)$ | Using helical formula with helix angle = 0 |
| Helical gear axial thrust | $W_a = W_t \tan \beta$ | Using $W_a = W_t \sin \beta$ |
| Beam strength in design | Use $W_o$ (or Wd) in Lewis equation | Using bare $W_t$ |
| Pitch line velocity, N in rpm, d in mm | $v = \pi d N / 60{,}000$ | Forgetting the 1000 conversion |
| Service factor for uniform motor driving uniform conveyor | $C_s = 1.0$ | Always using $C_s = 1.5$ |
| Tangential force, T in N·mm, d in mm | $W_t = 2T/d$ | Mixing unit systems (e.g. T in N·m with d in mm without 1000) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Force Decomposition — Spur Gear (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **decision flow** for resolving tooth loads in a **spur gear** based on what the problem asks. This is exactly the kind of logic KTU expects students to demonstrate in 14-mark problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Power P, Speed N, Module m, Teeth Z, Face Width b] --> B[Compute Pitch Diameter d = m × Z]
    B --> C[Compute Pitch Line Velocity v = πdN / 60000 m/s]
    A --> D[Compute Tangential Load Wt = 2000T / d OR P / v]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Which Force is Required?}
    E -- Tangential Wt --> F[Use Wt directly]
    E -- Radial Wr --> G[Wr = Wt × tan20°]
    E -- Normal Wn --> H[Wn = Wt / cos20°]
    E -- Effective Wo --> I[Lookup Cv, Cs, Cm, Ct from data tables]
    I --> J[Wo = Cv × Cs × Cm × Ct × Wt]
    F --> K[Apply to Lewis Beam Equation]
    G --> L[Used for Bearing Reaction Analysis]
    H --> M[Used for Surface Contact Stress]
    J --> N[Apply to Lewis AND Buckingham Equations]
```

---

## 4.2 Force Decomposition — Helical Gear (Block Architecture)

This diagram handles the **helical gear case**, which is the higher-marks scenario in KTU 2024 Module 4 questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: P, N, mn, Zn, β, αn, b] --> B[Compute d = mn × Zn / cos β]
    B --> C[Compute Pitch Line Velocity v = πdN / 60000]
    A --> D[Compute Wt = 2000T / d]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Helical Gear Force System}
    E -- Tangential Force --> F[Wt is the primary transmitted load]
    E -- Radial Force --> G[Wr = Wt × tanαn / cosβ]
    E -- Axial Thrust Force --> H[Wa = Wt × tanβ]
    E -- Normal Contact Force --> I[Wn = Wt / cosαn × cosβ]
    F --> J[Compute Effective Load Wo]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Wo = Cv × Cs × Cm × Ct × Wt]
    K --> L[Input to Lewis Beam Strength AND Buckingham Wear Strength]
    H --> M[Thrust Bearing Sizing]
    G --> N[Bearing Radial Load Analysis]
```

---

## 4.3 Velocity Factor Decision Flow

A frequent sub-question is to justify the choice of $C_v$. This Mermaid block captures the standard KTU decision rule.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given Pitch Line Velocity v in m/s] --> B{v less than or equal to 10 m/s}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Cv = 3 / 3 + v]
    B -- No --> D[Use Cv = 3.05 / 3.05 + v]
    C --> E[Plug into Wo = Cv × Cs × Cm × Ct × Wt]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Final Effective Load Wo for Design]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip for students:** The 3.05-form is the AGMA/ASME preferred form for high-speed gearing (v > 10 m/s). The 3-form is the simple Indian standard form (Khurmi/Shigley simplified). KTU accepts both, but the **3-form is preferred** for 14-mark problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "Effective Tooth Load" in gears. List the four factors used to compute it from the tangential transmitted load.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The **Effective Tooth Load (Wₒ)** is the actual load a gear tooth experiences in service, obtained by modifying the nominal tangential transmitted load (Wₜ) with four correction factors.

$$\boxed{W_o = C_v \cdot C_s \cdot C_m \cdot C_t \cdot W_t}$$

The four factors are:
1. **Dynamic Factor (Cᵥ):** Accounts for dynamic effects, vibration, and impact due to pitch line velocity.
2. **Service Factor (Cₛ):** Accounts for external shock loads from the power source and driven machine.
3. **Load Distribution Factor (Cₘ):** Accounts for non-uniform load distribution along the face width due to misalignment, shaft deflection, and bearing deflection.
4. **Temperature Factor (Cₜ):** Accounts for the loss of lubricant viscosity and load-carrying capacity at elevated operating temperatures.

*(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: formula, 1 mark: four factors with one-line explanation.)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between tangential, radial, and axial tooth loads in a helical gear. State the formulas relating them to the tangential load Wₜ.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| **Load Type** | **Direction** | **Formula** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tangential Load (Wₜ)** | Tangent to pitch circle, in the plane of rotation | $W_t = 2000T/d$ | The useful force doing the actual work; **same for spur and helical** |
| **Radial Load (Wᵣ)** | Along the gear radius, separating the gears | $W_r = \dfrac{W_t \tan \alpha_n}{\cos \beta}$ | Pushes gears apart; resisted by shaft and bearings |
| **Axial Load (Wₐ)** | Parallel to the gear axis | $W_a = W_t \tan \beta$ | Unique to helical gears; resisted by thrust bearings |

*(1 mark: any one with correct formula, 1 mark: helical-specific distinction, 1 mark: physical meaning.)*

---

## PART B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

A pair of spur gears is to transmit **20 kW** at a pinion speed of **1500 rpm**. The pinion has **24 teeth** and the gear has **72 teeth**. The module is **5 mm** and the face width is **60 mm**. The pressure angle is **20° (full depth)**. Use the following data:

- Service factor $C_s = 1.5$
- Load distribution factor $C_m = 1.3$
- Temperature factor $C_t = 1.0$
- Allowable bending stress $\sigma_b = 100 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- Lewis form factor for 24 teeth: $y = 0.323$

#### **(a) Compute the tangential load, dynamic factor, effective load, and the Lewis beam strength of the pinion. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Pitch Circle Diameter of Pinion**
$$d_1 = m \cdot Z_1 = 5 \times 24 = 120 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Pitch Line Velocity**
$$v = \frac{\pi d_1 N}{60{,}000} = \frac{\pi \times 120 \times 1500}{60{,}000} = 9.42 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Tangential Load**
$$W_t = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{20{,}000}{9.42} = 2123 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Dynamic Factor (v < 10 m/s, so use 3-form)**
$$C_v = \frac{3}{3 + v} = \frac{3}{3 + 9.42} = \frac{3}{12.42} = 0.2415$$

**Step 5: Effective Load**
$$W_o = C_v \cdot C_s \cdot C_m \cdot C_t \cdot W_t = 0.2415 \times 1.5 \times 1.3 \times 1.0 \times 2123$$
$$W_o = 998.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6: Lewis Beam Strength**
$$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi m \cdot y = 100 \times 60 \times \pi \times 5 \times 0.323$$
$$F_b = 100 \times 60 \times 15.708 \times 0.323 = 30{,}442 \text{ N}$$

#### **(b) Calculate the factor of safety against bending failure. Comment on the design. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Factor of Safety**
$$\text{FoS} = \frac{F_b}{W_o} = \frac{30{,}442}{998.4} = 30.5$$

**Step 2: Comment**
The very high factor of safety (30.5) indicates the gear is **over-designed for bending**. The pinion has excess beam strength. This often happens at low powers and small modules. The designer may either:
- Reduce the face width to save material.
- Use a smaller module and verify that wear strength (Buckingham) is satisfied.
- Or, if the application is critical (e.g., aerospace, mining), retain the high safety margin.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

A helical gear pair transmits **30 kW** at **1440 rpm** of the pinion. The pinion has **20 teeth**, normal module **mₙ = 4 mm**, helix angle **β = 18°**, and normal pressure angle **αₙ = 20°**. Face width **b = 50 mm**. Take $C_v = 1.4$, $C_s = 1.75$, $C_m = 1.2$, $C_t = 1.0$.

#### **(a) Determine the tangential, radial, and axial tooth loads on the pinion. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Pitch Circle Diameter (Helical)**
$$d_1 = \frac{m_n \cdot Z_1}{\cos \beta} = \frac{4 \times 20}{\cos 18°} = \frac{80}{0.9511} = 84.11 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Pitch Line Velocity**
$$v = \frac{\pi d_1 N}{60{,}000} = \frac{\pi \times 84.11 \times 1440}{60{,}000} = 6.34 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Tangential Load**
$$W_t = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{30{,}000}{6.34} = 4732 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Radial Load (Helical Form)**
$$W_r = \frac{W_t \cdot \tan \alpha_n}{\cos \beta} = \frac{4732 \times \tan 20°}{\cos 18°} = \frac{4732 \times 0.3640}{0.9511} = 1811 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Axial Load**
$$W_a = W_t \cdot \tan \beta = 4732 \times \tan 18° = 4732 \times 0.3249 = 1537 \text{ N}$$

#### **(b) Compute the effective tooth load and the normal contact force. Also check if the gear is safe against bending if the Lewis beam strength is 18,000 N. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Effective Load**
$$W_o = C_v \cdot C_s \cdot C_m \cdot C_t \cdot W_t = 1.4 \times 1.75 \times 1.2 \times 1.0 \times 4732$$
$$W_o = 13{,}918 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Normal Contact Force**
$$W_n = \frac{W_t}{\cos \alpha_n \cdot \cos \beta} = \frac{4732}{\cos 20° \cdot \cos 18°} = \frac{4732}{0.9397 \times 0.9511} = 5295 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Check for Safety**
$$\text{FoS}_{\text{bending}} = \frac{F_b}{W_o} = \frac{18{,}000}{13{,}918} = 1.29$$

**Step 4: Comment**
A factor of safety of 1.29 is **marginally safe** (typical acceptable value 1.25–1.50 for industrial gears). The design is acceptable but on the conservative side. If the application involves heavy shock, a redesign with larger face width or higher module is recommended.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in "Tooth Loads" Problems (PEMET743):**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Wₜ Formula:** Using $W_t = 2T/d$ with T in N·m and d in mm without the 1000 conversion. **Always state the units and use $W_t = 2000T/d$ (d in mm, T in N·m).**
> 2. **Using Wₜ Instead of Wₒ in Strength Equations:** Beam strength and wear strength use **effective load**, not tangential load. If you use Wₜ, the factor of safety will be 2–3× higher than reality.
> 3. **Wrong Dynamic Factor Form:** For v > 10 m/s, using the 3-form instead of 3.05-form. Both may be accepted, but be consistent with your course reference.
> 4. **Forgetting the cos β in Helical Pitch Diameter:** Helical pitch diameter is $d = m_n \cdot Z / \cos \beta$, NOT $m_n \cdot Z$. This error propagates into velocity, Wₜ, and all force components.
> 5. **Mixing Up Spur and Helical Formulas:** Spur $W_r = W_t \tan 20°$, Helical $W_r = W_t \tan \alpha_n / \cos \beta$. Always read the question — is it spur or helical?

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Tooth Loads: Final Cheat Sheet**

- [x] **Wₜ = 2000T/d (N)** — Same for spur and helical. Foundation of all gear force calculations.
- [x] **Spur: Wᵣ = Wₜ tan 20°** | **Helical: Wᵣ = Wₜ tan αₙ / cos β**
- [x] **Helical-only: Wₐ = Wₜ tan β** (thrust force; **never sin β**)
- [x] **Wₙ = Wₜ / (cos α · cos β)** — true line-of-action force
- [x] **Effective Load Formula: Wₒ = Cᵥ · Cₛ · Cₘ · Cₜ · Wₜ** — the heart of Module 4
- [x] **Dynamic Factor: 3/(3+v) for v ≤ 10 m/s; 3.05/(3.05+v) for v > 10 m/s**
- [x] **Service Factor: From standard table (1.0 uniform, 1.5 medium shock, 2.0 heavy shock)**
- [x] **Beam Strength: F_b = σ_b · b · πm · y** — input is Wₒ, not Wₜ
- [x] **Wear Strength: S_w = b · Q · K · d_p** — input is Wₒ, not Wₜ
- [x] **Factor of Safety = F_b / Wₒ** — typical acceptable range 1.25 to 2.0
- [x] **Pitch diameter: d = m·Z (spur) | d = mₙ·Z / cos β (helical)**
- [x] **Velocity: v = πdN/60,000** — N in rpm, d in mm, v in m/s
- [x] **Units consistency:** Force in N, stress in N/mm², torque in N·m, length in mm

> **End of Module 4 — Tooth Loads Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Stresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Stresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Design Stresses

In the context of machine design, **Design Stress** (also termed as **Allowable Working Stress** or **Permissible Stress**) is defined as the maximum stress that a machine component can safely withstand under the intended operating conditions without experiencing failure due to yielding, fracture, or excessive deformation.

Mathematically, the design stress is expressed as:

$$\sigma_d = \frac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{N}$$

or equivalently, based on yield criteria:

$$\sigma_d = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{N}$$

where:
- $\sigma_d$ = Design (allowable) stress in **N/mm²** or **MPa**
- $\sigma_{ultimate}$ = Ultimate tensile strength of the material
- $\sigma_{yield}$ = Yield strength of the material
- $N$ = **Factor of Safety (FOS)**, a dimensionless multiplier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Design stress is the **estimated maximum safe stress** to which a material can be subjected under operating conditions, obtained by dividing the material's strength property (ultimate or yield) by a suitable **Factor of Safety (FOS)** chosen by the designer based on loading conditions, material reliability, and consequences of failure.

## 1.2 Real-World Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are designing a **bridge cable** that must support a maximum expected load of **1000 kg**. However, you know the actual cable material can handle up to **5000 kg** before snapping. Being a responsible engineer, you would not push the cable to its absolute limit. Instead, you would:

1. **Reserve a safety margin** (say, factor of safety = 5)
2. **Limit the working load** to $\frac{5000}{5} = 1000$ kg
3. **Account for unpredictable factors** like sudden gusts of wind, fatigue, corrosion, or manufacturing defects

This "safe working limit" is precisely what **Design Stress** represents in machine design. It is the engineering compromise between **strength** (the material's natural resistance) and **practical safety** (real-world uncertainties).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to Remember:** Think of design stress as a **"stress budget"** — every machine component has a fixed "budget" of stress it can safely absorb, and the designer's job is to ensure operating stresses stay *strictly within* this budget.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Design

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|-----------|--------|---------------|------|
| Factor of Safety (Static Loading) | $N$ | 2 – 6 | Dimensionless |
| Factor of Safety (Dynamic Loading) | $N$ | 6 – 15 | Dimensionless |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength (Mild Steel) | $\sigma_u$ | 400 – 500 | MPa |
| Yield Strength (Mild Steel) | $\sigma_y$ | 250 – 350 | MPa |
| Endurance Limit (Steel) | $\sigma_e$ | (0.4 – 0.5)$\sigma_u$ | MPa |
| Modulus of Elasticity (Steel) | $E$ | 2 × 10⁵ | MPa |
| Poisson's Ratio (Steel) | $\nu$ | 0.3 | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In KTU board examinations, students are expected to **state the design stress formula first** and then justify the choice of FOS based on the type of loading (static, dynamic, impact, fatigue) and the material's reliability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Design Stresses: The Need for a Safety Factor

In an ideal, perfectly predictable world, a designer could load a component right up to its ultimate strength. However, real engineering involves **uncertainties**. Design stresses (and consequently, the factor of safety) exist to address these uncertainties, which can be broadly categorized:

1. **Uncertainties in Material Properties:**
   - Variations in manufacturing quality, heat treatment, composition, and grain structure mean that the *actual* material in a component may be slightly weaker than the *tested* specimen.

2. **Uncertainties in Load Magnitude and Nature:**
   - The applied load may be underestimated; impact, vibration, or shock loading may be higher than anticipated.
   - Misalignment, residual stresses from manufacturing, and stress concentrations at notches/holes are often overlooked.

3. **Uncertainties in Service Environment:**
   - Temperature fluctuations, corrosion, wear, radiation, and chemical exposure degrade material strength over time.

4. **Consequences of Failure:**
   - If failure would cause **loss of life** (e.g., aircraft, pressure vessels, medical devices), the FOS is chosen to be very high.
   - If failure only causes **economic loss** (e.g., a non-critical bracket), the FOS can be lower.

## 2.2 Selection of Factor of Safety (FOS)

The **Factor of Safety (N)** is selected based on engineering judgment, code requirements, and historical precedent. The table below summarizes typical values for different scenarios:

| Loading Type | Material Reliability | Typical FOS Range |
|---|---|---|
| Static Loading with Known Loads | Ductile Material | 2 – 4 |
| Static Loading with Unknown Loads | Ductile Material | 4 – 6 |
| Dynamic Loading (Variable/Fluctuating) | Ductile Material | 6 – 10 |
| Impact Loading | Ductile Material | 10 – 15 |
| Static Loading | Brittle Material (Cast Iron) | 6 – 9 |
| Fatigue Loading | All Materials | 10 – 20 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Examiners frequently test whether students can *justify* the choice of FOS. A 3-mark question often asks: "Why is the factor of safety for brittle materials higher than that for ductile materials?" The expected answer: Brittle materials fail **suddenly without prior yielding**, leaving no visual warning, so a higher safety margin is mandatory.

## 2.3 Types of Stresses Encountered in Design

### 2.3.1 Simple Stresses (Uniaxial)

- **Tensile Stress ($\sigma_t$):** Arises when a component is stretched.
  $$\sigma_t = \frac{P}{A}$$
- **Compressive Stress ($\sigma_c$):** Arises when a component is squeezed.
  $$\sigma_c = \frac{P}{A}$$
- **Shear Stress ($\tau$):** Arises when forces act parallel to a surface.
  $$\tau = \frac{F}{A_s}$$

### 2.3.2 Combined Stresses (Biaxial/Multiaxial)

When a component is subjected to multiple loads simultaneously (e.g., a shaft under bending and torsion), the resulting state of stress at any point is **biaxial or triaxial**.

For a 2D stress state with $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$:

**Principal Stresses** are found by solving the characteristic equation:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

The **Maximum Shear Stress** is:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** $\sigma_1$ is always the algebraically largest (most positive) principal stress, and $\sigma_2$ is the algebraically smallest (most negative) principal stress.

## 2.4 Stress Concentration Factor (SCF)

Whenever there is a **sudden change in geometry** (a hole, a notch, a keyway, a fillet, or a shoulder in a shaft), the stress lines crowd together at the discontinuity, causing a **localized stress peak** that can be much higher than the nominal stress. This phenomenon is quantified by the **Stress Concentration Factor ($K_t$)**:

$$K_t = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nominal}}$$

where:
- $\sigma_{max}$ = Maximum stress at the discontinuity
- $\sigma_{nominal}$ = Nominal stress calculated from simple strength-of-materials equations

**Key Parameters for Ductile vs. Brittle Materials:**

| Material Type | Effect of $K_t$ on Design |
|---|---|
| **Ductile** (Steel, Aluminum) | $K_t$ can often be **ignored** in static loading because local yielding redistributes stress. |
| **Brittle** (Cast Iron, Glass, Ceramics) | $K_t$ **must be applied** because brittle materials cannot redistribute stress through yielding. |

For dynamic/fatigue loading, $K_t$ (or the fatigue stress concentration factor $K_f$) **must always be applied**, even for ductile materials.

## 2.5 Theories of Failure and Equivalent Stresses

When a component is under combined loading, the designer must compare the **complex stress state** to a **simple allowable stress** (typically obtained from a uniaxial tensile test). This comparison is made using **Theories of Failure**, which yield an **Equivalent Stress** ($\sigma_{eq}$) that can be directly compared to the design stress.

| Theory | Equivalent Stress Formula | Material Applicability | Design Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine)** | $\sigma_{eq} = \max(\vert\sigma_1\vert, \vert\sigma_2\vert)$ | Brittle materials | $\sigma_{eq} \leq \sigma_d$ |
| **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)** | $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ | Ductile materials | $\sigma_{eq} \leq \sigma_d$ |
| **Maximum Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises)** | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}$ | Ductile materials | $\sigma_{eq} \leq \sigma_d$ |
| **Maximum Principal Strain Theory (Saint-Venant)** | $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2$ | Brittle materials | $\sigma_{eq} \leq \sigma_d$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most Commonly Used in KTU Examinations:** The **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)** and **Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises)** are most frequently tested, especially for shaft design under combined bending and torsion.

### 2.5.1 Application to Shafts: Combined Bending and Torsion

For a circular shaft subjected to bending moment $M$ and torque $T$, the equivalent stresses are:

**Bending stress (normal):**
$$\sigma_b = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$$

**Torsional shear stress:**
$$\tau = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$

**Equivalent stress by Maximum Shear Stress Theory (3-4 × $\tau$ formula):**
$$\sigma_{eq} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

**Equivalent torque (or equivalent bending moment) for shaft design:**
$$T_{eq} = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \quad \text{or} \quad M_{eq} = \frac{M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** For shafts, the **"3-4 rule"** — use $\frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ (which is mathematically $\sqrt{3^2 + 4^2}/4$ in form) for the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** and $\frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + 0.75T^2}$ for the **Distortion Energy Theory**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Design Stress (Based on Ultimate) | $\sigma_d = \sigma_u / N$ | MPa | Conservative |
| 2 | Design Stress (Based on Yield) | $\sigma_d = \sigma_y / N$ | MPa | Most common |
| 3 | Design Stress (Endurance-Based) | $\sigma_d = \sigma_e / N$ | MPa | Fatigue loading |
| 4 | Stress Concentration Factor | $K_t = \sigma_{max} / \sigma_{nom}$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Principal Stress | $\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | MPa | For plane stress |
| 6 | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$ | MPa | Also $= \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ |
| 7 | von Mises Equivalent Stress | $\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}$ | MPa | Most accurate for ductile |
| 8 | Tresca Equivalent Stress | $\sigma_{T} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ | MPa | Conservative for ductile |
| 9 | Shaft Bending Stress | $\sigma_b = 32M/(\pi d^3)$ | MPa | Solid circular shaft |
| 10 | Shaft Torsional Stress | $\tau = 16T/(\pi d^3)$ | MPa | Solid circular shaft |
| 11 | Equivalent Stress (Shaft, M.S.S. Theory) | $\sigma_{eq} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ | MPa | For ductile shafts |
| 12 | Equivalent Stress (Shaft, D.E. Theory) | $\sigma_{eq} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + 0.75T^2}$ | MPa | For ductile shafts |
| 13 | Shaft Diameter (D.E. Theory) | $d = \left(\frac{16}{\pi \sigma_d}\sqrt{M^2 + 0.75T^2}\right)^{1/3}$ | mm | **Most used KTU formula** |
| 14 | Shaft Diameter (M.S.S. Theory) | $d = \left(\frac{16}{\pi \sigma_d}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}\right)^{1/3}$ | mm | Conservative |
| 15 | Endurance Limit (Steel, $\sigma_u < 1400$ MPa) | $\sigma_e = 0.5 \sigma_u$ | MPa | Standard approximation |
| 16 | Endurance Limit (Steel, $\sigma_u \geq 1400$ MPa) | $\sigma_e = 700$ MPa | MPa | Capped value |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Isolation Notice:** All absolute value operations (e.g., $\vert\sigma_1\vert$) have been written using the `\vert` command to ensure they render correctly **inside** the markdown table above without breaking the column-pipe structure.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Design stress calculations are at the heart of virtually every engineered product:

- **Aerospace:** Wing spars, turbine blades, and landing gear are designed with FOS values derived from fatigue test data and damage tolerance analyses.
- **Automotive:** Crankshafts, connecting rods, and suspension components are sized using equivalent stress calculations (combined bending + torsion).
- **Civil Engineering:** Pressure vessels, boilers, and pipelines are designed using von Mises or Tresca criteria, in conjunction with strict code-based FOS (e.g., ASME Boiler Code).
- **Biomedical:** Orthopedic implants (hip stems, bone plates) are designed for both static (body weight) and fatigue (walking cycles) loading.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Principal Stresses for Plane Stress

**Starting Premise:** Consider a 2D element under normal stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$ and shear stress $\tau_{xy}$. We wish to find the orientation $\theta$ at which the **normal stress is maximized or minimized** (i.e., where shear stress is zero). These extreme normal stresses are called **principal stresses**.

**Step 1:** Resolve forces on a wedge element rotated by angle $\theta$:

The forces on the inclined face (with normal at angle $\theta$ to the x-axis) are obtained by equilibrium:

$$\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

**Step 2:** Apply the double-angle identities to simplify:

Using $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2}$, $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}$, and $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

**Step 3:** Maximize by setting $\frac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta} = 0$:

$$\frac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin 2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the critical angle $\theta_p$:

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute this condition back into the equation for $\sigma_\theta$ to obtain the principal stresses.

For a derivation-free presentation, the principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Step 6 (Verification — Invariant Check):** The sum of the principal stresses equals the sum of the original normal stresses (stress invariant):

$$\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$$

This is an important sanity check: **whatever the orientation, the sum of the orthogonal normal stresses remains constant.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent Bending Moment for a Shaft under Combined Bending and Torsion

**Starting Premise:** A solid circular shaft of diameter $d$ is subjected to a bending moment $M$ and a torque $T$ simultaneously.

**Step 1:** Bending generates a normal (tensile/compressive) stress on the outer fiber:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{M}{\pi d^3/32} = \frac{32M}{\pi d^3}$$

**Step 2:** Torsion generates a pure shear stress on the outer surface:

$$\tau = \frac{T}{J/r} = \frac{T}{\pi d^3/16} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}$$

**Step 3:** At the most critical point on the shaft surface, the state of stress is **biaxial** — a normal stress $\sigma_b$ perpendicular to the surface and a shear stress $\tau$ in the plane of the surface.

**Step 4:** Calculate principal stresses at this point. The stress state is $\sigma_x = \sigma_b$, $\sigma_y = 0$, and $\tau_{xy} = \tau$:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_b + 0}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b - 0}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)**, which says the equivalent stress is the difference between principal stresses:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = 2\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 4\tau^2}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the bending and torsional stress expressions:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{32M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + 4\left(\frac{16T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2}$$

**Step 7:** Factor out $\frac{16}{\pi d^3}$ from both terms. Note that $\left(\frac{32M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{16}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 \cdot (2M)^2$:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{4M^2 + 4T^2} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \cdot 2\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

$$\sigma_{eq} = \frac{32}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Discrepancy Check:** Some textbooks write the equivalent stress for the M.S.S. theory as $\frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ by using a different convention for the equivalent moment ($M_{eq} = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$). Both forms are dimensionally correct. The form $\frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ corresponds to defining $\sigma_{eq} = \frac{32 M_{eq}}{\pi d^3}$, i.e., $M_{eq} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{M^2+T^2}$ in some conventions, but in KTU textbooks (V.B. Bhandari pattern), the **standard form is $\frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$** for the equivalent stress derived via Tresca, which equals $\frac{32}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ in pure form. We will use the **distortion energy** form below for KTU boards.

**Step 8 (D.E. Theory - von Mises for shaft):** The von Mises equivalent stress gives:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau^2} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + 0.75\,T^2}$$

This is the **most commonly used** formula in KTU board questions for shaft diameter calculation:

$$d = \left(\frac{16}{\pi \sigma_d}\sqrt{M^2 + 0.75\,T^2}\right)^{1/3}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example: Shaft Diameter Calculation

**Problem:** A solid circular shaft is to transmit **120 kW** at **300 rpm**. The shaft is also subjected to a bending moment of **1500 N·m** due to gear/pulley forces. The material has yield strength $\sigma_y = 400$ MPa. Using a factor of safety of 4 and the **distortion energy theory**, determine the shaft diameter.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate the torque transmitted:**

$$T = \frac{60 P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 120{,}000}{2\pi \times 300}$$

$$T = \frac{7{,}200{,}000}{1884.96} = 3819.7 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2: Identify the given bending moment:**

$$M = 1500 \text{ N·m} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$T = 3819.7 \text{ N·m} = 3.8197 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the design stress:**

$$\sigma_d = \frac{\sigma_y}{N} = \frac{400}{4} = 100 \text{ MPa} = 100 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Apply the D.E. theory formula for shaft diameter:**

$$d = \left(\frac{16}{\pi \sigma_d}\sqrt{M^2 + 0.75 T^2}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 5: Compute the term under the square root:**

$$M^2 = (1.5 \times 10^6)^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{12} \text{ N}^2\text{mm}^2$$

$$0.75 T^2 = 0.75 \times (3.8197 \times 10^6)^2 = 0.75 \times 1.459 \times 10^{13} = 1.0943 \times 10^{13}$$

$$M^2 + 0.75T^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{12} + 1.0943 \times 10^{13} = 1.3193 \times 10^{13}$$

$$\sqrt{1.3193 \times 10^{13}} = 3.6322 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 6: Calculate $d^3$:**

$$d^3 = \frac{16 \times 3.6322 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 100} = \frac{5.8116 \times 10^7}{314.159} = 1.85 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 7: Calculate $d$:**

$$d = (1.85 \times 10^5)^{1/3} \approx 57.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8: Apply standard rounding (KTU pattern: round up to nearest standard value):**

$$d_{standard} = 60 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Check that the design stress is not exceeded with $d = 60$ mm:
>
> $$\sigma_{eq} = \frac{16 \times 3.6322 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 60^3} = \frac{5.8116 \times 10^7}{678{,}584} = 85.6 \text{ MPa} < 100 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example: Principal Stress at a Critical Point

**Problem:** A point on a machine element is subjected to the following stresses: $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = -40$ MPa, and $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa. Calculate the **principal stresses** and the **maximum shear stress**.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Identify the given values:**

$\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = -40$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa.

**Step 2: Calculate the average normal stress:**

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} = \frac{40}{2} = 20 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the radius of Mohr's circle (maximum shear stress):**

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - (-40)}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{60^2 + 30^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{3600 + 900} = \sqrt{4500} = 67.08 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the principal stresses:**

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_{avg} + R = 20 + 67.08 = 87.08 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \sigma_{avg} - R = 20 - 67.08 = -47.08 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: The maximum shear stress is simply the radius:**

$$\tau_{max} = R = 67.08 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6: Verify the invariant:**

$$\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = 87.08 + (-47.08) = 40 \text{ MPa} = \sigma_x + \sigma_y \checkmark$$

**Step 7 (Optional): Orientation of principal planes:**

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - (-40)} = \frac{60}{120} = 0.5$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_p = 13.28°$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mohr's Circle Representation of 2D Stress State

The state of plane stress and the principal stresses can be visualized elegantly using **Mohr's Circle**. The following Mermaid diagram maps the conceptual flow from a stress element to the Mohr's Circle representation:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stress Element in 2D] --> B[Plot Points X and Y on sigma-tau plane]
    B --> C[Point X: sigma_x, minus tau_xy]
    B --> D[Point Y: sigma_y, plus tau_xy]
    C --> E[Draw Line XY]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Find Center C: sigma_avg, 0]
    F --> G[Radius equals max shear stress]
    G --> H[Intersection with sigma axis gives sigma_1 and sigma_2]
    H --> I[Principal Stresses and Max Shear Stress Obtained]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Workflow for Shaft Design Using Design Stress

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Loading on Shaft] --> B[Compute Bending Moment M]
    A --> C[Compute Torque T from Power]
    B --> D[Identify Material: sigma_y, sigma_u]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Select Factor of Safety N based on Loading]
    E --> F[Calculate Design Stress: sigma_d = sigma_y / N]
    F --> G{Choose Failure Theory}
    G -->|Distortion Energy| H[Apply Formula: d to the power of 1 by 3 equals 16 over pi sigma_d times square root of M squared plus 0.75 T squared]
    G -->|Maximum Shear Stress| I[Apply Formula: d to the power of 1 by 3 equals 16 over pi sigma_d times square root of M squared plus T squared]
    H --> J[Compute Numerical Value of d]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Round Up to Standard Diameter]
    K --> L[Check Keyway and Shoulder Stress Concentration]
    L --> M[Final Design Validated]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: The Design Stress Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Determine Type of Loading] --> B{Static or Dynamic}
    B -->|Static| C{Is Material Ductile?}
    B -->|Dynamic| D{Is Material Ductile?}
    C -->|Yes| E[Use Yield Strength with FOS 2 to 4]
    C -->|No| F[Use Ultimate Strength with FOS 6 to 9]
    D -->|Yes| G[Use Endurance Limit with FOS 6 to 10]
    D -->|No| H[Use Ultimate Strength with FOS 10 to 15]
    E --> I[sigma_d equals sigma_y divided by N]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Apply Stress Concentration Factor if Brittle or Fatigue Loading]
    J --> K[Final Design Stress for Component]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Stress Distribution Around a Hole in a Plate (Conceptual)

A **hole in a plate** under uniaxial tension is the classic example of stress concentration. The stress lines "crowd" around the hole, creating a peak stress at the boundary of the hole that is **three times** the nominal stress. The following Mermaid block diagram represents this scenario:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Uniaxial Tension sigma_0 Applied Far Field] --> B[Uniform Stress Lines in Plate]
    B --> C[Stress Lines Approach the Hole]
    C --> D[Stress Lines Crowd Near Hole Edges]
    D --> E[Peak Stress at 90 Degrees: 3 times sigma_0]
    E --> F[Stress Concentration Factor K_t equals 3 for Circular Hole]
    F --> G[Engineer Must Apply K_t to Design Stress]
    G --> H[Reduced Design Stress for Safe Operation]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway from the Diagram:** Although the *average* stress in the plate is $\sigma_0$, the *local* stress at the edge of the hole is $3\sigma_0$. A designer who ignores $K_t$ will under-design the component, leading to a crack initiating at the hole boundary. The **applied design stress must be reduced** to $\sigma_d / K_t$ for brittle materials, or $K_t$ (or $K_f$ for fatigue) must be applied to the nominal stress.

## 4.5 Functional Architecture: Relationship Between Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Properties] --> B[Strength: sigma_u and sigma_y]
    A --> C[Reliability Factor]
    B --> D[Design Stress Calculation]
    C --> D
    E[Loading Type] --> F[Static, Dynamic, Impact, Fatigue]
    F --> G[Choice of Factor of Safety]
    G --> D
    H[Component Geometry] --> I[Stress Concentration Factor K_t]
    I --> D
    D --> J[Permissible Working Stress]
    J --> K[Compare with Calculated Induced Stress]
    K --> L{Is Induced Stress less than Permissible?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Design is Safe]
    L -->|No| N[Modify Design: Increase Size or Change Material]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
