topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lewis Equation and Lewis Form Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Lewis Equation** is a classical gear-tooth strength equation proposed by **Wilfred Lewis (1893)** for the design of spur gear teeth. It treats a gear tooth as a **short cantilever beam** of **parabolic cross-section** (approximately triangular near the root) fixed at the base of the tooth and loaded at the tip by the tangential tooth load $W_t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Lewis Equation (Beam Strength Criterion):**
> 
> $$\sigma = \frac{W_t}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y} \le \sigma_b$$
> 
> Equivalently, the safe tangential load (beam strength) is:
> 
> $$W_{tb} = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = induced bending stress at the critical root section of the tooth (**N/mm²**)
- $W_t$ = tangential load on the tooth (**N**)
- $b$ = face width of the gear (**mm**)
- $m$ = module of the gear (**mm**)
- $Y$ = **Lewis Form Factor** (dimensionless)
- $\sigma_b$ = allowable bending stress for the gear material (**N/mm²**)

---

## 1.2 The Lewis Form Factor (Y)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Lewis Form Factor (Y):** A dimensionless geometric constant that accounts for the shape of the gear tooth and the variation of stress along the fillet when the tooth is treated as a cantilever beam. It depends on:
> - Number of teeth on the gear ($Z$)
> - Pressure angle ($\phi$)
> - Type of tooth system (full-depth or stub)
> - Tooth profile (involute or cycloidal)

It is the ratio of beam strength to the maximum stress at the weakest section of the tooth.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **flag pole fixed into the ground**. When wind blows on the flag, the pole bends — the **maximum bending stress occurs at the base** where it is fixed. The stress at the base depends on:
- How strong the wind is (load $W_t$)
- How wide the pole is (face width $b$)
- The shape of the pole's cross-section (form factor $Y$)

A gear tooth behaves **exactly the same way**! The tangential force $W_t$ acts at the tip of the tooth (like wind on the flag), the tooth is "fixed" at the pitch circle base, and the **maximum stress occurs at the tooth root fillet** (like the base of the flag pole). The Lewis Form Factor $Y$ essentially captures the geometry of the tooth cross-section at the weakest point.

> [!TIP]  
> **Memory Hook:** Lewis = **L**oad at tip, **E**ffective width of base, **W**eak fillet section, **I**nverse of stress, **S**tress concentration, **Y** = shape advantage. Think "**L**ittle **E**lves **W**orry about **I**nverse **S**tress with **Y**our form."

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Values

| Parameter | Standard Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure angle $\phi$ | **20°** (standard) or **14.5°** (older) | KTU 2024 follows **20° full-depth** |
| Addendum coefficient $a$ | **1.0** (full-depth) or **0.8** (stub) | KTU 2024 prefers full-depth |
| Module (preferred) | **1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10** | IS 2532 standard series |
| Lewis Form Factor $Y$ | Typically **0.154 to 0.485** | Higher for more teeth |
| Velocity factor $C_v$ | Depends on pitch-line velocity $v$ | Used to modify $W_t$ |

---

## 1.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Lewis equation is the **foundation of gear design** taught across the globe. It is used in:
- **Automotive gearboxes** (transmission design)
- **Industrial machinery** (rolling mills, conveyors)
- **Machine tool gear trains**
- **Aerospace accessory drives**
- **Wind turbine gearboxes**

> [!NOTE]  
> Although the modern **AGMA (American Gear Manufacturers Association)** and **ISO 6336** standards have replaced the Lewis approach with more rigorous pitting, bending, and scuffing analyses, the **Lewis equation remains the first step in any undergraduate gear design problem** and is **explicitly required by the KTU 2024 syllabus** under the **beam strength** design criterion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of the Lewis Equation

For the Lewis equation to hold true, the following assumptions are made (these are **favorite KTU viva questions**):

1. **Cantilever Beam Analogy:** Each gear tooth acts as an independent short cantilever beam fixed at the root.
2. **Tangential Load Only:** Only the tangential component $W_t$ of the tooth load is considered; radial and axial components are neglected.
3. **Load Distribution:** The load is assumed to be uniformly distributed over the face width $b$.
4. **Single Tooth Contact:** The load is assumed to be carried by **one tooth at a time** (worst case).
5. **No Stress Concentration:** The stress concentration at the tooth root fillet is neglected (a major limitation corrected by AGMA).
6. **Parabolic Cross-Section:** The tooth cross-section at the critical section is approximated as a parabola (this is the **key insight that introduces the form factor**).

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Equation

| Step | Logic | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify the weakest tooth section | Maximum stress location |
| 2 | Model section as cantilever of length $h$ | Distance from tip to weakest section |
| 3 | Compute section modulus at root | Strength of section |
| 4 | Include tooth shape parameter | Lewis Form Factor $Y$ |
| 5 | Equate bending moment to load × length | $M = W_t \times h$ |
| 6 | Apply flexure formula $\sigma = M/Z$ | Engineering mechanics |
| 7 | Rearrange into standard Lewis form | Practical design use |

---

## 2.3 Standard KTU 2024 Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The following table is **exam-ready** and frequently used in KTU numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_t = \dfrac{2 \cdot M_t}{d} = \dfrac{M_t}{r}$ | Tangential tooth load | **N** |
| 2 | $d = m \cdot Z$ | Pitch circle diameter | **mm** |
| 3 | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60 \times 1000}$ | Pitch-line velocity | **m/s** |
| 4 | $\sigma = \dfrac{W_t}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y}$ | Lewis bending stress equation | **N/mm²** |
| 5 | $W_{tb} = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y$ | Beam strength of tooth | **N** |
| 6 | $W_{tw} = b \cdot d \cdot Q \cdot K$ | Wear strength of tooth | **N** |
| 7 | $C_v = \dfrac{3}{3 + v}$ (or Bartlett) | Velocity factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $W_{t,eff} = \dfrac{W_t}{C_v}$ | Effective design load | **N** |
| 9 | $Y = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{h}\right)^2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{\cos\phi}$ | Form factor (analytical) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $h = 2.25 \cdot m$ | Length of tooth as cantilever | **mm** |
| 11 | $L = \dfrac{\pi \cdot m}{2}$ (full depth) | Length of tooth along line of action | **mm** |
| 12 | $b = \phi_b \cdot m$ (where $\phi_b = 6$ to $12$) | Face width in terms of module | **mm** |
| 13 | $Z_v = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 - \sin\phi}{3 - \sin\phi}}$ | Virtual/form factor for stub teeth | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Where $Q$ is the ratio factor** (also called the **load-stress factor** or **material combination factor**) for wear strength, obtained from Buckingham's wear analysis:
> 
> $$Q = \frac{2 \cdot Z_g}{(Z_g + Z_p)} = \frac{2 \cdot Z_E}{Z_I}$$
> 
> This is **NOT** the Lewis Form Factor — students often confuse the two!

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Lewis equation is a **first-pass design tool**. In modern industry:
- **AGMA bending stress equation** uses Lewis's $Y$ but adds stress-concentration factor $K_f$ and load-distribution factor $K_m$.
- **ISO 6336 Part 3** uses a similar concept called the **tooth form factor $Y_{Fa}$**.
- **FEM-based gear analysis** (modern CAE) directly solves for tooth root stress — but the form factor concept remains the underlying baseline.

> [!TIP]  
> In KTU examinations, always state the **limitations of the Lewis equation** to score full marks:
> - No stress concentration at root
> - No dynamic load effect (handled separately by $C_v$)
> - Static analysis only
> - Only one tooth in contact

---

## 2.5 Wear Strength vs. Beam Strength (Comparison)

| Aspect | Beam Strength (Lewis) | Wear Strength (Buckingham) |
|---|---|---|
| Failure mode | Tooth bending fracture | Tooth surface pitting |
| Critical parameter | Bending stress $\sigma$ | Contact (Hertzian) stress $\sigma_c$ |
| Output | $W_{tb}$ (N) | $W_{tw}$ (N) |
| Design load | $W_t \le W_{tb}$ | $W_t \le W_{tw}$ |
| Key factor | Form factor $Y$ | Ratio factor $Q$ |
| Material property | $\sigma_b$ (bending) | Hardness / surface endurance |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lewis Equation (Full Step-by-Step)

### Step 1: Cantilever Beam Model

Consider one gear tooth as a **cantilever beam** of length $h$ (vertical distance from the tip of the tooth to the weakest section at the root). The tangential load $W_t$ acts at the tip.

The bending moment at the weakest section is:

$$M = W_t \times h$$

### Step 2: Section Modulus of the Tooth at Root

For a **rectangular section** (which Lewis assumed), the section modulus is:

$$Z_{section} = \frac{b \cdot t^2}{6}$$

where $t$ is the tooth thickness at the weakest section.

### Step 3: Apply the Flexure Formula

The maximum bending stress is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z_{section}} = \frac{W_t \cdot h}{\left(\dfrac{b \cdot t^2}{6}\right)} = \frac{6 \cdot W_t \cdot h}{b \cdot t^2}$$

### Step 4: Introduce the Concept of "Critical Section"

The tooth is weakest at the **fillet**, where the tooth thickness $t$ is small but stress concentration exists. Lewis assumed the critical section is **at the pitch line** (Lewis's approximation).

At the pitch line, the tooth thickness $t$ can be expressed as a function of the circular pitch $p_c = \pi \cdot m$. By geometry of an involute tooth:

$$t = \frac{p_c}{2} = \frac{\pi \cdot m}{2}$$

This length is sometimes called $L$ (length of the line of action portion of the tooth).

### Step 5: Substitute Back

$$\sigma = \frac{6 \cdot W_t \cdot h}{b \cdot \left(\dfrac{\pi \cdot m}{2}\right)^2} = \frac{6 \cdot W_t \cdot h \cdot 4}{b \cdot \pi^2 \cdot m^2} = \frac{24 \cdot W_t \cdot h}{b \cdot \pi^2 \cdot m^2}$$

### Step 6: Define the Lewis Form Factor

Lewis defined the form factor as:

$$Y = \frac{t^2}{6 \cdot h \cdot m} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos\phi}$$

Substituting $t = \pi m / 2$ and $h = 2.25 m$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= \frac{\left(\dfrac{\pi m}{2}\right)^2}{6 \cdot (2.25 m) \cdot m \cdot \cos\phi} \\
  &= \frac{\dfrac{\pi^2 m^2}{4}}{13.5 \cdot m^2 \cdot \cos\phi} \\
  &= \frac{\pi^2}{54 \cdot \cos\phi} \\
  &= \frac{\pi}{2 \cdot h/m \cdot \cos\phi}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **20° full-depth involute** system with $h = 2.25 m$:

$$Y = \frac{\pi}{4.5 \cdot \cos 20°} = \frac{3.1416}{4.5 \times 0.9397} = \frac{3.1416}{4.2287} \approx 0.743$$

However, the **actual experimental values** of $Y$ are much smaller (0.154 to 0.485) because Lewis's theoretical model overestimated the strength.

### Step 7: Final Lewis Equation

Using the form factor $Y$:

$$\sigma = \frac{W_t}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y}$$

The **beam strength** (maximum safe tangential load) is:

$$W_{tb} = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y$$

This is the **final KTU-ready Lewis equation**.

---

## 3.2 Standard Lewis Form Factor Values (Memorize for KTU!)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> These are the **standard tabulated values of $Y$** for 20° full-depth involute teeth. **KTU expects students to either remember or be given these values.**

| Number of Teeth $Z$ | Lewis Form Factor $Y$ | Number of Teeth $Z$ | Lewis Form Factor $Y$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **10** | 0.176 | **26** | 0.323 |
| **11** | 0.192 | **28** | 0.330 |
| **12** | 0.210 | **30** | 0.338 |
| **13** | 0.223 | **32** | 0.345 |
| **14** | 0.236 | **34** | 0.352 |
| **15** | 0.245 | **36** | 0.358 |
| **16** | 0.256 | **38** | 0.364 |
| **17** | 0.267 | **40** | 0.368 |
| **18** | 0.275 | **45** | 0.380 |
| **19** | 0.283 | **50** | 0.389 |
| **20** | 0.289 | **60** | 0.408 |
| **21** | 0.296 | **80** | 0.432 |
| **22** | 0.302 | **100** | 0.446 |
| **24** | 0.314 | **150** | 0.471 |
| **25** | 0.319 | **Rack** | 0.485 |

> [!TIP]  
> **For 14.5° full-depth involute system** (older standard), use the same table multiplied by a correction factor of **0.94** (approximately). For **20° stub teeth** (addendum coefficient = 0.8), $Y$ values are slightly higher. **For KTU 2024, use 20° full-depth** unless stated otherwise.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]  
> This is a **model solution** that students should follow when solving KTU numericals.

**Problem:** A spur gear with 20 teeth, module 4 mm, face width 40 mm, transmits 5 kW at 720 rpm. Pressure angle 20° full-depth. Take $\sigma_b = 100$ N/mm². Check the gear for beam strength using the Lewis equation. Take velocity factor from the Barth equation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Pitch circle diameter
$$d = m \cdot Z = 4 \times 20 = 80 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Pitch-line velocity
$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi \times 80 \times 720}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{180,955.7}{60,000} \approx 3.016 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Tangential load
$$W_t = \frac{2 \cdot M_t}{d} = \frac{2 \times 9.55 \times 10^6 \times 5}{720 \times 80} = \frac{9.55 \times 10^7}{57600} \approx 1658.0 \text{ N}$$

(Alternatively: $W_t = P/v = 5000/3.016 \approx 1658$ N)

**Step 4:** Lewis Form Factor (from table for $Z = 20$):
$$Y = 0.289$$

**Step 5:** Beam strength
$$W_{tb} = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y = 100 \times 40 \times \pi \times 4 \times 0.289$$
$$W_{tb} = 100 \times 40 \times 3.1416 \times 4 \times 0.289 = 14,524.9 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6:** Velocity factor (Barth):
$$C_v = \frac{3}{3 + v} = \frac{3}{3 + 3.016} = \frac{3}{6.016} \approx 0.4987$$

**Step 7:** Effective load
$$W_{t,eff} = \frac{W_t}{C_v} = \frac{1658}{0.4987} \approx 3325 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8:** Compare
$$W_{t,eff} (3325 \text{ N}) < W_{tb} (14,524.9 \text{ N}) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{GEAR IS SAFE}$$

The design is safe in bending with a factor of safety of:
$$FoS = \frac{W_{tb}}{W_{t,eff}} = \frac{14524.9}{3325} \approx 4.37$$

---

## 3.4 Stub Teeth (Virtual Form Factor)

For **20° stub teeth** (addendum = $0.8m$), the **Virtual Form Factor** is used:

$$Y_v = 0.124 - \frac{0.684}{Z}$$

This is used directly in the Lewis equation for stub teeth.

For **14.5° full-depth teeth** (older system):

$$Y = 0.39 \cdot \left(\frac{L}{h}\right) \cdot \text{(correction)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> In KTU problems, **if the tooth system is not specified, assume 20° full-depth involute** unless the problem mentions "stub" or "14.5°" explicitly.

```mermaid

    B --> C["Section Modulus Increases"]

    E --> F["More Teeth = Stronger Gear Tooth"]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Interpretation of the Diagram:** As the number of teeth $Z$ increases (with module $m$ held constant), the Lewis form factor $Y$ increases. This is because the tooth cross-section at the root becomes thicker (less slender), which increases the section modulus and hence the beam strength of the tooth. This is why **pinions (small $Z$) fail more easily** than gears with many teeth.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks Equivalent)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q: Define the Lewis form factor. On what factors does it depend?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Lewis Form Factor ($Y$)** is a dimensionless geometric constant used in the Lewis equation that accounts for the shape of the gear tooth at its weakest section. It is defined as the ratio of the beam strength of the tooth to the product of bending stress, face width, and circular pitch.

$$Y = \frac{W_{tb}}{\sigma \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m}$$

It depends on the following factors:
1. **Number of teeth** on the gear ($Z$)
2. **Pressure angle** ($\phi$)
3. **Tooth system** — full-depth or stub teeth
4. **Tooth profile** — involute or cycloidal
5. **Module** of the gear (indirectly)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q: State the Lewis equation for the design of spur gear teeth. What are its limitations?**

**Model Answer:**

**Lewis Equation:**
$$\sigma = \frac{W_t}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y} \le \sigma_b$$

Or, the beam strength:
$$W_{tb} = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y$$

**Limitations of the Lewis Equation:**

1. **No stress concentration** at the tooth root fillet is considered.
2. **Load is assumed to act on the tip** of the tooth — actually, the load is shared between multiple teeth in action.
3. **Dynamic effects** are not built in (handled separately by $C_v$).
4. **Radial and axial components** of the tooth force are neglected.
5. **Static analysis only** — fatigue effects are not considered.
6. **Uniform load distribution** across face width is assumed (no load concentration at ends).

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks Each)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Module 4 Internal Choice, Model Paper 2024]**

**Q: A pair of 20° full-depth involute spur gears consists of a 20-tooth pinion meshing with a 50-tooth gear. The module is 4 mm and the face width is 50 mm. The pinion rotates at 1450 rpm and transmits 7.5 kW. The allowable bending stress for both gears is 100 N/mm². Using the Lewis equation:**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Determine the beam strength of both the pinion and the gear. State which is the weaker member.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Calculate the effective design load using the Barth velocity factor and check whether the design is safe.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Beam Strength of Both Gears

**Given Data:**
- Module $m = 4$ mm
- Face width $b = 50$ mm
- Pinion teeth $Z_p = 20$
- Gear teeth $Z_g = 50$
- Allowable bending stress $\sigma_b = 100$ N/mm²
- Pressure angle $\phi = 20°$ (full-depth)
- Lewis Form Factors (from standard table):
  - $Y_p$ (for $Z = 20$) = **0.289**
  - $Y_g$ (for $Z = 50$) = **0.389**

**Step 1: Pitch Circle Diameters**

For the pinion:
$$d_p = m \cdot Z_p = 4 \times 20 = 80 \text{ mm}$$

For the gear:
$$d_g = m \cdot Z_g = 4 \times 50 = 200 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Beam Strength Calculation**

For the pinion:
$$W_{tb,p} = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y_p$$
$$W_{tb,p} = 100 \times 50 \times \pi \times 4 \times 0.289$$
$$W_{tb,p} = 100 \times 50 \times 3.1416 \times 4 \times 0.289$$
$$W_{tb,p} = 18,156.1 \text{ N} \approx 18.16 \text{ kN}$$

For the gear:
$$W_{tb,g} = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y_g$$
$$W_{tb,g} = 100 \times 50 \times \pi \times 4 \times 0.389$$
$$W_{tb,g} = 100 \times 50 \times 3.1416 \times 4 \times 0.389$$
$$W_{tb,g} = 24,440.7 \text{ N} \approx 24.44 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Identify the Weaker Member**

Since $W_{tb,p}$ (18.16 kN) $<$ $W_{tb,g}$ (24.44 kN), the **pinion is the weaker member**.

> **Conclusion for Part (a):** The beam strength of the pinion is **18.16 kN** and that of the gear is **24.44 kN**. The pinion is the weaker member and governs the design.

---

#### Part (b) — Effective Design Load and Safety Check

**Step 1: Pitch Line Velocity**

Using the pinion diameter (smaller, so higher velocity):
$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot d_p \cdot N_p}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi \times 80 \times 1450}{60 \times 1000}$$
$$v = \frac{364,424.8}{60,000} \approx 6.074 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Tangential Load**

$$W_t = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{7500}{6.074} \approx 1234.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Barth Velocity Factor**

$$C_v = \frac{3}{3 + v} = \frac{3}{3 + 6.074} = \frac{3}{9.074} \approx 0.3306$$

**Step 4: Effective Design Load**

$$W_{t,eff} = \frac{W_t}{C_v} = \frac{1234.8}{0.3306} \approx 3735.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Safety Check**

Compare $W_{t,eff}$ with $W_{tb,p}$ (weaker member):
$$W_{t,eff} = 3735.1 \text{ N} < W_{tb,p} = 18,156.1 \text{ N}$$

**Factor of Safety:**
$$FoS = \frac{W_{tb,p}}{W_{t,eff}} = \frac{18156.1}{3735.1} \approx 4.86$$

> **Conclusion for Part (b):** The effective design load (3735 N) is much less than the beam strength of the pinion (18,156 N). The design is **SAFE** with a factor of safety of approximately **4.86**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam - Module 4 Internal Choice, Model Paper 2024]**

**Q: A 20° full-depth involute spur gear has 18 teeth, a module of 6 mm, and a face width of 60 mm. The gear is made of steel with an allowable bending stress of 110 N/mm². It transmits power at 600 rpm.**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Calculate the pitch line velocity and the tangential tooth load when the gear transmits 10 kW of power.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Using the Lewis form factor for 18 teeth, determine the beam strength of the gear. Apply the Barth velocity factor and check if the gear is safe against bending failure.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Pitch Line Velocity and Tangential Load

**Given Data:**
- Number of teeth $Z = 18$
- Module $m = 6$ mm
- Face width $b = 60$ mm
- Allowable bending stress $\sigma_b = 110$ N/mm²
- Speed $N = 600$ rpm
- Power $P = 10$ kW

**Step 1: Pitch Circle Diameter**

$$d = m \cdot Z = 6 \times 18 = 108 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Pitch Line Velocity**

$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi \times 108 \times 600}{60 \times 1000}$$
$$v = \frac{203,575.2}{60,000} \approx 3.393 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Tangential Tooth Load**

$$W_t = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{10,000}{3.393} \approx 2947.0 \text{ N}$$

> **Conclusion for Part (a):** The pitch-line velocity is **3.393 m/s** and the tangential tooth load is **2947 N**.

---

#### Part (b) — Beam Strength and Safety Check

**Step 1: Lewis Form Factor**

For $Z = 18$ teeth (from standard table):
$$Y = 0.275$$

**Step 2: Beam Strength**

$$W_{tb} = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y$$
$$W_{tb} = 110 \times 60 \times \pi \times 6 \times 0.275$$
$$W_{tb} = 110 \times 60 \times 3.1416 \times 6 \times 0.275$$
$$W_{tb} = 34,156.6 \text{ N} \approx 34.16 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Barth Velocity Factor**

$$C_v = \frac{3}{3 + v} = \frac{3}{3 + 3.393} = \frac{3}{6.393} \approx 0.4692$$

**Step 4: Effective Design Load**

$$W_{t,eff} = \frac{W_t}{C_v} = \frac{2947.0}{0.4692} \approx 6281.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Safety Check**

$$W_{t,eff} = 6281 \text{ N} < W_{tb} = 34,156.6 \text{ N}$$

**Factor of Safety:**
$$FoS = \frac{W_{tb}}{W_{t,eff}} = \frac{34156.6}{6281.0} \approx 5.44$$

> **Conclusion for Part (b):** The beam strength of the gear is **34,156.6 N** and the effective design load is **6281 N**. The gear is **SAFE** against bending failure with a factor of safety of **5.44**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**

1. **Wrong Form Factor Selection:** Students often use the **ratio factor $Q$** (for wear strength) instead of the **Lewis form factor $Y$** (for beam strength). These are **two completely different things!** Memorize the distinction.

2. **Forgot to Apply Velocity Factor:** The Lewis equation gives the static beam strength. Always **divide $W_t$ by $C_v$** (or multiply $\sigma_b$ by $C_v$ in some textbooks). Both methods are accepted, but consistency matters.

3. **Used the Wrong Gear Diameter in Velocity Calculation:** Always use the **pinion diameter** (smaller, higher velocity) when calculating $v$.

4. **Used "Cast Iron" or "Steel" Form Factor Separately:** For KTU 2024 problems, unless specified, use the **standard 20° full-depth involute** form factor table. Material selection affects $\sigma_b$, not $Y$.

5. **Unit Confusion:** Power must be in **watts**, velocity in **m/s**, dimensions in **mm** to get $\sigma$ in **N/mm²**.

6. **Missing the "Compare Both Gears" Step:** Always check both the pinion AND the gear. The **pinion is almost always the weaker** member because it has fewer teeth (lower $Y$).

7. **Did Not State Assumptions:** Always write down "Assuming 20° full-depth involute system" at the start of your numerical solution.

8. **Velocity Factor Formula Variants:** The **Barth formula** ($C_v = 3/(3+v)$) and the **Buckingham formula** are different. KTU typically expects **Barth** unless otherwise stated.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Lewis Equation & Form Factor**

- [ ] Lewis equation: $\sigma = \dfrac{W_t}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y} \le \sigma_b$
- [ ] Beam strength: $W_{tb} = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y$
- [ ] Lewis form factor $Y$ is **dimensionless** and depends on $Z$, $\phi$, and tooth system
- [ ] $Y$ **increases with $Z$** (thicker tooth root = stronger)
- [ ] Pinion ($Z_p < Z_g$) → **lower $Y$** → **weaker member** (almost always)
- [ ] $d = m \cdot Z$ (pitch diameter)
- [ ] $v = \pi d N / (60 \times 1000)$ in m/s
- [ ] $W_t = P / v = 2 M_t / d$
- [ ] Barth velocity factor: $C_v = 3 / (3 + v)$
- [ ] Effective load: $W_{t,eff} = W_t / C_v$
- [ ] Design safe if: $W_{t,eff} \le W_{tb}$
- [ ] Standard $Y$ values for $Z = 20$: **0.289**; for $Z = 50$: **0.389**
- [ ] Assumptions: single tooth contact, no stress concentration, cantilever beam model
- [ ] Limitations: no stress concentration, no dynamic load, static analysis only
- [ ] Difference between **Lewis** (beam strength) and **Buckingham** (wear strength) criteria
- [ ] Use **20° full-depth** unless specified otherwise (KTU 2024 default)
- [ ] Stub teeth virtual form factor: $Y_v = 0.124 - 0.684/Z$ (for 20° stub)
- [ ] The Lewis equation is a **first-pass design**; modern AGMA/ISO use corrections
- [ ] Wear strength (Buckingham): $W_{tw} = b \cdot d \cdot Q \cdot K$ (different criterion)
- [ ] **Always compare with weaker member** of the gear pair
- [ ] The form factor is **NOT the same as the ratio factor** $Q$ for wear

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — LEWIS EQUATION & LEWIS FORM FACTOR**
>

topics.title: Working Stress in Gear Teeth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working Stress in Gear Teeth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Stress in Gear Teeth** is defined as the **maximum permissible repeated bending stress** that a gear tooth can safely withstand for an **infinite number of load cycles** under actual operating conditions, accounting for dynamic effects, load distribution, and the inherent fluctuating nature of gear tooth loading.

Mathematically, the working stress is expressed as:

$$\sigma_w = \sigma_0 \cdot C_m \cdot C_v$$

Where the symbols denote:

- **$\sigma_w$** = Working stress in gear teeth (in MPa or N/mm²)
- **$\sigma_0$** = Allowable static stress for the gear material (in MPa or N/mm²)
- **$C_m$** = Material correction factor (accounts for reliability, size, temperature, and surface finish effects)
- **$C_v$** = Velocity factor / Dynamic load factor (accounts for impact loading and dynamic amplification)

For gear tooth design, the **working stress** must always be compared against the **induced bending stress** (computed using the Lewis or modified Lewis equation) to verify safe operation. Additionally, the working stress is compared against the **wear strength** (surface durability stress) to prevent pitting failure.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEMET743 (Module 4 – Design of Gear Drives), the concept of working stress forms the **cornerstone of all gear design problems**. Without mastering this concept, the student cannot correctly apply either the Lewis beam-strength equation or the Buckingham wear-strength equation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Imagine you are carrying a heavy bag of rice on your shoulder, **every single day, multiple times a day**, for years together. The maximum weight you can lift **once** (static strength) is much higher than the weight you can comfortably carry **repeatedly** without your shoulder giving out. Your body adapts by:

1. **Reducing the allowed weight per trip** (factor of safety)
2. **Accounting for jerky or sudden movements** (velocity / dynamic factor)
3. **Considering your health, age, and conditioning** (material correction factor)

**A gear tooth behaves exactly like your shoulder:**

- The **static stress** ($\sigma_0$) represents the single-impact failure limit of the tooth material.
- The **working stress** ($\sigma_w$) represents the *safe, repeatable* stress that the tooth can endure **over millions of cycles**.
- Every rotation loads the tooth **once**, so after 1 crore rotations, the tooth has experienced **1 crore stress cycles** — well into the **fatigue (endurance) regime**.

Thus, working stress is essentially the **fatigue-allowable stress** for the gear tooth, corrected for the actual operating environment.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Example:** In an automobile gearbox, a typical spur gear operates at ~3000 RPM in 5th gear. Over a 2-hour highway drive, the gear completes approximately 3.6 lakh revolutions. Over the vehicle's lifetime (say 2 lakh km), each tooth experiences **billions of load cycles** — making fatigue and working stress the *governing* design criteria, not static strength.

---

### 1.3 Why Working Stress ≠ Allowable Stress

The allowable static stress from standard material tables (e.g., for **steel, cast iron, bronze**) cannot be used **directly** in gear design because gear teeth experience:

1. **Dynamic loading** due to inaccuracies in tooth profile, pitch errors, and elastic deflections → requires **velocity factor $C_v$**.
2. **Non-uniform load distribution** across the tooth face due to misalignment, shaft deflection, and bearing deflection → requires **load distribution factor $K_m$**.
3. **Stress concentration** at the tooth root fillet.
4. **Surface contact fatigue** (pitting, spalling) → handled by the **wear strength** check.
5. **Repeated loading** → handled by **fatigue / endurance limit** considerations.

Therefore, the working stress is a **reduced, environment-adjusted** version of the material's static strength.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard reference values used in KTU 2024 Scheme gear design problems:**
> - **Lewis form factor ($Y$)**: dimensionless, depends on number of teeth (Tabulated: 14T → 0.295, 18T → 0.308, 21T → 0.314, 25T → 0.320, 30T → 0.325)
> - **Velocity factor ($C_v$)**: Buckingham's empirical values based on pitch line velocity ($v$) in m/s
> - **Beam strength ($S_b$)**: $S_b = m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_w \cdot Y$ (in Newtons)
> - **Wear strength ($S_w$)**: Buckingham's equation $S_w = d \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K / (2 \cos \alpha)$ (in Newtons)
> - **Standard pressure angle ($\alpha$)**: 14.5° (full-depth) or 20° (full-depth modern standard)

---

### 1.5 Classification of Stresses in Gear Teeth

| Stress Type | Symbol | Governing Equation | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bending Stress** | $\sigma_b$ | $\sigma_b = \frac{F_t}{b \cdot m \cdot Y}$ | Tooth breakage (root fatigue) |
| **Working Stress** | $\sigma_w$ | $\sigma_w = \sigma_0 \cdot C_m \cdot C_v$ | Allowable fatigue stress |
| **Wear Stress / Surface Endurance Stress** | $\sigma_{es}$ | Buckingham's wear equation | Pitting, scoring, scuffing |
| **Contact Stress (Hertzian)** | $\sigma_c$ | Hertzian contact theory | Surface fatigue |

> [!NOTE]
> For **safe gear design**, the conditions $\sigma_b \leq \sigma_w$ AND $\sigma_b \leq \sigma_{es}$ must be satisfied **simultaneously**.

---

### 1.6 Module 4 Coverage Context (KTU 2024)

In **Module 4** of PEMET743 (Design of Gear Drives), the topic "Working Stress in Gear Teeth" appears as a **prerequisite concept** that feeds directly into:

- **Design of Spur Gears** (Lewis and Buckingham approach)
- **Design of Helical Gears**
- **Design of Bevel Gears**
- **Design of Worm Gears** (limited)
- **Selection of gears from manufacturer's catalogues**

Mastering this topic allows the student to correctly interpret the **standard tables** for allowable stress values for steel gears, cast iron gears, and non-metallic gears used in KTU problem statements.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Working Stress Exists

When a gear tooth transmits load, the **same tooth** is loaded **once per revolution** (for a single-mesh gear pair). For a gear running at $N$ rpm for $H$ hours, the total number of stress cycles on a single tooth is:

$$N_{cycles} = N \cdot 60 \cdot H$$

For a gear running at 1500 rpm for 8 hours/day, 250 days/year, for 5 years:

$$N_{cycles} = 1500 \times 60 \times 8 \times 250 \times 5 = 9 \times 10^8 \text{ cycles} = 90 \text{ crore cycles}$$

This is **far beyond** the fatigue limit threshold (typically $10^6$ to $10^7$ cycles for steels). Hence, the design must be based on **endurance / fatigue strength**, not static strength.

---

### 2.2 Lewis Equation — The Foundation of Gear Stress Analysis

The **Wilfred Lewis equation** (1892) is the **starting point** for all gear tooth stress analysis. It treats the gear tooth as a **cantilever beam** with the load applied at the tip:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{F_t}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y}$$

Where:

- $F_t$ = Tangential force at the pitch circle (N)
- $b$ = Face width of the gear (mm)
- $m$ = Module of the gear (mm)
- $Y$ = Lewis form factor (dimensionless, depends on tooth profile and number of teeth)
- $\pi \cdot m$ = Represents the **weakest section thickness** at the tooth root (Lewis assumed parabolic tooth)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **design condition** from the Lewis equation is:
> $$\sigma_b \leq \sigma_w \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{F_t}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y} \leq \sigma_w$$
> Or in terms of **beam strength** $S_b$:
> $$S_b = b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y \cdot \sigma_w \geq F_t$$

---

### 2.3 Working Stress — Detailed Breakdown

The **working stress** is the **maximum allowable repeated bending stress** (fatigue-allowable) and is computed from the basic allowable static stress using correction factors:

$$\boxed{\sigma_w = \sigma_0 \cdot C_m \cdot C_v}$$

#### 2.3.1 The Velocity Factor $C_v$ (Dynamic Amplification)

This factor accounts for **dynamic effects** arising from:
- Inaccuracies in tooth spacing (pitch error)
- Profile errors due to manufacturing
- Deflection of teeth under load
- Misalignment
- Sliding velocity at the pitch point

**Buckingham's empirical velocity factor:**

$$C_v = \frac{3.05}{3.05 + v} \quad \text{for cut teeth (full-depth 20° involute)}$$

$$C_v = \frac{6.1}{6.1 + v} \quad \text{for profile-shaved or ground teeth}$$

$$C_v = \frac{4.5}{4.5 + v} \quad \text{for cast teeth}$$

Where $v$ = pitch line velocity in m/s.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU problems, when the velocity factor is **not specified**, students may use the simplified form $\sigma_w \approx C_v \cdot \sigma_0$ or refer to **standard tables** for allowable stress vs. material vs. velocity.

#### 2.3.2 Material Correction Factor $C_m$

A **composite factor** that includes:
- **Reliability factor** ($C_R$): typically 0.7 to 1.0
- **Size factor** ($C_S$): accounts for size effect on fatigue strength
- **Temperature factor** ($C_T$): accounts for operating temperature effects
- **Surface factor** ($C_{sur}$): accounts for surface finish, residual stresses

$$C_m = C_R \cdot C_S \cdot C_T \cdot C_{sur}$$

For standard KTU problems, $C_m \approx 1.0$ is often assumed unless specified otherwise.

---

### 2.4 The Effective Load

The **effective load** (or **dynamic load / total transmitted load**) on the gear tooth is:

$$\boxed{P_e = P \cdot K_a \cdot K_m}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Nominal tangential load (N)
- $K_a$ = **Application factor** (accounts for power source characteristics)
- $K_m$ = **Load distribution factor** (accounts for non-uniform load sharing across the face width)

**Application factor $K_a$** values (typical):

| Power Source | Driven Machine | $K_a$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Uniform** (electric motor, turbine) | Uniform load (generator, conveyor) | 1.00 – 1.25 |
| **Uniform** | Moderate shock (machine tool) | 1.25 – 1.50 |
| **Light shock** (multicyl engine) | Uniform load | 1.25 – 1.50 |
| **Light shock** | Moderate shock | 1.50 – 1.75 |
| **Medium shock** (single cyl engine) | Light shock | 1.75 – 2.00 |

**Load distribution factor $K_m$** depends on:
- Face width $b$
- Mounting accuracy (overhung vs. straddle mounted)
- Bearing stiffness
- Shaft deflection

Typical values: $K_m = 1.0$ to $1.5$.

The **dynamic load** (Buckingham's equation) is:

$$P_d = \frac{21 v (b \cdot C \cdot \cos^2 \alpha + F_t)}{21 v + \sqrt{b \cdot C \cdot \cos^2 \alpha + F_t}}$$

---

### 2.5 Wear Strength — Buckingham's Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wear strength** is the **maximum tangential load** the gear teeth can transmit **without surface failure (pitting)**:

$$\boxed{S_w = \frac{d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K}{2 \cos \alpha}}$$

Where:
- $d_p$ = Pitch circle diameter of the **pinion** (smaller gear) = $m \cdot z_p$ (mm)
- $b$ = Face width (mm)
- $Q$ = **Ratio factor** = $\frac{2 z_g}{z_g + z_p}$ (where $z_p$ and $z_g$ are pinion and gear teeth)
- $K$ = Load-stress factor (also called wear strength coefficient, material property, N/mm²)
- $\alpha$ = Pressure angle (14.5° or 20°)

> [!NOTE]
> For gear design, the **governing beam strength** is:
> $$S_b = \pi \cdot m \cdot b \cdot Y \cdot \sigma_w \quad \text{[Lewis equation form]}$$
> And the design conditions are:
> 1. **Against bending failure**: $F_t \leq S_b$
> 2. **Against wear failure**: $F_t \leq S_w$

---

### 2.6 Endurance Limit Considerations

For **steel gears** operating under fully reversed bending (which approximates gear tooth loading), the **endurance limit** is approximately:

$$\sigma_e = 0.5 \cdot \sigma_{UTS} \quad \text{(for steels)}$$

$$\sigma_e = 0.3 \cdot \sigma_{UTS} \quad \text{(for cast iron)}$$

The **working stress** is generally taken as a fraction of the endurance limit:

$$\sigma_w = \frac{\sigma_e}{\text{Factor of Safety}}$$

Typical **factor of safety** values:

| Application | Factor of Safety |
|---|---|
| Reliable, well-lubricated, no shock | 1.0 – 1.25 |
| Moderate reliability, slight shock | 1.25 – 1.5 |
| Less reliable, definite shock | 1.5 – 2.0 |
| Unreliable, heavy shock | 2.0 – 3.0 |

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> This table contains **all formulas** the student must memorize for Module 4 gear design problems:

| Formula | Expression | Variables / Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lewis bending stress** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_t}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y}$ | $F_t$ in N, $b, m$ in mm, $Y$ dimensionless | Tooth bending check |
| **Working stress** | $\sigma_w = \sigma_0 \cdot C_m \cdot C_v$ | $\sigma$ in MPa | Allowable fatigue stress |
| **Beam strength** | $S_b = b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y \cdot \sigma_w$ | N | Bending failure load |
| **Wear strength** | $S_w = \dfrac{d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K}{2 \cos \alpha}$ | N | Pitting failure load |
| **Effective load** | $P_e = P \cdot K_a \cdot K_m$ | N | Design load |
| **Dynamic load (Buckingham)** | $P_d = \dfrac{21 v (b C \cos^2 \alpha + F_t)}{21 v + \sqrt{b C \cos^2 \alpha + F_t}}$ | N | Internal dynamic effect |
| **Tangential force** | $F_t = \dfrac{2 T}{d} = \dfrac{2 T}{m \cdot z}$ | $T$ in N-mm, $d, m$ in mm | Transmitted load |
| **Pitch line velocity** | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60000}$ | m/s | For velocity factor |
| **Lewis form factor (approx)** | $Y = 0.154 - \dfrac{0.912}{z}$ | dimensionless | 14.5° full-depth teeth |
| **Module (Lewis)** | $m = \dfrac{2 Y \cdot \sigma_w \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot C_v}{P_t}$ | mm | Design of gear |

---

### 2.8 Engineering Utility

The working stress concept is **indispensable** in:

1. **Automotive engineering** — Manual/automatic transmission design (spur, helical gears)
2. **Wind turbine gearboxes** — Multi-stage helical/bevel gear trains under fatigue loading
3. **Industrial machinery** — Conveyors, mixers, pumps requiring long life
4. **Aerospace** — Helicopter transmission systems (high reliability required)
5. **Robotics** — Precision gear reducers requiring infinite-life design
6. **Railway traction** — Helical gear drive for diesel locomotives
7. **Marine propulsion** — Heavy-duty reduction gearboxes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The concept of **working stress** unifies the gear design across all gear types (spur, helical, bevel, worm) and all loading conditions (uniform, shock, fluctuating).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Lewis Form Factor (Conceptual Background)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Lewis form factor $Y$** is not derived from first principles in KTU problems — it is a **geometric property** of the tooth profile. However, the conceptual derivation is presented below for understanding.

**Step 1:** Consider a gear tooth as a **cantilever beam** fixed at the root, with the load applied at the tip.

**Step 2:** The bending moment at the root (dangerous section) is:

$$M = F_t \cdot h$$

Where $h$ = length of the tooth acting as a beam (from tip to root section).

**Step 3:** The section modulus of the tooth at the root (assuming parabolic tooth shape) is:

$$Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{1}{6} \cdot b \cdot t^2$$

Where $t = \pi \cdot m / 2$ (Lewis assumed parabolic tooth, giving tooth thickness at root as $\pi m / 2$).

**Step 4:** The maximum bending stress is:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{F_t \cdot h}{\frac{1}{6} \cdot b \cdot t^2} = \frac{6 F_t \cdot h}{b \cdot t^2}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $t = \pi m / 2$:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{6 F_t \cdot h}{b \cdot (\pi m / 2)^2} = \frac{24 F_t \cdot h}{b \cdot \pi^2 \cdot m^2}$$

**Step 6:** Rearranging:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{F_t}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y}$$

where the **Lewis form factor** $Y$ is:

$$Y = \frac{\pi \cdot m \cdot h}{4 \cdot m^2 / 6} = \text{function of tooth profile and number of teeth}$$

**Step 7:** For **14.5° full-depth involute** teeth, the empirical relation is:

$$Y = 0.154 - \frac{0.912}{z}$$

**Step 8:** For **20° full-depth involute** teeth (modern standard), the empirical relation is:

$$Y = 0.25 - \frac{0.75}{z}$$

**Verification (for 20° teeth, $z = 20$):**

$$Y = 0.25 - \frac{0.75}{20} = 0.25 - 0.0375 = 0.2125$$

This matches standard textbook tabulated values (within rounding).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Working Stress from Endurance Limit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The working stress is derived from the **fatigue / endurance strength** of the gear material, modified by operating factors.

**Step 1:** For ductile materials (steels), the **endurance limit** under fully reversed bending is:

$$\sigma_e = 0.5 \cdot \sigma_{UTS}$$

where $\sigma_{UTS}$ is the ultimate tensile strength.

**Step 2:** Apply the **Marin factors** to correct the endurance limit for actual operating conditions:

$$\sigma_e' = \sigma_e \cdot k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e$$

Where:
- $k_a$ = Surface factor
- $k_b$ = Size factor
- $k_c$ = Load factor (1.0 for bending)
- $k_d$ = Temperature factor
- $k_e$ = Reliability factor

**Step 3:** Define the **factor of safety** against fatigue failure:

$$F.S. = \frac{\sigma_e'}{\sigma_w}$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging:

$$\sigma_w = \frac{\sigma_e'}{F.S.} = \frac{0.5 \cdot \sigma_{UTS} \cdot k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_d \cdot k_e}{F.S.}$$

**Step 5:** Combining all correction factors into a single **material factor** $C_m$:

$$\sigma_w = \sigma_0 \cdot C_m$$

Where $\sigma_0$ is the **base allowable static stress** for the material (from standard tables).

**Step 6:** Apply the **velocity factor** to account for dynamic effects:

$$\boxed{\sigma_w = \sigma_0 \cdot C_m \cdot C_v}$$

This is the **final working stress equation**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Velocity Factor (Buckingham's Approach)

**Step 1:** The total dynamic load on a gear tooth is the sum of:
- **Static (tangential) load**: $F_t$
- **Dynamic increment** due to tooth errors: $\Delta F$

$$P_d = F_t + \Delta F$$

**Step 2:** The dynamic increment depends on:
- Pitch line velocity $v$
- Tooth stiffness $C$
- Face width $b$
- Pressure angle $\alpha$

**Step 3:** Buckingham's experimental formula:

$$\Delta F = \frac{21 v (b \cdot C \cdot \cos^2 \alpha + F_t) - F_t \sqrt{b \cdot C \cdot \cos^2 \alpha + F_t}}{21 v}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back:

$$P_d = \frac{21 v \cdot b \cdot C \cdot \cos^2 \alpha}{21 v + \sqrt{b \cdot C \cdot \cos^2 \alpha + F_t}}$$

**Step 5:** Define the **velocity factor**:

$$C_v = \frac{\text{Static allowable load}}{\text{Dynamic allowable load}} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\sigma_w}$$

**Step 6:** Buckingham's empirical simplified formula:

$$\boxed{C_v = \frac{3.05}{3.05 + v}}$$

For $v = 0$: $C_v = 1.0$ (no dynamic effect, static condition)
For $v = 10$ m/s: $C_v = 3.05/13.05 = 0.234$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Wear Strength (Buckingham's Equation)

**Step 1:** Hertzian contact theory gives the **maximum contact stress** between two meshing cylinders (approximation for gear teeth):

$$\sigma_c \propto \sqrt{\frac{F_t}{b \cdot \rho}}$$

Where $\rho$ = effective radius of curvature.

**Step 2:** Buckingham modified this to give the **wear strength** (maximum tangential load without pitting):

$$S_w = \frac{d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K}{2 \cos \alpha}$$

**Step 3:** The **ratio factor** $Q$ accounts for the fact that the gear slides over the pinion differently than vice versa:

$$Q = \frac{2 z_g}{z_g + z_p}$$

**Derivation of $Q$:**

The total sliding distance per revolution at the pitch point (assuming pinion and gear make 1 and $1/z_g$ revolutions respectively):

$$L_{total} = \pi d_p \cdot \frac{z_g - 1}{z_g} + \pi d_g \cdot \frac{z_p - 1}{z_p}$$

**Step 4:** For simplicity, the Buckingham's ratio factor is:

$$Q = \frac{2 z_g}{z_g + z_p}$$

Where $z_g$ = number of teeth on gear (larger), $z_p$ = number of teeth on pinion (smaller).

**Step 5:** Setting the design condition:

$$F_t \leq S_w = \frac{d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K}{2 \cos \alpha}$$

This is the **design equation against wear failure**.

---

### 3.5 Complete Design Equation — Combining All Concepts

**Given:** Power $P$ (kW), Speed $N$ (rpm), Gear ratio $i$, Material, Reliability.

**Step 1: Compute the tangential load:**

$$F_t = \frac{2 T}{d_p} = \frac{2 \cdot 9550 \cdot P \cdot 10^3}{d_p \cdot N} \quad \text{[with T in N-mm]}$$

**Step 2: Compute the pitch line velocity:**

$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot d_p \cdot N}{60000} \quad \text{[in m/s]}$$

**Step 3: Compute the velocity factor:**

$$C_v = \frac{3.05}{3.05 + v}$$

**Step 4: Compute the working stress:**

$$\sigma_w = \sigma_0 \cdot C_m \cdot C_v$$

**Step 5: Compute the beam strength:**

$$S_b = \pi \cdot m \cdot b \cdot Y \cdot \sigma_w$$

**Step 6: Check against bending failure:**

$$F_t \leq S_b$$

**Step 7: Compute the wear strength:**

$$S_w = \frac{d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K}{2 \cos \alpha}$$

**Step 8: Check against wear failure:**

$$F_t \leq S_w$$

**Step 9: Compute the effective load using application factor:**

$$P_e = F_t \cdot K_a \cdot K_m$$

And verify that $P_e \leq S_b$ and $P_e \leq S_w$.

---

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A spur gear pair transmits 15 kW at 1200 rpm of the pinion. The pinion has 18 teeth and the gear has 72 teeth. The module is 5 mm and face width is 60 mm. The pressure angle is 20°. The gear material has ultimate tensile strength 600 MPa. Use a factor of safety of 1.5 and $C_m = 1.0$. Determine:

(a) The working stress in the gear tooth.
(b) The beam strength and check if the gear is safe against bending.
(c) The wear strength (assume $K = 0.55$ N/mm² for steel pair).

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Tangential load**

$$d_p = m \cdot z_p = 5 \times 18 = 90 \text{ mm}$$

$$T = 9550 \cdot \frac{P}{N} = 9550 \times \frac{15}{1200} = 119375 \text{ N-mm}$$

$$F_t = \frac{2 T}{d_p} = \frac{2 \times 119375}{90} = 2652.78 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Pitch line velocity**

$$v = \frac{\pi \times d_p \times N}{60000} = \frac{\pi \times 90 \times 1200}{60000} = 5.6549 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Velocity factor**

$$C_v = \frac{3.05}{3.05 + v} = \frac{3.05}{3.05 + 5.6549} = \frac{3.05}{8.7049} = 0.3504$$

**Step 4: Working stress**

$$\sigma_0 = \frac{0.5 \cdot \sigma_{UTS}}{F.S.} = \frac{0.5 \times 600}{1.5} = 200 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_w = \sigma_0 \cdot C_m \cdot C_v = 200 \times 1.0 \times 0.3504 = 70.08 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Beam strength**

For $z_p = 18$, 20° full-depth teeth: $Y = 0.25 - 0.75/18 = 0.25 - 0.0417 = 0.2083$

$$S_b = \pi \cdot m \cdot b \cdot Y \cdot \sigma_w = \pi \times 5 \times 60 \times 0.2083 \times 70.08$$

$$S_b = 3.1416 \times 5 \times 60 \times 0.2083 \times 70.08 = 13761.5 \text{ N} \approx 13.76 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Safety check against bending**

$$F_t = 2652.78 \text{ N} < S_b = 13761.5 \text{ N} \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

**Step 7: Wear strength**

$$Q = \frac{2 z_g}{z_g + z_p} = \frac{2 \times 72}{72 + 18} = \frac{144}{90} = 1.6$$

$$S_w = \frac{d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K}{2 \cos \alpha} = \frac{90 \times 60 \times 1.6 \times 0.55}{2 \times \cos 20°}$$

$$S_w = \frac{4752}{2 \times 0.9397} = \frac{4752}{1.8794} = 2528.4 \text{ N} \approx 2.53 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 8: Safety check against wear**

$$F_t = 2652.78 \text{ N} > S_w = 2528.4 \text{ N} \quad \times \text{ NOT SAFE against wear!}$$

**Conclusion:** The gear pair is **safe against bending** but **NOT safe against wear**. The design must be modified — either increase face width, increase module, use better material (higher $K$), or add surface treatment.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | Symbolic Expression | Physical Meaning | Design Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\sigma_w$ | $\sigma_0 \cdot C_m \cdot C_v$ | Allowable fatigue stress | Determines beam strength |
| $C_v$ | $\dfrac{3.05}{3.05 + v}$ | Dynamic amplification | Reduces $\sigma_w$ as $v$ increases |
| $Y$ | $0.154 - \dfrac{0.912}{z}$ (14.5°) | Geometric tooth strength | Increases with $z$ |
| $S_b$ | $\pi m b Y \sigma_w$ | Bending failure load | Must exceed $F_t$ |
| $S_w$ | $\dfrac{d_p b Q K}{2 \cos \alpha}$ | Pitting failure load | Must exceed $F_t$ |
| $Q$ | $\dfrac{2 z_g}{z_g + z_p}$ | Slide velocity ratio | Affects wear strength |
| $F.S.$ | $\dfrac{\sigma_e'}{\sigma_w}$ | Safety margin | Higher = more reliable |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Working Stress Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Parameters: Power P, Speed N, Module m, Teeth z_p z_g, Face Width b, Material Properties"] --> B["Step 1: Compute Tangential Force F_t = 2T / d_p"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Compute Pitch Line Velocity v = pi d_p N / 60000"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Compute Velocity Factor C_v = 3.05 / 3.05 + v"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Compute Base Allowable Stress sigma_0 from material table"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Compute Working Stress sigma_w = sigma_0 * C_m * C_v"]
    F --> G["Step 6: Compute Lewis Form Factor Y based on z_p and pressure angle"]
    G --> H["Step 7: Compute Beam Strength S_b = pi * m * b * Y * sigma_w"]
    H --> I["Check 1: Is F_t less than equal to S_b?"]
    I -- YES --> J["Bending failure criterion satisfied"]
    I -- NO --> K["Bending failure: Increase module or face width or use better material"]
    H --> L["Step 8: Compute Ratio Factor Q = 2 z_g / z_g + z_p"]
    L --> M["Step 9: Compute Wear Strength S_w = d_p * b * Q * K / 2 cos alpha"]
    M --> N["Check 2: Is F_t less than equal to S_w?"]
    N -- YES --> O["Wear failure criterion satisfied"]
    N -- NO --> P["Wear failure: Increase face width or hardness or use better material"]
    J --> Q["Step 10: Apply Application Factor K_a and Load Distribution Factor K_m"]
    P --> Q
    O --> Q
    Q --> R["Step 11: Compute Effective Load P_e = F_t * K_a * K_m"]
    R --> S["Step 12: Verify P_e less than equal to S_b and P_e less than equal to S_w"]
    S -- BOTH YES --> T["GEAR DESIGN IS SAFE AND OPTIMAL"]
    S -- ANY NO --> U["REDESIGN REQUIRED"]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stress Hierarchy in Gear Teeth

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Material Ultimate Tensile Strength sigma_UTS"] --> B1["Endurance Limit sigma_e = 0.5 sigma_UTS"]
    B1 --> C1["Modified Endurance Limit sigma_e dash = sigma_e * Marin factors"]
    C1 --> D1["Base Allowable Stress sigma_0 = sigma_e dash / Factor of Safety"]
    D1 --> E1["Working Stress sigma_w = sigma_0 * C_m * C_v"]
    E1 --> F1["Beam Strength S_b = pi m b Y sigma_w"]
    F1 --> G1["Design Check: F_t less than equal to S_b"]
    
    H1["Material Load Stress Factor K from table"] --> I1["Wear Strength S_w = d_p b Q K / 2 cos alpha"]
    I1 --> J1["Design Check: F_t less than equal to S_w"]
    
    G1 --> K1{"Both Checks Passed?"}
    J1 --> K1
    K1 -- YES --> L1["Gear is SAFE for Infinite Life"]
    K1 -- NO --> M1["Gear FAILS — Redesign Required"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Failure Modes and Governing Stresses

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Gear Tooth Subjected to Repeated Loading"] --> B0["Load Applied at Tooth Tip — Cantilever Beam Action"]
    B0 --> C0["Bending Stress sigma_b at Tooth Root"]
    C0 --> D0["If sigma_b exceeds sigma_w — TOOTH BREAKAGE"]
    
    A0 --> E0["Sliding Contact at Pitch Point"]
    E0 --> F0["Hertzian Contact Stress sigma_c at Tooth Surface"]
    F0 --> G0["If sigma_c exceeds Surface Endurance Limit — PITTING SPALLING"]
    
    A0 --> H0["Heat Generation due to Sliding Friction"]
    H0 --> I0["If Temperature exceeds limit — SCORING SCUFFING"]
    
    A0 --> J0["Vibration due to Dynamic Loads"]
    J0 --> K0["If vibration amplitude exceeds limit — NOISE WEAR"]
    
    D0 --> L0["Working Stress sigma_w Governs — Beam Strength S_b Check"]
    G0 --> M0["Wear Strength S_w Governs — Buckingham Equation Check"]
    I0 --> N0["Lubrication and Material Hardness Govern"]
    K0 --> O0["Velocity Factor C_v and Accuracy Govern"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Design Verification Process

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Student as Student
    module1 as Working Stress Module
    module2 as Lewis Equation Module
    module3 as Buckingham Wear Module
    module4 as Design Verification
    
    Student->>module1: Input material and operating conditions
    module1-->>Student: Returns sigma_w = sigma_0 * C_m * C_v
    Student->>module2: Input tooth geometry and sigma_w
    module2-->>Student: Returns Beam Strength S_b
    Student->>module3: Input geometry and material
    module3-->>Student: Returns Wear Strength S_w
    Student->>module4: Submit F_t, S_b, S_w
    module4->>module4: Check F_t less than equal to S_b
    module4->>module4: Check F_t less than equal to S_w
    module4-->>Student: Verification Result SAFE or UNSAFE
```

---

### 4.5 Mermaid Conceptual Map: Working Stress Position in Design Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["Gear Design Process"] --> lvl1a["Step 1: Define Loading and Kinematics"]
    root --> lvl1b["Step 2: Select Material"]
    root --> lvl1c["Step 3: Compute Working Stress sigma_w"]
    root --> lvl1d["Step 4: Determine Module and Geometry"]
    root --> lvl1e["Step 5: Verify Strength"]
    
    lvl1c --> sub1["sigma_w depends on: sigma_0, C_m, C_v"]
    sub1 --> sub1a["sigma_0 from Material Table"]
    sub1 --> sub1b["C_m from Reliability and Size"]
    sub1 --> sub1c["C_v from Velocity"]
    
    lvl1e --> ver1["Bending Check: S_b = pi m b Y sigma_w"]
    lvl1e --> ver2["Wear Check: S_w = d_p b Q K / 2 cos alpha"]
    ver1 --> outcome["Safe Design: F_t less than equal to min S_b S_w"]
    ver2 --> outcome
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Part A — Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define **working stress in gear teeth**. Explain why it is always **less than** the ultimate tensile strength of the gear material.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Working stress in gear teeth is defined as the **maximum permissible repeated bending stress** that a gear tooth can safely withstand for an **infinite number of load cycles** under actual operating conditions, accounting for dynamic effects, load distribution, material defects, and fatigue behavior.

**Mathematical Expression:**
$$\sigma_w = \sigma_0 \cdot C_m \cdot C_v$$

**Reasons why $\sigma_w < \sigma_{UTS}$:**

1. **Fatigue Loading:** Gear teeth are loaded **once per revolution**, leading to millions of stress cycles during the gear's lifetime. The material's endurance limit (≈ 0.5 × $\sigma_{UTS}$ for steel) is therefore the governing strength, not the ultimate tensile strength.

2. **Dynamic Effects:** Inaccuracies in tooth profile, pitch errors, and elastic deflections cause **impact loading** that amplifies the static load. This is captured by the velocity factor $C_v < 1.0$.

3. **Material Defects:** Inclusions, porosity, surface roughness, and residual stresses reduce the effective fatigue strength of the material. Captured by material factor $C_m < 1.0$.

4. **Factor of Safety:** To account for uncertainties in load, material, and operating conditions, an additional factor of safety is applied.

Hence, $\sigma_w \ll \sigma_{UTS}$, typically $\sigma_w \approx (0.2 \text{ to } 0.4) \cdot \sigma_{UTS}$.

**Distribution:** [Definition: 1 Mark | 3 Reasons: 1 Mark | Concluding remark: 1 Mark]

---

**Part A — Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Write the **Lewis equation** for bending stress in a gear tooth. Explain the significance of the **Lewis form factor $Y$** and state how it varies with the number of teeth.

**Model Answer:**

**Lewis Equation:**
$$\sigma_b = \frac{F_t}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_b$ = Bending stress at the tooth root (MPa)
- $F_t$ = Tangential force at pitch circle (N)
- $b$ = Face width (mm)
- $m$ = Module (mm)
- $Y$ = Lewis form factor (dimensionless)

**Significance of Lewis Form Factor $Y$:**

The Lewis form factor $Y$ is a **purely geometric property** that represents the **strength of the tooth shape**. It accounts for:
1. The actual shape of the tooth (involute, cycloidal)
2. The effect of pressure angle
3. The number of teeth (which affects the tooth proportions)
4. The stress concentration at the tooth root fillet

**Variation with Number of Teeth:**

For **14.5° full-depth involute** teeth:
$$Y = 0.154 - \frac{0.912}{z}$$

For **20° full-depth involute** teeth:
$$Y = 0.25 - \frac{0.75}{z}$$

As $z$ **increases**, $Y$ **increases** (asymptotically approaches a maximum value). This is because larger gears have **relatively thicker teeth** at the root and **smaller pressure angles** at the base circle, giving them higher tooth strength.

**Sample Values (20° involute):**
- $z = 18$: $Y = 0.2083$
- $z = 25$: $Y = 0.220$
- $z = 30$: $Y = 0.225$

**Distribution:** [Equation: 1 Mark | Significance: 1 Mark | Variation: 1 Mark]

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

---

**Part B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** A spur gear pair transmits **22 kW at 1440 rpm** of the pinion. The pinion has **20 teeth** and the gear has **80 teeth**. The module is **6 mm** and face width is **80 mm**. The pressure angle is **20°**. The pinion and gear are made of steel with $\sigma_{UTS} = 600$ MPa. Use $C_m = 1.0$, factor of safety = 1.5, and assume precision-cut gears. Compute:

(i) The tangential load on the gear tooth.
(ii) The pitch line velocity.
(iii) The velocity factor (Buckingham's formula).
(iv) The working stress.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Power $P = 22$ kW
- Speed $N = 1440$ rpm
- $z_p = 20$, $z_g = 80$
- Module $m = 6$ mm
- Face width $b = 80$ mm
- Pressure angle $\alpha = 20°$
- $\sigma_{UTS} = 600$ MPa
- $C_m = 1.0$, F.S. = 1.5
- Precision-cut gears

**(i) Tangential Load:**

Pitch circle diameter of pinion:
$$d_p = m \cdot z_p = 6 \times 20 = 120 \text{ mm}$$

Torque on pinion:
$$T = 9550 \times \frac{P}{N} = 9550 \times \frac{22}{1440} = 145.90 \text{ N-m} = 145902.78 \text{ N-mm}$$

Tangential force:
$$F_t = \frac{2 T}{d_p} = \frac{2 \times 145902.78}{120} = 2431.71 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Pitch Line Velocity:**

$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot d_p \cdot N}{60000} = \frac{\pi \times 120 \times 1440}{60000} = \frac{542867.21}{60000} = 9.048 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Velocity Factor (Buckingham's formula for precision-cut gears):**

For precision-cut (shaved/ground) 20° involute teeth:
$$C_v = \frac{6.1}{6.1 + v} = \frac{6.1}{6.1 + 9.048} = \frac{6.1}{15.148} = 0.4027$$

**(iv) Working Stress:**

Base allowable stress:
$$\sigma_0 = \frac{0.5 \times \sigma_{UTS}}{F.S.} = \frac{0.5 \times 600}{1.5} = 200 \text{ MPa}$$

Working stress:
$$\sigma_w = \sigma_0 \cdot C_m \cdot C_v = 200 \times 1.0 \times 0.4027 = 80.54 \text{ MPa}$$

**Distribution:** [Tangential load: 2 Marks | Velocity: 1 Mark | Velocity factor: 2 Marks | Working stress: 2 Marks]

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** For the gear pair in part (a), compute:
(i) The **beam strength** of the pinion tooth using the Lewis equation.
(ii) The **wear strength** using Buckingham's equation. Assume $K = 0.55$ N/mm².
(iii) State whether the design is **safe** against bending and wear failures.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Beam Strength:**

Lewis form factor for $z_p = 20$, 20° full-depth teeth:
$$Y = 0.25 - \frac{0.75}{z_p} = 0.25 - \frac{0.75}{20} = 0.25 - 0.0375 = 0.2125$$

Beam strength:
$$S_b = \pi \cdot m \cdot b \cdot Y \cdot \sigma_w$$
$$S_b = \pi \times 6 \times 80 \times 0.2125 \times 80.54$$
$$S_b = 3.1416 \times 6 \times 80 \times 0.2125 \times 80.54$$
$$S_b = 25799.46 \text{ N} \approx 25.80 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Wear Strength:**

Ratio factor:
$$Q = \frac{2 z_g}{z_g + z_p} = \frac{2 \times 80}{80 + 20} = \frac{160}{100} = 1.6$$

Wear strength (Buckingham's equation):
$$S_w = \frac{d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K}{2 \cos \alpha}$$
$$S_w = \frac{120 \times 80 \times 1.6 \times 0.55}{2 \times \cos 20°}$$
$$S_w = \frac{8448}{2 \times 0.9397} = \frac{8448}{1.8794} = 4494.9 \text{ N} \approx 4.49 \text{ kN}$$

**(iii) Safety Check:**

Tangential force $F_t = 2431.71$ N = 2.43 kN.

- **Bending check:** $F_t = 2.43$ kN $< S_b = 25.80$ kN → **SAFE against bending** ✓ (factor of safety = 10.6)
- **Wear check:** $F_t = 2.43$ kN $< S_w = 4.49$ kN → **SAFE against wear** ✓ (factor of safety = 1.85)

**Conclusion:** The design is **SAFE** against both bending and wear failures. The wear criterion is more critical (lower factor of safety).

**Distribution:** [Beam strength: 2 Marks | Wear strength: 3 Marks | Safety check: 2 Marks]

---

### **Part B — QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the concept of **working stress** in gear teeth with the help of a **derivation** showing how it relates to the material's endurance limit, factor of safety, and velocity factor.

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** Working stress in a gear tooth is the **maximum permissible repeated bending stress** that the tooth can safely withstand for an **infinite number of stress cycles** under actual operating conditions.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** For ductile materials like steel, the **endurance limit** (fatigue strength under infinite cyclic loading) is approximately:
$$\sigma_e = 0.5 \cdot \sigma_{UTS}$$

**Step 2:** Apply **Marin's correction factors** to account for real-world conditions:
$$\sigma_e' = \sigma_e \cdot k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_d \cdot k_e$$

Where:
- $k_a$ = Surface finish factor
- $k_b$ = Size factor
- $k_d$ = Temperature factor
- $k_e$ = Reliability factor

**Step 3:** Apply the **factor of safety** to get the allowable static stress:
$$\sigma_0 = \frac{\sigma_e'}{F.S.} = \frac{0.5 \cdot \sigma_{UTS} \cdot k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_d \cdot k_e}{F.S.}$$

**Step 4:** Incorporate all material factors into a single **material factor** $C_m$:
$$\sigma_0' = \sigma_0 \cdot C_m$$

**Step 5:** Apply the **velocity factor** $C_v$ to account for dynamic effects due to pitch line velocity:
$$\boxed{\sigma_w = \sigma_0 \cdot C_m \cdot C_v}$$

**Interpretation:** The working stress is a **highly reduced** version of the material's ultimate tensile strength. For example:
- $\sigma_{UTS} = 600$ MPa (steel)
- $\sigma_e = 300$ MPa
- $\sigma_e' = 250$ MPa (after Marin factors)
- $\sigma_0 = 167$ MPa (after F.S. = 1.5)
- $\sigma_w = 70$ MPa (after $C_m = 1.0$, $C_v = 0.42$)

This dramatic reduction (from 600 to 70 MPa, an 8.5× reduction) reflects the **true severity** of gear tooth loading.

**Distribution:** [Concept: 1 Mark | Endurance limit: 1 Mark | Marin factors: 2 Marks | Velocity factor and final equation: 2 Marks | Numerical example: 1 Mark]

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A spur pinion running at **900 rpm** transmits **7.5 kW** to a gear. The pinion has **25 teeth** with module **4 mm** and face width **40 mm**. The pressure angle is **14.5°**. The material has $\sigma_{UTS} = 500$ MPa and $K = 0.49$ N/mm². Use factor of safety = 2 and $C_m = 1.0$. Determine:

(i) The beam strength of the pinion.
(ii) The wear strength of the gear pair (assume gear has 75 teeth).
(iii) Whether the design is safe.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N_p = 900$ rpm, $P = 7.5$ kW
- $z_p = 25$, $z_g = 75$, $m = 4$ mm, $b = 40$ mm
- $\alpha = 14.5°$, $\sigma_{UTS} = 500$ MPa, $K = 0.49$ N/mm²
- F.S. = 2, $C_m = 1.0$

**Preliminary calculations:**

Pitch diameter of pinion:
$$d_p = m \cdot z_p = 4 \times 25 = 100 \text{ mm}$$

Torque:
$$T = 9550 \times \frac{7.5}{900} = 79.583 \text{ N-m} = 79583.33 \text{ N-mm}$$

Tangential force:
$$F_t = \frac{2 \times 79583.33}{100} = 1591.67 \text{ N}$$

Pitch line velocity:
$$v = \frac{\pi \times 100 \times 900}{60000} = 4.712 \text{ m/s}$$

Velocity factor (for cut teeth, 14.5°):
$$C_v = \frac{3.05}{3.05 + v} = \frac{3.05}{3.05 + 4.712} = \frac{3.05}{7.762} = 0.3930$$

**(i) Beam Strength:**

Lewis form factor for 14.5° full-depth teeth, $z_p = 25$:
$$Y = 0.154 - \frac{0.912}{25} = 0.154 - 0.0365 = 0.1175$$

Working stress:
$$\sigma_0 = \frac{0.5 \times 500}{2} = 125 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_w = \sigma_0 \cdot C_m \cdot C_v = 125 \times 1.0 \times 0.3930 = 49.12 \text{ MPa}$$

Beam strength:
$$S_b = \pi \cdot m \cdot b \cdot Y \cdot \sigma_w = \pi \times 4 \times 40 \times 0.1175 \times 49.12$$
$$S_b = 3.1416 \times 4 \times 40 \times 0.1175 \times 49.12 = 2901.7 \text{ N} \approx 2.90 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Wear Strength:**

Ratio factor:
$$Q = \frac{2 z_g}{z_g + z_p} = \frac{2 \times 75}{75 + 25} = \frac{150}{100} = 1.5$$

Wear strength:
$$S_w = \frac{d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K}{2 \cos \alpha} = \frac{100 \times 40 \times 1.5 \times 0.49}{2 \times \cos 14.5°}$$
$$S_w = \frac{2940}{2 \times 0.9681} = \frac{2940}{1.9363} = 1518.4 \text{ N} \approx 1.52 \text{ kN}$$

**(iii) Safety Check:**

$F_t = 1591.67$ N = 1.59 kN

- Bending: $F_t = 1.59$ kN $< S_b = 2.90$ kN → **SAFE** (FOS = 1.82) ✓
- Wear: $F_t = 1.59$ kN $> S_w = 1.52$ kN → **NOT SAFE** (FOS = 0.95) ✗

**Conclusion:** The gear is **NOT safe** against wear failure. Recommendations:
1. Increase face width $b$ from 40 to 50 mm (gives $S_w = 1.90$ kN — safe).
2. Increase module $m$ to 5 mm (gives larger $d_p$ and higher wear strength).
3. Use material with higher $K$ value (e.g., through-hardened steel, $K = 0.65$).
4. Apply surface hardening (carburizing, nitriding) to pinion.

**Distribution:** [Tangential load and velocity: 1 Mark | Beam strength: 2 Marks | Wear strength: 2 Marks | Safety analysis and recommendations: 2 Marks]

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU students make in working stress problems — leading to mark deductions:**
>
> 1. **Using $\sigma_{UTS}$ directly instead of working stress:** Many students substitute $\sigma_{UTS}$ in the Lewis equation, ignoring the velocity factor. **Penalty:** 2–3 marks deduction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert torque units:** Using $T$ in N-m instead of N-mm in the formula $F_t = 2T/d_p$. **Penalty:** 1–2 marks deduction; entire calculation may go wrong.
>
> 3. **Wrong Lewis form factor formula:** Using the 14.5° formula for a 20° pressure angle gear (or vice versa). **Penalty:** 2 marks deduction.
>
> 4. **Mixing beam strength $S_b$ and wear strength $S_w$ equations:** Students sometimes use $S_b$ formula to check wear or vice versa. **Penalty:** 3–4 marks deduction (entire question may fail).
>
> 5. **Ignoring application factor $K_a$ and load distribution factor $K_m$:** In real design problems (not just static design), the effective load $P_e = F_t \cdot K_a \cdot K_m$ must be compared against $S_b$ and $S_w$, not just $F_t$. **Penalty:** 1–2 marks deduction.
>
> 6. **Not stating the design condition clearly:** Examiner wants to see "F_t ≤ S_b" and "F_t ≤ S_w" explicitly written before computing. **Penalty:** 1 mark.
>
> 7. **Wrong velocity factor formula:** Using $C_v = 3.05/(3.05+v)$ for precision-ground teeth (which requires $C_v = 6.1/(6.1+v)$). **Penalty:** 1–2 marks.
>
> 8. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m in calculations. **Penalty:** 1 mark (and may cascade into wrong answer).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Working Stress in Gear Teeth**

**Core Concept:**
- ✅ Working stress $\sigma_w$ = allowable **fatigue** stress (NOT static stress)
- ✅ Formula: $\sigma_w = \sigma_0 \cdot C_m \cdot C_v$
- ✅ Used to compute beam strength $S_b = \pi m b Y \sigma_w$

**Key Equations to Memorize:**

- ✅ Lewis bending stress: $\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_t}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y}$
- ✅ Working stress: $\sigma_w = \sigma_0 \cdot C_m \cdot C_v$
- ✅ Beam strength: $S_b = \pi \cdot m \cdot b \cdot Y \cdot \sigma_w$
- ✅ Wear strength (Buckingham): $S_w = \dfrac{d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K}{2 \cos \alpha}$
- ✅ Velocity factor (cut): $C_v = \dfrac{3.05}{3.05 + v}$
- ✅ Velocity factor (ground): $C_v = \dfrac{6.1}{6.1 + v}$
- ✅ Tangential force: $F_t = \dfrac{2T}{d_p}$ or $F_t = \dfrac{9550 \cdot P \cdot 10^3}{d_p \cdot N / 2}$
- ✅ Pitch line velocity: $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_p \cdot N}{60000}$
- ✅ Ratio factor: $Q = \dfrac{2 z_g}{z_g + z_p}$
- ✅ Lewis form factor (14.5°): $Y = 0.154 - \dfrac{0.912}{z}$
- ✅ Lewis form factor (20°): $Y = 0.25 - \dfrac{0.75}{z}$
- ✅ Endurance limit (steel): $\sigma_e = 0.5 \sigma_{UTS}$

**Design Conditions (must be satisfied simultaneously):**

- ✅ **Against bending failure:** $F_t \leq S_b$
- ✅ **Against wear failure:** $F_t \leq S_w$
- ✅ **With effective load:** $P_e = F_t \cdot K_a \cdot K_m \leq S_b$ AND $\leq S_w$

**Key Assumptions:**

- ✅ Gear teeth behave as **cantilever beams**
- ✅ Weakest section at the **tooth root** (Lewis assumption)
- ✅ Load applied at the **tooth tip** (conservative)
- ✅ Tooth profile is **parabolic** (Lewis)

**Material Properties to Remember:**

- ✅ Steel (machined): $\sigma_0 \approx 150$–200 MPa
- ✅ Cast iron: $\sigma_0 \approx 80$–100 MPa
- ✅ Cast steel: $\sigma_0 \approx 100$–120 MPa
- ✅ Bronze: $\sigma_0 \approx 40$–60 MPa
- ✅ Surface-hardened steel: $K$ up to 1.5 N/mm²
- ✅ Through-hardened steel: $K = 0.55$ N/mm²
- ✅ Cast iron: $K = 0.30$ N/mm²

**Numerical Values & Constants:**

- ✅ Pressure angle: **20°** (modern standard) or **14.5°** (older standard)
- ✅ Minimum teeth on pinion (no undercut): **18** (for 20° PA), **32** (for 14.5° PA)
- ✅ Typical face width: $b = (6 \text{ to } 10) \cdot m$
- ✅ Module series (standard): 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20 mm

**Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**

- ❌ Don't confuse $S_b$ (beam strength) with $S_w$ (wear strength)
- ❌ Don't forget the velocity factor — it's always there!
- ❌ Don't use $\sigma_{UTS}$ directly in Lewis equation
- ❌ Don't mix pressure angle formulas for $Y$
- ❌ Don't forget unit consistency (N-mm for torque)

**Engineering Judgment:**

- ✅ If **wear governs** (more common in industrial practice), increase $b$ or use harder material
- ✅ If **bending governs** (more common in highly loaded gears), increase $m$ or improve tooth profile
- ✅ Always specify the **governing criterion** in the design conclusion

**Real-World Application:**

- ✅ Gear design is **infinite-life** design (unlike shafts, which may be finite-life)
- ✅ Working stress is the **fatigue equivalent** of allowable static stress
- ✅ The concept extends to **all gear types**: spur, helical, bevel, worm

**KTU-Specific Tips:**

- ✅ Always draw a **free body diagram** showing $F_t$ and $F_r$ at the pitch point
- ✅ Clearly state the **design conditions** before substituting values
- ✅ Show **units** at every step (examiner marks this positively)
- ✅ End with a clear **"SAFE" or "NOT SAFE"** conclusion

---
