2. The friction coefficient is governed by **fluid shear (viscous drag)** rather than boundary friction → **low and predictable friction**, low power loss, and minimal heating.
3. (Bonus) No metallic contact → **no adhesive wear, no scuffing**, and bearing life is limited only by **rolling-contact fatigue**, predictable by the **L₁₀ rating**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up units:** Many students write $D$ in mm when computing $V$ in m/s. The conversion $\pi D N/60$ requires $D$ in **metres**. **Lost marks: 1–2 per question.**
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of safety:** In PV calculations, you **must divide** the PV limit by FoS. Simply comparing with raw PV limit is worth only partial credit.
> 3. **Archard derivation skipped steps:** Examiners expect **every line of the derivation** (especially $F_a = HA_r$ and the integration step). Skipping directly to the final formula = **0 marks for derivation**.
> 4. **Confusing $\sigma_{H,max}$ with $\tau_{max}$:** Pitting is driven by **sub-surface shear stress**, not contact pressure. Always mention $\tau_{max} \approx 0.3 \, p_0$ at depth $0.5 b$.
> 5. **In pitting mechanism answers, students often forget to draw the sketch.** Always include a **labeled diagram** — without it, expect to lose **2–3 marks** out of 7.
> 6. **For lambda-ratio questions, students use $R_a$ instead of $R_q$.** The formula strictly uses **RMS roughness $R_q$**. Use $R_q \approx 1.25 R_a$ for conversion if only $R_a$ is given.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and stick it on your wall before the exam.**

- [ ] **Surface strength** = resistance to failure at the contact surface; **durability** = expected life before failure.
- [ ] **5 wear types** = abrasive, adhesive, corrosive, surface-fatigue, erosive.
- [ ] **Archard equation** $V = KWL/H$ — wear $\propto$ load, sliding distance; wear $\propto 1/$hardness.
- [ ] **PV product** is the **frictional heat per unit area** and is the **primary design check** for journal / thrust bearings.
- [ ] **Limiting PV** for tin babbit = **15 MPa·m/s**, lead babbit = **10 MPa·m/s**, bronze = **25 MPa·m/s**, PTFE = 4 MPa·m/s.
- [ ] **Hertzian contact** produces **sub-surface shear stress max at depth $0.5 b$** → this is where **pitting cracks initiate**.
- [ ] **Lambda ratio** $\Lambda = h_0 / \sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}$:
  - $\Lambda < 1$ → **Boundary** → adhesive wear, scuffing
  - $1 \le \Lambda < 3$ → **Mixed** → mild adhesive + abrasive wear
  - $3 \le \Lambda < 10$ → **Hydrodynamic** → low wear
  - $\Lambda \ge 10$ → **Thick-film EHL** → negligible wear
- [ ] **Roughness conversions** to memorize: $R_q \approx 1.25 R_a$, $R_z \approx 4 R_a$, $R_{max} \approx 8 R_a$.
- [ ] **Carburising** = high hardness (58–62 HRC) + slight distortion; **Nitriding** = high hardness (60–65 HRC) + **minimal distortion** (no quench).
- [ ] **Brinelling** = static overload damage; **Spalling** = large surface flaking (more severe than pitting).
- [ ] For **gears**, both **bending strength** AND **surface durability (pitting)** must be checked — KTU almost always asks both in 14-mark questions.
- [ ] For **rolling bearings**, the **basic rating life** is $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$, with $p = 3$ (ball) or $p = 10/3$ (roller).
- [ ] **Design always includes a factor of safety** — typically 1.5–2 for PV and 1.2–1.5 for Hertzian contact stress.
- [ ] **Surface treatments ranked by cost:** (Low) Induction hardening → Carburising → Nitriding → PVD/CVD (High).
- [ ] **Shot peening** does **not change hardness** — it induces **compressive residual stress** that delays fatigue-crack growth.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Surface Strength and Durability.**
> *Best of luck for your KTU University Examination!* 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Strength and Wear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design for Strength and Wear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design for Strength** is the analytical and procedural process of proportioning the dimensions of a machine element such that the **induced stresses** (static, dynamic, or fluctuating) due to applied service loads remain below the **allowable stress** limits of the constructional material, with an adequate factor of safety against yielding, fracture, or fatigue failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design for Wear** is the engineering methodology used to proportion and select materials/surface treatments for machine elements (e.g., gears, bearings, cams, brakes) such that the rate of **progressive loss of material** from contacting surfaces under relative motion does not exceed a prescribed service life, typically governed by **Archard's Law of Adhesive Wear** and surface fatigue theories.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, these two design philosophies are treated as **complementary pillars** of mechanical design. Strength governs the **bulk integrity** of the component, whereas wear governs its **surface durability** and long-term dimensional stability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Bone vs. Cartilage" Paradigm**
>
> Imagine the human body. The *skeleton* (bones) provides the **strength** — it must not break under daily loads. The *joints* (cartilage) must resist **wear** — it must not grind away when bones rub against each other during walking. A machine element is designed the same way:
> - **Strength Design (Skeleton):** The cross-section of a shaft or gear tooth must not yield or fracture.
> - **Wear Design (Cartilage):** The contacting surfaces of meshing gears or sliding bearings must not lose material that would change geometry and cause vibration, noise, or seizure.
>
> You can have the strongest gear tooth in the world, but if its flank surface wears out in 100 hours, the gear is a **design failure**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Key Standard Metrics for KTU Examinations**
> - **Factor of Safety (FoS or n):** Dimensionless, typically **1.5 to 4.0** for machine elements.
> - **Endurance Limit (S_e or σ_e):** Fatigue strength at 10⁶ to 10⁷ cycles, expressed in **MPa** or **N/mm²**.
> - **Wear Coefficient (K):** Dimensionless, typically **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁸** depending on lubrication regime.
> - **Hardness (H):** Expressed in **HB (Brinell)**, **HV (Vickers)**, or **HRC (Rockwell C)**.
> - **Universal Gas Constant for Thermal Design:** 8.314 J/(mol·K), though not used in this module.
> - **Maximum Design Temperature for Standard Steels:** 350 °C without creep considerations.

## 1.4 Classification of Design Loads

| Load Type | Symbol | Description | Typical KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static Load** | $P$ | Constant magnitude, no fluctuation | Hydraulic press frame |
| **Fluctuating Load** | $P_m \pm P_a$ | Mean + alternating component | Rotating shaft in a vehicle |
| **Reversed Load** | $-P_a$ to $+P_a$ | Fully reversed, zero mean | Connecting rod of I.C. engine |
| **Shock Load** | $P_{sh}$ | Sudden, impulsive loading | Cam follower return spring |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theories of Failure (Critical for Static Strength Design)

When a machine element is subjected to **combined stresses** (e.g., a shaft under bending + torsion), we need a hypothesis to predict yielding. The KTU syllabus focuses on the following four theories:

### 2.1.1 Maximum Principal (Normal) Stress Theory — *Rankine Theory*
- **Postulate:** Failure occurs when the **maximum principal stress** reaches the yield strength in a simple tension test.
- **Governing Equation:**

$$ \sigma_1 \leq \frac{S_{yt}}{n} $$

- **Use Case:** **Brittle materials** like cast iron and concrete.

### 2.1.2 Maximum Shear Stress Theory — *Tresca Theory*
- **Postulate:** Failure occurs when the **maximum shear stress** in the complex state equals the shear yield stress in a tension test.
- **Governing Equations:**

$$ \tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} \leq \frac{S_{sy}}{n} = \frac{S_{yt}}{2n} $$

- **Use Case:** **Ductile materials** under static loading; gives conservative (safe) results.

### 2.1.3 Maximum Distortion Energy Theory — *von Mises Theory* (Most Widely Used)
- **Postulate:** Failure occurs when the **distortion energy** per unit volume equals that at yield in a simple tension test.
- **Governing Equation for 2D Stress (σ_x, σ_y, τ_xy):**

$$ \sigma_1^{\,2} + \sigma_2^{\,2} - \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \leq \left( \frac{S_{yt}}{n} \right)^{2} $$

- **Governing Equation for Combined Bending + Torsion (Pure DE Formula):**

$$ \left( \frac{\sigma_x}{S_{yt}/n} \right)^{2} + 3 \left( \frac{\tau_{xy}}{S_{yt}/n} \right)^{2} \leq 1 $$

- **Use Case:** **Most ductile metals** under static or fluctuating loads; recommended by ASME.

### 2.1.4 Maximum Principal Strain Theory
- **Postulate:** Failure occurs when the **maximum principal strain** reaches the strain at yield in tension.
- Less common in design, used for brittle materials where Poisson effects matter.

## 2.2 Design for Fluctuating Stresses (Fatigue Strength)

Most machine elements (shafts, springs, connecting rods) experience **cyclic stresses**:

$$ \sigma(t) = \sigma_m + \sigma_a \sin(\omega t) $$

where $\sigma_m$ is the **mean stress** and $\sigma_a$ is the **stress amplitude**.

The KTU syllabus requires the following four fatigue failure criteria:

### 2.2.1 Soderberg Criterion (Most Conservative)
- **Governing Line Equation:**

$$ \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} = \frac{1}{n} $$

- **Use Case:** Ductile materials where yielding must never occur.

### 2.2.2 Goodman Criterion (Linear, Most Common)
- **Governing Line Equation:**

$$ \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n} $$

- **Use Case:** General rotating equipment; **most frequently asked in KTU exams.**

### 2.2.3 Gerber Criterion (Parabolic, Least Conservative)
- **Governing Parabola Equation:**

$$ \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left( \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \right)^{2} = \frac{1}{n} $$

- **Use Case:** Ductile materials (steel, aluminum) where slight plastic deformation is acceptable.

### 2.2.4 ASME-Elliptic Criterion (Recommended by ASME)
- **Governing Ellipse Equation:**

$$ \left( \frac{n \sigma_a}{S_e} \right)^{2} + \left( \frac{n \sigma_m}{S_{yt}} \right)^{2} = 1 $$

- **Use Case:** Ductile materials with no plastic deformation; combines yielding and fatigue.

## 2.3 Design for Wear

### 2.3.1 Types of Wear
1. **Abrasive Wear** — Hard particles/ploughing cut softer surface.
2. **Adhesive Wear** — Microscopic welding at asperity contacts; sheared under sliding.
3. **Corrosive Wear** — Chemical reaction + mechanical removal.
4. **Surface Fatigue (Pitting/Spalling)** — Cyclic subsurface stress → crack → material loss.
5. **Erosive Wear** — Impact of solid particles or fluid jets.

### 2.3.2 Archard's Equation for Adhesive Wear (King of Wear Design)
- **Volume of material worn away:**

$$ V = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H} $$

where:
- $V$ = worn volume (mm³)
- $K$ = dimensionless wear coefficient
- $W$ = normal load (N)
- $L$ = sliding distance (mm)
- $H$ = hardness of softer material (N/mm²)

- **Wear Rate:**

$$ \dot{W}_r = \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot v}{H} $$

where $v = dL/dt$ is the sliding velocity (mm/s).

### 2.3.3 Specific Wear Rate
- **Rearranged to obtain K experimentally:**

$$ K = \frac{V \cdot H}{W \cdot L} $$

Typical K values:
- Severe unlubricated: $K \approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$
- Poorly lubricated: $K \approx 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$
- Well-lubricated: $K \approx 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table is the **Kern's KTU Formula Sheet** for Module 4. All these equations are **frequently asked** in university examinations.

| # | Design Domain | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rankine Theory | $\sigma_1 \le S_{yt}/n$ | $\sigma_1$ = max principal | MPa |
| 2 | Tresca (Max Shear) | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \le S_{yt}/n$ | Principal stresses | MPa |
| 3 | von Mises (DE) | $\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2 \le (S_{yt}/n)^2$ | Principal stresses | MPa² |
| 4 | Combined Bending + Torsion DE | $(\sigma_x/S_{yt})^2 + 3(\tau_{xy}/S_{yt})^2 \le 1/n^2$ | Bending, torsional | MPa |
| 5 | Soderberg Line | $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{yt} = 1/n$ | Mean, alt. stresses | MPa |
| 6 | Goodman Line | $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1/n$ | Mean, alt. stresses | MPa |
| 7 | Gerber Parabola | $\sigma_a/S_e + (\sigma_m/S_{ut})^2 = 1/n$ | Mean, alt. stresses | MPa |
| 8 | ASME Ellipse | $(n\sigma_a/S_e)^2 + (n\sigma_m/S_{yt})^2 = 1$ | Mean, alt. stresses | MPa |
| 9 | Endurance Limit (Steel) | $S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa | Uncorrected endurance | MPa |
| 10 | Marin Modified Endurance | $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S_e'$ | Surface, size, etc. | MPa |
| 11 | Stress Concentration Factor | $K_t = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\text{nom}}$ | Theoretical SCF | dimensionless |
| 12 | Notch Sensitivity | $q = (K_f - 1)/(K_t - 1)$ | Fatigue strength reduction | dimensionless |
| 13 | Archard Wear Volume | $V = K W L / H$ | Volume wear | mm³ |
| 14 | Archard Wear Rate | $\dot{V} = K W v / H$ | Volumetric rate | mm³/s |
| 15 | Specific Wear Rate | $K_s = V H / (W L)$ | Wear coefficient | dimensionless |
| 16 | Sliding Distance (shaft) | $L = \pi d N$ | Diameter, revolutions | mm |
| 17 | Hertzian Contact Pressure | $p_0 = \sqrt{2WF/(\pi b L) \cdot (1/R_1 + 1/R_2)}$ | Cylinder/cylinder | MPa |
| 18 | Brinell Hardness Approx. | $S_{ut} \approx 3.4 \, HB$ (steel) | Hardness to strength | MPa |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Aerospace:** Soderberg or ASME-Elliptic criteria are mandatory for turbine shafts (no yielding allowed).
> - **Automotive Crankshafts:** Goodman criterion is the de-facto industry standard.
> - **Gear Design (AGMA):** Combines bending fatigue (Lewis equation) and **pitting resistance** (Hertzian wear) for surface durability.
> - **Brake Pad Design:** Archard's equation with $K \approx 10^{-3}$ to estimate pad life; $H$ is critical.
> - **Mining Equipment:** Abrasive wear governs crusher liner life; high $H$ materials (e.g., Hadfield steel) used.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Soderberg Fatigue Criterion

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
Consider a ductile element subjected to a **fluctuating axial load** $P(t) = P_m + P_a \sin(\omega t)$, producing stresses $\sigma_m = P_m/A$ and $\sigma_a = P_a/A$. The material has yield strength $S_{yt}$ and endurance limit $S_e$. The design factor of safety is $n$.

### 3.1.2 Derivation Steps

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Logic:** The Soderberg line is a **linear interpolation** between two distinct failure points in the $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ plane.

**Step 1: Identify the two limiting cases (failure points).**

- **Case A — Pure Fatigue Failure (no mean stress):** $\sigma_m = 0$, failure when $\sigma_a = S_e$. Safety condition:

$$ \sigma_a \le \frac{S_e}{n} $$

- **Case B — Pure Static Yielding (no alternating stress):** $\sigma_a = 0$, failure when $\sigma_m = S_{yt}$. Safety condition:

$$ \sigma_m \le \frac{S_{yt}}{n} $$

**Step 2: Construct a straight line connecting these two safety limits** in the $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ plane.

The straight line connecting $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a) = (S_{yt}/n, \, 0)$ to $(0, \, S_e/n)$ has the intercept form:

$$ \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}/n} + \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e/n} = 1 $$

**Step 3: Rearrange into the canonical Soderberg form.**

$$ \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} = \frac{1}{n} $$

**Step 4: Verify with limit checks.**

- If $\sigma_m = 0$: $\sigma_a = S_e/n$ ✓
- If $\sigma_a = 0$: $\sigma_m = S_{yt}/n$ ✓
- For any point **inside** the line, the design is safe. The perpendicular distance from the origin gives the actual factor of safety.

**Step 5: Closed-form Solution for the Factor of Safety (FoS).**

The factor of safety is the ratio of the **distance to the line** (infinite) to the **distance of the operating point from the origin** along the same normal.

For Soderberg, the closed-form FoS is:

$$ n = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}}} $$

> **Logical Interpretation:** The design is safe if and only if the sum of the **normalized alternating stress** and the **normalized mean stress** does not exceed unity divided by the desired factor of safety.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Goodman Criterion (Following Analogous Logic)

**Step 1:** Replace the yield strength $S_{yt}$ in Soderberg with the **ultimate tensile strength** $S_{ut}$ (since fatigue failure in ductile materials ultimately approaches ultimate strength at high mean stresses).

**Step 2:** The line connecting $(0, S_e/n)$ to $(S_{ut}/n, 0)$ is:

$$ \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n} $$

**Step 3:** Factor of Safety for Goodman:

$$ n = \frac{\sigma_a \, S_{ut}}{S_{ut} - \sigma_m \, S_e} \quad \text{(for $\sigma_m \ne S_{ut}$)} $$

> **Engineering Insight:** Goodman's line lies **above** Soderberg's line, hence it is **less conservative** (allows higher stresses for a given FoS). For a given operating point $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$, the Goodman FoS will be **higher** than the Soderberg FoS.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Gerber Parabolic Criterion

**Step 1:** Gerber (1874) proposed a **parabolic envelope** based on empirical data for ductile materials. The equation is:

$$ \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left( \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \right)^{2} = \frac{1}{n} $$

**Step 2:** Cross-multiplying:

$$ n \, \sigma_a \, S_{ut}^{\,2} + n^{2} \, \sigma_m^{\,2} \, S_e = S_e \, S_{ut}^{\,2} $$

**Step 3:** Factor of Safety (after rearranging as a quadratic in $n$):

$$ n^{2} \left( \frac{\sigma_m^{\,2} S_e}{S_{ut}^{\,2}} \right) - n \left( \frac{\sigma_a S_{ut}^{\,2} - 2 S_e S_{ut}^{\,2} / S_e}{S_{ut}^{\,2}} \right) + \cdots $$

The standard closed form for Gerber FoS is:

$$ n = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_m} \right)^{2} \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \left[ -1 + \sqrt{1 + \left( \frac{2 \sigma_m S_e}{S_{ut} \sigma_a} \right)^{2} } \right] $$

> **Engineering Insight:** The Gerber parabola lies **above** the Goodman line for $\sigma_m > 0$, hence it is the **least conservative** of the three ductile criteria. It is the best fit for **experimental fatigue data** on ductile steels.

## 3.4 Derivation of the ASME-Elliptic Criterion

**Step 1:** The ASME combines yielding (Tresca) and fatigue (Goodman) into a single **ellipse** in the $\sigma_m$–$\sigma_a$ plane.

**Step 2:** The ellipse equation is constructed such that:
- At $\sigma_m = 0$: $\sigma_a = S_e/n$ (fatigue limit)
- At $\sigma_a = 0$: $\sigma_m = S_{yt}/n$ (yield)

**Step 3:** A quarter-ellipse connecting these intercepts has the canonical form:

$$ \left( \frac{n \, \sigma_a}{S_e} \right)^{2} + \left( \frac{n \, \sigma_m}{S_{yt}} \right)^{2} = 1 $$

**Step 4:** Solving for the factor of safety (closed form):

$$ n = \frac{1}{\sqrt{ \left( \dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \right)^{2} + \left( \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} \right)^{2} }} $$

> **Engineering Insight:** The ASME ellipse is **midway** between Soderberg (most conservative) and Goodman (less conservative), and is **recommended by ASME Section III** for nuclear and high-reliability components.

## 3.5 Derivation of Archard's Adhesive Wear Equation

### 3.5.1 Microscopic Picture
Two surfaces in sliding contact touch only at **discrete asperities** (microscopic peaks). At each asperity:
1. The normal load $W_i$ creates a **local plastic junction**.
2. Under tangential motion, the junction **shears** and a fragment of material is transferred (or lost).
3. The volume of the fragment $V_i$ is roughly proportional to the **real area of contact** $A_{r,i}$ and the **sliding distance** $L$.

### 3.5.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** For a single asperity, the volume of the wear fragment is:

$$ V_i = k_0 \, A_{r,i} \, L $$

where $k_0$ is a proportionality constant ($k_0 \le 1$).

**Step 2:** The real area of contact for **plastically deformed asperities** is given by hardness:

$$ A_{r,i} = \frac{W_i}{H} $$

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$ V_i = k_0 \, \frac{W_i}{H} \, L $$

**Step 4:** Sum over all asperities (the $k_0$ values are lumped into a single statistical coefficient $K$):

$$ V = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i = K \, \frac{L}{H} \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i = K \, \frac{W \, L}{H} $$

**Step 5:** This is the **Archard Wear Equation:**

$$ V = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H} $$

### 3.5.3 Physical Meaning of K
- $K = 1$: Every asperity junction forms a wear particle (severe adhesive wear).
- $K = 10^{-4}$: Typical unlubricated metal-metal contact.
- $K = 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$: Well-lubricated, smooth surfaces (boundary or mixed lubrication).

### 3.5.4 Wear Rate Form
Differentiating Archard's equation with respect to time:

$$ \frac{dV}{dt} = K \, \frac{W}{H} \, \frac{dL}{dt} = K \, \frac{W \, v}{H} $$

where $v$ = sliding velocity.

### 3.5.5 Linear Wear Rate (depth per unit time)
The linear wear depth $h$ is related to volume by $V = A \cdot h$, where $A$ is the apparent contact area. Hence:

$$ \frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{K \, W \, v}{A \, H} = \frac{K \, p \, v}{H} $$

where $p = W/A$ is the apparent contact pressure.

## 3.6 Combined Bending + Torsion Distortion Energy Equation (Derivation)

For a circular shaft of diameter $d$ subjected to:
- Bending moment $M$ → bending stress $\sigma_x = 32M/(\pi d^3)$
- Torque $T$ → torsional shear stress $\tau_{xy} = 16T/(\pi d^3)$

The von Mises equivalent stress is:

$$ \sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^{\,2} + 3 \, \tau_{xy}^{\,2}} $$

Setting $\sigma_{VM} = S_{yt}/n$ and squaring:

$$ \sigma_x^{\,2} + 3 \, \tau_{xy}^{\,2} = \left( \frac{S_{yt}}{n} \right)^{2} $$

Solving for the shaft diameter $d$:

$$ d = \left( \frac{16 n}{\pi S_{yt}} \sqrt{ 4M^{2} + 3T^{2} } \right)^{1/3} $$

> **The quantity $M_e = \sqrt{M^2 + (3/4)T^2}$ is called the *equivalent bending moment* in the KTU/Gupta design approach.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Decision Tree for Selecting a Failure Theory

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **decision logic** that an engineer (or KTU student solving a problem) must follow when choosing a failure theory for combined loading.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Stresses sigma1 sigma2 sigma3] --> B{Material Type?}
    B -- Brittle --> C[Use Rankine or Max Principal Strain]
    B -- Ductile --> D{Load Type?}
    D -- Static --> E{Material Reliability Required?}
    E -- High --> F[Use von Mises DE Theory]
    E -- Moderate --> G[Use Tresca Max Shear Theory]
    D -- Fluctuating Cyclic --> H{Mean Stress Level?}
    H -- High sigma_m --> I[Use Soderberg Conservative]
    H -- Moderate --> J[Use Goodman Linear]
    H -- Low sigma_m --> K[Use Gerber Parabolic]
    D -- ASME Code Required --> L[Use ASME Elliptic]
    F --> M[Compute Equivalent Stress]
    G --> M
    I --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Compare to Syt / n]
    N --> O{sigma_VM less than Syt by n?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Design SAFE]
    O -- No --> Q[Increase Cross Section]
    Q --> A
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Archard Wear Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Given Load W, Sliding Distance L, Hardness H] --> B[Identify Lubrication Regime]
    B -- Dry --> C[K = 10^-3 to 10^-4]
    B -- Boundary --> D[K = 10^-4 to 10^-5]
    B -- Mixed --> E[K = 10^-5 to 10^-6]
    B -- Full Fluid --> F[K = 10^-6 to 10^-8]
    C --> G[Compute V = K W L / H]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute Linear Wear h = V / A]
    H --> I{Service Life h / dh_dt less than Required?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Accept Design]
    I -- No --> K[Modify Material Increase H or Reduce Load]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Mapping of Design Criteria to Stress Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Machine Element] --> B1[Compute Bending Stress sigma_x]
    A0 --> B2[Compute Torsional Shear tau_xy]
    A0 --> B3[Compute Axial Stress sigma_y]
    B1 --> C1{Fatigue or Static?}
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 -- Static --> D1[Apply von Mises or Tresca]
    C1 -- Fatigue --> D2[Resolve into sigma_m and sigma_a]
    D2 --> D3[Apply Soderberg, Goodman, Gerber, or ASME]
    D1 --> E1[Compute Diameter d or Section Modulus Z]
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Check Deflection and Wear]
    F1 --> G1[Final Design]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Surface Wear Modes Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wear Problem] --> B{Contact Condition}
    B -- Sliding No Lubricant --> C[Abrasive or Adhesive]
    B -- Rolling Contact --> D[Surface Fatigue Pitting]
    B -- High Temperature --> E[Oxidative or Corrosive]
    B -- Particle Impact --> F[Erosive]
    C --> G[Use Archard Equation]
    D --> H[Use Hertzian Contact Theory]
    E --> I[Use Oxidation Kinetics]
    F --> J[Use Erosion Models]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Soderberg Criterion.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The Soderberg criterion is a **linear fatigue failure theory** for ductile materials subjected to **fluctuating (cyclic) stresses** with mean stress $\sigma_m$ and alternating stress $\sigma_a$. It states that the design is safe if the sum of the normalized alternating stress and the normalized mean stress does not exceed the reciprocal of the factor of safety:
>
> $$ \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} = \frac{1}{n} $$
>
> where $S_e$ is the **endurance limit**, $S_{yt}$ is the **yield strength**, and $n$ is the **factor of safety**. It is the **most conservative** among the ductile fatigue criteria. (1 mark: definition; 1 mark: equation; 1 mark: application/conservative remark.)

### Question 2: State Archard's Equation and explain the significance of the wear coefficient K.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> Archard's equation for adhesive wear is:
>
> $$ V = \frac{K W L}{H} $$
>
> where $V$ is the **worn volume**, $W$ is the **normal load**, $L$ is the **sliding distance**, and $H$ is the **hardness of the softer material**.
>
> The **wear coefficient $K$** (dimensionless) is a measure of the severity of wear; it depends on the **lubrication regime**, surface finish, and material pair. Typical values: $K \approx 10^{-3}$ for dry unlubricated contact, $K \approx 10^{-4}$ for boundary lubrication, and $K \approx 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$ for well-lubricated contact. Lower $K$ means longer service life. (1 mark: equation; 1 mark: variable definitions; 1 mark: significance of $K$.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [Internal Choice Option A]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

> **A shaft is subjected to a fluctuating torque that varies from +400 N·m to -200 N·m, and a fluctuating bending moment that varies from +500 N·m to 0 N·m. The shaft is made of 30 C 8 steel ($S_{ut} = 550$ MPa, $S_{yt} = 380$ MPa) with corrected endurance limit $S_e = 240$ MPa. Using (a) Soderberg and (b) Goodman criteria, determine the required shaft diameter. Take factor of safety $n = 2$.**

#### **Part (a) — Soderberg Criterion [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Resolve Torque and Bending Moment into Mean and Alternating Components.**

$$ T_m = \frac{T_{\max} + T_{\min}}{2} = \frac{400 + (-200)}{2} = 100 \text{ N·m} $$

$$ T_a = \frac{T_{\max} - T_{\min}}{2} = \frac{400 - (-200)}{2} = 300 \text{ N·m} $$

$$ M_m = \frac{M_{\max} + M_{\min}}{2} = \frac{500 + 0}{2} = 250 \text{ N·m} $$

$$ M_a = \frac{M_{\max} - M_{\min}}{2} = \frac{500 - 0}{2} = 250 \text{ N·m} $$

**Step 2: Express Mean and Alternating Stresses in Terms of Diameter $d$.**

Bending: $\sigma = 32M/(\pi d^3)$. Torsion: $\tau = 16T/(\pi d^3)$.

$$ \sigma_m = \frac{32 M_m}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{32 \times 250 \times 10^{3}}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{2.546 \times 10^{6}}{d^{3}} \text{ MPa} $$

$$ \sigma_a = \frac{32 M_a}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{2.546 \times 10^{6}}{d^{3}} \text{ MPa} $$

$$ \tau_m = \frac{16 T_m}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{16 \times 100 \times 10^{3}}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{5.093 \times 10^{5}}{d^{3}} \text{ MPa} $$

$$ \tau_a = \frac{16 T_a}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{16 \times 300 \times 10^{3}}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{1.528 \times 10^{6}}{d^{3}} \text{ MPa} $$

**Step 3: Apply Soderberg Combined Equation.**

For combined bending and torsion, the **Soderberg equation for von Mises equivalent stresses** is:

$$ \frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} $$

In terms of the equivalent alternating and mean stresses using DE:

$$ \sigma_{a,eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_a^{2} + 3\tau_a^{2}} = \frac{1}{d^{3}} \sqrt{(2.546)^2 \times 10^{12} + 3 \times (1.528)^2 \times 10^{12}} $$

$$ = \frac{1}{d^{3}} \sqrt{6.482 + 7.004} \times 10^{6} = \frac{\sqrt{13.486} \times 10^{6}}{d^{3}} = \frac{3.673 \times 10^{6}}{d^{3}} \text{ MPa} $$

$$ \sigma_{m,eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_m^{2} + 3\tau_m^{2}} = \frac{1}{d^{3}} \sqrt{(2.546)^2 \times 10^{12} + 3 \times (0.5093)^2 \times 10^{12}} $$

$$ = \frac{1}{d^{3}} \sqrt{6.482 + 0.778} \times 10^{6} = \frac{\sqrt{7.260} \times 10^{6}}{d^{3}} = \frac{2.694 \times 10^{6}}{d^{3}} \text{ MPa} $$

**Step 4: Substitute into Soderberg.**

$$ \frac{1}{2} = \frac{3.673 \times 10^{6}}{240 \cdot d^{3}} + \frac{2.694 \times 10^{6}}{380 \cdot d^{3}} $$

$$ 0.5 = \frac{1}{d^{3}} \left( \frac{3.673 \times 10^{6}}{240} + \frac{2.694 \times 10^{6}}{380} \right) $$

$$ 0.5 = \frac{1}{d^{3}} (15{,}304 + 7{,}089) = \frac{22{,}393}{d^{3}} $$

$$ d^{3} = \frac{22{,}393}{0.5} = 44{,}786 \text{ mm}^{3} $$

$$ d = (44{,}786)^{1/3} = 35.5 \text{ mm} $$

> **Answer (a):** $d \approx 35.5$ mm (Soderberg) — adopt standard **40 mm**.

#### **Part (b) — Goodman Criterion [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Same equivalent mean and alternating stresses as above.**

**Step 2: Goodman Equation.**

$$ \frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_{a,eq}}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_{m,eq}}{S_{ut}} $$

$$ 0.5 = \frac{1}{d^{3}} \left( \frac{3.673 \times 10^{6}}{240} + \frac{2.694 \times 10^{6}}{550} \right) $$

$$ 0.5 = \frac{1}{d^{3}} (15{,}304 + 4{,}898) = \frac{20{,}202}{d^{3}} $$

$$ d^{3} = \frac{20{,}202}{0.5} = 40{,}404 \text{ mm}^{3} $$

$$ d = (40{,}404)^{1/3} = 34.3 \text{ mm} $$

> **Answer (b):** $d \approx 34.3$ mm (Goodman) — adopt standard **35 mm**.
>
> **Conclusion:** The Goodman criterion gives a smaller required diameter than Soderberg, confirming that Soderberg is more conservative. The final design should use **max(35.5, 34.3) ≈ 40 mm** (next standard size) for safety.

---

### Question B: [Internal Choice Option B]
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

> **A brake pad of a car has apparent contact area $A = 4000$ mm² and is pressed against a cast-iron disc with normal force $W = 2000$ N. The disc surface moves at $v = 25$ m/s. The pad is made of an organic friction material with hardness $H = 80$ MPa and wear coefficient $K = 5 \times 10^{-4}$. Determine: (a) the volumetric wear rate of the pad in mm³/s, and (b) the linear wear rate in mm/s. Also, if the maximum allowable linear wear is 4 mm, find the service life in hours.**

#### **Part (a) — Volumetric Wear Rate [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Identify the given data.**

$$ W = 2000 \text{ N}, \quad v = 25 \text{ m/s} = 25{,}000 \text{ mm/s}, \quad H = 80 \text{ MPa} = 80 \text{ N/mm}^{2}, \quad K = 5 \times 10^{-4} $$

**Step 2: Apply Archard's Wear Rate Equation.**

$$ \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot v}{H} $$

**Step 3: Substitute and compute.**

$$ \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-4})(2000)(25{,}000)}{80} $$

$$ = \frac{25{,}000}{80} = 312.5 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{s} $$

> **Answer (a):** Volumetric wear rate = **312.5 mm³/s**.

#### **Part (b) — Linear Wear Rate and Service Life [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Compute Linear Wear Rate.**

$$ \frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{1}{A} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{312.5}{4000} = 0.0781 \text{ mm/s} $$

> **Engineering Note (1 mark):** This extremely high linear wear rate (≈ 281 mm/hr) confirms the pad is unsuitable for continuous service — typical pads operate with $dh/dt \approx 10^{-5}$ mm/s in normal braking. The given numbers are illustrative.

**Step 2: Compute Service Life.**

If maximum allowable linear wear $h_{\max} = 4$ mm:

$$ t_{\text{life}} = \frac{h_{\max}}{dh/dt} = \frac{4}{0.0781} = 51.2 \text{ s} $$

Converting to hours:

$$ t_{\text{life}} = \frac{51.2}{3600} = 0.0142 \text{ hours} \approx 51 \text{ seconds} $$

> **Answer (b):** Linear wear rate = **0.0781 mm/s**; Service life ≈ **51 seconds** (≈ 0.0142 hours).
>
> **Design Interpretation:** Such a short life indicates the design is impractical. Either the **load must be reduced**, the **hardness increased** (use a harder pad), or the **sliding velocity reduced**.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing $S_{yt}$ and $S_{ut}$:** Soderberg uses **$S_{yt}$** in the mean term; Goodman uses **$S_{ut}$**. Swapping them is a guaranteed 2-mark penalty.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 3 in DE theory:** The von Mises equivalent stress is $\sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$, NOT $\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \tau^2}$. This is a classic sign-error.
> 3. **Not using "Equivalent" quantities:** For combined bending and torsion, you MUST compute the equivalent mean and alternating stresses separately using the $\sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ form before applying Soderberg/Goodman.
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Ensure $M$ is in **N·mm** and $d$ in **mm** to get stress in MPa. Mixing N·m and mm will give a 1000× error.
> 5. **Wear Equation Hardness:** $H$ must be in the **same units as $W/A$** (i.e., pressure units). Common error: using HV or HRC numbers directly without converting to MPa (HV ≈ 0.0098 × MPa, but Brinell HB can be approximated as MPa directly for steel).
> 6. **Mean/Alternating Calculation Sign Errors:** For $P_{\min} = -200$, $P_{\max} = 400$: $P_m = 100$ and $P_a = 300$, NOT $P_a = 200$. Always use $P_a = (P_{\max} - P_{\min})/2$.
> 7. **Standard Diameter:** After computing $d$, always round up to the next **standard size** (e.g., 30, 32, 35, 36, 40, 45 mm for shafts).

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Design for Strength and Wear**

**A. Static Strength Design (Failure Theories):**
- ☐ Rankine — **brittle** materials, uses $\sigma_1$ only.
- ☐ Tresca (Max Shear) — ductile, conservative, linear in principal stresses.
- ☐ **von Mises (DE) — most widely used**, equation: $\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2 = (S_{yt}/n)^2$.
- ☐ For combined bending + torsion: $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$.
- ☐ Equivalent moment: $M_e = \sqrt{M^2 + (3/4)T^2}$; Equivalent torque: $T_e = \sqrt{T^2 + (4/3)M^2}$.

**B. Fatigue Strength Design (Fluctuating Loads):**
- ☐ Mean stress: $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min})/2$.
- ☐ Alternating stress: $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min})/2$.
- ☐ **Soderberg** (most conservative): $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{yt} = 1/n$.
- ☐ **Goodman** (most common): $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1/n$.
- ☐ **Gerber** (least conservative, parabolic).
- ☐ **ASME Elliptic** (recommended for high-reliability ductile designs).
- ☐ Order of conservativeness: **Soderberg < ASME < Goodman < Gerber** (in terms of allowed stress).
- ☐ Uncorrected endurance limit: $S_e' = 0.5 S_{ut}$ for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa.
- ☐ Corrected: $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \cdot S_e'$.

**C. Design for Wear:**
- ☐ **Archard equation:** $V = K W L / H$.
- ☐ **Wear rate:** $dV/dt = K W v / H$.
- ☐ **Linear wear rate:** $dh/dt = K p v / H$ (where $p$ = apparent pressure).
- ☐ $K$ ranges from $10^{-3}$ (dry) to $10^{-8}$ (well-lubricated).
- ☐ Higher **hardness** = lower wear.
- ☐ Five wear modes: Abrasive, Adhesive, Corrosive, Fatigue, Erosive.

**D. Useful Material Approximations:**
- ☐ $S_{ut} \approx 3.4$ × HB (Brinell) for steels.
- ☐ $S_{yt} \approx 0.7 S_{ut}$ for ductile steels.
- ☐ Poisson's ratio for steel: $\nu = 0.3$.
- ☐ Modulus of elasticity: $E = 210$ GPa for steel, $E = 70$ GPa for aluminum.

**E. Key Safety Margins:**
- ☐ Static design: $n = 1.5$ to $3$.
- ☐ Fatigue design: $n = 2$ to $4$.
- ☐ Wear-limited design: $n = 1.5$ to $2.5$ (often a service-life based check).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Spur Gear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Spur Gear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Spur Gear Design

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spur Gear:** A spur gear is a cylindrical gear with straight teeth cut parallel to the axis of rotation, used to transmit power and motion between parallel shafts without axial thrust. The **Design of Spur Gear** is the engineering process of proportioning the gear tooth geometry and material such that it safely transmits the required power at a given speed, while resisting **bending fatigue failure (tooth breakage)** and **contact (pitting/wear) failure** over its design life.

In KTU 2024 scheme context, the design process involves two fundamental strength criteria established by **Wilfred Lewis (1893)** for tooth bending and **Buckingham (1899)** for tooth wear:

1. **Beam Strength (Lewis Equation)** – Resistance against bending fracture at the root of the tooth.
2. **Wear Strength (Buckingham's Equation)** – Resistance against pitting/contact fatigue on the tooth flank.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Engineering Intuition"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Crowbar and the Cylinder:**
> Imagine a gear tooth as a **cantilever beam (like a crowbar)** projecting out of the gear rim. When the driving gear pushes the driven gear, the tooth acts as a short cantilever loaded at its tip by the tangential force (P_t). The tooth root acts like the **fixed end of the crowbar**, where bending stress is maximum — this is exactly why gear teeth break at the **root fillet**.
>
> Now imagine pressing two cylinders together (the flanks of two meshing teeth) — at the line of contact, **Hertzian contact stress** develops, which over time causes tiny pits to form on the surface, much like pressing two ball bearings together. This is **wear or pitting failure**.

So a gear designer must ensure:
- The tooth is **strong enough as a beam** (so it doesn't snap at the root).
- The tooth flank is **tough enough at the contact patch** (so it doesn't pit).

### 1.3 Standard Design Constants & Metrics (KTU Highlighted Values)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Constants for Spur Gears:**
> - **Lewis Form Factor (Y):** Depends on number of teeth; **Y = 0.485 for 20° full depth system with 20 teeth**.
> - **Velocity Factor (C_v):** Accounts for dynamic load; ranges from 0.4 to 1.0 based on pitch line velocity.
> - **Service Factor (C_s):** Accounts for fluctuating loads and shock; ranges from 1.0 (uniform) to 2.0 (heavy shock).
> - **Face Width (b):** Typically **b = 9.5m to 12m** where m is the module.
> - **Standard Pressure Angle (φ):** **20°** (full depth involute system) — globally adopted by ISO/AGMA/KTU.
> - **Addendum (a) = 1 module (m)**, **Dedendum (d) = 1.157 m to 1.25 m** depending on system.
> - **Minimum number of teeth (z_min) = 17** for 20° full depth system to avoid interference.
> - **Material Property:** Surface durability factor (C) and Bending stress (σ_b) defined per material tables.

### 1.4 Why Spur Gear Design is a Core KTU Module

> [!NOTE]
> Every machine — from an **automobile gearbox**, **CNC spindle**, **conveyor drive**, to a **wind turbine gearbox** — uses spur gears at some stage. The 2024 KTU module specifically tests a student's ability to:
> - Select module and face width based on bending and wear criteria.
> - Calculate design power, equivalent pitch point radius, dynamic load, and wear load.
> - Apply Lewis Form Factor correctly based on the number of teeth.
> - Justify material selection based on **surface durability and bending fatigue allowable stress**.
> This is a **14-mark KTU standard question** topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of Spur Gears

A spur gear drive consists of two meshing gears (pinion and gear) with involute teeth. The line of action is inclined at the **pressure angle (φ = 20°)** to the common tangent at the pitch point. The transmitted tangential load **P_t** acts along this line of action.

**Operating logic (Step-by-step):**

- **Step 1 — Power Transmission:** The pinion (driver) rotates and applies a tangential force **P_t** at the pitch circle of the gear, causing it to rotate.
- **Step 2 — Load Distribution:** This load acts at the pitch point and is shared between the meshing pair based on face width **b** and module **m**.
- **Step 3 — Stress Generation:** Two primary stresses develop:
  - **Bending stress** at the tooth root (Lewis).
  - **Contact (Hertzian) stress** at the flank surface (Buckingham).
- **Step 4 — Design Verification:** The calculated stresses must be less than or equal to the allowable stresses of the chosen material under given service conditions.
- **Step 5 — Safety Margin:** KTU expects a **minimum factor of safety = 1.5** between design values and final selection.

### 2.2 Lewis Equation — Beam Strength of Spur Gear Tooth

Wilfred Lewis idealized the gear tooth as a **cantilever beam** of triangular cross-section, fixed at the root, with the load applied at the tip.

**Tangential load that the tooth can safely transmit (Beam Strength):**

$$
S_b = m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y
$$

Where:
- **S_b** = Beam strength of tooth (in N)
- **m** = Module (in mm)
- **b** = Face width (in mm)
- **σ_b** = Allowable bending stress for the gear material (in N/mm²)
- **Y** = Lewis form factor (dimensionless, function of number of teeth)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** If the design is based on **beam strength**, then the **Tangential Tooth Load (P_t) = Beam Strength / (C_v × C_s)**, where C_v is the velocity factor and C_s is the service factor.

### 2.3 Buckingham Equation — Wear Strength of Spur Gear Tooth

Buckingham derived an equation for **Hertzian contact stress** between two meshing involute flanks, accounting for the geometry and the **radius of curvature** at the pitch point.

**Tangential load that the tooth can transmit without surface pitting (Wear Strength):**

$$
S_w = b \cdot d_p \cdot Q \cdot K
$$

Where:
- **S_w** = Wear strength (in N)
- **b** = Face width (in mm)
- **d_p** = Pitch circle diameter of pinion (in mm) = m × z_p
- **Q** = Ratio factor = (2 z_g) / (z_g ± z_p) for external/internal gears
- **K** = Load stress factor = (σ_es)² × sin(φ) × (1/E_p + 1/E_g) / 1.4

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** **σ_es** is the surface endurance limit of the gear material, **E_p** and **E_g** are the elastic moduli of pinion and gear materials. This formula becomes the **limiting criterion for high-speed or heavily-loaded drives** (e.g., automotive gearboxes).

### 2.4 Velocity Factor (C_v) — Barth's Equation

The dynamic increment in load depends on the pitch line velocity. KTU commonly uses:

$$
C_v = \frac{A}{A + \sqrt{V}}
$$

Where:
- **A** = 0.4 to 0.5 depending on tooth profile accuracy and material.
- **V** = Pitch line velocity in m/s = π × d × N / (60 × 1000)

### 2.5 Service Factor (C_s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Service Factors:**
> - Uniform power source, light shock → **C_s = 1.0 – 1.25**
> - Light shock, moderate fluctuations → **C_s = 1.25 – 1.5**
> - Heavy shock, heavy duty → **C_s = 1.5 – 2.0**

### 2.6 Design Procedure (Standard KTU 14-Mark Algorithm)

1. Compute the **Design Power** = (Rated Power × Service Factor) / Efficiency.
2. Compute the **Tangential Tooth Load** based on pitch circle and rotational speed.
3. **Assume** approximate values for velocity factor C_v, Lewis form factor Y (based on assumed pinion teeth).
4. **Compute the module** from beam strength: **m = (P_t × F.S.) / (b × σ_b × Y × C_v)**.
5. **Standardize the module** to preferred ISO values: 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12 mm.
6. **Compute face width** = 9.5 m to 12 m.
7. **Compute actual Lewis form factor** based on final number of teeth.
8. **Check for wear** using Buckingham's equation.
9. **Check for dynamic load** and ensure pitch line velocity is acceptable.
10. **Verify centre distance** and addendum/dedendum dimensions.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Reference Table)

| # | Formula / Term | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Module (m) | m = d / z | mm (standard ISO preferred series) |
| 2 | Pitch circle diameter (d) | d = m × z | mm |
| 3 | Pitch line velocity (V) | V = π × d × N / 60000 | m/s |
| 4 | Tangential load (P_t) | P_t = (Power × 60000) / (π × d × N) | N |
| 5 | Beam strength (S_b) | S_b = m × b × σ_b × Y | N |
| 6 | Wear strength (S_w) | S_w = b × d_p × Q × K | N |
| 7 | Lewis design condition | P_t × C_s ≤ m × b × σ_b × Y | N |
| 8 | Velocity factor (C_v) | C_v = A / (A + √V) | dimensionless |
| 9 | Ratio factor (Q) | Q = 2 z_g / (z_g + z_p) | dimensionless (external) |
| 10 | Load stress factor (K) | K = (σ_es)² × sin(φ) × (1/E_p + 1/E_g) / 1.4 | depends on materials |
| 11 | Addendum | a = 1 module | mm |
| 12 | Dedendum | d_f = 1.157 m (full depth) or 1.25 m | mm |
| 13 | Circular pitch (p_c) | p_c = π × m | mm |
| 14 | Centre distance (C) | C = (d_p + d_g) / 2 | mm |
| 15 | Minimum teeth (no undercut) | z_min = 17 (20° full depth) | dimensionless |
| 16 | Minimum teeth (no interference, profile shifted) | z_min = (2 × ha* × sec²φ) / (sin 2φ) | dimensionless |

### 2.8 Engineering Utility of Spur Gear Design

> [!NOTE]
> Spur gears are used in **automotive gearboxes, machine tool drives, conveyor systems, electric motor speed reducers, printing machinery, and robotics**. The KTU module trains students in proportioning gear drives that are **durable, compact, quiet, and efficient (98–99%)**. This knowledge is directly applicable to **Vibration Engineering, Tribology, and Machine Design internships in PSUs like BHEL, ISRO, DRDO, and Tata Motors**.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Lewis Beam Strength Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Logic:** Lewis idealized the gear tooth as a **cantilever beam of triangular cross-section** with its apex at the tip and base at the root. The full tangential load **P_t** is assumed to act at the tip.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the triangular section:**

Let the tooth be a trapezoidal section with thickness 't' at the root and 't_n' at the pitch line. The tooth acts as a cantilever of length 'l' (height of tooth from pitch line to tip = module = m).

**Step 2 — Bending stress in cantilever:**

The bending stress at the root of the tooth is:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{P_t \cdot h}{\frac{b \cdot t^2}{6}}
$$

Where:
- **P_t** = tangential load at tip
- **h** = lever arm ≈ module (m)
- **b** = face width
- **t** = tooth thickness at root = **P × m / 2** for full-depth system (P = circular pitch)
- **Z** = section modulus = b × t² / 6

**Step 3 — Substituting t:**

Since **P = π × m**, we have **t = π × m / 2**. So:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{6 \cdot P_t \cdot h}{b \cdot t^2} = \frac{6 \cdot P_t \cdot m}{b \cdot \left(\frac{\pi m}{2}\right)^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplification:**

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{6 \cdot P_t \cdot m}{b \cdot \frac{\pi^2 m^2}{4}} = \frac{24 \cdot P_t}{\pi^2 \cdot b \cdot m}
$$

**Step 5 — Rearranging for Tangential Load:**

$$
P_t = \frac{\pi^2}{24} \cdot m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b
$$

$$
P_t = 0.412 \cdot m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b
$$

**Step 6 — Empirical Modification by Lewis:**

Lewis observed that due to the load-sharing between meshing teeth and the actual tooth shape, the effective stress was lower. He introduced the **Lewis Form Factor (Y)** such that:

$$
\boxed{P_t = m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y}
$$

This is the **Beam Strength Equation (S_b)** — the maximum tangential load a tooth can transmit without bending failure.

### 3.2 Analytical Design Derivation — Module Calculation

The **design tangential load** to be transmitted is:

$$
P_d = \frac{C_s \cdot P_t}{C_v}
$$

For safe design:

$$
P_d \leq S_b = m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{C_s \cdot P_t}{C_v} = m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y
$$

Solving for the module:

$$
\boxed{m = \frac{P_t \cdot C_s}{b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y \cdot C_v}}
$$

Substituting **P_t** in terms of power and rotational speed:

$$
P_t = \frac{60 \cdot 10^3 \cdot \text{Power (kW)}}{2 \pi \cdot \frac{d \cdot N}{60}} = \frac{60 \cdot 10^3 \cdot \text{Power}}{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{m = \frac{60 \cdot 10^3 \cdot P_{design}}{b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y \cdot C_v \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot N}}
$$

Since **d = m × z_p**, this gives a direct iterative solution for the module.

### 3.3 Analytical Design Derivation — Wear Strength (Buckingham)

**Step 1 — Hertzian Contact Stress between two cylinders:**

The Hertzian contact stress for two cylinders in line contact is:

$$
\sigma_c^2 = \frac{2 P_n \cdot E^*}{\pi \cdot b \cdot \rho}
$$

Where:
- **P_n** = Normal load on the contact = P_t / cos(φ)
- **E*** = Composite modulus of elasticity = (1/E_p + 1/E_g)⁻¹
- **ρ** = Effective radius of curvature = (1/r_1 + 1/r_2)⁻¹

**Step 2 — Radius of Curvature at Pitch Point:**

At the pitch point, the radii of curvature of the two meshing tooth flanks are:

$$
r_1 = \frac{d_p \sin\phi}{2} \quad ; \quad r_2 = \frac{d_g \sin\phi}{2}
$$

For external gears:

$$
\rho = \frac{r_1 \cdot r_2}{r_1 + r_2} = \frac{d_p d_g \sin\phi}{2(d_p + d_g)}
$$

**Step 3 — Equating Contact Stress to Endurance Limit:**

For safe operation: σ_c = σ_es (surface endurance limit):

$$
\sigma_{es}^2 = \frac{2 P_t \cdot \sec\phi}{\pi \cdot b \cdot \rho} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{E_p} + \frac{1}{E_g}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Rearranging for Tangential Load:**

$$
P_t = \frac{\pi \cdot b \cdot \rho \cdot \sigma_{es}^2 \cdot \cos\phi}{2 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{E_p} + \frac{1}{E_g}\right)}
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting ρ and simplifying:**

$$
P_t = \frac{b \cdot d_p \cdot \pi \cdot \sigma_{es}^2 \cdot \sin\phi \cdot \cos\phi}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{\frac{1}{E_p} + \frac{1}{E_g}} \cdot \frac{2 z_g}{z_g + z_p}
$$

The combined form is:

$$
\boxed{S_w = b \cdot d_p \cdot Q \cdot K}
$$

Where:
- **Q = 2 z_g / (z_g ± z_p)** (external/internal)
- **K = (σ_es)² × sin(φ) × (1/E_p + 1/E_g) / 1.4**

### 3.4 Worked Example — Standard KTU 14-Mark Type Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Style):** Design a pair of spur gears to transmit 7.5 kW at 1440 rpm of the pinion to 480 rpm of the gear. Take pressure angle = 20°, service factor = 1.5, material for pinion is steel with σ_b = 100 N/mm² and σ_es = 560 N/mm², and material for gear is cast iron with σ_b = 60 N/mm² and σ_es = 350 N/mm². Take tooth system 20° full depth. Take velocity factor constant = 0.4/(0.4 + √V). Lewis form factor for 20 teeth = 0.32 and for 60 teeth = 0.302. E_p = 2.1 × 10⁵ N/mm², E_g = 1.0 × 10⁵ N/mm².

#### Step 1 — Speed and Power Calculations

$$
\text{Gear Ratio } (i) = \frac{N_p}{N_g} = \frac{1440}{480} = 3
$$

Assume z_p = 20, so z_g = 60.

#### Step 2 — Design Power

$$
P_{design} = P \times C_s = 7.5 \times 1.5 = 11.25 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Step 3 — Pitch Line Velocity (assume d = 100 mm)

$$
V = \frac{\pi \times d_p \times N_p}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi \times 100 \times 1440}{60 \times 1000} = 7.54 \text{ m/s}
$$

#### Step 4 — Velocity Factor

$$
C_v = \frac{0.4}{0.4 + \sqrt{7.54}} = \frac{0.4}{0.4 + 2.746} = 0.127
$$

#### Step 5 — Tangential Tooth Load

$$
P_t = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times P_{design}}{\pi \times d_p \times N_p} = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times 11.25}{\pi \times 100 \times 1440} = 1492 \text{ N}
$$

#### Step 6 — Beam Strength Equation

$$
S_b = m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y
$$

Take face width **b = 10 m**. For pinion (limiting), use σ_b = 100 N/mm² and Y = 0.32 (for 20 teeth):

$$
1492 \times 1.0 \leq m \times 10m \times 100 \times 0.32
$$

Wait, b is in mm, so b = 10 × m (where m is in mm). This gives:

$$
1492 \leq m \times (10m) \times 100 \times 0.32
$$

Wait, **b is taken as a multiple of m**: b = 10 m where m is in mm. So:

$$
1492 \leq m \times (10m) \times 100 \times 0.32 = 320 m^2
$$

$$
m^2 \geq \frac{1492}{320} = 4.66
$$

$$
m \geq \sqrt{4.66} = 2.16 \text{ mm}
$$

#### Step 7 — Standardize Module

Take **m = 2.5 mm** (next standard value).

#### Step 8 — Recompute Face Width

$$
b = 10 \times m = 10 \times 2.5 = 25 \text{ mm}
$$

#### Step 9 — Pitch Circle Diameters

$$
d_p = m \times z_p = 2.5 \times 20 = 50 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
d_g = m \times z_g = 2.5 \times 60 = 150 \text{ mm}
$$

#### Step 10 — Centre Distance

$$
C = \frac{d_p + d_g}{2} = \frac{50 + 150}{2} = 100 \text{ mm}
$$

#### Step 11 — Wear Strength Check

$$
Q = \frac{2 z_g}{z_g + z_p} = \frac{2 \times 60}{60 + 20} = 1.5
$$

$$
K = \frac{\sigma_{es}^2 \cdot \sin\phi \cdot \left(\frac{1}{E_p} + \frac{1}{E_g}\right)}{1.4}
$$

$$
K = \frac{(560)^2 \cdot \sin 20° \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2.1 \times 10^5} + \frac{1}{1.0 \times 10^5}\right)}{1.4}
$$

$$
K = \frac{313600 \times 0.342 \times (4.76 \times 10^{-6} + 1.0 \times 10^{-5})}{1.4}
$$

$$
K = \frac{313600 \times 0.342 \times 1.476 \times 10^{-5}}{1.4} = \frac{1.583}{1.4} = 1.131
$$

$$
S_w = b \cdot d_p \cdot Q \cdot K = 25 \times 50 \times 1.5 \times 1.131 = 2120 \text{ N}
$$

#### Step 12 — Verification

$$
S_w = 2120 \text{ N} > P_t \times C_s = 1492 \times 1.0 = 1492 \text{ N}
$$

✅ Design is safe in wear.

#### Step 13 — Final Design Summary

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Design Output:**
> - **Module (m) = 2.5 mm**
> - **Pinion (z_p) = 20 teeth, d_p = 50 mm**
> - **Gear (z_g) = 60 teeth, d_g = 150 mm**
> - **Face width (b) = 25 mm**
> - **Centre distance = 100 mm**
> - **Pressure angle = 20° (full depth)**
> - **Material: Pinion Steel, Gear Cast Iron**

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Spur Gear Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Power, Speed, Ratio] --> B[Compute Design Power P_design = P x C_s]
    B --> C[Assume z_p, z_g, b = 10m]
    C --> D[Compute Tangential Load P_t]
    D --> E[Apply Beam Strength: m = P_t / b x sigma_b x Y]
    E --> F{Module > Standard?}
    F -->|No| G[Increment Trial]
    G --> E
    F -->|Yes| H[Standardize Module m_std]
    H --> I[Compute Face Width b_std = 10m_std]
    I --> J[Compute Pitch Diameters d_p, d_g]
    J --> K[Compute Centre Distance C]
    K --> L[Check Wear Strength S_w > P_t x C_s]
    L --> M{Wear Safe?}
    M -->|No| N[Increase b or upgrade material]
    N --> L
    M -->|Yes| O[Final Output: Module, Face Width, Centre Distance, Materials]
```

### 4.2 Spur Gear Tooth Geometry Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Pinion] -->|m| PG[Pitch Circle d_p]
    PG --> PA[Addendum = 1m]
    PG --> PD[Dedendum = 1.25m]
    PG --> PC[Circular Pitch p_c = πm]
    PA --> T[Tooth Tip]
    PD --> RT[Root Fillet]
    T --> LA[Line of Action at 20 deg]
    RT --> LA
    LA --> F[Forces: P_t Tangential, P_r Radial]
```

### 4.3 Lewis Form Factor vs Number of Teeth (Reference Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z14[z=14, Y=0.245] --> Z17[z=17, Y=0.272]
    Z17 --> Z20[z=20, Y=0.320]
    Z20 --> Z25[z=25, Y=0.358]
    Z25 --> Z30[z=30, Y=0.389]
    Z30 --> Z40[z=40, Y=0.436]
    Z40 --> Z60[z=60, Y=0.485]
    Z60 --> Z_R[z=Rack, Y=0.485]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** As the number of teeth increases, the Lewis Form Factor increases and approaches the rack value (0.485 for 20° full depth). This means **larger gears have proportionally stronger teeth** under the Lewis assumption.

### 4.4 Failure Mode Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FM[Spur Gear Failure Modes] --> FM1[Bending Fatigue at Root]
    FM --> FM2[Pitting / Wear on Flank]
    FM --> FM3[Scoring / Abrasive Wear]
    FM --> FM4[Spalling]
    FM1 --> SOL1[Solution: Increase m or use higher sigma_b material]
    FM2 --> SOL2[Solution: Increase b or use higher sigma_es material / improve lubrication]
    FM3 --> SOL3[Solution: Improve surface finish, viscosity]
    FM4 --> SOL4[Solution: Subsurface treatment, case hardening]
```
