## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**

#### **Question A1:** State and explain Lewis equation for the design of spur gear teeth. *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Lewis developed an equation to determine the **beam strength** of a gear tooth based on the assumption that the tooth behaves as a **cantilever beam** of uniform strength. The tooth is loaded at its tip by the tangential force P_t, and bending stress is maximum at the **root fillet**. The equation is:

$$
\boxed{S_b = m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y}
$$

Where m is the module, b is the face width, σ_b is the allowable bending stress, and Y is the **Lewis Form Factor** which depends on the number of teeth and pressure angle. The beam strength represents the maximum tangential load a tooth can transmit without bending failure.

---

#### **Question A2:** What is the significance of the Lewis Form Factor in spur gear design? *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The **Lewis Form Factor (Y)** is an empirical dimensionless factor that accounts for the **shape of the tooth, load-sharing between multiple meshing teeth, and stress concentration at the root fillet**. It is a function of the **number of teeth (z)** and the **pressure angle (φ)**. For a 20° full-depth involute system, Y increases with the number of teeth, ranging from approximately **0.245 for 14 teeth** to **0.485 for a rack (∞ teeth)**. This factor allows the actual stress in the gear tooth to be compared with the allowable bending stress, making it critical in selecting the correct module.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU ESE Module 4 — Internal Choice Pattern as per 2024 Scheme Regulations]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** Design a Pair of Spur Gears

A pair of spur gears is required to transmit **10 kW** from a shaft running at **1000 rpm** to a machine shaft running at **250 rpm**. The service is **moderate with light shock** (service factor = 1.5). The pinion is made of steel with σ_b = 105 N/mm² and σ_es = 560 N/mm², and the gear is made of cast iron with σ_b = 70 N/mm² and σ_es = 420 N/mm². The tooth system is **20° full depth**. Use Barth's velocity factor with A = 0.4. E_steel = 2.1 × 10⁵ N/mm², E_cast_iron = 1.0 × 10⁵ N/mm².

#### (a) Calculate the module and face width based on beam strength criteria. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Speed ratio and assumed teeth:**
$$
i = \frac{N_p}{N_g} = \frac{1000}{250} = 4
$$
Assume z_p = 20, then z_g = 80.

**Step 2 — Design Power:**
$$
P_{design} = 10 \times 1.5 = 15 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Assume pitch diameter and compute velocity:**
Assume d_p = 80 mm (will be checked later).

$$
V = \frac{\pi \times 80 \times 1000}{60 \times 1000} = 4.19 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Velocity Factor:**
$$
C_v = \frac{0.4}{0.4 + \sqrt{4.19}} = \frac{0.4}{0.4 + 2.047} = 0.164
$$

**Step 5 — Tangential Load:**
$$
P_t = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times 15}{\pi \times 80 \times 1000} = 3581 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Beam Strength for Pinion (limiting):**
Take face width **b = 10 m** and Y_p = 0.32 (for 20 teeth):

$$
3581 \times 1.0 = m \times (10m) \times 105 \times 0.32
$$

$$
3581 = 336 m^2
$$

$$
m = \sqrt{10.66} = 3.26 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Standardize:**
Take **m = 4 mm**.

**Step 8 — Final values:**
- Face width b = 10 × 4 = **40 mm**
- d_p = 4 × 20 = **80 mm**
- d_g = 4 × 80 = **320 mm**
- Centre distance = (80 + 320)/2 = **200 mm**

---

#### (b) Check the design for wear strength using Buckingham's equation. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recalculate actual C_v with new dimensions:**
$$
V = \frac{\pi \times 80 \times 1000}{60000} = 4.19 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
C_v = 0.164
$$

**Step 2 — Ratio Factor Q:**
$$
Q = \frac{2 \times 80}{80 + 20} = 1.6
$$

**Step 3 — Load Stress Factor K:**
$$
K = \frac{(560)^2 \times \sin 20° \times \left(\frac{1}{2.1 \times 10^5} + \frac{1}{1.0 \times 10^5}\right)}{1.4}
$$

$$
K = \frac{313600 \times 0.342 \times 1.476 \times 10^{-5}}{1.4} = 1.131
$$

**Step 4 — Wear Strength:**
$$
S_w = b \times d_p \times Q \times K = 40 \times 80 \times 1.6 \times 1.131 = 5791 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Required Strength:**
$$
P_{required} = \frac{P_t \times C_s}{C_v} = \frac{3581 \times 1.5}{0.164} = 32750 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
Since **S_w = 5791 N < 32750 N required**, the design **fails in wear**.

**Step 7 — Redesign:**
Increase face width. New b = 60 mm:

$$
S_w = 60 \times 80 \times 1.6 \times 1.131 = 8687 \text{ N}
$$

Still insufficient. Use **case-hardened pinion** with σ_es = 700 N/mm²:

$$
K_{new} = \frac{(700)^2 \times 0.342 \times 1.476 \times 10^{-5}}{1.4} = 1.766
$$

$$
S_w = 60 \times 80 \times 1.6 \times 1.766 = 13565 \text{ N}
$$

✅ Design safe.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students forget to multiply by **C_s / C_v** when checking wear. The wear strength S_w must be compared with the **dynamic service load** (P_t × C_s / C_v), not just P_t. Always show both sides of the inequality explicitly.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** Forces, Dynamic Load, and Selection of Standard Module

A spur pinion running at **720 rpm** drives a gear at **180 rpm** and transmits **20 kW**. The pitch circle diameter of the pinion is **150 mm**. The pressure angle is **20°**. Determine:

#### (a) The tangential, radial, and separating forces on the pinion. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pitch line velocity:**
$$
V = \frac{\pi \times 150 \times 720}{60000} = 5.65 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Tangential Force:**
$$
P_t = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times 20}{\pi \times 150 \times 720} = 3540 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Radial Force (separating):**
$$
P_r = P_t \times \tan(20°) = 3540 \times 0.364 = 1288 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Separating Force (Normal component at line of action):**
$$
P_n = \frac{P_t}{\cos(20°)} = \frac{3540}{0.9397} = 3768 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Summary:**
- Tangential force P_t = **3540 N** (drives the rotation)
- Radial force P_r = **1288 N** (separates the gears — important for bearing design)
- Separating force P_n = **3768 N** (along the line of action)

---

#### (b) Determine the dynamic load and check if the gear is safe against dynamic loading. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Dynamic load by Buckingham's equation:**

$$
P_d = \frac{0.164 \cdot V \cdot (b \cdot C \cdot \cos^2\phi + P_t)}{0.164 \cdot V + \sqrt{b \cdot C \cdot \cos^2\phi + P_t}}
$$

Wait — using **dynamic load formula from Buckingham**:

Assume **b = 50 mm**, **C = 19600 N/mm** (error in profile), b·C·cos²(20°) = 50 × 19600 × 0.883 = 865340 N.

**Step 2 — Dynamic increment:**

$$
P_d = P_t + \frac{21 \cdot V \cdot (b \cdot C \cdot \cos^2\phi + P_t)}{21 \cdot V + \sqrt{b \cdot C \cdot \cos^2\phi + P_t}}
$$

Actually, the KTU standard form uses:

$$
P_d = \frac{P_t \cdot C_v'}{C_v}
$$

where C_v' is the dynamic factor based on AGMA. Or we use Barth's velocity factor approach:

For typical cut gear, C_v = 0.164. Thus dynamic increment = P_t × (1 - C_v) = 3540 × 0.836 = 2960 N. Total dynamic load = P_t / C_v = 3540 / 0.164 = 21585 N.

**Step 3 — Compare with beam strength (if m = 5 mm, b = 50 mm, σ_b = 100, Y = 0.32):**

$$
S_b = 5 \times 50 \times 100 \times 0.32 = 8000 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
S_b = 8000 N < Dynamic load 21585 N. **Gear is unsafe.** Increase module to m = 8 mm:

$$
S_b = 8 \times 50 \times 100 \times 0.32 = 12800 \text{ N}
$$

Still unsafe. Take **m = 10 mm**:

$$
S_b = 10 \times 50 \times 100 \times 0.32 = 16000 \text{ N} < 21585 \text{ N}
$$

Increase face width to b = 75 mm with m = 8 mm:

$$
S_b = 8 \times 75 \times 100 \times 0.32 = 19200 \text{ N} < 21585 \text{ N}
$$

Use **superior material** σ_b = 150 N/mm² with m = 8 mm, b = 75 mm:

$$
S_b = 8 \times 75 \times 150 \times 0.32 = 28800 \text{ N} > 21585 \text{ N} \quad \checkmark
$$

✅ **Safe against dynamic loading.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Students often **omit the velocity factor C_v** while calculating the module. The dynamic load is always higher than the static tangential load, and the velocity factor must be applied to ensure safety under actual operating conditions.
> 2. Many students **forget to use the correct Lewis Form Factor** for the chosen number of teeth. Using Y = 0.485 (rack value) for a 20-tooth pinion under-designs the module by approximately 50%.
> 3. The **wear strength S_w must be checked separately** — beam strength alone is insufficient for high-speed or high-load drives.
> 4. Students incorrectly apply the **service factor C_s to beam strength directly** instead of dividing the dynamic load by it. The correct form is: **P_design = (P_t × C_s) / C_v ≤ S_b**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 — Rapid Revision Checklist for Spur Gear Design**

- ⭐ Spur gears transmit power between parallel shafts with **straight teeth parallel to the axis** — no axial thrust.
- ⭐ **Lewis Equation (Beam Strength):** $S_b = m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y$ — limits **bending failure at tooth root**.
- ⭐ **Buckingham Equation (Wear Strength):** $S_w = b \cdot d_p \cdot Q \cdot K$ — limits **pitting/contact failure on tooth flank**.
- ⭐ **Module (m)** is the fundamental design parameter; **standardize to ISO preferred series**: 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12 mm.
- ⭐ **Face width (b) = 9.5m to 12m** is a standard empirical rule.
- ⭐ **Pressure angle (φ) = 20°** full-depth involute system is the universal KTU standard.
- ⭐ **Lewis Form Factor (Y)** is a function of number of teeth; **higher number of teeth = higher Y = stronger tooth**.
- ⭐ **Minimum teeth to avoid undercut/interference = 17** for 20° full-depth system.
- ⭐ **Velocity factor (C_v)** by Barth: $C_v = A / (A + \sqrt{V})$ with A = 0.4 to 0.5.
- ⭐ **Service factor (C_s)** ranges from 1.0 (uniform) to 2.0 (heavy shock).
- ⭐ **Design equation for module:**
$$
m^2 = \frac{P_t \cdot C_s}{b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y \cdot C_v}
$$
- ⭐ **Three checks must be satisfied:** Beam strength, Wear strength, and Dynamic load.
- ⭐ **Pinion is the weaker member** — always design for the pinion material (typically steel).
- ⭐ **Centre distance** = (d_p + d_g) / 2.
- ⭐ **Addendum = 1m, Dedendum = 1.25m** (or 1.157m in standard system).
- ⭐ **Circular pitch (p_c) = πm.**
- ⭐ The **load stress factor K** depends on materials of pinion and gear through their **surface endurance limits and elastic moduli**.
- ⭐ For KTU 14-mark problems, follow this sequence: **Speed ratio → Design Power → Assume module → Beam strength → Standardize → Recompute → Wear check → Final output**.
- ⭐ **Final design must report:** Module, Number of teeth (pinion and gear), Face width, Pitch diameters, Centre distance, Pressure angle, Materials.

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Module Output — Design of Spur Gear**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Helical Gear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Helical Gear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Helical Gear** is a cylindrical gear in which the teeth are cut at an angle (called the **helix angle**, $\alpha$) to the axis of rotation, instead of being parallel to it as in a spur gear. The teeth trace a helical path on the pitch cylinder, hence the name. Because the teeth engage gradually (progressive contact), helical gears run more smoothly and quietly than spur gears, and can transmit higher loads at higher speeds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET743 - Module 4):**
> The design of helical gears involves:
> - **Virtual (Formative) Number of Teeth** concept
> - **Force Analysis** (Tangential, Radial, and Axial/Separation)
> - **Design based on Beam Strength** (Lewis Equation) and **Wear Strength** (Buckingham's Equation)
> - **AGMA Design Procedure** (Bending & Surface Durability)
> - **Effective Load** and **Service Factor** considerations

## 1.2 Types of Helical Gears

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Single Helical** | Teeth cut at helix angle on a single cylinder | Parallel shafts, smooth & silent operation |
| **Double Helical (Herringbone)** | Two opposing helices on same gear | Eliminates axial thrust; heavy-duty drives (turbines, rolling mills) |
| **Crossed Helical (Screw Gears)** | Non-intersecting, non-parallel shafts | Low-power motion transmission (gauge settings) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Screw Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:** Imagine a **standard wood screw** — the threads wrap around the shaft in a helix. Now imagine carving teeth on the *outside* of two parallel cylindrical shafts, the same way a screw thread wraps around. When these two shafts mesh, the angled teeth slide into engagement progressively — first the tip of a tooth, then the flank, then the heel — rather than slamming across the full face width all at once. This is why helical gears are **smoother, quieter, and stronger** than spur gears. The trade-off? They generate an **axial (separation) force** along the shaft, just like a screw trying to push the shafts apart.

## 1.4 Why Use Helical Gears? (Engineering Motivation)

- **Higher contact ratio** (overlap of more than one tooth pair at a time) → smoother power transmission.
- **Higher load capacity** for the same tooth size.
- **Reduced noise and vibration** at high speeds.
- **Permits non-intersecting and non-parallel shaft arrangements** (when used as crossed gears).

> [!WARNING]
> **Trade-off:** Helical gears produce a **thrust (axial) force** on the supporting shaft and bearings. This must be accommodated by appropriate thrust bearings, otherwise the shafts will axially displace.

## 1.5 Standard Terminology at a Glance

- **Helix angle ($\alpha$):** Angle between the tooth helix and the axis of rotation. **Standard value = 20°.**
- **Transverse plane (t):** The plane perpendicular to the axis of rotation.
- **Normal plane (n):** The plane perpendicular to the tooth helix (the plane of the face of the tooth).
- **Normal module ($m_n$):** Module measured in the normal plane — the **standard module** (as per standard tables).
- **Transverse module ($m_t$):** Module measured in the transverse plane — the *actual* circular pitch divided by $\pi$.
- **Normal pressure angle ($\phi_n$):** Pressure angle in the normal plane — **standard = 20°**.
- **Transverse pressure angle ($\phi_t$):** Pressure angle in the transverse plane.
- **Face width ($b$):** Axial length of the gear tooth.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Relationship Between Normal and Transverse Planes

The geometry of a helical gear in the **transverse plane (front view)** is identical to that of a spur gear, but the actual tooth is cut in the **normal plane (side view)**. Therefore, the normal and transverse parameters are related through the helix angle.

### 2.1.1 Module Relationship

Consider one complete helix. The axial pitch $p_a$ and the circular (transverse) pitch $p_t$ are related by:

$$
p_a = p_t \cdot \tan(\alpha)
$$

But more usefully, the normal pitch $p_n$ relates to transverse pitch $p_t$ as:

$$
p_n = p_t \cdot \cos(\alpha)
$$

Since $p = \pi m$, we get:

$$
\pi m_n = \pi m_t \cdot \cos(\alpha)
$$

$$
\boxed{m_n = m_t \cos(\alpha) \quad \text{or} \quad m_t = \frac{m_n}{\cos(\alpha)}}
$$

### 2.1.2 Pressure Angle Relationship

By similar trigonometric reasoning on the pressure angle:

$$
\boxed{\tan(\phi_t) = \frac{\tan(\phi_n)}{\cos(\alpha)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Values (from design data books):**
> - $\phi_n = 20°$ → **Standard normal pressure angle**
> - $\alpha = 20°$ → **Standard helix angle**
> - $m_n$ → **Standard module** (chosen from standard tables)
> - $m_t$ is computed from the relation above (usually a non-standard value).

## 2.2 Virtual (Formative) Number of Teeth

A helical gear in the transverse plane *behaves* like a spur gear. However, the **strength and interference properties depend on the normal plane geometry**. To use spur-gear design equations (like the Lewis form factor $y$, or the minimum teeth rule), we transform the actual number of teeth $z$ into an **equivalent spur gear's number of teeth**, called the **virtual or formative number of teeth**, $z'$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "virtual"?** Because the helical gear is *not* actually a spur gear with $z'$ teeth — but for design calculations (Lewis bending stress, minimum teeth for no interference, etc.), it behaves *as if* it were a spur gear with $z'$ teeth seen from the normal plane.

$$
\boxed{z' = \frac{z}{\cos^3(\alpha)}}
$$

**Minimum number of teeth to avoid interference (for $\phi_n = 20°$):**

$$
\boxed{z_{min} = 17 \cos^3(\alpha)}
$$

For $\alpha = 20°$: $z_{min} = 17 \times (0.9397)^3 \approx 14.1$, i.e., **15 teeth** (safe value).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Transverse module | $m_t = \dfrac{m_n}{\cos \alpha}$ | $m_n$ = standard normal module |
| 2 | Transverse pressure angle | $\tan \phi_t = \dfrac{\tan \phi_n}{\cos \alpha}$ | $\phi_n = 20°$ standard |
| 3 | Pitch circle diameter | $d = m_t \cdot z$ | $z$ = number of teeth |
| 4 | Virtual (formative) teeth | $z' = \dfrac{z}{\cos^3 \alpha}$ | For Lewis, interference checks |
| 5 | Min. teeth (no interference) | $z_{min} = 17 \cos^3 \alpha$ | For $\phi_n = 20°$ |
| 6 | Pitch line velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi d N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi m_t z N}{60}$ m/s | $N$ in rpm |
| 7 | Tangential tooth force | $F_t = \dfrac{P}{v} = \dfrac{2 M_T}{d}$ | $P$ = power (W), $M_T$ = torque |
| 8 | Radial (separating) force | $F_r = \dfrac{F_t \tan \phi_n}{\cos \alpha}$ | Acts toward gear center |
| 9 | Axial (thrust) force | $F_a = F_t \tan \alpha$ | Acts along shaft axis |
| 10 | Beam strength (Lewis) | $F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m_n \cdot y$ | $y$ = Lewis form factor (for $z'$) |
| 11 | Wear strength (Buckingham) | $F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$ | $d_p$ = pitch diameter pinion, $Q$ = ratio factor, $K$ = load stress factor |
| 12 | Effective load | $W_{eff} = C_s \cdot P$ | $C_s$ = service factor, $P$ = tangential load |
| 13 | Dynamic load (Buckingham) | $P_d = P + \dfrac{21 v (b \cdot C \cos^2 \alpha + P)}{21 v + \sqrt{b \cdot C \cos^2 \alpha + P}}$ | $C$ = dynamic coefficient |
| 14 | AGMA bending stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{W_t K_o K_v K_s K_m K_B}{F_m J K_I K_T}$ | $K_B = 1$ for non-pitting |
| 15 | AGMA contact stress | $\sigma_c = C_p \sqrt{\dfrac{W_t K_o K_v K_s K_m K_B}{d_p F_m I} \cdot \dfrac{C_f}{C_v}}$ | $C_p$ = elastic coefficient, $I$ = geometry factor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU problems:** Standard design data books (Khurmi/Gupta/Shigley) must be referenced for the values of the **Lewis form factor $y$**, **load stress factor $K$**, **velocity factor $C_v$**, **form factor $Y$**, and **geometry factor $I$**.

## 2.4 Force Analysis — The Three Components

The total tooth force $F$ on a helical gear has **three orthogonal components**:

1. **Tangential Force ($F_t$):** Drives the gear. Same as for spur gear.
2. **Radial Force ($F_r$):** Tends to separate the two gears (acts towards the center of the driven gear).
3. **Axial Force ($F_a$):** Acts along the shaft axis. This is the *unique* force in helical gears. It must be balanced — for single helical gears, it requires thrust bearings; for double helical (herringbone), it is internally balanced.

**Magnitudes:**

$$
F_t = \frac{P}{v}
$$

$$
F_r = \frac{F_t \tan \phi_n}{\cos \alpha}
$$

$$
F_a = F_t \tan \alpha
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** For $\alpha = 0°$ (spur gear), $F_a = 0$ and $F_r = F_t \tan \phi$, recovering the spur-gear result. ✅

## 2.5 Why Helical Gears Are Stronger

- The **virtual teeth $z'$** is greater than the actual teeth $z$ (since $\cos^3 \alpha < 1$). Therefore, the Lewis form factor $y$ is higher, and the **beam strength increases**.
- The **normal module $m_n$** (used in Lewis equation) is smaller than the transverse module, but the *effective* tooth thickness projected on the transverse plane is larger.
- **Load sharing:** Multiple teeth share the load simultaneously → lower load per tooth → higher capacity.
- **Reduced dynamic loads** due to gradual engagement.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Module Relationship ($m_n = m_t \cos \alpha$)

**Setup:** Unwrap a single tooth helix onto a flat plane (the **development of the helix**).

The helix becomes a straight line inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the axis.

- One complete revolution of the helix (circumference) corresponds to the **transverse pitch** $p_t$ along the circular direction.
- The same revolution corresponds to the **axial advance** equal to the **lead**, $L = p_t \tan \alpha$.

Now look at one tooth thickness in the **normal direction** (perpendicular to the helix line):

The normal pitch $p_n$ is the projection of the transverse pitch $p_t$ onto the normal plane:

$$
p_n = p_t \cdot \cos \alpha
$$

Since pitch $p = \pi m$:

$$
\pi m_n = \pi m_t \cos \alpha
$$

$$
\boxed{m_n = m_t \cos \alpha}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Pressure Angle Relationship

The pressure angle also lies in the normal plane, so by the same projection logic:

- The normal pressure angle $\phi_n$ is the projection of the transverse pressure angle $\phi_t$ onto the normal plane.
- For small angle, $\tan \phi_n = \tan \phi_t \cdot \cos \alpha$.

Solving:

$$
\boxed{\tan \phi_t = \frac{\tan \phi_n}{\cos \alpha}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Virtual (Formative) Number of Teeth

The number of teeth is the ratio of the pitch circle circumference to the transverse pitch:

$$
z = \frac{\pi d}{p_t} = \frac{d}{m_t}
$$

In the **normal plane**, the *apparent* pitch circle radius and apparent pitch need to satisfy the spur-gear equations. The **virtual number of teeth** is defined as the number of teeth a *spur gear* would have *in the normal plane* with the same geometry as the helical gear.

In the normal plane, the apparent radius of the (spur-equivalent) pitch circle is $r' = r \cos \alpha$, and the apparent pitch is $p_n$.

The virtual teeth count:

$$
z' = \frac{\pi (2 r \cos \alpha)}{p_n} = \frac{2 \pi r \cos \alpha}{\pi m_t \cos \alpha} = \frac{2 r}{m_t} \cdot \frac{\cos \alpha}{\cos \alpha}
$$

Wait — let me redo this carefully:

$$
z' = \frac{2 \pi (r \cos \alpha)}{p_n} = \frac{2 \pi r \cos \alpha}{\pi m_t \cos \alpha} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos^2 \alpha} \cdot \cos^2 \alpha
$$

Using $z = \dfrac{2r}{m_t}$ and $m_t = \dfrac{m_n}{\cos \alpha}$:

$$
z' = \frac{2r \cos \alpha}{m_n} = \frac{2r \cos \alpha}{m_t \cos \alpha} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos^2 \alpha} \cdot \cos^2 \alpha
$$

A cleaner derivation: 
- $z = d / m_t$ → $d = z \cdot m_t$
- $z' = d / m_n$ (i.e., same pitch diameter $d$, but with module $m_n$)
- $z' = \dfrac{z \cdot m_t}{m_n} = \dfrac{z \cdot m_t}{m_t \cos \alpha} = \dfrac{z}{\cos \alpha}$ ... but this is the **first-order** form.

The **cubed** form comes from a more rigorous treatment involving the curvature of the helicoid tooth surface and its effect on tooth strength. The textbook (Khurmi/Shigley) gives:

$$
\boxed{z' = \frac{z}{\cos^3 \alpha}}
$$

This accounts for the fact that the actual tooth profile in the normal plane is "stretched" relative to a spur tooth, affecting both the tooth thickness and the curvature at the root.

## 3.4 Derivation: Beam Strength for Helical Gear (Lewis Equation)

The Lewis equation for a spur gear gives the **beam strength** (maximum tangential load without bending failure):

$$
F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y
$$

For a helical gear, we substitute:
- The **normal module** $m_n$ (because bending stress is computed in the normal plane).
- The **Lewis form factor** $y$ corresponding to the **virtual number of teeth** $z'$ (because tooth shape in the normal plane behaves like a spur gear with $z'$ teeth).

$$
\boxed{F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m_n \cdot y}
$$

where $y$ is obtained from standard tables (Khurmi Table) for $z' = z / \cos^3 \alpha$.

## 3.5 Derivation: Wear Strength (Buckingham's Equation)

For a spur gear, Buckingham's wear equation is:

$$
F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K
$$

For a helical gear, the same form holds, but $Q$ is computed using $z'$ instead of $z$:

$$
\boxed{F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K}
$$

where:
- $d_p$ = pitch diameter of the **pinion** (smaller gear)
- $b$ = face width
- $Q = \dfrac{2 z'}{(z'_1 + z'_2)} \cdot \dfrac{i+1}{i}$ (ratio factor; $i$ = speed ratio)
- $K$ = load stress factor (from Buckingham's table, in MPa), function of $v$.

## 3.6 Derivation: Effective Load and Dynamic Load

**Static tangential load on the gear tooth:**

$$
P = \frac{\text{Power (W)}}{v \text{ (m/s)}} \quad \text{or} \quad P = \frac{2 M_T}{d}
$$

**Effective (design) load:**

$$
P_{eff} = C_s \cdot P
$$

where $C_s$ is the **service factor** (from standard tables based on power source, driven machine, and operating conditions).

**Dynamic load (Buckingham's equation):**

$$
P_d = P + \frac{21 v (b C \cos^2 \alpha + P)}{21 v + \sqrt{b C \cos^2 \alpha + P}}
$$

where $C$ is the **deformation coefficient** (from tables; depends on tooth material and accuracy class).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the $\cos^2 \alpha$ factor in Buckingham's dynamic load formula — this is the helical-gear-specific correction to spur-gear dynamic load.

## 3.7 Design Procedure — Step-by-Step (KTU Standard)

1. **Select** the helix angle $\alpha = 20°$ (standard).
2. **Decide** the normal module $m_n$ from standard tables.
3. **Compute** $m_t = m_n / \cos \alpha$.
4. **Compute** virtual teeth $z'_1 = z_1 / \cos^3 \alpha$ and $z'_2 = z_2 / \cos^3 \alpha$.
5. **Compute** Lewis form factors $y_1, y_2$ for $z'_1, z'_2$ (from tables).
6. **Compute** beam strength $F_b$ for both pinion and gear. The **design beam strength** = $\min(F_{b1}, F_{b2})$.
7. **Compute** wear strength $F_w$ (using pinion pitch diameter, $Q$ for virtual teeth, $K$ for $v$).
8. **Compute** design load = $C_s \cdot P$ where $P = P / v$.
9. **Check** that $F_b \geq P_{eff}$ and $F_w \geq P_{eff}$. If not, **increase face width $b$ or module $m_n$** and iterate.
10. **Compute** the three force components ($F_t, F_r, F_a$) and check shaft and bearing design.

## 3.8 Complete Numerical Template (For KTU Problems)

Given: $P$ (power, W), $N_1$ (pinion speed, rpm), $z_1, z_2$ (teeth), $\alpha$ (helix angle), $m_n$ (normal module), $\phi_n = 20°$.

**Step 1: Pitch diameters**
$d_1 = \dfrac{m_n}{\cos \alpha} \cdot z_1$ and $d_2 = \dfrac{m_n}{\cos \alpha} \cdot z_2$

**Step 2: Pitch line velocity**
$v = \dfrac{\pi d_1 N_1}{60}$ m/s

**Step 3: Tangential load**
$P = \dfrac{P_{power}}{v}$ N

**Step 4: Virtual teeth**
$z'_1 = \dfrac{z_1}{\cos^3 \alpha}$, $z'_2 = \dfrac{z_2}{\cos^3 \alpha}$

**Step 5: Beam strength**
$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m_n \cdot y_{\min}$ (using smaller of $y_1, y_2$)

**Step 6: Wear strength**
$F_w = d_1 \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$, with $Q$ for virtual teeth.

**Step 7: Check**
$F_b \geq C_s \cdot P$ AND $F_w \geq C_s \cdot P$ — if not, revise $b$ or $m_n$.

**Step 8: Forces**
$F_t = P$, $F_r = F_t \tan \phi_n / \cos \alpha$, $F_a = F_t \tan \alpha$.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Helical Gear Geometry — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Helical Gear Assembly] --> B[Normal Plane]
    A --> C[Transverse Plane]
    A --> D[Axial Direction]

    B --> B1[Normal Module mn]
    B --> B2[Normal Pressure Angle phin equals 20 deg]
    B --> B3[Tooth Profile in Helicoid View]
    B --> B4[Used for: Beam Strength, Lewis Form Factor]

    C --> C1[Transverse Module mt equals mn divided by cos alpha]
    C --> C2[Transverse Pressure Angle phit]
    C --> C3[Behaves like Spur Gear]
    C --> C4[Used for: Pitch Diameter, Pitch Line Velocity]

    D --> D1[Helix Angle alpha equals 20 deg standard]
    D --> D2[Face Width b]
    D --> D3[Axial Thrust Force Fa]

    B4 --> E1[Virtual Teeth z prime equals z divided by cos cubed alpha]
    E1 --> E2[Lewis Form Factor y from z prime]
    E2 --> E3[Beam Strength Fb equals sigma_b times b times pi times mn times y]

    C4 --> F1[Pitch Diameter d equals mt times z]
    F1 --> F2[Velocity v equals pi d N by 60]
    F2 --> F3[Tangential Load Ft equals P by v]
    F3 --> F4[Radial Fr equals Ft tan phin by cos alpha]
    F3 --> F5[Axial Fa equals Ft tan alpha]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style E3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style F5 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Force Decomposition on a Helical Gear Tooth

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Total Tooth Force F] --> FT[Tangential Ft]
    P --> FR[Radial Fr]
    P --> FA[Axial Thrust Fa]

    FT --> FT1[Direction: along pitch circle tangent]
    FT --> FT2[Drives the gear]
    FT --> FT3[Formula: Ft equals P by v]

    FR --> FR1[Direction: towards gear center]
    FR --> FR2[Tries to separate meshing gears]
    FR --> FR3[Formula: Fr equals Ft tan phin by cos alpha]

    FA --> FA1[Direction: parallel to shaft axis]
    FA --> FA2[Must be absorbed by thrust bearing]
    FA --> FA3[Formula: Fa equals Ft tan alpha]

    style P fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style FT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style FR fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style FA fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Helical Gear Design — Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Given Power P, Speed N, Teeth z1 z2] --> S2[Choose alpha and mn]
    S2 --> S3[Compute mt equals mn by cos alpha]
    S3 --> S4[Compute d1 equals mt z1, d2 equals mt z2]
    S4 --> S5[Compute velocity v equals pi d1 N by 60]
    S5 --> S6[Compute tangential load Pt equals P by v]
    S6 --> S7[Compute virtual teeth z1 prime, z2 prime]
    S7 --> S8[Find y1 and y2 from Lewis table]
    S8 --> S9[Compute beam strength Fb for both gears]
    S9 --> S10[Compute wear strength Fw using dp and Q]
    S10 --> S11[Compute design load P design equals Cs times Pt]
    S11 --> S12{Check Fb greater than P design AND Fw greater than P design}
    S12 -->|Yes| S13[Design Safe: Compute Forces Ft, Fr, Fa]
    S12 -->|No| S14[Increase face width b OR module mn]
    S14 --> S4

    style S1 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000
    style S12 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style S13 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style S14 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
```

## 4.4 Comparison: Spur vs. Helical Gear

| Feature | Spur Gear | Helical Gear |
|---|---|---|
| Tooth orientation | Parallel to axis | Inclined at helix angle $\alpha$ |
| Engagement | Sudden, full-width | Progressive, gradual |
| Noise level | High | Low |
| Load capacity | Lower | Higher |
| Axial thrust | **None** | **Present** ($F_a = F_t \tan \alpha$) |
| Bearing type | Radial only | Radial + Thrust |
| Lewis form factor | Lower | Higher (uses $z'$) |
| Standard pressure angle | $\phi = 20°$ | $\phi_n = 20°$, $\phi_t > 20°$ |
| Minimum teeth (no interference) | 17 | $\approx 15$ (for $\alpha = 20°$) |
| Module used in design | $m$ (transverse) | $m_n$ (normal) |

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the following terms with reference to helical gears:**
(a) **Helix angle** (b) **Virtual number of teeth** (c) **Normal module**.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Helix angle ($\alpha$):** The helix angle is the angle between the direction of the tooth helix (i.e., the tangent to the tooth at the pitch circle) and the axis of rotation of the gear. For standard helical gears, $\alpha = 20°$.

**(b) Virtual (Formative) Number of Teeth ($z'$):** The number of teeth on an equivalent spur gear that would have the same tooth strength and profile characteristics as the helical gear when viewed in the normal plane. It is given by $z' = z / \cos^3 \alpha$. This is used for computing the Lewis form factor and for interference checks.

**(c) Normal Module ($m_n$):** The module measured in the **normal plane** (i.e., perpendicular to the tooth helix). It is the **standard module** selected from design data books. The transverse module is related by $m_t = m_n / \cos \alpha$. **Unit: mm**.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List and explain the three force components acting on a helical gear tooth.**

**Model Answer:**

When a helical gear tooth transmits power, the total force $F$ at the pitch point is resolved into three mutually perpendicular components:

1. **Tangential Force ($F_t$):** Acts tangent to the pitch circle in the plane of rotation. It is the useful force that transmits torque. $F_t = P / v$, where $P$ is power in W and $v$ is pitch line velocity in m/s.

2. **Radial Force ($F_r$):** Acts along the radius of the gear, directed toward the center. It tends to push the two meshing gears apart. $F_r = F_t \tan \phi_n / \cos \alpha$.

3. **Axial (Thrust) Force ($F_a$):** Acts parallel to the gear axis. It is the unique force in helical gears and must be supported by thrust bearings. $F_a = F_t \tan \alpha$.

The total force is the vector sum: $F = \sqrt{F_t^2 + F_r^2 + F_a^2}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** A pair of helical gears consists of a 20-tooth pinion meshing with a 60-tooth gear. The normal module is 4 mm, helix angle is 20°, and the normal pressure angle is 20°. The pinion runs at 1450 rpm and transmits 11 kW. Determine: (1) transverse module, (2) transverse pressure angle, (3) pitch circle diameters, (4) pitch line velocity, (5) virtual number of teeth for both gears, and (6) the three force components. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** The face width of the gears in part (a) is 60 mm. The material for both gears is steel with allowable bending stress $\sigma_b = 84$ MPa. The Lewis form factor for the pinion ($z' = 25.84$) is 0.305 and for the gear ($z' = 77.52$) is 0.422. Determine the **beam strength** of the pinion and gear. If the service factor is 1.75, check whether the gears are safe in bending. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Given:**
- $z_1 = 20$, $z_2 = 60$
- $m_n = 4$ mm, $\alpha = 20°$, $\phi_n = 20°$
- $N_1 = 1450$ rpm, $P_{power} = 11$ kW = 11,000 W
- $b = 60$ mm, $\sigma_b = 84$ MPa
- $y_1 = 0.305$ (for $z'_1$), $y_2 = 0.422$ (for $z'_2$)
- $C_s = 1.75$

**Part (a) Solution:**

**(1) Transverse module:**

$$
m_t = \frac{m_n}{\cos \alpha} = \frac{4}{\cos 20°} = \frac{4}{0.9397} = 4.257 \text{ mm}
$$

**(2) Transverse pressure angle:**

$$
\tan \phi_t = \frac{\tan \phi_n}{\cos \alpha} = \frac{\tan 20°}{\cos 20°} = \frac{0.3640}{0.9397} = 0.3874
$$

$$
\phi_t = \tan^{-1}(0.3874) = 21.17° \approx 21.17°
$$

**(3) Pitch circle diameters:**

$$
d_1 = m_t \cdot z_1 = 4.257 \times 20 = 85.14 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
d_2 = m_t \cdot z_2 = 4.257 \times 60 = 255.42 \text{ mm}
$$

**(4) Pitch line velocity:**

$$
v = \frac{\pi d_1 N_1}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi \times 85.14 \times 1450}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{387,830}{60,000} = 6.464 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(5) Virtual number of teeth:**

$$
z'_1 = \frac{z_1}{\cos^3 \alpha} = \frac{20}{(0.9397)^3} = \frac{20}{0.8298} = 24.10
$$

$$
z'_2 = \frac{z_2}{\cos^3 \alpha} = \frac{60}{(0.9397)^3} = \frac{60}{0.8298} = 72.31
$$

**(6) Force components:**

Tangential force:
$$
F_t = \frac{P_{power}}{v} = \frac{11,000}{6.464} = 1701.8 \text{ N}
$$

Radial force:
$$
F_r = \frac{F_t \tan \phi_n}{\cos \alpha} = \frac{1701.8 \times \tan 20°}{\cos 20°} = \frac{1701.8 \times 0.3640}{0.9397} = 658.97 \text{ N}
$$

Axial force:
$$
F_a = F_t \tan \alpha = 1701.8 \times \tan 20° = 1701.8 \times 0.3640 = 619.5 \text{ N}
$$

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Beam strength of pinion:**

$$
F_{b1} = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m_n \cdot y_1
$$

$$
F_{b1} = 84 \times 60 \times \pi \times 4 \times 0.305
$$

$$
F_{b1} = 84 \times 60 \times 3.1416 \times 4 \times 0.305
$$

$$
F_{b1} = 84 \times 60 \times 3.834 = 19,321 \text{ N} \approx 19.32 \text{ kN}
$$

**Beam strength of gear:**

$$
F_{b2} = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m_n \cdot y_2
$$

$$
F_{b2} = 84 \times 60 \times \pi \times 4 \times 0.422
$$

$$
F_{b2} = 84 \times 60 \times 5.305 = 26,738 \text{ N} \approx 26.74 \text{ kN}
$$

**Effective design load:**

$$
P_{eff} = C_s \cdot F_t = 1.75 \times 1701.8 = 2978.2 \text{ N} \approx 2.978 \text{ kN}
$$

**Safety Check:**

- $F_{b1} = 19.32$ kN $> P_{eff} = 2.978$ kN ✅
- $F_{b2} = 26.74$ kN $> P_{eff} = 2.978$ kN ✅

**Conclusion:** The gears are **safe in bending** by a comfortable margin. The pinion is the weaker gear (lower $y$), and governs the design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes on this type of problem:**
> 1. **Using $m_t$ instead of $m_n$** in the Lewis equation. Always use **$m_n$** (normal module) for helical gears.
> 2. **Using $z$ instead of $z'$** when looking up the Lewis form factor $y$. The form factor must be taken at $z' = z/\cos^3 \alpha$.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\cos \alpha$** in the radial force formula. Use $F_r = F_t \tan \phi_n / \cos \alpha$, **not** $F_t \tan \phi_t$.
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply by $C_s$** when comparing $F_b$ and $F_w$ with the design load.
> 5. **Confusing pinion and gear** — the *pinion* is usually the smaller gear and has lower $y$ (weaker in bending).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Two helical gears of cast steel (allowable bending stress $\sigma_b = 56$ MPa) are required to transmit 7.5 kW at 1200 rpm of the pinion. The gear ratio is 3:1. The normal pressure angle is 20° and the helix angle is 15°. Use a normal module of 5 mm. Determine: (1) the number of teeth to avoid interference, (2) the face width using the beam strength criterion, and (3) the three force components. Take a service factor of 1.5 and a Lewis form factor of 0.32. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** For the gears in part (a), assuming a pitch line velocity of 4.5 m/s and using Buckingham's equation, find the **wear strength** of the gears. Take the load stress factor $K = 0.6$ N/mm² and ratio factor $Q = 0.292$. Check if the gears are safe in wear. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Given:**
- $P_{power} = 7.5$ kW = 7,500 W
- $N_1 = 1200$ rpm (pinion)
- $i = z_2/z_1 = 3$
- $\phi_n = 20°$, $\alpha = 15°$
- $m_n = 5$ mm
- $\sigma_b = 56$ MPa
- $C_s = 1.5$, $y = 0.32$

**Part (a) Solution:**

**(1) Minimum number of teeth (no interference):**

For $\alpha = 15°$:
$$
z_{min} = 17 \cos^3 \alpha = 17 \times (\cos 15°)^3 = 17 \times (0.9659)^3 = 17 \times 0.9010 = 15.32
$$

So $z_{min} \approx 16$ teeth. Choose $z_1 = 17$ (standard minimum for cast steel pinion).

Then $z_2 = i \cdot z_1 = 3 \times 17 = 51$ teeth.

**(2) Transverse module and pitch diameter:**

$$
m_t = \frac{m_n}{\cos \alpha} = \frac{5}{\cos 15°} = \frac{5}{0.9659} = 5.177 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
d_1 = m_t \cdot z_1 = 5.177 \times 17 = 88.01 \text{ mm}
$$

**(3) Pitch line velocity:**

$$
v = \frac{\pi d_1 N_1}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi \times 88.01 \times 1200}{60,000} = \frac{331,827}{60,000} = 5.530 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(4) Tangential force:**

$$
F_t = \frac{P_{power}}{v} = \frac{7,500}{5.530} = 1356.2 \text{ N}
$$

**(5) Effective (design) load:**

$$
P_{eff} = C_s \cdot F_t = 1.5 \times 1356.2 = 2034.3 \text{ N}
$$

**(6) Face width from beam strength criterion:**

The beam strength must equal or exceed the effective load:

$$
F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m_n \cdot y \geq P_{eff}
$$

Solving for $b$:

$$
b = \frac{P_{eff}}{\sigma_b \cdot \pi \cdot m_n \cdot y}
$$

$$
b = \frac{2034.3}{56 \times \pi \times 5 \times 0.32}
$$

$$
b = \frac{2034.3}{56 \times 3.1416 \times 5 \times 0.32} = \frac{2034.3}{281.5} = 7.23 \text{ mm}
$$

For safety, take $b = 25$ mm (standard practice is $b = (8 \text{ to } 12) m_n$; here $b = 5 \times 5 = 25$ mm). Use $b = 25$ mm.

**(7) Force components (using $b = 25$ mm for documentation; force values don't depend on $b$):**

Radial:
$$
F_r = \frac{F_t \tan \phi_n}{\cos \alpha} = \frac{1356.2 \times \tan 20°}{\cos 15°} = \frac{1356.2 \times 0.3640}{0.9659} = 511.2 \text{ N}
$$

Axial:
$$
F_a = F_t \tan \alpha = 1356.2 \times \tan 15° = 1356.2 \times 0.2679 = 363.3 \text{ N}
$$

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Given (additional):** $v = 4.5$ m/s (assumed for the wear check), $K = 0.6$ N/mm², $Q = 0.292$.

**Wear Strength (Buckingham's equation):**

$$
F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K
$$

Here $d_p = d_1 = 88.01$ mm, $b = 25$ mm, $Q = 0.292$, $K = 0.6$ N/mm².

$$
F_w = 88.01 \times 25 \times 0.292 \times 0.6
$$

$$
F_w = 88.01 \times 25 \times 0.1752 = 88.01 \times 4.380 = 385.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Hmm, this gives a low value because the example numbers are illustrative. In KTU exam problems, $K$ is typically 0.6–1.4 N/mm² and you should use the table value.**

Let me re-evaluate with corrected numbers using the formula directly:

$$
F_w = d_1 \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K = 88.01 \times 25 \times 0.292 \times 0.6
$$

$$
F_w = 88.01 \times 25 = 2200.25
$$

$$
2200.25 \times 0.292 = 642.47
$$

$$
642.47 \times 0.6 = 385.48 \text{ N}
$$

For a properly designed gear this is much lower than expected; in practice, $K$ is in MPa units (N/mm²) and may be larger. The illustrative example is for **the procedure**.

**Safety Check (Pitting/Wear):**

$P_{eff} = 2034.3$ N

For a safely designed gear, $F_w \geq P_{eff}$.

If $F_w < P_{eff}$: **The gears are NOT safe in wear**; we must increase $b$ or use better material.

Solving for required $b$:

$$
b_{req} = \frac{P_{eff}}{d_1 \cdot Q \cdot K} = \frac{2034.3}{88.01 \times 0.292 \times 0.6} = \frac{2034.3}{15.42} = 131.9 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Wear Strength Mistakes:**
> 1. **Units mismatch in $K$:** The load stress factor $K$ from Buckingham's table is in **N/mm²** (MPa). Keep units consistent: $d_p$ in mm, $b$ in mm, $Q$ is dimensionless, and $F_w$ comes out in **N**.
> 2. **Forgetting $Q$ in wear strength:** Many students omit the ratio factor $Q$ (which depends on $z'$ and $i$). This is a direct **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Not using virtual teeth in $Q$:** $Q$ is computed using $z'_1$ and $z'_2$ (not $z_1$ and $z_2$). The given $Q = 0.292$ is the *final computed value*; if the problem provides the table of $Q$ values, choose the right one for the given $z'$ and $i$.
> 4. **Ignoring the service factor in the safety check:** Always compare $F_w$ and $F_b$ with $C_s \cdot P$, not with $P$ alone.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Helical Gear Design (KTU Module 4)**

### 🔑 Key Conceptual Points
- Helical gear teeth are **inclined at helix angle $\alpha$** to the shaft axis; standard $\alpha = 20°$.
- The **normal plane** (perpendicular to tooth helix) is where the design parameters ($m_n$, $\phi_n$) are standardized.
- The **transverse plane** (perpendicular to shaft axis) is where the gear *behaves like a spur gear* (pitch diameter, circular pitch).
- **Three force components:** Tangential ($F_t$), Radial ($F_r$), Axial ($F_a$). The axial force is unique to helical gears.

### 📐 Must-Memorize Formula Triplets
- $m_t = m_n / \cos \alpha$ and $\tan \phi_t = \tan \phi_n / \cos \alpha$
- $z' = z / \cos^3 \alpha$ and $z_{min} = 17 \cos^3 \alpha$
- $F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m_n \cdot y$ (with $y$ for $z'$)
- $F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$
- $F_t = P / v$, $F_r = F_t \tan \phi_n / \cos \alpha$, $F_a = F_t \tan \alpha$

### 🎯 Design Verification (ALWAYS)
- **Beam strength check:** $F_b \geq C_s \cdot F_t$ for both pinion and gear.
- **Wear strength check:** $F_w \geq C_s \cdot F_t$.
- If unsafe, **increase face width $b$** first, then **increase module $m_n$**.

### ⚠️ Common KTU Traps
- Use $m_n$ (not $m_t$) in **Lewis beam strength** equation.
- Use $z'$ (not $z$) to look up **Lewis form factor $y$**.
- Use $z'$ (not $z$) to look up **ratio factor $Q$** in wear strength.
- Don't forget the **$\cos \alpha$** in the radial force formula.
- **Don't omit** the service factor $C_s$ in the final safety check.
- The **pinion** is usually the weaker (smaller $y$); it governs the design.

### 🏭 Real-World Applications
- Automotive transmissions (smooth, quiet operation).
- Marine and industrial gearboxes (high-load, high-speed).
- Printing machinery, turbines, rolling mills (double-helical/herringbone for no axial thrust).
- Helicopter gearboxes (high power-to-weight, low noise).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bevel Gear and Worm Gear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET743 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bevel Gear and Worm Gear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bevel Gears

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **bevel gear** is a toothed gear which has a conical shape and is used to transmit motion and power between two intersecting shafts (typically at right angles, though any angle is possible). The pitch surfaces of two mating bevel gears are truncated cones (frustums) that roll on each other without slipping.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine two **ice-cream cones joined at their wide open ends** with their tips meeting at a single point. If you cut teeth on the slanted surfaces where they meet, you get a bevel gear pair. When the cones "roll" on each other, the small cone (pinion) drives the large cone (gear), and the direction of rotation changes — usually by 90°. This is exactly what happens in the **differential of a car** or the **hand-drill** in your workshop.

### Key Characteristics of Bevel Gears
- Operate on **intersecting shafts** (commonly 90°, called **mitre gears** if the ratio is 1:1).
- Tooth elements are **tapered** — wider at the outer (heel) end and narrower at the apex end.
- Use a **virtual (imaginary) back cone** and **pitch cone** for design calculations.
- Common types:
  - **Straight bevel gears** (simplest, noisy at high speed)
  - **Spiral bevel gears** (curved teeth, smoother, quieter)
  - **Zerol bevel gears** (curved but with zero spiral angle)
  - **Hypoid bevel gears** (non-intersecting, used in automotive differentials)

---

## 1.2 Worm Gears

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **worm gear** is a gear consisting of a **worm** (a screw-shaped gear on the driving shaft) meshing with a **worm wheel** (a standard spur gear) on the driven shaft. They are used to transmit motion and power between two **non-intersecting, non-parallel shafts**, usually at right angles.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **screw being turned into a wooden block** — the screw is the worm and the wood's surface takes the shape of the screw threads. A worm gear pair is essentially a **screw driving a wheel**. Each full rotation of the worm advances the worm wheel by **one tooth**. This gives worm gears an inherent **self-locking property** — the worm wheel cannot drive the worm backwards (like how a screw cannot push itself out under load). This is why **elevators, lifting jacks, and steering systems** use worm gears.

### Key Characteristics of Worm Gears
- The worm has **lead angle (λ)** and the wheel has **helix angle (γ)**; for proper meshing, **λ = γ**.
- Provide **high reduction ratios (10:1 to 100:1 or more)** in a single stage.
- **Sliding contact** (not rolling) → high friction, heat generation, and need for good lubrication.
- Standard ratio range: **5 to 75** (KTU syllabus).
- **Self-locking** when the lead angle is less than the friction angle.

### Important KTU Standard Values
- **Pressure angle (α)** for worm gears: **20°** (standard).
- **Standard modules** are based on the **axial module of the worm** = **transverse module of the worm wheel**.
- Bevel gear standard pressure angle: **20°**.
- **Standard shaft angle (Σ)** for bevel gears: **90°**.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** As per the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 of PEMET743, students must be able to:
> - Design bevel gears based on **Lewis equation** and **Bending / Wear (Buckingham's) equations**.
> - Design worm gear pairs using **Worm gear design equations** including **wear strength, bending strength, and thermal capacity**.
> - Analyze **forces acting on gears** and select appropriate materials.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bevel Gear Geometry and Terminology

### 2.1.1 Pitch Cone Geometry
For a standard 90° bevel gear pair with shaft angle Σ = 90°:

$$\gamma = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{N}{n}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{Z_P}{Z_G}\right)$$

$$\Gamma = 90° - \gamma$$

Where:
- **γ** = Pitch angle of the **pinion** (smaller gear)
- **Γ** = Pitch angle of the **gear** (larger gear)
- **N, n** = Speeds of pinion and gear respectively
- **Z_P, Z_G** = Number of teeth on pinion and gear

### 2.1.2 Virtual / Imaginary Gear Concept
Because the teeth of a bevel gear are tapered, the design is based on an **equivalent spur gear** at the **mean (mid) radius** or, more commonly, at the **back cone**. The number of teeth on the virtual (imaginary) gear is given by:

$$Z_v = \frac{Z}{\cos \gamma}$$

This is one of the most important KTU concepts.

### 2.1.3 Standard Proportions (AGMA / KTU Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For standard bevel gears with **90° shaft angle**, the following standard ratios are used:
> - **Face width (b) = 0.3 × R**, where R is the pitch cone radius of the gear.
> - **Pitch cone radius (R) = 0.5 × D** (where D is the larger pitch diameter).
> - **Addendum (a) = Module (m)** for both gears.
> - **Dedendum (b) = 1.25 × m** for both gears (full-depth teeth).

---

## 2.2 Bevel Gear Design Equations

### 2.2.1 Lewis Bending Equation (Modified for Bevel Gears)

The **Lewis equation** is used to find the **safe bending (beam) strength** of the gear tooth:

$$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y$$

For bevel gears, the modification factors (Lewis form factor Y and beam strength) are applied to the **virtual spur gear**:

$$F_{b,\text{bevel}} = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m_v \cdot Y_v \cdot K_v$$

Where:
- **F_b** = Beam strength (N)
- **σ_b** = Allowable bending stress of the gear material (N/mm²)
- **b** = Face width (mm)
- **m** = Module (mm)
- **Y** = Lewis form factor (depends on number of virtual teeth)
- **K_v** = Velocity factor

### 2.2.2 Buckingham's Wear Equation (Surface Durability)

The **wear load capacity** prevents **pitting** (surface fatigue) failure:

$$F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot K \cdot Q$$

Where:
- **F_w** = Wear load (N)
- **d_p** = Pitch diameter of pinion (mm)
- **b** = Face width (mm)
- **K** = Load stress factor (≈ 0.16 N/mm² for steel pinion and cast iron gear)
- **Q** = Ratio factor

### 2.2.3 Effective Load (Bevel Gears)

$$P_{\text{eff}} = \frac{C_s \cdot C_v \cdot C_{mc}}{C_c}$$

Where:
- **C_s** = Service factor
- **C_v** = Velocity factor
- **C_mc** = Mounting condition factor
- **C_c** = Contact ratio factor

The **design power** is then:

$$P_d = P_{\text{eff}} \cdot P$$

Where **P** is the rated power transmitted.

---

## 2.3 Worm Gear Geometry

### 2.3.1 Center Distance

$$C = \frac{d_w + D}{2}$$

Where:
- **d_w** = Pitch diameter of the worm wheel
- **D** = Pitch diameter of the worm
- **C** = Center distance

### 2.3.2 Lead, Lead Angle, and Velocity Ratio

$$\text{Lead } (L) = z_1 \times p_x = z_1 \times \pi \times m$$

$$\tan \lambda = \frac{L}{\pi D} = \frac{z_1 \cdot m}{D}$$

$$\text{Velocity Ratio } (i) = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{z_2}{z_1}$$

Where:
- **z₁** = Number of threads (starts) on worm
- **z₂** = Number of teeth on worm wheel
- **m** = Axial module of worm = transverse module of worm wheel
- **D** = Pitch diameter of worm
- **λ** = Lead angle

### 2.3.3 Worm Wheel Tooth Geometry
The worm wheel is essentially a **helical gear** that meshes with the worm. The worm's **axial pitch (p_x)** must equal the worm wheel's **transverse circular pitch (p_t)**:

$$p_x = p_t = \pi m$$

### 2.3.4 Proportions of the Worm

| Parameter | Standard Value (KTU) |
|---|---|
| Pitch diameter (D) | **D = m × q** where **q = 6, 8, 10, 12, 15, 20** (standard q values) |
| Centre distance (C) | **0.875 × (D + d_w)** (approx.) |
| Lead (L) | **L = z₁ × π × m** |
| Length of worm (l) | **l = (6 + z₂) × m** (approx., for ZI worms) |
| Throat diameter of worm wheel | **d_{th} = d_w + 2a** where **a = m** |
| Outside diameter of worm wheel | **D_0 = d_w + 1.5m** (for ZA worm) |

---

## 2.4 Worm Gear Design Equations

### 2.4.1 Wear (Surface Durability) Equation — **Most Critical for KTU**

The **most commonly asked KTU question** on worm gears uses the **wear equation** based on **Buckingham's wear load**:

$$F_w = K \cdot d_w \cdot b \cdot \cos \lambda$$

Where:
- **F_w** = Wear load (N)
- **K** = Load stress factor (≈ 0.50 N/mm² for steel worm and phosphor-bronze wheel)
- **d_w** = Pitch diameter of worm wheel (mm)
- **b** = Face width of worm wheel (mm)
- **λ** = Lead angle

The face width of the worm wheel:

$$b = 0.73 \cdot d_w \quad \text{or} \quad b = 2.5 \times \text{axial pitch}$$

### 2.4.2 Bending Strength Equation

$$F_b = \pi \cdot m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y$$

Where **Y** is the form factor for worm wheel teeth (for 20° pressure angle, **Y = 0.124 + 0.684/T** when T is in the range 1-3, and **Y ≈ 0.154** for higher T).

### 2.4.3 Effective Load and Design Power

$$P_d = C_s \cdot C_v \cdot P$$

Where:
- **C_s** = Service factor (1.25 to 1.5 for uniform load, 1.5 to 1.75 for moderate shocks, etc.)
- **C_v** = Velocity factor
- **P** = Rated power

### 2.4.4 Thermal Capacity (Heat Dissipation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Worm gears generate significant heat due to sliding friction. The **thermal capacity** equation ensures safe operating temperature:

$$P_{\text{rated}} = \frac{H \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{1000 \cdot C_s}$$

Or in terms of allowable power:

$$P_{\text{allow}} = \frac{H \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{1000 \cdot C_s}$$

Where:
- **H** = Heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·°C), typical values:
  - Natural convection, no fan: **H = 8 to 12 W/m²·°C**
  - With cooling fan: **H = 17 to 21 W/m²·°C**
  - With fan and oil splash: **H = 25 to 35 W/m²·°C**
- **A** = Effective heat dissipation surface area (m²)
- **T₁ - T₂** = Allowable temperature rise (typically **40°C to 50°C**)
- **C_s** = Service factor

### 2.4.5 Efficiency of Worm Gear

$$\eta = \frac{\tan \lambda (1 - \mu \tan \lambda)}{\tan \lambda + \mu}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\eta = \frac{\cos \alpha - \mu \tan \lambda}{\cos \alpha + \mu \cot \lambda}$$

Where:
- **μ** = Coefficient of friction (≈ 0.04 to 0.08 for steel worm + bronze wheel with good lubrication)
- **α** = Normal pressure angle (20°)
- **λ** = Lead angle

**Self-locking condition:** λ ≤ (90° - 2.5 × φ), where **φ = tan⁻¹(μ)** is the friction angle. For practical purposes, **self-locking occurs when λ < 5°**.

---

## 2.5 Force Analysis

### 2.5.1 Forces on a Bevel Gear Pinion (Shaft Angle = 90°)

Let **P_t** = Tangential force on pinion. The three components are:

| Force on Pinion | Magnitude | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| **Tangential (P_t)** | $\frac{P}{v_p}$ | Along pitch circle (driving direction) |
| **Radial / Separating (P_r)** | $P_t \tan \alpha \cos \gamma$ | Toward cone center |
| **Axial / Thrust (P_a)** | $P_t \tan \alpha \sin \gamma$ | Along shaft (away from cone apex) |

> [!NOTE]
> For the **gear**, by **Newton's third law**:
> - Tangential force on gear = **P_t** (opposite direction).
> - Radial force on gear = **Axial force on pinion** = $P_t \tan \alpha \sin \gamma$
> - Axial force on gear = **Radial force on pinion** = $P_t \tan \alpha \cos \gamma$

### 2.5.2 Forces on a Worm and Worm Wheel

For a worm driving a worm wheel (shaft angle 90°):

**Forces on the Worm (Driving Element):**
- **Tangential force (W_{t1})** = $\frac{2P}{v_1}$ (along pitch diameter, opposing motion)
- **Radial force (W_{r1})** = $W_{t1} \tan \alpha_n$ (toward center of worm)
- **Axial / Thrust force (W_{a1})** = $\frac{W_{t1}}{\tan \lambda}$ (along worm axis)

**Forces on the Worm Wheel (Driven Element):**
- **Tangential force (W_{t2})** = $\frac{2P}{v_2}$ (opposes W_{a1})
- **Radial force (W_{r2})** = $W_{t2} \tan \alpha_n$ (toward center)
- **Axial / Thrust force (W_{a2})** = $W_{t1} \tan \lambda$ (along wheel axis, resists W_{t1})

Where **v₁** = worm pitch line velocity = $\frac{\pi D N_1}{60}$ and **v₂** = worm wheel pitch line velocity = $\frac{\pi d_w N_2}{60}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key relationship:** The **tangential force on the worm (W_{t1}) equals the axial force on the wheel (W_{a2})**, and the **axial force on the worm (W_{a1}) equals the tangential force on the wheel (W_{t2})**. This is analogous to a **screw and nut mechanism**.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Comprehensive Table)

| # | Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma = \tan^{-1}(Z_P / Z_G)$ | Pinion pitch angle (90° shaft) |
| 2 | $\Gamma = 90° - \gamma$ | Gear pitch angle |
| 3 | $Z_v = Z / \cos \gamma$ | Virtual number of teeth |
| 4 | $F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y \cdot K_v$ | Bevel gear beam strength |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Gear Type | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Automobile differential** | Hypoid / Spiral Bevel | Right-angle drive, smooth, quiet |
| **Hand drill / Power tool** | Straight / Spiral Bevel | Compact right-angle drive |
| **Conveyor speed reducers** | Worm gear | Self-locking, high reduction |
| **Elevators / Lifts** | Worm gear | Safety (no back-driving) |
| **Car steering (older vehicles)** | Worm and sector | Self-locking + large reduction |
| **Printing presses, mixers** | Worm gear | Compact, smooth, damped operation |
| **Rolling mills, cranes** | Spiral Bevel | High torque, heavy duty |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pinion Pitch Angle for Bevel Gears

### 3.1.1 Geometric Setup
Consider two bevel gears with intersecting axes at 90°. The pitch cones touch along a common line and have a common apex **O**. Let:
- **R** = pitch cone radius (length of slant from apex to base)
- **D** = large pitch diameter
- **d** = small pitch diameter (pinion)
- **γ** = pitch angle of pinion
- **Γ** = pitch angle of gear

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** From the geometry of the larger cone (gear):

$$\tan \Gamma = \frac{D/2}{R} = \frac{D}{2R}$$

**Step 2:** From the geometry of the smaller cone (pinion):

$$\tan \gamma = \frac{d/2}{R} = \frac{d}{2R}$$

**Step 3:** Divide the two equations:

$$\frac{\tan \Gamma}{\tan \gamma} = \frac{D}{d}$$

**Step 4:** The gear ratio (in terms of teeth) is:

$$i = \frac{Z_G}{Z_P} = \frac{D}{d} = \frac{N_P}{N_G}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore:

$$\tan \Gamma = i \cdot \tan \gamma$$

**Step 6:** For 90° shaft angle: $\gamma + \Gamma = 90°$, so $\Gamma = 90° - \gamma$, which means $\tan \Gamma = \cot \gamma$.

**Step 7:** Substituting:

$$\cot \gamma = i \cdot \tan \gamma \implies \frac{1}{\tan \gamma} = i \tan \gamma$$

$$\implies \tan^2 \gamma = \frac{1}{i} = \frac{N_G}{N_P} = \frac{Z_P}{Z_G}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma = \tan^{-1}\sqrt{\frac{Z_P}{Z_G}} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{N_G}{N_P}\right)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Virtual Number of Teeth (Z_v)

### 3.2.1 Concept
A bevel gear's tooth is **tapered** — the module increases from the apex to the base. To apply the **Lewis form factor** (derived for spur gears), we project the bevel gear tooth onto a **spur gear of radius R** (the pitch cone radius). The equivalent spur gear has the same tooth profile but at constant circular pitch.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

**Step 1:** Pitch circle radius of bevel gear (pinion) at mean position: **r_m = d/2** (using mean diameter).

**Step 2:** The **back cone** of the bevel gear is constructed with apex at the pitch cone apex and element perpendicular to the gear's pitch element.

**Step 3:** Radius of the back cone at the large end (where the spur gear equivalent is drawn):

$$R_b = \frac{d/2}{\cos \gamma}$$

**Step 4:** The equivalent spur gear has its pitch radius equal to **R_b** and circular pitch **p = πm**.

**Step 5:** Number of teeth on this virtual spur gear:

$$Z_v = \frac{\pi \cdot 2 R_b}{p} = \frac{2 \pi R_b}{\pi m} = \frac{2 R_b}{m} = \frac{2}{m} \cdot \frac{d/2}{\cos \gamma} = \frac{d}{m \cos \gamma} = \frac{Z_P \cdot m}{m \cos \gamma}$$

$$\boxed{Z_v = \frac{Z_P}{\cos \gamma}}$$

This **virtual gear concept** allows us to use the **Lewis form factor Y** from standard spur gear tables.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Forces on Bevel Gear Pinion (90° Shaft Angle)

### 3.3.1 Setup
The tooth force **F** acts along the pressure line (at pressure angle α) on the mean cone. Decompose into three components using the **pitch angle γ**.

### 3.3.2 Derivation

**Step 1:** The **tangential force** is along the pitch cone at the mean radius:

$$P_t = \frac{\text{Torque}}{r_m} = \frac{2P}{v_p}$$

**Step 2:** The total tooth force **F** is along the pressure line; in the normal plane (perpendicular to the pitch cone surface), it makes angle **α** with the tangent:

$$F = \frac{P_t}{\cos \alpha}$$

**Step 3:** Decompose **F** into a **radial component (in the plane of the pitch cone)** and a **normal-to-cone component**:

The **radial component** (away from the cone center, in the pitch cone plane):

$$F_r' = F \sin \alpha = P_t \tan \alpha$$

**Step 4:** This radial component is further resolved into the **true radial (P_r)** and **axial (P_a)** directions using the pitch angle γ:

$$P_r = F_r' \cos \gamma = P_t \tan \alpha \cos \gamma$$

$$P_a = F_r' \sin \gamma = P_t \tan \alpha \sin \gamma$$

**Step 5:** Final boxed equations:

$$\boxed{P_t = \frac{2P}{v_p}}$$

$$\boxed{P_r = P_t \tan \alpha \cos \gamma}$$

$$\boxed{P_a = P_t \tan \alpha \sin \gamma}$$

Where:
- **v_p** = pitch line velocity of pinion = $\frac{\pi d N_P}{60}$
- **α** = pressure angle (20°)
- **γ** = pitch angle of pinion

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Worm Gear Efficiency (Sliding + Rolling)

### 3.4.1 Power In, Power Out, and Heat Generated

**Step 1:** Input power at worm shaft (driving):

$$P_{\text{in}} = W_{t1} \cdot v_1$$

where **v₁** is the pitch line velocity of the worm.

**Step 2:** Output power at worm wheel (driven):

$$P_{\text{out}} = W_{t2} \cdot v_2$$

**Step 3:** For equilibrium: $W_{a1} = W_{t2}$ and $W_{t1} = W_{a2}$. Also, $v_2 = v_1 \cos \lambda$ (since v₂ is the component of v₁ perpendicular to worm axis, projected onto wheel's pitch circle).

**Step 4:** Efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{W_{t2} \cdot v_2}{W_{t1} \cdot v_1} = \frac{W_{a1} \cdot v_1 \cos \lambda}{W_{t1} \cdot v_1} = \frac{W_{a1}}{W_{t1}} \cos \lambda$$

**Step 5:** Now, considering friction: The friction force opposes sliding along the worm axis. Friction angle φ = tan⁻¹(μ). The normal force N, the friction force μN, and the resultant must balance the applied force W_{t1}.

**Step 6:** From the force triangle (involving W_{t1}, W_{a1}, μN, and N):

$$W_{a1} = W_{t1} \frac{\cos \alpha - \mu \tan \lambda}{\cos \alpha + \mu \cot \lambda}$$

**Step 7:** Substituting back:

$$\eta = \frac{W_{a1}}{W_{t1}} \cos \lambda = \frac{(\cos \alpha - \mu \tan \lambda) \cos \lambda}{\cos \alpha + \mu \cot \lambda}$$

**Step 8:** An alternative form using **tan λ**:

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{\tan \lambda (1 - \mu \tan \lambda)}{\tan \lambda + \mu}}$$

This equation shows that **efficiency increases as lead angle λ increases**, peaking around **λ ≈ 45°** for μ ≈ 0.05.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Design of a Bevel Gear Pair

> **Problem:** A bevel gear pair transmits 10 kW at 1450 rpm of the pinion. The speed ratio is 3:1. Shaft angle is 90°. Design the gear pair using Lewis equation. Assume σ_b = 100 N/mm² for pinion, σ_b = 80 N/mm² for gear, C_s = 1.5, C_v = 1.25, C_mc = 1.25. Service factor for moderate shock. Take Y = 0.32.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Velocity ratio and pitch angle**
Ratio = 3, so Z_G = 3 Z_P. For 90° shaft:

$$\gamma = \tan^{-1}\sqrt{\frac{Z_P}{Z_G}} = \tan^{-1}\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} = \tan^{-1}(0.577) = 30°$$

$$\Gamma = 90° - 30° = 60°$$

**Step 2: Design power**
$$P_d = P \times C_s \times C_v = 10 \times 1.5 \times 1.25 = 18.75 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Design tangential load**
Assume velocity v_p = 5 m/s (assume), then:

$$P_t = \frac{2 P_d \times 10^3}{v_p} = \frac{2 \times 18750}{5} = 7500 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Beam strength equation**

$$F_b = \pi \cdot m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y$$

Assume face width b = 10m (a common assumption):

$$F_b = \pi \cdot m \cdot 10m \cdot 100 \cdot 0.32 = 1005.3 m^2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Equate F_b = P_t**

$$1005.3 m^2 = 7500 \implies m^2 = 7.46 \implies m = 2.73 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Round up and verify**

Take **m = 3 mm**. Then:
- Z_P = 20 (assumed), Z_G = 60
- Pinion pitch diameter: **d = m × Z_P = 60 mm**
- Gear pitch diameter: **D = 3 × 60 = 180 mm**
- Face width: **b = 0.3 × R = 0.3 × 90 = 27 mm**
- Velocity check: $v_p = \frac{\pi \times 60 \times 1450}{60000} = 4.56 \text{ m/s}$ ✓ (close to assumed)

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Worm Gear Design

> **Problem:** Design a worm gear pair to transmit 7.5 kW at 1440 rpm. Speed ratio is 24. Take m = 6 mm, z₁ = 4, q = 8, b = 50 mm. K = 0.56 N/mm², C_s = 1.25, μ = 0.04, α = 20°.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Worm geometry**
- Pitch diameter of worm: D = m × q = 6 × 8 = **48 mm**
- Lead: L = z₁ × π × m = 4 × π × 6 = **75.4 mm**
- Lead angle: $\tan \lambda = L / (\pi D) = 75.4 / (π × 48) = 0.5$

$$\lambda = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57°$$

**Step 2: Worm wheel**
- Number of teeth: z₂ = z₁ × i = 4 × 24 = **96**
- Pitch diameter: d_w = m × z₂ = 6 × 96 = **576 mm**
- Centre distance: C = (48 + 576)/2 = **312 mm**

**Step 3: Velocities**
- Worm velocity: $v_1 = \frac{\pi D N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 48 \times 1440}{60000} = 3.62 \text{ m/s}$
- Wheel velocity: $v_2 = v_1 \cos \lambda = 3.62 \times 0.894 = 3.24 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 4: Tangential force on worm**
$$W_{t1} = \frac{2 \times 7500}{3.62} = 4143 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Wear load capacity**
$$F_w = K d_w b \cos \lambda = 0.56 \times 576 \times 50 \times 0.894 = 14418 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6: Check safety**
Design load = W_{t1} = 4143 N < F_w = 14418 N ✓ **Safe in wear.**

**Step 7: Efficiency**
$$\eta = \frac{\tan \lambda (1 - \mu \tan \lambda)}{\tan \lambda + \mu} = \frac{0.5 (1 - 0.04 \times 0.5)}{0.5 + 0.04} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.98}{0.54} = 0.907 = 90.7\%$$

**Step 8: Axial force on worm (thrust)**
$$W_{a1} = \frac{W_{t1}}{\tan \lambda} = \frac{4143}{0.5} = 8286 \text{ N}$$

> This is a **typical KTU 14-mark question** structure.

## 4.1 Bevel Gear Pair — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Shaft Pinion Z_P Teeth Z_P] --> B[Pitch Cone Angle gamma]
    A --> C[Virtual Gear Concept Z_v = Z_P over cos gamma]
    B --> D[Forces Tangential Pt]
    B --> E[Forces Radial Pr = Pt tan alpha cos gamma]
    B --> F[Forces Axial Pa = Pt tan alpha sin gamma]
    C --> G[Lewis Form Factor Y from Z_v]
    G --> H[Beam Strength Fb = pi m b sigma b Y]
    I[Output Shaft Gear Z_G Teeth Z_G] --> J[Pitch Cone Angle Gamma = 90 - gamma]
    I --> K[Forces Reciprocal of Pinion]
    H --> L[Design Check Fb vs Pt]
    E --> L
    F --> L
    L --> M{Design Safe?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Final Design with Module m and Face Width b]
    M -->|No| O[Revise m or Z]
    O --> A
```

## 4.2 Worm Gear — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Worm z1 starts] --> B[Pitch Diameter D = m q]
    A --> C[Lead L = z1 pi m]
    C --> D[Lead Angle lambda = arctan L over pi D]
    B --> E[Worm Velocity v1 = pi D N1 over 60]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Tangential Force Wt1 = 2P over v1]
    F --> G[Axial Force on Worm Wa1 = Wt1 over tan lambda]
    F --> H[Wear Load Fw = K dw b cos lambda]
    H --> I{Design Safe?}
    G --> J[Efficiency eta = tan lambda 1 minus mu tan lambda over tan lambda plus mu]
    J --> K[Thermal Check P_allow = H A dT over 1000 Cs]
    K --> L[Self Locking Check lambda less than 5 degrees]
    I -->|Yes| M[Final Worm Gear Design]
    I -->|No| N[Increase Module or Face Width]
    N --> A
    L --> M
```

## 4.3 Force Diagram — Bevel Gear Pinion (3D Decomposition)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Tooth Force F] --> B[Resolve in Pitch Cone Plane]
    B --> C[Tangential Pt = F cos alpha]
    B --> D[In Plane Radial F sin alpha = Pt tan alpha]
    D --> E[Resolve with Pitch Angle gamma]
    E --> F[True Radial Pr = Pt tan alpha cos gamma]
    E --> G[Axial Pa = Pt tan alpha sin gamma]
    C --> H[Pinion Output Torque]
    F --> I[Shaft Bearing Load Radial]
    G --> J[Shaft Thrust Bearing Load]
```

## 4.4 Force Diagram — Worm and Worm Wheel Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Worm Driving Rotation] --> B[Forces on Worm]
    B --> B1[Tangential Wt1 opposing motion]
    B --> B2[Radial Wr1 toward worm center]
    B --> B3[Axial Wa1 along worm axis]
    A --> C[Forces on Worm Wheel by Newtons Third Law]
    C --> C1[Tangential Wt2 equals Wa1]
    C --> C2[Radial Wr2 equal in magnitude to Wr1]
    C --> C3[Axial Wa2 equals Wt1]
    B1 --> D[Resistive Torque at Wheel]
    C1 --> D
    D --> E[Efficiency Output eta]
    B3 --> F[Thrust Bearing Selection]
    C3 --> F
```

## 4.5 Comparison Block — Bevel vs Worm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bevel Gear] --> A1[Intersecting Shafts 90 degree]
    A --> A2[Rolling plus Sliding]
    A --> A3[Ratio up to 6 to 1]
    A --> A4[Uses Lewis and Buckingham equations]
    B[Worm Gear] --> B1[Non Intersecting Non Parallel Shafts]
    B --> B2[Mostly Sliding Contact]
    B --> B3[Ratio 5 to 1 up to 100 to 1]
    B --> B4[Uses Wear Bending and Thermal equations]
    A --> C[KTU Design Procedure]
    B --> C
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
> **Define the term "virtual number of teeth" for a bevel gear. Why is it used?**

**Model Answer:**

The **virtual number of teeth (Z_v)** of a bevel gear is defined as the number of teeth that the equivalent **spur gear** (projected onto the back cone) would have if its circular pitch were the same as the mean circular pitch of the bevel gear.

$$Z_v = \frac{Z}{\cos \gamma}$$

where **Z** is the actual number of teeth and **γ** is the pitch angle of the bevel gear.

It is used because:
1. The teeth of a bevel gear are **tapered** (variable module), so the **Lewis form factor Y** (tabulated for spur gears) cannot be applied directly.
2. The **virtual gear concept** converts the tapered tooth to an equivalent spur gear with constant module.
3. This allows the use of standard **Lewis equation** with Y obtained from tables based on **Z_v**.

---

> **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> **State the self-locking condition for a worm gear. When is it desirable?**

**Model Answer:**

A worm gear is **self-locking** when the worm wheel **cannot drive the worm** under load, even if the driving torque on the worm is removed. The condition is:

$$\lambda \leq (90° - 2.5 \phi) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \lambda < 5°$$

where **λ** is the lead angle and **φ = tan⁻¹(μ)** is the friction angle.

**Self-locking is desirable in applications where:**
- **Safety is critical** — e.g., **lifts, elevators, hoists, cranes, screw jacks**.
- Holding the load **without continuous braking** is essential.
- **Back-driving must be prevented** to avoid runaway motion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]

> **A bevel gear pair transmits 22 kW at 720 rpm of the pinion. The speed ratio is 3.5:1. The shaft angle is 90°. Design the gear pair using the Lewis equation. Assume the pinion has 18 teeth. Take σ_b = 105 N/mm² for pinion and σ_b = 80 N/mm² for gear. C_s = 1.5, C_v = 1.25, K_v = 1.0, m = 8 mm (assume). Use Lewis form factor for pinion Y = 0.32 and for gear Y = 0.39.**

#### (a) Calculate the pitch cone angles and the design power. [7 Marks]

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Given data**
- Power P = 22 kW
- Pinion speed N_P = 720 rpm
- Ratio = 3.5 = N_P / N_G = Z_G / Z_P
- Z_P = 18, so Z_G = 3.5 × 18 = **63 teeth**
- Σ = 90°

**Step 2: Pitch cone angles**
$$\tan \gamma = \frac{N_G}{N_P} = \frac{1}{3.5} = 0.2857$$

$$\gamma = \tan^{-1}(0.2857) = 15.95° \approx 16°$$

$$\Gamma = 90° - 16° = 74°$$

**Step 3: Design power**
$$P_d = P \times C_s \times C_v = 22 \times 1.5 \times 1.25 = 41.25 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Velocity of pinion**
- Pitch diameter: d = m × Z_P = 8 × 18 = 144 mm = 0.144 m
- v_p = (π × 0.144 × 720) / 60 = 5.43 m/s

#### (b) Determine the beam strength and check the design. [7 Marks]

**Step 5: Tangential force**
$$P_t = \frac{2 P_d \times 10^3}{v_p} = \frac{2 \times 41250}{5.43} = 15193 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6: Virtual number of teeth**
For pinion: $Z_{v,P} = \frac{Z_P}{\cos \gamma} = \frac{18}{\cos 16°} = \frac{18}{0.9613} = 18.73$

**Step 7: Face width**
$$b = 0.3 \times R, \quad R = \frac{D}{2 \sin \Gamma} = \frac{m Z_G}{2 \sin \Gamma} = \frac{8 \times 63}{2 \sin 74°} = \frac{504}{1.923} = 262.1 \text{ mm}$$

$$b = 0.3 \times 262.1 = 78.6 \text{ mm}$$

But standard practice: b ≤ 0.3R and b ≤ 10m. Take b = min(78.6, 80) = **78.6 mm** (or 10m = 80 mm).

**Step 8: Beam strength (pinion)**
$$F_{b,P} = \pi \cdot m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y_P = \pi \times 8 \times 78.6 \times 105 \times 0.32 = 66300 \text{ N}$$

**Step 9: Beam strength (gear)**
$$F_{b,G} = \pi \times 8 \times 78.6 \times 80 \times 0.39 = 61568 \text{ N}$$

**Step 10: Design check**
- F_{b,P} = 66300 N > P_t = 15193 N ✓
- F_{b,G} = 61568 N > P_t = 15193 N ✓
- **Factor of safety (pinion) = 66300 / 15193 = 4.36 ✓**

**Design is safe.**

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 4]

> **A worm gear pair is required to transmit 3.7 kW from an electric motor running at 1440 rpm to a machine running at 60 rpm. The shaft angle is 90°. Take q = 6, m = 6 mm, z₁ = 2, K = 0.56 N/mm², σ_b = 84 N/mm², C_s = 1.25, μ = 0.05, α = 20°.**

#### (a) Design the worm gear pair using the wear strength equation. [7 Marks]

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Velocity ratio**
$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{1440}{60} = 24$$

**Step 2: Worm teeth and wheel teeth**
- z₁ = 2 (given)
- z₂ = z₁ × i = 2 × 24 = **48 teeth**

**Step 3: Worm geometry**
- Pitch diameter: D = m × q = 6 × 6 = **36 mm**
- Lead: L = z₁ × π × m = 2 × π × 6 = **37.7 mm**
- Lead angle: $\tan \lambda = \frac{L}{\pi D} = \frac{37.7}{\pi \times 36} = 0.3333$, so **λ = 18.43°**

**Step 4: Worm wheel**
- Pitch diameter: d_w = m × z₂ = 6 × 48 = **288 mm**
- Face width: b = 0.73 × d_w = 0.73 × 288 = **210 mm**
  (or b = 2.5 × π × m = 47 mm, take the smaller = 47 mm, but conventionally use larger: b = 47 mm)

**Step 5: Velocities**
- Worm: $v_1 = \frac{\pi \times 36 \times 1440}{60000} = 2.71 \text{ m/s}$
- Wheel: $v_2 = v_1 \cos \lambda = 2.71 \times 0.949 = 2.57 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 6: Design power and tangential force on worm**
$$P_d = P \times C_s = 3.7 \times 1.25 = 4.625 \text{ kW}$$

$$W_{t1} = \frac{2 \times 4625}{2.71} = 3413 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7: Wear load capacity**
$$F_w = K \cdot d_w \cdot b \cdot \cos \lambda = 0.56 \times 288 \times 47 \times 0.949 = 7194 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8: Check design**
- F_w = 7194 N > W_{t1} = 3413 N ✓
- **Factor of safety = 7194 / 3413 = 2.11 ✓**

#### (b) Calculate the efficiency of the worm gear and the axial thrust on the worm. [7 Marks]

**Step 9: Efficiency**
$$\eta = \frac{\tan \lambda (1 - \mu \tan \lambda)}{\tan \lambda + \mu} = \frac{0.3333 (1 - 0.05 \times 0.3333)}{0.3333 + 0.05} = \frac{0.3333 \times 0.9833}{0.3833} = 0.855$$

**η = 85.5%**

**Step 10: Axial thrust on worm**
$$W_{a1} = \frac{W_{t1}}{\tan \lambda} = \frac{3413}{0.3333} = 10240 \text{ N}$$

**Step 11: Check self-locking**
- Friction angle: φ = tan⁻¹(0.05) = 2.86°
- (90° - 2.5φ) = 90° - 7.15° = 82.85°
- λ = 18.43° < 82.85° → **Self-locking condition satisfied ✓**

**Step 12: Tangential force on worm wheel**
$$W_{t2} = W_{a1} = 10240 \text{ N}$$

**Step 13: Axial force on worm wheel**
$$W_{a2} = W_{t1} = 3413 \text{ N}$$

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Marks Lost by KTU Students:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Z_v and Z in Lewis equation**: Students often use the actual number of teeth (Z) to look up the Lewis form factor Y. **Always** use **Z_v = Z / cos γ** for bevel gears. Marks lost: **2 marks per question**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the design power factor C_s**: Using rated power directly in place of design power (P_d = C_s × C_v × P). Marks lost: **2-3 marks**.
>
> 3. **Mixing up the wear equation for bevel vs worm gears**:
>    - Bevel gear: $F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot K \cdot Q$
>    - Worm gear: $F_w = K \cdot d_w \cdot b \cdot \cos \lambda$
>    The worm gear equation has the **cos λ** term — many students forget this! Marks lost: **3 marks**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to check self-locking for worm gears**: KTU examiners specifically check for this. Marks lost: **2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Force direction errors**: For bevel gears, the radial force on the pinion equals the axial force on the gear (and vice versa). For worm gears, the **tangential force on the worm = axial force on the wheel**. Forgetting this reciprocity is a common error.
>
> 6. **Units confusion in thermal capacity**: A is in **m²** (not mm²), H is in **W/m²·°C**, and P_allow is in **kW**. Marks lost: **2-3 marks**.
>
> 7. **Standard values of q not remembered**: Standard q values are 6, 8, 10, 12, 15, 20. Marks lost: **1-2 marks** for wrong value of D.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bevel & Worm Gear Design**

### Bevel Gears
- ☐ Bevel gears transmit motion between **intersecting shafts** (typically 90°).
- ☐ **Pitch angles**: γ = tan⁻¹(Z_P / Z_G), Γ = 90° - γ.
- ☐ **Virtual number of teeth**: Z_v = Z / cos γ — used to look up Lewis form factor Y.
- ☐ **Face width** b = 0.3R (or 0.3 × D/2), where R is the pitch cone radius.
- ☐ **Beam strength**: F_b = π × m × b × σ_b × Y × K_v.
- ☐ **Wear strength**: F_w = d_p × b × K × Q.
- ☐ **Standard K for steel pinion + cast iron gear = 0.16 N/mm²**.
- ☐ Forces: P_t = 2P/v, P_r = P_t tan α cos γ, P_a = P_t tan α sin γ.
- ☐ Radial force on pinion = Axial force on gear (and vice versa).

### Worm Gears
- ☐ Worm gears transmit motion between **non-intersecting, non-parallel shafts** (90°).
- ☐ **Worm pitch diameter** D = m × q, where q is the **diameter quotient** (standard: 6, 8, 10, 12, 15, 20).
- ☐ **Lead** L = z₁ × π × m; **Lead angle** tan λ = L / (πD).
- ☐ **Worm wheel pitch diameter** d_w = m × z₂.
- ☐ **Centre distance** C = (D + d_w)/2.
- ☐ **Wear equation** (most important): F_w = K × d_w × b × cos λ.
- ☐ **Standard K** for steel worm + phosphor-bronze wheel = **0.50 N/mm²** (range 0.50 to 0.56).
- ☐ **Bending equation**: F_b = π × m × b × σ_b × Y.
- ☐ **Efficiency**: η = tan λ (1 - μ tan λ) / (tan λ + μ).
- ☐ **Self-locking**: λ < 5° (or λ < 90° - 2.5φ).
- ☐ **Thermal capacity**: P_allow = H × A × ΔT / (1000 × C_s).
- ☐ **Force reciprocity**: W_{t1} = W_{a2} and W_{a1} = W_{t2}.
- ☐ **Axial thrust on worm** W_{a1} = W_{t1} / tan λ (often very large — needs proper thrust bearing).

### Numerical Values to Memorize
- ☐ Pressure angle α = 20° (standard).
- ☐ Standard module series: 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20 mm.
- ☐ Velocity ratio range: bevel (1:1 to 6:1), worm (5:1 to 100:1).
- ☐ Material: Pinion **harder** than gear (steel pinion + cast iron gear; or steel + bronze).

### KTU Examiner's Tip
> [!NOTE]
> In the **14-mark question**, always follow this structure to score full marks:
> 1. Given data and assumption (2 marks)
> 2. Geometric calculations (pitch angles, virtual teeth, velocities) (3 marks)
> 3. Force calculation (P_t or W_{t1}) (2 marks)
> 4. Beam/wear strength computation (3 marks)
> 5. Design check and factor of safety (2 marks)
> 6. Final summary with module, face width, materials (2 marks)
